Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and structured around the theory, covering transition timing (5 points), CPT budget (5 points), operator design (6 strategies), activation ratio (4 levels), and architecture (interleaved vs. full MoE). The theory-to-experiment mapping is explicit: each ablation tests a specific prediction of Theorem 3.1. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported — standard practice for pretraining-scale experiments but a limitation for the 7B main result. The authors use disjoint pre-training and CPT data splits, which correctly avoids data leakage. Ablation experiments use DCLM; the 7B main experiment uses a curated internal mixture emphasizing instruction following, reasoning, and math — this data mixture difference means the 7B result may not be directly comparable to the ablation recipe. Code and training configurations are released at https://github.com/amazon-science/expert-upcycling. This is a preprint; the results have not undergone peer review. The convexity assumption underlying Theorem 3.1 is explicitly acknowledged as non-literal and the authors point to empirical justification from [45, 4] for the theory's predictive validity. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on NVIDIA A100 GPU clusters using standard data parallelism and tensor parallelism; no specialized hardware required beyond what frontier MoE training already demands. - Efficient: The 7B→13B experiment measures smE ≈ 1.9×sE per step; expert upcycling saves ~32% GPU hours vs. fixed-size training when τ≈2/3T, and ~67% when starting from an existing checkpoint. The operator itself (gradient computation + expert duplication) is a one-time cost at transition step τ. - Effective: At 100% CPT, the upcycled model matches Fixed-64 on both validation loss and 11 downstream benchmarks; the evidence that it solves the stated problem (quality-matched, compute-efficient capacity expansion) is direct and convincing at the tested scale. - Scalable: The method is validated only at up to 7B total parameters and expansion factor m=2 (doubling); the authors explicitly acknowledge that scaling to frontier models, larger m, or distribution shift between pre-training and CPT may introduce router collapse or balancing brittleness. No experiments at 70B+ total parameters are reported. The software artifact (code + training configs) is publicly released. Strengths: - The theoretical framework is tightly coupled to the experimental design: Term I and Term II of Theorem 3.1 each have dedicated ablation tables, and the framework correctly predicts the qualitative reversal (upcycling efficiency increases with pre-training duration in MoE→MoE, opposite to dense→MoE) observed in Table 3a vs. the scaling-law findings of Liew et al. [32]. - The counterintuitive finding that diversity-inducing initialization (20 methods) uniformly fails to beat copy-paste is well-documented across expert and router levels; the Spearman rank-correlation analysis (ρ=0.80/0.86, n=65 runs) quantitatively establishes that initialization quality dominates over initialization diversity, which is a non-obvious and broadly applicable insight. - The comparison to sparse upcycling (dense→MoE) across four activation ratios is a direct competitive test that contextualizes when expert upcycling is preferable; the widening gap at lower activation ratios (0.026→0.241) is a clean, predictable result grounded in Term I. - Code and training configurations are released, enabling practical adoption. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that experiments validate m=2 (doubling) only; larger expansion factors and frontier model scales may introduce new failure modes (router collapse, balancing brittleness). They acknowledge that distribution shift between pre-training and CPT is an unaddressed risk. The theoretical convexity assumption is directly confronted and its non-literalness acknowledged. These are confronted credibly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 7B main experiment uses a curated CPT data mixture biased toward instruction following and math, while ablations use DCLM — this confounds the data effect with the architectural effect, making it unclear how much of the strong 7B result is attributable to the CPT data quality versus the upcycling method itself. No evaluation of inference throughput or latency is provided; the paper focuses on training compute, but the implication that inference cost is unchanged assumes identical deployment parallelism, which may not hold when expert count doubles and communication patterns change. Downstream benchmark evaluation is absent for all ablation runs (only validation loss), making it impossible to verify that gap closure in perplexity translates to downstream improvement at the 1B ablation scale — the authors justify this by citing [54] on emergent abilities, but this is a weak defense given that several benchmarks (ARC, HellaSwag) are reliably informative at 1B. No analysis of expert specialization post-upcycling (e.g., expert utilization entropy, routing overlap between duplicated pairs) is provided, leaving the claimed symmetry-breaking mechanism unobserved directly. Bottom Line: A practitioner training or upgrading MoE models gains concrete value from this work: the practical recipe (upcycle at τ/T ≤ 0.25, budget ≥ 50% CPT, use gradient-norm importance scores) is actionable, the 32% compute saving is well-supported at 7B scale, and the code is public — but the absence of experiments beyond m=2 and 7B total parameters means adopters at frontier scale are extrapolating beyond the evidence.
Recommended 2604.19835 PDF
#7 — Highly Innovative (78)

Understanding Evolving Graph Structures for Large Discrete-Time Dynamic Graph Representation

vldb.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical/systems profile. Rigor (80) strong — thorough ablations across 8 datasets, 10 runs with std, public code, PVLDB peer-reviewed. Novelty (75) — temporal influence score with proven properties is genuinely new. Non-obviousness (75) — replacing adjacency matrix with bounded score-ranked store is a principled insight. Completeness (75) and Resilience (75) solid; some gaps on inductive setting and hyperparameter ρ. Impact (85) — single-GPU framework outperforms multi-GPU TGL by 94× sampling and 10× training, with released artifact. Weighted (systems): 0.15*75+0.15*80+0.10*75+0.15*75+0.15*75+0.30*85 = ~78.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 75 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UnderGS, a scalable GPU-resident framework for discrete-time dynamic graph (DTDG) representation that replaces adjacency-matrix neighbor management with a temporal-cohesive neighbor store, achieving up to 9× training speed-up and 31.36% average accuracy improvement over nine baselines across eight datasets. - Why it's important: Adjacency-matrix-based neighbor management for DTDGs consumes O(T|V|²) memory, prevents GPU-native batch processing, and captures only binary connectivity — making existing methods unable to scale to graphs with millions of edges or to faithfully represent structural evolution across snapshots. - How it does it: A GPU key-value store retains the top-K neighbors per node scored by a composite temporal influence score combining structural cohesiveness (node importance via subgraph density), path-length decay (α₁^(l-1)), and snapshot recency decay (α₂^(t-t')), together with a late-snapshot gradient aggregation mechanism that prevents information leakage during batch training. - The good: UnderGS is the only evaluated method that scales to the Stack-Overflow (2.6M nodes, 63M edges, 92 snapshots) and Wiki-Talk (1.1M nodes, 7.8M edges) datasets without out-of-memory errors, while simultaneously outperforming all baselines on both AUC and MRR tasks. - The missing: All experiments run on a single RTX 3090 (24 GB); the paper makes no claim about multi-GPU performance, and scalability beyond this hardware — or behavior on graphs with denser temporal dynamics than the tested datasets — is unevaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and engineers working on DTDG representation learning, temporal GNNs, or scalable graph ML infrastructure; it provides a principled, hardware-aware alternative to adjacency-matrix neighbor management with strong empirical evidence. Problem & Motivation: Discrete-time dynamic graphs model systems (retail, finance, social networks) that evolve through discrete snapshots, where each snapshot is a static graph. The central challenge for representation learning is neighbor management: existing approaches either maintain a full adjacency matrix per snapshot — consuming O(T|V|²) memory and restricting scalability — or preprocess multiple adjacency matrices into node sequences for sequence models, incurring hours of preprocessing and CPU–GPU transfer overhead (e.g., FALCON requires 2.2 hours on Reddit-title). Both lines fail simultaneously: snapshot-focused GNN approaches lose cross-snapshot temporal context, while sequence-based approaches lose intra-snapshot structural context. Neither line scales to graphs with millions of nodes or hundreds of snapshots because they cannot batch-process efficiently on a single GPU. The gap is a neighbor management strategy that captures structural evolution, preserves temporal context, and fits in GPU memory without preprocessing. Key Idea: The core insight is to replace the adjacency matrix with a GPU-resident key-value store that, for each node, retains exactly K neighbors selected by a temporal influence score — a single scalar that fuses three quantities: structural importance of the neighbor (cohesiveness of its local subgraph), path-length discount (closer hops score higher), and snapshot recency discount (more recent interactions score higher). Because the store caps at K entries per node, total memory drops from O(T|V|²) to O(|V|K), independent of snapshot count. The store is maintained incrementally as new snapshots arrive: L-hop traversals compute influence scores for candidates; new neighbors displace incumbents if their scores are higher; a snapshot decay pass reduces all stored scores at snapshot boundaries. This design allows direct GPU access for message passing without preprocessing, and the influence score serves as an aggregation weight replacing degree normalization. Methodology: UnderGS consists of four interacting components. (1) Temporal influence score: For node vᵢ(t) and neighbor vⱼ(t') interacting over l hops, Iᵢⱼ(t) = Cⱼ(t') · α₁^(l-1) · α₂^(t-t'), where Cⱼ(t') is the node importance defined as a sum of exponential terms over the interaction counts within vⱼ's subgraph, α₁ ∈ (0,1) is the path-decay hyperparameter, and α₂ ∈ (0,1) is the snapshot-decay factor. Node importance distinguishes structurally cohesive neighbors from peripheral ones, which degree alone cannot. The paper proves three formal properties: Snapshot Decay (Theorem 4.6 — more recent neighbors are preferred when structurally comparable), Cohesive Priority (Theorem 4.7 — neighbors in cohesive subgraphs are retained over sparser ones), and Path Decay (Theorem 4.8 — direct neighbors score higher than equidistant distant ones). (2) Temporal-cohesive neighbor store: A GPU key-value store with O(1) lookup and update per node; initialized from scratch and updated incrementally per batch or snapshot; reduces storage from O(T|V|²) to O(|V|K). K=16 and L=2 in all experiments. (3) Time-aware filter: For nodes with sparse current-snapshot interactions, historical neighbors can dominate. A Bernoulli mask with probability ρ (tuned by dataset density: ρ=0.3 for high density, ρ=0.5 for low density) randomly zeros out historical neighbor influence scores before aggregation, shifting focus toward current interactions. (4) Late-snapshot gradient aggregation: Each snapshot G(t) is partitioned into B(t) mini-batches; losses are computed independently per batch using binary cross-entropy on link prediction labels, but gradients are accumulated across all batches of the snapshot and applied once after the full snapshot is processed (θ ← θ − η · (1/B(t)) ΣᵦᵢB(t) ∇L^b_{G(t)}). This prevents later batches from being influenced by parameter updates from earlier batches within the same snapshot (information leakage). The framework is compatible with six neural network architectures (GCN, GIN, GAT, GraphSAGE, MLP Mixer, Transformer), using influence scores as aggregation weights in lieu of degree normalization; message embedding Mᵢⱼ(t) = [Zⱼ(t-1) ‖ eᵢⱼ(t') ‖ φ(t-t')]Wₘ concatenates node embedding, edge features, and a time encoding. Key Results: Evaluated on 8 datasets spanning multiple domains and scales (UCI-Message: 1,899 nodes; Stack-Overflow: 2.6M nodes, 63M edges, 92 snapshots). Baselines are 9 models: 4 GNN-based (EvolveGCN-H, EvolveGCN-O, Roland, WinGNN) and 5 sequence-based (DGNN-LSTM, DGNN-GRU, DGNN-Transformer, SimpleDyG, FALCON). All experiments use a single GeForce RTX 3090 (24 GB RAM). Key quantitative outcomes: (1) AUC (link prediction): UnderGS achieves best results on 7/8 datasets; up to 13.56% improvement over the best baseline; e.g., 99.97% AUC on Bitcoin-OTC vs. 99.75% for second-best. Only Roland is competitive on small datasets. (2) MRR (link ranking): UnderGS achieves best on all 8 datasets; average 59.20% improvement; e.g., 95.01% MRR on Bitcoin-Alpha vs. 36.20% for second-best (SimpleDyG). (3) Training efficiency: Up to 9× overall speed-up vs. best baseline; up to 6× per-epoch speed-up. (4) Scalability: 4 of 9 baselines (DGNN variants, FALCON) OOM on 5+ datasets; only UnderGS completes all 8 including Stack-Overflow and Wiki-Talk. (5) vs. TGL (multi-GPU framework): UnderGS achieves 94× per-epoch sampling speed-up, 10× overall training speed-up, and 24.07% better AUC+MRR on average — while running on a single GPU. Ablation (Table 3): removing snapshot decay drops MRR by up to 82.30%; removing path decay drops by up to 26.02%; replacing node importance with k-core drops MRR by 9.29% on average; the time-aware filter contributes 4.35% on average. Training pipeline ablation (Table 4): pipeline yields 6.93–10.40× overall speed-up and 3.65–10.44× per-epoch speed-up vs. snapshot-by-snapshot training. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The temporal influence score combining structural cohesiveness, path decay, and snapshot decay as a unified neighbor selection criterion is genuinely new; the GPU-resident incremental neighbor store that replaces adjacency matrices in GNN training is a novel infrastructure contribution; the late-snapshot gradient aggregation addressing information leakage in DTDG mini-batch training is a targeted engineering advance. The individual components (influence-weighted aggregation, GPU key-value stores, gradient accumulation) are known; the novelty is their integration into a principled, theoretically grounded neighbor management framework for DTDGs. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough: 5 structural variants tested across all 8 datasets (Table 3); training pipeline ablation on 2 datasets (Table 4); compatibility tested across 6 neural networks with and without influence scores (Figures 6–7). Experiments are repeated 10 times and mean ± standard deviation is reported. Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/dani0108/UnderGS (PVLDB Artifact Availability badge confirmed). The paper was published in PVLDB Vol. 19, No. 5, 2026 (peer-reviewed). Hyperparameters (K=16, L=2, α₁=0.1, α₂=0.05, filter ratio 40%) are reported; ρ is density-dependent (ρ=0.3 or 0.5) which introduces a tuning step not fully automated. The embedding dimension is fixed at 128 and batch size at 200 for all models, ensuring fair comparison. Theoretical proofs for the three core properties are deferred to a technical report (Sections 2.1 and 2.2 thereof); the main paper contains proof sketches, which limits independent verification from the paper alone. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (built framework evaluated by deployment on real datasets). - Practical: Runs on a single commodity GPU (RTX 3090, 24 GB) on all 8 tested datasets including the 63M-edge Stack-Overflow graph. - Efficient: Neighbor store reduces memory from O(T|V|²) to O(|V|K); eliminates preprocessing (2.2 hours for FALCON on Reddit-title); achieves 6–9× training speed-up over the best baselines; per-epoch sampling is 94× faster than TGL. - Effective: Outperforms all 9 baselines on AUC (7/8 datasets) and MRR (8/8 datasets) with statistically meaningful margins (error bars reported); ablations confirm each component contributes materially, not just the aggregate. - Scalable: Tested at up to 2.6M nodes and 63M edges (Stack-Overflow), 7.8M edges with 324 snapshots (Wiki-Talk); no experiment exceeds this scale; behavior at 10×–100× scale on billion-edge graphs is not tested, though the O(|V|K) theoretical bound is favorable. The paper delivers a software artifact (GitHub repository with source code and data). Strengths: - The temporal influence score is principled and theoretically grounded with three proven properties (Theorems 4.6–4.8), giving the neighbor selection mechanism a formal foundation absent in prior adjacency-matrix approaches. - The scalability gap versus competitors is substantial and not marginal: UnderGS is the only method completing all 8 datasets on a single GPU, including graphs where every alternative OOMs; the 94× sampling speed-up over TGL (a multi-GPU system) is the most striking empirical result. - Compatibility with six heterogeneous neural architectures (message-passing and non-message-passing) without architectural modifications demonstrates genuine generality of the neighbor store abstraction. - The late-snapshot gradient aggregation addresses a concrete information-leakage failure mode in DTDG batch training that prior work ignores, and ablation (Table 4) shows it yields 6.93–10.40× speed-up over snapshot-by-snapshot training. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper does not include a dedicated limitations section. The conclusion notes that UnderGS targets single-GPU settings, contrasting it with multi-GPU approaches; no explicit failure modes or scope boundaries are stated. The density-dependent filter probability ρ is acknowledged implicitly as a dataset-specific hyperparameter but not discussed as a limitation. The authors do not discuss behavior on highly skewed degree distributions (power-law graphs), adversarially structured temporal graphs, or directed weighted graphs beyond those tested. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Hyperparameter sensitivity for ρ: The time-aware filter probability is set per dataset based on an undisclosed density threshold (10⁻⁴). The paper does not report a sensitivity analysis for ρ, leaving unclear how sensitive performance is to this choice and how a practitioner would set it without labeled validation data. - Single-hardware evaluation: All experiments use one specific GPU. The O(|V|K) memory claim is theoretically favorable, but the paper does not demonstrate behavior when the neighbor store itself exceeds GPU memory (e.g., billion-node graphs), nor does it discuss CPU-offload strategies for this regime. - Transductive setting only: All downstream tasks (link prediction, link ranking) are evaluated on edges within the training graph's node set. Inductive generalization to entirely unseen nodes at inference time is not evaluated, limiting applicability to dynamic graphs with frequent node arrivals. - Theoretical proof completeness: The three key theorems are proved in a separate technical report not included in the PVLDB submission; the main paper contains only proof sketches, which a peer reviewer cannot independently verify from the paper alone. - No comparison to hyperbolic or geometry-aware DTDG methods (e.g., the authors' own prior work on hyperbolic temporal graphs, refs [23, 47]) which have shown strong performance on sparse social graphs — an omission that slightly narrows the baseline comparison. Bottom Line: A practitioner building DTDG representation learning pipelines gains a concretely useful contribution here — UnderGS offers a principled, GPU-efficient replacement for adjacency-matrix neighbor management with strong empirical support across 8 diverse datasets and publicly released code, making it the current method of choice for single-GPU DTDG training at scale.
Citations
#8 — Highly Innovative (78)

KumoRFM-2: Scaling Foundation Models for Relational Learning

Valter Hudovernik, Federico López, Vid Kocijan, Akihiro Nitta, Jan Eric Lenssen, Jure Leskovec, Matthias Fey · arxiv.org · Jure Leskovec - new articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (88) is dominant — released Python SDK, public inference endpoint, benchmark scripts, tested on 2.35B rows across 41 tasks. Novelty (75) — hierarchical attention with early task-conditioning is a genuine architectural contribution and the first foundation model claimed to surpass supervised relational baselines. Rigor (65) reduced by self-reported vendor numbers, no error bars, model weights not released, model size/compute undisclosed; offset by broad benchmark coverage and explicit no-contamination guarantee. Completeness (70) — robustness ablations strong but cost/latency missing. Resilience (70) — strong baselines, but vendor self-report invites skepticism. Non-Obv (75) — co-occurrence expressivity argument is crisp. Weighted: 0.15*75+0.15*65+0.10*75+0.15*70+0.15*70+0.30*88 ≈ 76.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 88
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces KumoRFM-2, a pre-trained relational foundation model that performs in-context learning and fine-tuning directly on multi-table relational databases without manual table flattening or task-specific training. - Why it's important: Deploying predictive models on relational databases currently requires hours to days of expert feature engineering or full supervised retraining per task; a zero-shot foundation model that matches or exceeds these pipelines would eliminate that bottleneck entirely. - How it does it: A two-stage hierarchical attention mechanism first processes intra-table row/column relationships with task-conditioned targets injected early, then aggregates information across tables via foreign-key graph attention and cross-sample attention — enabling the model to learn which columns and relationships are predictive without any task-specific gradient updates. - The good: On RelBenchV1, KumoRFM-2 achieves avg AUROC 79.60 with best overall rank 3.08, surpassing the strongest supervised relational baseline (RelGNN, 78.06/3.75) using as little as 0.2% of available training data as context examples — the paper's claimed first demonstration of a few-shot foundation model exceeding supervised approaches on relational benchmarks. - The missing: The paper does not report model size, pre-training compute, or inference latency comparisons against baselines, making it impossible to assess cost-benefit trade-offs for practitioners; and all results are self-reported by the commercial vendor with no independent replication. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners and researchers in relational/tabular ML and database-native AI, as it establishes a new empirical baseline for what zero-shot prediction on multi-table data can achieve; can be skipped by those uninterested in the relational setting. Problem & Motivation: Predictive modeling on relational databases — churn, LTV, fraud, conversion rate — is pervasive in industry but requires either expensive manual feature engineering (hours to days per task) or task-specific model training on large labeled datasets. Existing tabular foundation models operate on single flat tables and extend to multi-table settings only by pre-aggregating relational subgraphs into wide feature vectors via fixed, column-wise encoders (deep feature synthesis). This flattening destroys row-level interaction signals: any label that depends on the co-occurrence of two features within a row (rather than their marginals) is invisible to such encoders. Moreover, temporal consistency — ensuring predictions use only data available at the anchor timestamp — is difficult to enforce post-hoc when tables are materialized offline. Key Idea: The key technical insight is that task-relevant feature selection in relational data is fundamentally a row-level, cross-table reasoning problem that cannot be decomposed into column-wise encodings. KumoRFM-2 solves this by injecting context targets (the known labels from in-context examples) directly into the input representations before any attention is computed, creating task-conditioned row embeddings that distinguish predictive from uninformative columns adaptively. A two-stage hierarchy then aggregates these embeddings first within each table (row + column attention) and then across tables (graph + cross-sample attention), maintaining temporal consistency throughout via the temporal heterogeneous graph formalism. Methodology: The pipeline operates in three stages. (1) Intra-table encoding: For each table in the relational subgraph, a lightweight network computes row embeddings via alternating column attention (across features within a row) and row attention (across rows within the table). Context targets are appended to input tokens before this stage, making representations task-conditioned. This avoids attending over all cells jointly (quadratic in rows × columns) while preserving intra-row dependencies. (2) Inter-table aggregation: A larger graph attention network propagates embeddings across primary-foreign key edges, effectively performing multi-hop message passing over the relational schema graph. A cross-sample attention layer additionally allows the query entity to attend to context examples, implementing in-context learning analogously to how transformers implement it for sequences. (3) Prediction readout: The final embedding of the query entity is decoded to the target variable (classification or regression). The model is pre-trained on a combination of synthetic tables/graphs generated via Structural Causal Models (SCMs) and real-world public relational databases, in a curriculum from single tables to multi-table structures. At inference, context examples are generated automatically by replaying historical database states up to anchor timestamps, preventing data leakage. Ensembling randomizes column and class order shuffles and aggregates over subgraph sampling configurations. Three critical design decisions: (a) hierarchical rather than flat attention — justified on quadratic complexity grounds and empirically shown to improve robustness to noise; (b) early target injection for task-conditioning — justified by an expressivity argument showing column-wise encoders achieve AUROC=0.5 on a synthetic co-occurrence task where KumoRFM-2 achieves AUROC=1.0; (c) structured local+global context selection combining the entity's own temporal history with recent database snapshots. Key Results: Evaluated on 41 tasks across 15 relational databases from four benchmarks (RelBenchV1, RelBenchV2, SALT, 4DBInfer), covering 2.35 billion rows and 914 columns. On RelBenchV1 binary classification (12 tasks, AUROC metric): KumoRFM-2 avg 79.60 AUROC with rank 3.08, outperforming KumoRFM-1 (76.71/8.33), the best supervised relational model RelGNN (78.06/3.75), and the best feature-engineered tabular approach DS+AutoGluon (75.36/8.50). On RelBenchV1 regression (MAE, normalized): KumoRFM-2 normalized score 0.822 with rank 2.67 vs. RelGNN's 0.861/4.44. On SALT multi-class classification (MRR): KumoRFM-2 ICL 0.83 (rank 1.88) vs. DS+AutoGluon 0.77/3.75 and DS+CARTE 0.79/3.88; fine-tuning lifts to 0.89 (rank 1.38). On RelBenchV2 regression: normalized MAE 0.601 with rank 1.0. On 4DBInfer binary classification: avg AUROC 79.96 (rank 3.6), matching the best supervised models. Notably, KumoRFM-2 uses at most 0.2% of available training data as context in the most data-rich settings. Robustness studies show performance stable up to 75% feature removal, 75% edge removal, and heavy injection of noisy columns. Subgraph depth ablations show a sharp performance gain from 0 to 1 hops, saturating around 4 hops. Fine-tuning completes in under 2 minutes per task. Baselines are current and strong, including recent competitors RT_zero (ICLR 2026), RDBLearn (2026), Griffin (ICML 2025), and TabICLv2 (2026). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The hierarchical two-stage attention with early task-conditioning for native relational in-context learning is a genuine architectural contribution not reducible to prior tabular or graph foundation models. The empirical finding — that a few-shot foundation model surpasses carefully tuned supervised pipelines on standardized relational benchmarks — is reported as a first-of-kind result. The pre-training curriculum and SCM-based synthetic data generation build on prior work (KumoRFM-1, Hoppe et al. 2025) rather than introducing new data generation methodology. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover context size, subgraph depth, subgraph breadth, feature scarcity, link scarcity, and noise injection — this is a thorough sensitivity analysis. Error bars and significance tests are not reported; all results are single-run point estimates. Benchmark evaluation scripts are publicly available at https://github.com/kumo-ai/kumo-rfm, and the model is accessible via `pip install kumoai`. However, the model weights are served via a proprietary inference endpoint (not openly released), so independent reproduction of the pre-trained model is not possible. All results are from the authors' own system (vendor self-report), and the pre-training dataset and model architecture hyperparameters (size, depth, head count) are not disclosed. None of the benchmark datasets were used during pre-training (explicitly stated), which guards against Goodhart's Law contamination. The paper's claimed first surpassing of supervised methods on relational benchmarks is a strong claim that warrants independent replication. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS profile — KumoRFM-2 is a deployed system evaluated on production-scale databases. - Practical: The inference endpoint is accessible via Python SDK (`pip install kumoai`) from Python 3.10+; no local GPU required for ICL inference; fine-tuning is claimed to complete in under 2 minutes per task, though hardware specs for this claim are not stated. - Efficient: Inference cost is not quantified in absolute terms (latency, GPU-hours, cost per prediction); the SSD-based graph engine supports 5 GB/sec bandwidth and 20M lookups/sec for 500B-row databases, but no comparison to supervised pipeline inference cost is provided. - Effective: On 41 tasks across four benchmarks, the model achieves best-overall rank on RelBenchV1 and RelBenchV2, outperforming supervised baselines on average — evidence that it solves the stated problem, not merely a proxy, though task-level variance is high and supervised methods retain advantages on some large-dataset tasks. - Scalable: Tested at database scale up to 2.35 billion rows across 15 databases; the graph engine is documented to handle 500B+ rows via memory-mapped SSD I/O, but no empirical latency or throughput numbers at that scale are reported in the paper — this remains a claim rather than a measured result. The work delivers a software artifact (Python SDK, public inference endpoint, benchmark scripts); model weights are not openly released. Strengths: - The expressivity argument with the formal co-occurrence example (AUROC 0.5 for column-wise encoders vs. 1.0 for KumoRFM-2) provides a crisp, theoretically grounded motivation for the architectural design rather than relying purely on empirical ablations. - Evaluation scope is unusually broad: 41 tasks, 15 databases, 4 benchmark suites, with a rich and current set of ~20 baseline methods spanning supervised tabular ML, relational GNNs, LLMs, tabular foundation models, and relational foundation models. - The robustness studies (feature scarcity, link scarcity, noise injection) directly address practical deployment concerns and show KumoRFM-2 degrades substantially more gracefully than tabular baselines under data quality issues. - The temporal consistency enforcement via database snapshot replay at context generation time is a principled solution to data leakage that prior approaches handle informally or ignore. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that in large-scale supervised training regimes (e.g., amazon, stack, hm tasks), supervised relational models retain slight advantages that KumoRFM-2 has not yet closed; they note context size limitations as the primary bottleneck and flag fine-tuning as the path to close this gap. They note the optimal subgraph neighborhood size is task-dependent. These are stated directly and credited credibly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper omits model size, FLOPs, and inference latency numbers entirely — a significant gap for practitioners weighing deployment cost against accuracy gains, especially since competing supervised pipelines (LightGBM, AutoGluon) are extremely cheap to run. All numbers are self-reported by Kumo AI (a commercial entity with direct financial interest in favorable results); the open-sourced benchmark scripts allow independent evaluation of the model via the inference endpoint, but independent replication of the pre-training is impossible without released weights. The fine-tuning results on SALT are the strongest in the paper (avg MRR 0.89 vs. 0.77 for DS+AutoGluon), but fine-tuning ablations are limited to a single benchmark; it is unclear whether fine-tuning generalizes across all 41 tasks or specializes well only in the SALT domain. The comparison to LLM baselines is brief and uses two specific prompting strategies; a more carefully prompted LLM (e.g., with schema-aware chain-of-thought) might be more competitive. The pre-training data composition and curriculum are described at a high level only. Bottom Line: A practitioner building predictive pipelines on relational databases will find this work directly actionable: it demonstrates that zero-shot foundation model inference can replace multi-day feature engineering workflows and match or beat supervised approaches on standardized benchmarks, with a usable Python SDK — but the closed model weights and missing cost/latency numbers require treating vendor-reported numbers with appropriate caution until independent replication.
Recommended 2604.12596 PDF
#9 — Highly Innovative (78)

The Illusion of Certainty: Decoupling Capability and Calibration in On-Policy Distillation (10 ▲ )

Jiaxin Zhang, Xiangyu Peng, Qinglin Chen, Qinyuan Ye, Caiming Xiong, Chien-Sheng Wu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Strong across the board: Novelty (80) — the target-replacement mechanism plus the µ(X) projection insight is genuinely new and distinct from RL calibration approaches. Non-Obviousness (82) — the proof that the optimal X-measurable projection of the teacher's confidence is the student's own success rate is a clean, non-obvious result. Rigor (72) — three formal propositions with proofs, K-ablation, scale sweep, OOD and continual-learning tests, code released, but no error bars/seeds and the headline API comparison is acknowledged-confounded. Completeness (78) and Resilience (75) — limitations confronted honestly; weaknesses (two domains, unvalidated cascade routing, overstated 'scaling law' framing) noted. Impact (75) — code released, cheap to integrate into SDPO, addresses a real deployment pathology. Composite ≈ 0.20·80 + 0.25·72 + 0.15·82 + 0.15·78 + 0.15·75 + 0.10·75 = 76.7 → 77; bumped to 78 reflecting strong theory+empirics combination.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 78 Resilience 75 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CaOPD, a calibration-aware on-policy distillation framework that decouples confidence supervision from capability cloning by replacing the teacher's privileged confidence target with an empirically measured student success rate before computing the reverse-KL distillation loss. - Why it's important: LLMs trained via on-policy distillation (OPD) — the dominant post-training paradigm — systematically saturate at near-1.0 confidence regardless of actual accuracy, undermining their reliability as autonomous agents and scientific co-pilots, and this pathology persists even at frontier model scale. - How it does it: At training time, K student rollouts are sampled and scored by an objective verifier to compute an empirical success rate µ̂(x); this value then overwrites the confidence token in both the student's completion and the teacher's privileged context before the per-token reverse-KL loss is computed, algorithmically separating what the model answers from how certain it should be. - The good: A CaOPD-trained Qwen3-8B achieves a reliability score (1-Brier Score = 0.87) that exceeds most evaluated proprietary APIs in calibration, while matching the task accuracy of the SDPO backbone (80.3% on Science Q&A Chemistry) without any accuracy degradation. - The missing: The headline API comparison is confounded — CaOPD is fine-tuned on the test domain while proprietary models are evaluated zero-shot — making the accuracy comparison misleading; evaluation is also restricted to two domains with objective verifiers, and the proposed cascade-routing application is never empirically validated. - Read it or not: Researchers working on LLM post-training, uncertainty calibration, or agentic deployment reliability should read this; the target replacement idea is simple, principled, and effective, though the API comparisons should be interpreted cautiously. Problem & Motivation: Modern LLMs are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents and scientific reasoning tools, roles that require reliable uncertainty signaling. On-policy distillation (OPD) methods — SDFT, SDPO, and related self-distillation frameworks — are the dominant technique for post-training capability improvement, but they induce a severe and previously undercharacterized pathology: systematic overconfidence. Even frontier proprietary models (GPT-5.x, Claude-4.x, Gemini-3.1, DeepSeek-V3.1) exhibit massive gaps between mean confidence (~0.97–1.00) and actual accuracy (~57–84%), a gap that does not close with scale. The gap has a structural cause: the teacher policy operates with privileged context (correct demonstrations, verifier feedback, ground-truth solutions) unavailable at deployment, creating an information asymmetry that previous work did not formalize or address. Key Idea: The core insight is that, because the teacher's success probability µ_T(X,Z) is conditioned on privileged information Z not available to the deployed student, it is mathematically non-identifiable from deployment-time information X alone. The optimal X-measurable projection of µ_T(X,Z) onto the student's information state is exactly the student's own marginal success rate µ(X) = E_{a∼πθ(·|x)}[R(x,a)]. Standard OPD ignores this and forces the student to chase µ_T(X,Z) via reverse KL, inducing artificial logit sharpening (entropy collapse) and an optimism bias inherited from success-filtered teacher trajectories. The fix is to estimate µ(X) directly from student rollouts and substitute it as the confidence supervision target — leaving the capability cloning objective untouched. Methodology: The CaOPD pipeline has three stages. First, student-grounded confidence estimation: sample K independent rollouts (a_k, c_k) ∼ πθ(·|x), evaluate each with a binary task verifier R(x, a_k) ∈ {0,1}, and compute µ̂(x) = (1/K)∑R(x,a_k). Under SDPO, these rollouts are already generated as part of the base training loop and reused at zero marginal sampling cost. Second, target replacement: take a student-generated trajectory y=(a,c); replace the confidence segment c with µ̂(x) to produce ỹ=(a, µ̂(x)); simultaneously overwrite the confidence score in the privileged teacher context z (originally saturated near 1.0) with µ̂(x) to produce revised context z̃. Third, distillation via the standard per-token reverse KL between πθ(·|ỹ<t, x) and πθ(·|ỹ<t, x, z̃), which naturally decomposes into a capability cloning term over reasoning token positions (Ia) and a calibration term over confidence token positions (Ic). The causal structure of autoregressive generation ensures the confidence replacement does not bleed into reasoning logits: at all t∈Ia, the prefix ỹ<t contains only reasoning tokens, so the teacher's signal at those positions is unchanged from standard OPD. The theoretical grounding consists of three propositions with full proofs: (1) information gap and non-identifiability (Prop 1), (2) entropy collapse via privileged conditioning using the chain rule for mutual information (Prop 2), and (3) selection bias inducing systematic optimism bias when teacher contexts are conditioned on successful outcomes (Prop 3). Critical design decisions: K=8 rollouts (ablated from K∈{1,2,4,8,16,32}; K<4 causes target quantization that re-induces saturation; K>8 yields diminishing returns), online EMA teacher tracking to handle target non-stationarity, and vLLM for parallelized rollout generation. A Teacher-Anchored Self-Consistency variant replaces the binary verifier with LLM-as-a-Judge agreement rate for open-ended domains. Key Results: On Qwen3-8B Science Q&A Chemistry (test accuracy reported), CaOPD+SDFT achieves 50.0% accuracy (vs 49.1% SDFT baseline), ECE 0.266 vs 0.486 (−0.22), Brier Score 0.281 vs 0.477 (−0.20), and SPR 0.599 vs 0.387 (+0.21). On Tool Use, accuracy improves from 67.6% to 70.6%, SPR from 0.085 to 0.555 — recovering nearly complete discriminative collapse induced by SDFT. Against RL-based calibration baselines on Qwen3-8B Science Q&A, RLCR and CAR achieve 65.8% and 61.6% accuracy respectively (far below SDPO at 80.6%); CaOPD+SDPO achieves 80.3% with ECE 0.141 vs RLCR's 0.143. The OCG for Qwen3-8B Tool Use collapses from +32.0% (SDFT) to −0.7% (CaOPD+SDFT). Under OOD transfer (Tool Use → Chemistry), SDFT ECE spikes to 0.599 while CaOPD achieves 0.358 (40% relative reduction). Under continual training, SDFT SPR collapses to 0.230 while CaOPD achieves 0.662 with ECE 0.126. Across the Qwen3 family (0.6B–32B), SDFT mean confidence is flat at ~1.0 regardless of scale; CaOPD mean confidence tracks true accuracy at every scale. API comparison (Table 10) shows CaOPD-trained Qwen3-8B at reliability 0.87 vs Claude-Opus-4.6 at 0.91, GPT-5.4 at 0.85 — but this comparison is domain-specific fine-tuned vs zero-shot. Baselines (SDFT, SDPO 2026; RLCR 2025; GRPO 2024; CAR 2026) are current. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The target replacement mechanism — decoupling confidence supervision from capability cloning within the existing reverse-KL distillation pipeline — is genuinely novel and distinct from RL-based reward shaping approaches. The theoretical formalization of OPD-induced overconfidence as a consequence of information asymmetry (entropy collapse + optimism bias) is new in this context. The "Scaling Law of Miscalibration" finding (OPD overconfidence persisting from 0.6B to 32B and across frontier proprietary APIs) is a substantial empirical contribution. The "calibration forgetting" pathology in continual learning is newly characterized. The method is not a straightforward combination of existing ideas applied to a new setting; the insight that the optimal X-measurable projection of the teacher's confidence is exactly µ(X) is non-obvious and cleanly motivates the target replacement. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for rollout budget K, the TA-SC fallback verifier, training dynamics across model scales, and format adherence. However, error bars, multiple seeds, and significance tests are not reported; conclusions appear drawn from single training runs per configuration. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/SalesforceAIResearch/CaOPD. A concern: the API comparison that anchors the headline claim (Figure 1, Table 10) conflates in-domain fine-tuning with zero-shot evaluation — the paper acknowledges this, but it is a non-trivial confound. Evaluation on open-ended tasks without objective verifiers is limited to a single ablation table (Table 4), not a full evaluation setting. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on 4× NVIDIA H200 GPUs with vLLM and PyTorch FSDP2; accessible to well-resourced academic labs and industry teams. - Efficient: Under SDPO, CaOPD adds only a verifier evaluation pass over already-generated rollouts (zero extra sampling cost); under SDFT, K=8 additional rollouts increase training compute by a constant factor but wall-clock time per step is nearly indistinguishable from baseline per Figure 2. Deployment cost is O(1) — identical to an uncalibrated model. - Effective: Evidence across two domains, two model families, OOD shifts, and continual learning is consistent and shows genuine discriminative improvement (SPR), not just mean confidence reduction; the causal decoupling of capability and calibration training trajectories (Figure 2) is a credible mechanistic validation. - Scalable: Tested from 0.6B to 32B on Qwen3; cascade routing at scale is proposed but not empirically validated. Behavior on domains requiring open-ended generation at scale is untested beyond the TA-SC ablation. The work delivers a code repository (training pipeline implementation); pre-trained model weights are not released. Strengths: - The three-proposition theoretical framework proves OPD-induced overconfidence is a mathematical necessity under information asymmetry, not a tuning failure — this elevates the paper beyond an engineering contribution. - Target replacement is non-invasive (no reward modification, no additional optimization stages), avoiding the optimization instability and capability tax that consistently afflict RL-based calibration baselines (RLCR drops accuracy from 80.6% to 65.8% on Science Q&A). - The "calibration forgetting" characterization in continual learning is a useful new finding: SDFT preserves reasoning accuracy but SPR collapses to 0.000 after sequential domain updates, a failure mode not previously identified. - The SPR metric, which penalizes confidence saturation by assigning zero credit to ties, is a methodologically sound contribution that exposes discrimination collapse invisible to AUROC. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The method requires sufficient base model in-context learning capacity; capability gains are bounded by the base model's reasoning ability. Format failures can occur at test time, particularly for small models, though CaOPD's target replacement provides a corrective formatting signal. Computing µ̂(x) requires K=8 rollouts during training, increasing training compute (fully amortized at deployment). Open-ended domains without objective verifiers require the TA-SC fallback. Step-level calibration for long-horizon CoT reasoning is deferred to future work. Authors confront limitations directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The headline API comparison (Figure 1, Table 10) is the paper's marquee result but is not controlled: CaOPD is fine-tuned on SciKnowEval Chemistry while proprietary APIs are zero-shot — this confound inflates the apparent accuracy advantage and makes the "8B model rivals frontier LLMs" framing misleading on capability (calibration comparison is more valid). The term "Scaling Law of Miscalibration" implies a quantitative functional relationship (e.g., a power law) but the paper only demonstrates that overconfidence is scale-invariant — no scaling law is fit or derived. Evaluation is limited to two domains, both having clean binary verifiers; the TA-SC fallback for open-ended domains is ablated in one table with one model on two domains, insufficient to characterize its reliability in production settings. No error bars or significance tests are reported despite the quantitative framing. The proposed cascade routing application (Appendix F) is speculative — cost-accuracy tradeoffs under different confidence thresholds are not measured. Bottom Line: This is a well-motivated, cleanly executed paper with a genuinely novel mechanism and a useful theoretical formalization; researchers working on LLM post-training or agentic deployment reliability will find the target replacement idea and the calibration forgetting finding immediately useful, but should treat the frontier API comparisons as illustrative rather than controlled benchmarks.
Recommended 2604.16830 PDF
#10 — Highly Innovative (78)

Cut Your Losses! Learning to Prune Paths Early for Efficient Parallel Reasoning (18 ▲ )

Jiaxi Bi, Tongxu Luo, Wenyu Du, Zhengyang Tang, Benyou Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (80) — data-quality ablation cleanly isolates architectural advantage by retraining external PRM on identical MC labels; standardized protocol across 4 models × 5 benchmarks; rigorous decontamination. Single-run results and unvalidated scaling law are weaknesses. Novelty (75) — taxonomy plus the [STOP] token+LoRA-on-frozen-model design is a real new mechanism. Non-Obviousness (80) — internal-state PRM beating external PRM with identical data is concrete and non-obvious. Completeness (80), Resilience (75) — limitations confronted directly. Impact (80) — promised release, plug-and-play with LRM backbones. Composite ≈ 0.20*75+0.25*80+0.15*80+0.15*80+0.15*75+0.10*80 = 78.25.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 80 Resilience 75 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces STOP (Super TOken for Pruning), a lightweight LoRA-based module attached to a frozen Large Reasoning Model that learns to score and discard futile reasoning prefixes early during parallel inference, along with the first systematic taxonomy of path pruning methods. - Why it's important: Parallel reasoning with Large Reasoning Models costs up to ~$6 per query and wastes over 70% of compute on trajectories that fail from their earliest tokens, a waste that compounds as sampling budgets scale. - How it does it: A special [STOP] vocabulary token is appended to a frozen model's KV cache after prefix generation; a LoRA adapter activates only for this token, producing a scalar quality score trained via Monte Carlo soft labels, enabling a three-stage Launch-Check-Resume inference pipeline with negligible overhead. - The good: On AIME24 with a 1.5B model, STOP raises avg@8|64 from 30.10% to 37.92% while cutting total tokens by 73.88%, and consistently dominates all three competing paradigms (surface heuristics, external judges, raw confidence) across four models (1.5B–20B) and five benchmarks. - The missing: Evaluation is capped at 20B parameters and N=64 sampling budget; behavior on 70B+ models and N≥1000 regimes is unverified, and the power-law scaling formula is fit on a single model (DS-Qwen-2.5-1.5B) on two tasks. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building cost-efficient parallel reasoning pipelines and researchers working on LRM inference optimization; the taxonomy alone has organizational value for the field. Problem & Motivation: Parallel reasoning over Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) samples N independent trajectories and aggregates via majority voting, achieving strong accuracy at the cost of compute that scales linearly in N. Inference costs reach ~$6 per query at production scale, and recent empirical work shows that many trajectories are wrong from their very first tokens — once a prefix takes a wrong turn, the LRM rarely self-corrects. Existing attempts to prune futile paths early (reward models, confidence heuristics, semantic similarity filters) are ad-hoc, lack a common evaluation protocol, and occupy only three of the four conceptually possible paradigm quadrants defined by signal source (internal vs. external) and learnability (fixed vs. trained). The fourth quadrant — trainable modules that read the LRM's own internal states — remained unexplored despite being theoretically superior on both axes. Key Idea: The core insight is that a tiny learnable module — a special token plus a LoRA adapter — can read the KV cache of a partially generated reasoning prefix and emit a scalar quality score in a single forward pass, reusing the already-computed prefix representations at essentially zero marginal cost. The key trick is that the LoRA adapter is frozen during generation and activated only when the [STOP] token is processed, so the base model's generative behavior is never disturbed. This decouples generation from evaluation and avoids re-encoding the prefix from scratch. Methodology: The approach operates in three pipeline stages. (1) Launch: generate N short prefixes (e.g., 1,024 tokens) for a query in parallel, caching KV states. (2) Check: append a small sequence of learned [STOP] tokens to each cached prefix; the LoRA-augmented model processes only these tokens in one forward pass, producing a scalar quality score per prefix via a linear classification head. (3) Resume: retain the top-k scoring prefixes and generate them to completion for final majority-vote aggregation. Training targets soft labels s^mc ∈ [0,1] estimated via Monte Carlo rollout: for each prefix, K=32 continuations are sampled and the empirical pass rate becomes the training target. The loss is soft binary cross-entropy over the classifier output. Training data is constructed model-specifically: ~1,000 AIME problems (1984–2023) plus non-Diamond GPQA, filtered by difficulty (pass rate between 4/32 and 28/32 to exclude trivial and intractable problems), with evaluation sets (AIME 2024/2025, GPQA Diamond) strictly withheld. The MC data construction costs 38–76 H100-GPU-hours per model depending on scale. Critical design decisions: (a) LoRA adapter necessity — ablation shows removing it drops AIME24 accuracy from 36.67% to 31.67%, because native LRM hidden states are optimized for next-token prediction, not value discrimination; (b) soft vs. hard labels — K=32 soft labels improve Cons@N on AIME24 from 46.67% (K=1 binary) to 53.33%; (c) number of [STOP] tokens — performance peaks at 4–6 tokens, degrading at higher counts due to overfitting. The paper also derives a power-law scaling law γ⁻¹ = a·C^b·L^c_prefix/L^d_task (a≈1.17×10⁴, b≈0.46, c≈0.40, d≈4.55) to predict the optimal retention ratio γ from compute budget, prefix length, and task length. Key Results: Evaluated on AIME 2024, AIME 2025, BRUMO 2025, HMMT 2025, and GPQA-Diamond across DS-Qwen-2.5-1.5B/7B, DS-Qwen-3-8B, and GPT-OSS-20B, pruning 64 paths to 8 at a 2,048-token checkpoint. STOP (Type IV) outperforms all baselines on every model-benchmark combination: - DS-Qwen-2.5-1.5B / AIME24: baseline avg@64=30.10%; Type I=26.25%, Type II=32.50%, Type III=32.92%, STOP=37.92%, 73.88% token reduction. - DS-Qwen-2.5-7B / GPQA-D: baseline=45.95%; STOP=55.75%, 62.61% token reduction. - DS-Qwen-3-8B / GPQA-D: baseline=56.87%; STOP=63.32%. - GPT-OSS-20B / AIME25: baseline=70.99%; STOP=75.42%, 71.62% token reduction. Verification overhead is 0.20s per batch (0.59% of generation time) vs. 1.13s for external PRM (Type II) and 0.38s for similarity heuristic (Type I), but Type I paradoxically degrades system throughput by 17.71% due to GPU pipeline fragmentation despite its low explicit cost. The data-quality ablation (Type II retrained on identical MC data) confirms STOP's gains are architectural, not solely data-driven: STOP beats retrained Type II consistently (e.g., 61.67% vs. 59.17% on 7B/AIME24). A ZebraLogic logic-puzzle generalization experiment shows STOP improves accuracy from 73.73% to 77.23%, suggesting domain transferability. The AIMO3 competition experiment (GPT-OSS-120B + tools) shows score improvement from 39 to 43, reaching silver-level performance. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The taxonomy (classifying path pruning by signal source × learnability) is a conceptual contribution of moderate originality. STOP itself is a novel method: appending a learnable token to a frozen model's KV cache for lightweight prefix quality scoring is not a direct combination of prior techniques. The empirical finding that external PRMs lose information relative to internal state access — and that this gap persists even when data quality is controlled — is a concrete, non-obvious result. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover the two most critical design choices (LoRA adapter necessity and soft vs. hard labels) and two hyperparameters (token count, LoRA rank). Error bars and significance tests are absent; results are single-run. The evaluation protocol is standardized across all methods (64 paths → 8, 2,048-token checkpoint, same sampling hyperparameters). The data-quality ablation (Appendix D) is the paper's strongest methodological contribution — it explicitly controls for supervision quality, isolating architectural effect. Training data decontamination is rigorous (AIME 2024/2025 and GPQA Diamond excluded). Code, data, and model checkpoints are promised at https://bijiaxihh.github.io/STOP. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.16029, April 2026), not peer-reviewed. The power-law scaling law is fit on one model (1.5B) on two tasks; its generalization to other architectures is asserted but not independently validated. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The inference pipeline requires only standard transformer infrastructure (vLLM, H100 GPUs); the LoRA module adds negligible VRAM overhead and is plug-and-play with any compatible LRM backbone. - Efficient: Verification overhead is 0.20s per batch (0.59% of generation time), and total wall-clock time with STOP (34.33s) nearly matches the no-pruning baseline (33.20s), while achieving 70%+ token savings; data construction is a one-time cost of 38–76 8×H100 GPU-hours. - Effective: STOP consistently raises avg@8|64 above the full-64-path baseline on 18 of 20 model-benchmark pairs, demonstrating it actually improves accuracy over the unconstrained no-pruning baseline, not just matching it at lower cost. - Scalable: Tested at 1.5B–20B parameters and N up to 64 paths; behavior at 70B+ or N≥1000 is explicitly acknowledged as unverified; the paper makes no extrapolation claim but the gap is real and consequential for production deployments. The paper delivers trained model checkpoints, a dataset, and code (promised at release). Strengths: - The data-quality ablation is rare and methodologically sound: by retraining the external PRM (Type II) on identical MC-estimated soft labels, the paper isolates the architectural advantage of internal state access, which is the central claim. - The three-stage Launch-Check-Resume pipeline is practically elegant: verification adds only 0.59% overhead by reusing the KV cache and restricting the adapter to a handful of special tokens, avoiding the GPU fragmentation that makes seemingly cheap heuristic methods (Type I, 17.71% throughput loss) worse than they appear. - The power-law formulation for optimal retention ratio provides practitioners with a concrete deployment guide that avoids exhaustive hyperparameter search — an unusual and useful empirical contribution. - The attention visualization provides mechanistic evidence that the [STOP] token functions as a process-oriented evaluator (attending to cognitive pivot tokens like negations in high-quality paths) rather than simply reading off the answer, lending interpretability credibility. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state (1) the method has not been tested on models larger than 20B or under N≥1000 sampling regimes; (2) pruning is single-stage at a fixed position (Lprefix), and multi-stage or dynamically-positioned pruning has not been explored. They identify progressive multi-stage pruning and use of STOP as an online RL rejection mechanism as future directions. These limitations are confronted directly, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The scaling law (Eq. 7) is fit on DS-Qwen-2.5-1.5B on two benchmarks (AIME and GPQA) — the coefficients (a≈1.17×10⁴, d≈4.55) may not transfer to architectures with different generation length distributions, and the paper does not validate the formula on the 7B/8B/20B models. The evaluation uses a single fixed pruning checkpoint at 2,048 tokens; it is unclear whether the 2,048-token checkpoint is optimal or just convenient — earlier or later pruning might yield different relative rankings of the four paradigms. No per-category failure analysis is provided: it would be valuable to know whether STOP's gains are concentrated on specific problem types or broadly distributed. The cons@N vs. total-compute curves (Figure 4) are visually dense but the paper does not report statistical significance of the differences, which matters when gaps are small (e.g., Type III vs. Type IV on DS-Qwen-3-8B where the improvement is 0.84% on AIME24). The AIMO3 competition result (Table 9) is a single-run competition submission, not a controlled experiment — it provides suggestive but not conclusive evidence for the 120B + tool-use setting. Bottom Line: A practitioner building parallel inference pipelines for LRMs should read this paper: STOP is a credible, cheap, and well-ablated method that measurably improves accuracy while cutting token costs by ~70%, and the taxonomy and deployment guidelines add further value beyond the method itself.
Recommended 2604.16029 PDF
#11 — Highly Innovative (78)

Elucidating the SNR-t Bias of Diffusion Probabilistic Models (22 ▲ )

Meng Yu, Lei Sun, Jianhao Zeng, Xiangxiang Chu, Kun Zhan · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (80) drives the score—broad coverage across 8 architectures and 4 datasets, clean ablations isolating pixel-space DC vs wavelet subbands, repeated seeds on the key SNR-t finding, public code released, recent strong baselines, additive gains over already-corrected exposure-bias methods confirming orthogonality. Novelty (75) is high—both a new mechanistic framing (SNR-timestep decoupling) and a new training-free method, plus a refined γ<1 reconstruction assumption with proof. Non-Obviousness (80) strong—the differential signal already computed at each step contains a usable correction direction is a clean insight. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) are good but pulled down by no quantitative FID at 512/1024 resolutions where FLUX/Qwen actually run, and Assumption 5.1 measured only on ADM/CIFAR-10. Impact (80) is excellent—near-zero overhead, plug-and-play, code released. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*80 ≈ 77.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DCW (Differential Correction in Wavelet domain), a training-free, plug-and-play inference-time correction that mitigates the SNR-timestep misalignment bias in diffusion probabilistic models. - Why it's important: Every deployed diffusion model suffers this bias at inference — it silently degrades generation quality with no obvious symptom other than elevated FID — and fixing it requires no retraining and adds less than 0.5% compute. - How it does it: At each denoising step, the method computes the differential signal between the denoised sample and the reconstruction prediction, decomposes it into wavelet frequency subbands, and applies frequency-specific, dynamically-weighted corrections that align with the model's coarse-to-fine denoising order. - The good: Consistent FID improvements across eight DPM architectures (IDDPM, ADM, DDIM, EDM, PFGM++, DiT, FLUX, Qwen-Image) and four datasets, including additive gains on top of already-corrected exposure-bias models (ADM-ES, DPM-FR), demonstrating genuine orthogonality. - The missing: All quantitative FID experiments are capped at 256×256 resolution; no FID evaluation at 512×512 or 1024×1024, which is where FLUX and modern text-to-image models actually operate at scale. - Read it or not: Worth reading for any practitioner or researcher building on diffusion model inference pipelines — the method is nearly free to adopt and the mechanistic explanation is the clearest account to date of why inference trajectories systematically drift toward lower SNR. Problem & Motivation Diffusion probabilistic models fix a deterministic SNR(t) = ᾱ_t/(1−ᾱ_t) for every timestep t during training: the network ε_θ(·, t) only ever sees samples whose noise level exactly matches t. At inference, accumulated errors from both neural network predictions and ODE/SDE discretization push the reverse-process trajectory off the ideal path, so the actual sample xˆ_t at timestep t has a lower SNR than the forward-process sample x_t at the same t. The network, conditioned on t but receiving a sample with mismatched SNR, overestimates noise — and this overestimation propagates and compounds across all denoising steps. The authors distinguish this SNR-t bias (a sample-timestep misalignment within a single trajectory) from previously studied exposure bias (inter-sample discrepancy between training and inference distributions) and argue the former is the more fundamental root cause that induces the latter. Key Idea The differential signal between the denoised sample xˆ_{t-1} and the reconstruction prediction x^0_θ(xˆ_t, t) — already computed as a byproduct of every denoising step — implicitly encodes a gradient direction pointing from the biased trajectory toward the ideal forward-process trajectory. Adding a scaled version of this signal requires no new neural function evaluations and directly nudges the sample toward the SNR level that the network was trained to handle. Performing this correction per frequency subband in the wavelet domain further reduces noise interference and aligns the correction schedule with the DPM's known coarse-to-fine reconstruction order. Methodology The pipeline has four components. (1) Bias identification: Two experiments on ADM/CIFAR-10 with 2,000 samples establish that (a) feeding a network a sample with mismatched SNR and timestep causes systematically over- or under-estimated noise predictions (Key Finding 1), and (b) reverse-process samples at every timestep consistently exhibit lower SNR than forward-process samples at the same t (Key Finding 2). (2) Theoretical proof: Under Assumption 5.1 — that the reconstruction output x^0_θ(xˆ_t, t) = γ_t·x_0 + φ_t·ε with 0 < γ_t ≤ 1 (justified via Tweedie's formula, the L2-norm loss's mean-estimation bias, and empirical plots of reconstruction norms) — the paper derives a closed-form expression for the actual SNR of xˆ_t in the reverse process (Theorem 5.1, Eq. 12), showing it is always strictly lower than the forward SNR due to the γˆ²_t < 1 factor in the numerator and an extra positive term in the denominator. (3) Pixel-space differential correction (DC): At each step, update xˆ_{t-1} ← xˆ_{t-1} + λ_t·(xˆ_{t-1} − x^0_θ(xˆ_t, t)), where λ_t is a scalar. This adds zero NFE since x^0_θ is already computed. (4) Wavelet-domain extension (DCW): Apply DWT to decompose xˆ_{t-1} and x^0_θ into four subbands {ll, lh, hl, hh}, apply per-subband correction with coefficients λ^l_t = λ_l·σ_t (decays as denoising advances, prioritizing low-frequency early) and λ^h_t = (1−λ_h)·σ_t (increases emphasis on high-frequency later), then apply iDWT. The reverse-process variance σ_t serves as the dynamic schedule proxy. Critical design decisions: (a) correcting xˆ_{t-1} rather than xˆ_t avoids an extra forward pass; (b) the wavelet decomposition is justified by DPMs' empirically documented frequency-ordered reconstruction; (c) the assumption γ_t < 1 (rather than prior work's γ_t = 1) is both theoretically argued and validated empirically. Key Results All FID figures are computed over 50K generated samples against the full training set. On CIFAR-10 32×32, DCW reduces IDDPM's FID from 13.19 to 7.57 at T=20 (42.6%) and from 5.55 to 4.16 at T=50 (25%). On LSUN Bedroom 256×256, IDDPM FID drops from 18.69 to 11.03 at T=20 (41%). For EDM on CIFAR-10, DCW cuts FID from 10.66 to 5.67 at 13 NFE (47.1%), 5.91 to 3.37 at 21 NFE (43%), and 3.74 to 2.41 at 35 NFE (36%). Against ICLR 2025 baselines on CIFAR-10: DCW (Base+Ours FID 9.36 at 10 steps) outperforms DPM-AE (13.98) and DPM-AT (15.88) on ADM. On DiT with ImageNet 256×256, FID improves from 12.83 to 7.99 at T=20 and 3.78 to 3.09 at T=50, beating ADM-ES (10.00 / 3.30). Additive gains above already-corrected models: DCW reduces EDM-ES FID by 7.0%, 5.3%, 3.5% at 13/21/35 NFE; reduces PFGM-FR FID by 6.6%, 5.7%, 2.0%. Computational overhead measured on NVIDIA A6000: 0.47% (CelebA 64), 0.08% (ImageNet 128), 0.26% (LSUN 256). Baselines (DPM-AE, DPM-AT, ADM-IP, ADM-ES, DPM-FR) are all recent, published at top venues (ICML 2023, ICLR 2024/2025, ACM MM 2025). Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The SNR-t bias is a genuinely novel mechanistic framing: prior work (ADM-ES) observed the symptom (overestimated reverse-process norms) but offered no causal explanation. The DCW correction mechanism is new. The refinement of the linear reconstruction assumption from x^0_θ = x_0 + φ·ε (prior work) to x^0_θ = γ·x_0 + φ·ε with γ < 1 is a meaningful theoretical contribution with both proof and empirical support. The wavelet-domain frequency-aware scheduling is adapted from prior use in super-resolution but is novel in this inference-correction context. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Table 6 cleanly isolates pixel-space DC vs. high-frequency-only DH vs. low-frequency-only DL vs. full DCW, showing each contributes. Figure 4 and Table 9 demonstrate smooth FID-vs-hyperparameter curves with clear optima, supporting robustness claims. The critical Key Finding 2 experiment is repeated across three random seeds (16, 42, 99) and four batch sizes (10, 100, 1000, 2000), with consistent results (Figure 5). No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported on FID tables. Code is released at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/DCW. A concern: Assumption 5.1 is justified theoretically and empirically on ADM/CIFAR-10 only; its validity across FLUX, DiT, and Qwen-Image (where γ_t values are never measured) is asserted rather than verified. This is not a fatal flaw but limits the strength of the theoretical claim's universality. This is an arXiv preprint (v1, April 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs at inference on any GPU that can run the underlying DPM; DWT/iDWT are standard operations available in PyTorch and similar frameworks; no retraining or fine-tuning required. - Efficient: Adds 0.08%–0.47% wall-clock time per batch on an NVIDIA A6000 with zero additional NFE; memory overhead is a single extra tensor per denoising step. - Effective: FID improves consistently across all 8+ tested architectures and all 4 datasets; additive gains on already-corrected models confirm the correction addresses a distinct failure mode beyond exposure bias. - Scalable: Quantitative evaluation is limited to resolutions up to 256×256; qualitative results on FLUX and Qwen-Image suggest visual improvement at text-to-image scale, but no FID or other quantitative metric is reported at 512×512 or 1024×1024 — the regime where production deployments actually run. The work delivers a software artifact (GitHub repository with code) and a reusable plug-and-play correction applicable to any iterative diffusion sampler. Strengths - The causal mechanistic explanation — SNR-timestep decoupling as the root cause of error accumulation — is the most precise account in the literature; prior methods corrected the symptom without naming the mechanism. - The method is genuinely training-free, adds negligible overhead, and composes additively with existing exposure-bias correction methods, making adoption low-risk. - Theoretical derivation of the actual SNR of reverse-process samples (Theorem 5.1, with full proof in Appendix C) gives the correction a principled target, unlike purely heuristic inference-time tricks. - Breadth of empirical coverage (IDDPM, ADM, DDIM, EDM, PFGM++, DiT, FLUX, Qwen-Image) across architectures spanning 2020–2026 is unusually wide for a single paper. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not include a dedicated limitations section. They acknowledge that Assumption 5.1 (γ_t < 1) differs from prior work's assumption and provide both theoretical and experimental support for it, but they do not flag that γ_t and φ_t are unmeasured scalars across most tested architectures. They characterize their bias as distinct from and more fundamental than exposure bias but do not claim the method fully eliminates the bias — only that it alleviates it. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most significant omission is the absence of quantitative FID evaluation at high resolutions (512×512, 1024×1024). FLUX and Qwen-Image are shown only qualitatively; the paper's central claim of broad applicability cannot be fully assessed without quantitative results at the scale these models are designed for. - Assumption 5.1 is the load-bearing theoretical premise. The scalars γ_t and φ_t are never directly estimated from any of the tested models; the empirical validation (Figure 6) uses only ADM on CIFAR-10. Whether the assumption holds for flow-matching models or transformer-based architectures like FLUX is untested. - Hyperparameters λ_l and λ_h require per-model, per-dataset tuning via grid search. The paper does not provide a single universal configuration that transfers across architectures, which limits the plug-and-play claim in practice. - No evaluation under very-few-step regimes (4–8 NFE) as used by consistency models or LCM, where the bias accumulation and the correction's behavior might differ qualitatively. - No comparison with or integration into flow-matching samplers (e.g., Stable Diffusion 3 or Flux Flow) at the quantitative level, despite FLUX being qualitatively tested. Bottom Line A diffusion model practitioner gains a near-zero-cost inference improvement with a credible mechanistic explanation; the unresolved gap is quantitative validation at the high resolutions where modern generative models are deployed.
Recommended 2604.16044 PDF
#12 — Highly Innovative (76)

Efficient GNN Training on Giant Graphs with Collective Batching and Scheduling

vldb.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (70) — collective batching with adaptive CPU/GPU split and dual-stream PCIe orchestration is a non-obvious systems contribution. Rigor (75) — NP-hardness proof, approximation bound (loose but present), DUCATI^Δ ablation cleanly isolates scheduling, 27 settings, public code; no error bars and synthetic features on 2/3 datasets. Non-obviousness (75) — DUCATI^Δ proves naive combination hurts; insight is non-trivial. Completeness (75) — limitations stated honestly. Resilience (70) — approx ratio vacuously loose, profiling stability assumption may break elsewhere. Impact (85) — directly deployable, released code, addresses 70%+ of cloud GPU instances. Weighted (systems): 0.15*70+0.15*75+0.10*75+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.30*85 = ~76.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MorphGL, a GNN training system that adaptively splits mini-batch preparation between CPU and GPU using offline profiling and a Dual-Buffer Scheduling algorithm to eliminate hardware idleness and PCIe contention simultaneously. - Why it's important: Existing systems bind mini-batch preparation exclusively to CPU (leaving GPU idle >70% of training time) or GPU (wasting 87.5% of CPU cores), and neither adapts to the CPU:GPU ratios found on over 70% of cloud GPU instances. - How it does it: Offline profiling measures stable mean stage durations, a Dispatcher solves a relaxed closed-form optimization for an initial CPU:GPU workload split, and a Dual-Buffer Scheduler uses two CUDA streams to interleave DMA and UVA transfers with model training without PCIe contention, iterating to convergence via buffer-blocking feedback. - The good: MorphGL achieves up to 2.76× speedup over SALIENT and 2.2× over DUCATI across 27 settings (4 machines × 3 models × 3 billion-edge graphs) with no accuracy trade-off, and a DUCATI^Δ ablation confirms that naive hybrid batching without proper scheduling is insufficient. - The missing: Evaluation is single-GPU and in-memory only; two of three datasets use synthetically generated node features; no error bars on epoch times; the theoretical approximation ratio (3 + Bdw_p/Bdw_g) is vacuously loose for real hardware. - Read it or not: Systems researchers building GNN training infrastructure or ML practitioners training on billion-scale graphs with standard cloud hardware (4–12 CPU cores per GPU) should read this for a directly deployable, well-engineered solution. Problem & Motivation Mini-batch GNN training on billion-scale graphs requires two workloads: batching (neighbor sampling + feature collection) and GPU model training. Existing systems assign batching exclusively to CPU (Category I: SALIENT, DGL, PyG) or GPU (Category II: DUCATI, Quiver, DSP). On a machine with 8 CPU cores and an RTX 3090, SALIENT leaves the GPU idle over 70% of training time; DUCATI uses only 1 of 8 CPU cores. Analysis of 141 GPU instances across AWS, Azure, and GCP shows that over 70% have CPU:GPU core ratios between 4 and 12, where neither approach is efficient. The gap is not a hardware corner case — it is the norm on commodity and cloud hardware. Key Idea The core insight is that mini-batch sizes follow a narrow normal distribution (small coefficient of variation) for a given dataset, making offline profiling of mean stage durations sufficiently accurate to drive scheduling decisions. This stability enables a tractable two-level approach: (1) solve a relaxed closed-form optimization to determine what fraction of batches CPU and GPU should each produce, then (2) coordinate the resulting heterogeneous stream of CPU-produced (DMA-transferred) and GPU-produced (UVA-transferred) batches using dual CUDA streams so that DMA and UVA transfers — which both compete for PCIe — are never concurrent. Methodology MorphGL comprises four components. The Profiler runs k=1000 sample mini-batches to measure mean durations for five stage types: CPU batching (T_CBT), GPU batching (T_GBT), DMA transfer (T_DMA), UVA transfer (T_UVA), and model training (T_Model). The Dispatcher computes an initial distribution ratio x (CPU:GPU batch count = 1:x) by minimizing a bounded piecewise function of x under relaxed buffer and divisibility constraints (Eq. 6), yielding a closed-form solution. The ratio is then iteratively refined via feedback: if the Scheduler reports that the GPU buffer blocks more than the CPU buffer, x is decreased by adjusting the CPU buffer size (cbs) by ±1 per round; convergence is guaranteed because cbs is an integer bounded below by 1. The Scheduler implements the Dual-Buffer Scheduling algorithm (Algorithm 1): a CPU buffer (main memory, capacity cbs = ⌊gbs/x⌋) and a GPU buffer (device memory, capacity gbs=10) are maintained.
