Execution cycles through three phases — filling (both buffers filling in parallel: CPU occupies CPU, GPU batching occupies GPU+PCIe, no contention), blocking (one buffer full, wait), and flushing (GPU buffer drains via model training on CUDA stream s0 while CPU buffer items are DMA-transferred asynchronously on stream s1 and appended to the GPU buffer). The NP-hardness of the scheduling problem is proved via reduction to 3-machine job-shop scheduling. Theorem 2 establishes an approximation ratio of 3 + Bdw_p/Bdw_g for the converged solution. Critical design decisions: (i) profiling over cost modeling — well-justified by the stability observation; (ii) dual CUDA streams to separate DMA and UVA traffic — necessary but not independently ablated; (iii) buffer-blocking feedback as convergence signal — elegant but the convergence argument relies on integer buffer sizes and is only valid under the assumption that CPU batching dominates. Key Results Datasets: Ogbn-Papers100M (PA: 111M nodes, 3.0B edges), Twitter (TW: 41.7M, 2.1B), UK-2006-05 (UK: 77.7M, 5.3B). Machines: A (A30, 12 cores), B (RTX 3090, 8 cores), C (MIG-A30, 6 cores), D (V100, 8 cores). Models: GCN, GraphSAGE, GAT, 3-layer, fanouts (15,10,5), batch size 1024. MorphGL vs. SALIENT: up to 2.76× speedup (Machine B, GraphSAGE, PA), 1.55× average. MorphGL vs. DUCATI: up to 2.2× (Machine A, GAT, UK), 1.41× average. Both baselines are current and strong (SALIENT 2022, DUCATI 2023). MorphGL vs. DUCATI^Δ (naive hybrid, grid-searched over r ∈ [0,1]): MorphGL wins by 1.05×–1.34× even at DUCATI^Δ's best configuration, and DUCATI^Δ is sometimes slower than pure DUCATI (1.37× slower on PA), demonstrating that naive hybrid batching without scheduling introduces PCIe contention. Hardware utilization (GraphSAGE, Machine B, UK): MorphGL achieves ~800% CPU / 46% PCIe / 89% GPU vs. SALIENT's 801%/34%/30% and DUCATI's 100%/25%/86%. Preprocessing overhead averages 3.9 training epochs — negligible for multi-hundred-epoch training. CPU batching workload share adapts from 33% to 75% across settings. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM, NEW METHOD. The collective batching concept — splitting mini-batch preparation between CPU and GPU with adaptive dispatch — is new. The formal NP-hardness reduction and the Dual-Buffer Scheduling algorithm with a convergence guarantee and approximation ratio are technically novel. The insight that DMA and UVA transfers can be orchestrated via separate CUDA streams to eliminate PCIe contention while exploiting both paths is a non-obvious engineering contribution. This is not a straightforward combination of SALIENT and DUCATI: the DUCATI^Δ ablation directly shows that naive combination degrades performance. Rigor & Reproducibility Source code is publicly released at https://github.com/initzhang/MorphGL. The DUCATI^Δ ablation is well-designed and cleanly isolates the scheduling contribution. Epoch times are averaged over 5 epochs after 1 warmup epoch, but no variance or confidence intervals are reported — given hardware variability, this is a gap. The theoretical proof sketch is in the paper; the full proof is deferred to a technical report at the same GitHub URL. No significance tests are reported. Two of three datasets (UK, TW) use randomly generated 256-dimensional node features, which may not reflect realistic feature access patterns. The evaluation covers 27 settings across 4 machines, 3 models, and 3 datasets — broad by systems-paper standards. Deployability (SYSTEMS) - Practical: Runs on standard NVIDIA GPU machines (RTX 3090, A30, V100) with multi-core CPUs, all commercially available. - Efficient: Preprocessing costs ~3.9 training epochs on average; GPU buffer occupies under 2 GB; CPU buffer scales with distribution ratio. Runtime overhead is negligible for training tasks requiring hundreds of epochs. - Effective: Demonstrates 1.41–1.55× average speedup over two strong baselines on three billion-edge graphs with no change to mini-batch content or model accuracy; hardware utilization measurements corroborate the throughput gains. - Scalable: Tested on graphs up to 5.3B edges (UK-2006-05); multi-GPU and disk-based training (for graphs exceeding main memory) are explicitly left for future work and undemonstrated. Delivers a software artifact at https://github.com/initzhang/MorphGL. Strengths - The NP-hardness proof + approximation bound gives theoretical grounding beyond a pure engineering contribution; the reduction to 3-machine JSSP is clean. - The DUCATI^Δ ablation is the strongest piece of evidence in the paper: it shows that naive CPU+GPU co-batching can actually hurt performance, isolating the scheduling algorithm as the essential ingredient. - Mini-batch size stability observation (narrow normal distribution, small CV across all three datasets) is both empirically validated and load-bearing for the entire approach — a genuine insight rather than an assumption. - Breadth of evaluation over the top-3 cloud CPU:GPU ratios, 3 GNN models of increasing compute complexity, and 3 datasets with different locality properties, with consistent wins in all 27 settings. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly limit scope to single-GPU in-memory training, noting disk-based and multi-GPU settings as future work. They acknowledge that MorphGL's advantage diminishes at extreme CPU:GPU ratios (very few or very many cores). These are stated clearly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The approximation ratio 3 + Bdw_p/Bdw_g is vacuously loose in practice: for PCIe 3.0×16 (~16 GB/s) vs. GPU HBM (~900 GB/s on A100), the ratio is ~56, giving a bound of ~59×. The paper does not acknowledge that the theoretical guarantee provides no meaningful assurance on real hardware. - UK and TW use synthetically generated random features; if real features have skewed or correlated access patterns, the profiling-based stability assumption may not hold and the dispatcher's initial ratio could be misaligned. - No per-epoch convergence curves are shown; the claim that MorphGL converges identically to baselines rests on argument (same mini-batches) rather than empirical verification across epochs. - Only GraphSAGE-style random neighbor sampling is evaluated; layer-wise (LADIES) or subgraph (GraphSAINT) sampling methods have different batching cost profiles and may not exhibit the same mini-batch size stability. - The sensitivity of system performance to the profiling budget k=1000 is not evaluated; if profiling is noisy on irregular graphs, the dispatch ratio could be suboptimal. Bottom Line A practitioner training GNNs on billion-scale in-memory graphs on standard cloud or workstation hardware will gain a directly deployable system with 1.4–2.8× speedup and zero accuracy trade-off — this is a concrete, well-validated systems contribution worth reading.
Search Results
#13 — Highly Innovative (76)

Rethinking On-Policy Distillation of Large Language Models: Phenomenology, Mechanism, and Recipe

Yaxuan Li, Yuxin Zuo, Bingxiang He, Jinqian Zhang, Chaojun Xiao, Cheng Qian, Tianyu Yu, Huan-ang Gao, Wenkai Yang, Zhiyuan Liu, Ning Ding · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — new empirical findings (governing conditions, reverse-distillation counterexample) and a new mechanistic formulation (overlap-token theory), though not a new algorithm. Rigor (78) — controlled reverse-distillation and overlap/non-overlap interventional ablations are unusually clean, but no error bars/significance tests, math-only domain raises Goodhart concerns, code-only release. Non-Obviousness (85) — the stronger-teacher-fails-equally-to-weaker result is a hard-to-dismiss surprise. Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) — limitations confronted honestly but missing RL baseline and >7B scaling leave the practically decisive question open. Impact (70) — diagnostic metrics and recipes are directly implementable but no library/weights. Composite ≈ 0.20·75 + 0.25·78 + 0.15·85 + 0.15·72 + 0.15·70 + 0.10·70 = 75.5 → 76.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper identifies two governing conditions for on-policy distillation (OPD) success — thinking-pattern consistency between student and teacher, and the teacher possessing genuinely new knowledge beyond what the student has seen — reveals the causal token-level mechanism (progressive alignment on shared high-probability overlap tokens), and proposes two practical recipes (off-policy cold start and teacher-aligned prompt selection) to recover failing OPD runs. - Why it's important: OPD is deployed in production post-training pipelines for Qwen3, MiMo, and GLM-5, yet practitioners use teacher benchmark scores as a proxy for distillation quality — a proxy this paper demonstrates is unreliable and can predict the wrong outcome. - How it does it: Controlled reverse-distillation experiments hold the student fixed while varying the teacher, and token-level metrics (overlap ratio, overlap-token advantage, entropy gap) are tracked throughout training to distinguish successful from failing runs causally, not just correlationally. - The good: The reverse distillation finding — that a weaker 1.5B teacher and a strictly stronger 7B teacher from the same model family drive the student to virtually identical (poor) outcomes despite the 7B outperforming on benchmarks — is a concrete, hard-to-dismiss counter-example to the dominant intuition that stronger teachers are always better. - The missing: All experiments use mathematical reasoning benchmarks (AIME 2024, AIME 2025, AMC 2023) with models in the 1.5B–7B range; generalization to code, open-ended tasks, or frontier-scale models is entirely untested. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers designing LLM post-training pipelines should read this; it provides precisely defined diagnostic metrics and empirically validated recipes, though its math-only scope and absence of RL baselines leave the most practically decisive comparison open. Problem & Motivation On-policy distillation uses the student's own sampled rollouts as training contexts and leverages the teacher's per-token log-probabilities as dense reward, avoiding the exposure-bias problem of supervised fine-tuning on fixed teacher sequences. Despite rapid adoption in industry post-training pipelines, OPD exhibits a striking failure mode: a stronger teacher can completely fail to improve the student while a weaker teacher succeeds. No prior work has systematically identified the conditions under which OPD succeeds or fails, nor explained the token-level mechanism through which teacher supervision steers the student. Practitioners therefore lack principled tools to select teachers, configure runs, or diagnose failures — and the implicit assumption that higher teacher benchmark scores guarantee better distillation is empirically wrong. Key Idea The central insight is that OPD's effective gradient signal is concentrated almost entirely on the small set of tokens that appear in both the student's and the teacher's high-probability top-k sets (the "overlap" region), which carries 97–99% of the combined probability mass. For OPD to succeed, (1) this overlap must be non-negligible from the outset (thinking-pattern consistency), and (2) the teacher must assign different probabilities to those shared tokens than the student already learned (new knowledge). When both conditions hold, the overlap set grows self-reinforcingly — each reverse-KL update pushes more student mass toward teacher-preferred tokens within the overlap, enlarging it further — creating a virtuous cycle. When either condition fails, the overlap stagnates and gradient updates point in incoherent directions despite the teacher's reward being globally informative. Methodology The paper is organized into three empirical components. In phenomenology (§3), the authors design controlled OPD experiments holding the student fixed and varying the teacher across two model families: (i) Qwen3-1.7B-Base with Qwen3-4B (non-thinking) vs. Qwen3-4B-Base-GRPO, and (ii) R1-Distill-1.5B with R1-Distill-7B vs. Skywork-OR1-Math-7B. A reverse-distillation experiment uses JustRL-1.5B (an RL-trained 1.5B model) as the student, distilling from its own pre-RL checkpoint (R1-Distill-1.5B) and from R1-Distill-7B, to disentangle thinking-pattern alignment from benchmark performance. In mechanism (§4), three dynamic metrics — overlap ratio M_overlap (fraction of shared top-k tokens), overlap-token advantage M_adv (average log-ratio within overlap), and entropy gap ΔH (|H(q_t)−H(p_t)|) — are tracked throughout training. An ablation partitions the top-k support into overlap and non-overlap subsets and trains on each in isolation to test causal sufficiency. In recipe (§5), off-policy cold start (SFT on teacher rollouts before OPD) and teacher-aligned prompts (matching the prompt template or content from the teacher's post-training data) are each tested as interventions on otherwise-failing configurations. In §6, reward reliability is probed by training across six maximum response lengths (0.5K–15K tokens) and measuring teacher continuation accuracy from student-generated prefixes at truncation depths of 1K, 4K, 8K, 16K tokens. The loss throughout is per-token reverse KL over student-generated trajectories. All math experiments use DAPO-Math-17K or subsets of DeepMath, evaluated at avg@16 (temperature 0.7, top-p 0.95) on AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and AMC 2023. Key Results Thinking-pattern consistency: Skywork-OR1-Math-7B (post-RL teacher) achieves a 16.9% gap recovery rate vs. 5.3% for same-family R1-Distill-7B in the DeepSeek family; in the Qwen family, Qwen3-4B-Non-Thinking-RL-Math achieves 58.6% vs. 15.6% for Qwen3-4B (non-thinking), despite the RL teachers sharing the same base checkpoint as the student. Reverse distillation: both R1-Distill-1.5B and R1-Distill-7B as teachers drive JustRL-1.5B to the same regressed accuracy level (~0.28 on AIME 2025), with indistinguishable training curves despite the 7B scoring higher than 1.5B on benchmarks. Overlap sufficiency: Overlap Top-k recovers full Student Top-k performance on all three benchmarks, while Non-Overlap Top-k consistently underperforms; overlap ratio rises from ~72% to ~91% in successful runs. Off-policy cold start: SFT-initialized OPD outperforms pure OPD throughout training and maintains a higher overlap ratio and lower entropy gap from the start. Reward degradation: teacher continuation advantage over student prefixes decreases monotonically from +0.37 at 1K tokens to +0.02 at 16K tokens; moderate response lengths (3K–7K tokens) yield the strongest downstream accuracy, while 10K–15K exhibits late-stage collapse with entropy spikes and overlap ratio drops. Sampled-token OPD matches Top-4 through Top-64 on all three benchmarks; Top-1 consistently underperforms due to biased mode-concentrated selection. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. The two governing conditions and the associated diagnostic metrics are new empirical contributions — prior OPD work focused on demonstrating promise across varied objectives without analyzing when or why it fails. The reverse distillation design is an original controlled experiment not seen in the literature. The overlap-token mechanism provides a new formulation of what OPD optimizes in practice. This is a mechanistic study of an existing technique rather than a new training algorithm; the genuinely novel contribution is the causal evidence (from ablations and controlled reversals) rather than a new method. Rigor & Reproducibility The overlap vs. non-overlap ablation directly tests the causal claim (not just observational correlation). The reverse distillation experiment is a well-designed internal control isolating thinking-pattern from benchmark performance. Multiple model families (DeepSeek, Qwen) and three benchmarks provide empirical breadth. avg@16 reduces sample variance at the problem level, though no formal significance tests, confidence intervals, or error bars appear anywhere in the paper. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/thunlp/OPD; trained model weights and full training datasets are not released. The paper is an arXiv preprint (April 2026), not peer-reviewed. One Goodhart's Law concern: all evaluation benchmarks (AIME, AMC) are in the same mathematical reasoning domain as the training data; the recipes may be optimizing for the evaluation domain rather than generalizable OPD behavior. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All experiments use 1.5B–7B parameter models and standard GPU training; no specialized hardware is required, and the recommended sampled-token OPD is the cheapest computational variant. - Efficient: The finding that sampled-token OPD matches Top-4 through Top-64 directly simplifies implementation; the off-policy cold start adds a SFT phase but the paper does not report its compute cost relative to additional RL training. - Effective: The overlap-token ablation provides causal evidence rather than correlation, and the recovery recipes show measurable gains across all three benchmarks on previously failing configurations. - Scalable: Not evaluated beyond 7B parameter models or 200 training steps; the reward degradation finding at long trajectories (>7K tokens) is a concrete limitation for long-horizon and agentic settings that the authors explicitly acknowledge but do not resolve. The work delivers code (scripts and training configs); no pre-trained model weights, no new dataset, and no standalone library. Strengths - The reverse distillation experiment is an elegant natural experiment that simultaneously validates both conditions — by showing a weaker and a stronger same-family teacher produce identical outcomes, the paper rules out benchmark performance as a confound in a single controlled design. - The overlap-token sufficiency ablation provides interventional (not just observational) evidence that the overlap set is the causal gradient locus, distinguishing this analysis from descriptive diagnostic papers. - The reward degradation analysis (§6) identifies a fundamental ceiling for OPD in long-horizon settings: teacher continuation advantage collapses from +0.37 at 1K to +0.02 at 16K prefix depth, with collapse propagating backward through the trajectory — an important finding for practitioners targeting agentic or extended chain-of-thought settings. - The three diagnostic metrics (overlap ratio, overlap-token advantage, entropy gap) are precisely defined and consistently discriminate successful from failing runs across model families and teacher-student pairs, providing monitoring tools that a practitioner can implement directly. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: (1) all experiments are on mathematical reasoning benchmarks and generalization to code and open-ended tasks remains open; (2) isolating the new-knowledge condition from pre-training corpus differences (vs. tokenizer or architecture differences) requires controlled pre-training ablations that are prohibitively expensive; (3) the anisotropy hypothesis for why globally informative rewards fail locally is suggestive but not directly verified; (4) teacher-aligned prompts substantially reduce student entropy, requiring mixing with out-of-distribution prompts to preserve exploration. These are confronted explicitly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper does not compare OPD-with-recipe against RL (GRPO/PPO) with the same compute budget; this is the most practically decisive comparison for post-training pipeline designers, and its absence leaves the central design question — when to choose OPD over RL — unanswered. - All students are base or lightly RL-trained models at 1.5B–7B scale; whether thinking-pattern alignment plays the same role at 70B+ is unknown and cannot be inferred from the current experiments. - The AIME/AMC evaluation domain matches the training data domain exactly; no out-of-domain generalization test is reported, which creates a risk that the recipes optimize for benchmark-specific patterns rather than general OPD behavior. - "Thinking pattern" is operationalized through the observable overlap ratio metric but is not defined a priori; a practitioner cannot determine compatibility before training begins, limiting the prescriptive value of the framework. - The off-policy cold start recipe's total compute cost (SFT + OPD) versus just running more RL from the base model is never reported, which is necessary for a cost-benefit assessment. Bottom Line Practitioners designing OPD post-training pipelines gain concrete diagnostic tools and empirically validated recipes from this work, anchored by a well-designed reverse-distillation experiment that overturns the "stronger teacher is always better" assumption — but the math-only evaluation scope, absence of RL baselines, and untested scaling behavior mean the work answers "why does OPD fail?" more definitively than "should you use OPD at all?"
Recommended 2604.13016 PDF
#14 — Highly Innovative (76)

TEMPO: Scaling Test-time Training for Large Reasoning Models (25 ▲ )

Qingyang Zhang, Xinke Kong, Haitao Wu, Qinghua Hu, Minghao Wu, Baosong Yang, Yu Cheng, Yun Luo, Ganqu Cui, Changqing Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — the EM reformulation of TTT identifying the missing E-step is a genuine conceptual contribution. Rigor (75) — clean frozen-critic ablation, broad nine-benchmark coverage across two model families, code released, but no error bars/significance tests, LLM-judge dependence, recalibration frequency unablated, train-on-test-distribution asymmetry not flagged; preprint penalty. Non-Obviousness (80) — diagnosing prior TTT failure as M-step-only is a clarifying insight that reframes the literature. Completeness (75) — limitations acknowledged honestly. Resilience (75) — main mechanistic claim survives the frozen-critic ablation, though scaling beyond 14B is uncertain. Impact (75) — code and weights released. Weighted: 0.20*75+0.25*75+0.15*80+0.15*75+0.15*75+0.10*75 = 75.75 ≈ 76.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 75 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TEMPO, a test-time training (TTT) framework that alternates between periodic critic recalibration on a small labeled dataset (E-step) and policy refinement on unlabeled test questions (M-step), formalized as an Expectation-Maximization procedure. - Why it's important: Existing TTT methods for large reasoning models plateau quickly and suffer diversity collapse because their self-generated reward signals drift as the policy grows more confident, making additional test-time compute counterproductive. - How it does it: A token-level critic is trained on labeled data to approximate the posterior over correct responses; its scores drive policy gradient updates on unlabeled questions, and the critic is re-grounded on labeled data every iteration to prevent reward drift. - The good: TEMPO improves Qwen3-14B avg@16 accuracy on AIME 2024 from 42.3% to 65.8% and sustains a monotonically increasing trajectory over 350 steps without diversity collapse, whereas all baselines plateau and degrade pass@k. - The missing: The labeled calibration dataset DL is always in-domain math (DAPO-Math-17K), leaving unanswered whether critic quality degrades under domain mismatch; recalibration frequency is never ablated; and no experiments exceed 14B parameters. - Read it or not: Researchers working on test-time compute scaling or self-improving LLMs should read it — the EM diagnosis of prior TTT failures is genuinely clarifying and the empirical case is broad. Problem & Motivation: Large reasoning models achieve strong performance via extended test-time chain-of-thought computation, but their parameters remain static after training. Test-time training (TTT) attempts to adapt parameters on unlabeled test questions using self-generated reward signals — entropy minimization, majority voting, self-consistency. These signals are bounded by the model's initial capabilities: as the policy grows confident in a narrow reasoning pattern, heuristic rewards overestimate that pattern's quality, creating a self-reinforcing loop that produces performance plateaus and output diversity collapse. The gap addressed here is that no prior TTT method corrects for this drift by grounding the reward signal in any external supervision during test-time adaptation. Key Idea: Response correctness is an unobserved latent variable at test time. Framing TTT as EM over this latent variable reveals that prior methods execute only the M-step (policy gradient with self-estimated quality labels) while omitting the E-step (re-estimating the posterior over correct responses). Re-introducing the E-step as periodic critic recalibration on a labeled dataset keeps the ELBO tight, preventing reward drift and enabling sustained improvement with additional compute. Methodology: TEMPO proceeds in two stages. In Stage 1 (initialization), both an actor policy πθ and a token-level critic Vϕ are trained jointly via RLVR (PPO) on a labeled dataset DL (DAPO-Math-17K, 17K math problems). In Stage 2 (alternating TTT), each iteration performs: (E-step) sample a batch from DL and update the critic by minimizing MSE between its token-level value predictions Vϕ(x, yt) and a binary correctness indicator I ∈ {0,1} — Lcritic(ϕ) = E||Vϕ(x,yt) − I||²; (M-step) sample unlabeled questions from Du, generate responses under πθ, compute per-token advantages At = Vϕ(x,yT) − Vϕ(x,y1:t) using the critic's final-token score as realized reward and intermediate predictions as baselines, then optimize Lpolicy(θ) = −E[Σ At log πθ(yt|x,y<t)] via policy gradient. The sequence clip mechanism from GSPO (dual-clip ratio 3×10⁻⁴ and 5×10⁻⁴) stabilizes off-policy training. Maximum response length is 16K tokens. Critical design decisions: (1) using a token-level critic rather than a response-level scalar — justified for variance reduction in advantage estimation; (2) re-calibrating the critic every iteration rather than once — validated by the frozen-critic ablation; (3) requiring an initial RLVR stage — not ablated, so its necessity relative to starting from a pretrained base is unknown. Key Results: On mathematical reasoning, TEMPO improves OLMO3-7B avg@16 on AIME 2024 from 33.0% to 51.1% (+18.1 absolute) and Qwen3-14B from 42.3% to 65.8% (+23.5 absolute), versus baselines TTRL and EMPO that reach 40.8–55.6% and then plateau or degrade. Pass@8 on AIME 2024 is 61.6 (OLMO3-7B) and 73.3 (Qwen3-14B) under TEMPO versus 43.3–45.6 and 56.7–59.7 under baselines. On general reasoning, OLMO3-7B gains +21.4 on BigBenchHard (46.8→68.2%), +24.5 on AGI Eval (37.9→62.4%), and +12.9 on ZebraLogic, surpassing specialized 7B frontier models (General-Reasoner-7B at 65.6% BBH). Qwen3-8B gains are smaller (+4.3 BBH, +4.4 AGI Eval, +8.2 ZebraLogic) from a higher base. The frozen-critic ablation (Figure 6) shows the full method continues improving after step 100 while the frozen-critic variant plateaus, isolating the E-step contribution. Supervised PPO continuation from a converged OLMO3 model yields negligible gains while TEMPO gains 15+ avg@16 points in 200 steps (Figure 5). Qwen3-14B results are reported at 224 steps and Figure 4 shows accuracy still rising, implying reported numbers are conservative. Benchmarks used are AIME 2024/2025/2026, Beyond AIME, OlymMath, BBH, AGI Eval, ZebraLogic, and GPQA-Diamond — a reasonably broad evaluation. Baselines (TTRL, EMPO, PPO-RLVR) are current and representative of the TTT literature. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The EM formalization of TTT and the diagnosis of prior methods as M-step-only degenerate variants is the genuine intellectual contribution; actor-critic RL, PPO, and token-level value estimation are all existing components. The novelty is architectural and conceptual — identifying which step was missing and why its absence causes failure — rather than algorithmic invention from scratch. Rigor & Reproducibility: The frozen-critic ablation (Figure 6) and supervised-continuation ablation (Figure 5) are clean and directly support the paper's core claims. Multiple model families (Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-14B, OLMO3-7B) and five math benchmarks plus four general reasoning benchmarks provide genuine breadth. No error bars or significance tests appear in the main results table; avg@16 and pass@8 over 16 samples per problem provides some variance control for the math benchmarks, but single-sample Avg@1 on BBH/AGI Eval/ZebraLogic provides less confidence. Code is publicly released (GitHub link in paper). The correctness judge for general reasoning tasks is gpt-oss-120b, a proprietary model, introducing a non-reproducible evaluation dependency. The recalibration frequency (every step per Algorithm 1) is never ablated — it is a free hyperparameter that could significantly affect the compute-accuracy tradeoff. The labeled dataset DL is always math-domain (DAPO-Math-17K), even for the general reasoning experiments; how the critic generalizes across domain shift is not analyzed. Finally, the test questions themselves (e.g., AIME 2024) are used as Du during TTT, so TEMPO literally trains on the test distribution — accuracy numbers are not comparable to offline methods on a held-out test set, which is the nature of TTT but must be understood when interpreting the gains. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Requires running two LLMs simultaneously (actor + critic) of identical or similar size, roughly doubling GPU memory vs. single-model TTT; feasible on multi-GPU academic clusters but heavier than TTRL/EMPO. - Efficient: Approximately 2× parameter overhead versus single-model TTT baselines; the per-step compute includes labeled-data critic updates and unlabeled policy rollouts with up to 16K token responses — significantly more expensive per test instance than inference-only scaling. - Effective: Evidence is strong for math reasoning; general-reasoning gains are large for OLMO3-7B and modest for Qwen3-8B, with the correctness signal depending on an LLM judge that introduces its own error rate. - Scalable: All experiments are ≤14B parameters; no experiments above 14B are reported; Figure 4 shows performance still rising at 350 steps, suggesting further gains with more compute, but scaling to 70B+ models or longer training horizons is claimed but not demonstrated. A software artifact (code + model weights via HuggingFace) is released. Strengths: - The EM formalization is genuinely clarifying: expressing response correctness as a latent variable unifies diverse TTT heuristics under one framework and identifies the missing E-step as the mechanistic cause of plateau and collapse — this is a real conceptual contribution, not a repackaging. - Diversity preservation is concretely measured via pass@k, and TEMPO's advantage is large and consistent (pass@8 gap of 10–17 points over baselines on AIME 2024 for Qwen3-14B), addressing a failure mode that prior work largely glossed over. - Generalization across two model families and nine benchmarks spanning math, logic, and STEM is unusually broad for a TTT paper and meaningfully strengthens the claim that the alternating design is domain-agnostic. - The ablation isolating the E-step (frozen critic vs. full TEMPO, Figure 6) is clean and directly falsifiable — the curves diverge at exactly the point where drift accumulates. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge four limitations: (1) dual-model overhead relative to single-model TTT; (2) dependence on a labeled dataset DL whose size and distribution affect critic generalization; (3) no evaluation on code generation; (4) no formal convergence guarantees for the alternating EM procedure. These are stated plainly and represent genuine scope boundaries. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The recalibration frequency (every step) is a critical hyperparameter that is never ablated — it directly controls the compute-accuracy tradeoff and could be tuned to favor one side substantially. The labeled dataset DL is always in-domain math even for general-reasoning experiments; if the critic's correctness signal depends on domain match, the reported general-reasoning gains may require a domain-matched DL in practice, which is not acknowledged. No experiments exceed 14B parameters, so scaling behavior is unknown; the community's main interest is in 70B+ frontier models. The train-on-test-distribution nature of TTT means TEMPO's accuracy numbers cannot be compared to offline methods' numbers in the same table — Table 1 mixes TEMPO (trained on AIME 2024 questions) with offline frontier models (not trained on them), an asymmetry not highlighted in the paper. The initial RLVR stage is treated as a prerequisite but never ablated — whether TEMPO works from a pretrained base without this warmup is unstated. Bottom Line: This preprint presents a principled and empirically well-supported fix for a real failure mode in test-time training; despite the expected gaps in a preprint (no convergence theory, limited scale, unablated hyperparameters), any researcher working on test-time compute or self-improving reasoning models will find the EM diagnosis and diversity results directly useful.
Recommended 2604.19295 PDF
#15 — Highly Innovative (76)

(1D) Ordered Tokens Enable Efficient Test-Time Search (11 ▲ )

Zhitong Gao, Parham Rezaei, Ali Cy, Mingqiao Ye, Nataša Jovanović, Jesse Allardice, Afshin Dehghan, Amir Zamir, Roman Bachmann, Oğuzhan Fatih Kar · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — identifying tokenizer ordering (not search algorithm or verifier) as the binding constraint on test-time scaling is a genuine new empirical finding plus the SoTo framework. Rigor (75) — the matched 2D-baseline controlled experiment is the methodological centerpiece and credibly isolates the causal factor; thorough multi-axis ablations; code/weights/visualizations released; weakened by 300-image evaluation subsets without confidence intervals and Janus scale mismatch; preprint penalty. Non-Obviousness (80) — that token order, not search sophistication, dominates is non-obvious; training-free generation via pure beam search is a striking demonstration. Completeness (75) — limitations honestly enumerated. Resilience (75) — the controlled comparison would survive most reviewer attacks; verifier hacking and Lipschitz assumption are residual concerns. Impact (75) — released artifacts, actionable design principle, reusable framework. Weighted: 0.20*75+0.25*75+0.15*80+0.15*75+0.15*75+0.10*75 = 75.75 ≈ 76.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 75 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates that autoregressive image generation models trained on 1D coarse-to-fine ordered tokenizers (e.g., FlexTok) exhibit substantially better test-time scaling than models trained on standard 2D grid tokenizers, and introduces the Search-over-Tokens (SoTo) framework to systematically characterize this advantage. - Why it's important: Test-time scaling is a critical lever for improving generation quality without retraining, but its effectiveness has been poorly understood as a function of tokenizer design — practitioners currently optimize search algorithms without knowing whether their token representation is even amenable to search. - How it does it: Coarse-to-fine 1D tokenizers (trained with nested dropout) ensure that every token prefix decodes into a semantically coherent intermediate image, making partial sequences evaluable by verifiers during beam search; in contrast, 2D raster-scan prefixes expose only a spatial corner, providing weak verifier signal. - The good: A controlled experiment pairing identical data, architecture, and training compute confirms beam search yields large CLIPScore gains for 1D tokens but only marginal gains for 2D tokens, isolating tokenizer structure as the causal factor. - The missing: The paper evaluates only two tokenizer families in depth (FlexTok, Semanticist) and all experiments use a single image domain (natural images); it is untested whether the advantage persists for video, text, or multimodal autoregressive generation. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners building test-time scaling systems for autoregressive image generation should read this; those focused on diffusion models or text-only LLMs can skip it. Problem & Motivation: Autoregressive generative models tokenize data into discrete units, and the order in which those tokens are generated determines what information each partial sequence carries. For 2D grid tokenizers using raster-scan order, the first tokens correspond to a fixed spatial region (e.g., the upper-left corner), providing little global semantic signal. This makes verifier-guided beam search ineffective at early steps, because the partial image reveals nothing about the final composition. Meanwhile, 1D ordered tokenizers trained with nested dropout (e.g., FlexTok, Semanticist) produce sequences where every prefix decodes into a valid, semantically coherent image at progressively higher fidelity. The question this paper addresses is whether this structural property translates into measurably better test-time scalability — and specifically whether the tokenizer, not the search algorithm, is the binding constraint on inference-time compute utilization. Key Idea: The core insight is that coarse-to-fine token ordering creates a hierarchical latent space where verifiers can reliably evaluate partial sequences, enabling beam search to prune the search tree early and efficiently. Because the first token in FlexTok reconstructs the entire image at high compression (capturing global semantics), and subsequent tokens add progressively finer detail, a verifier (e.g., CLIP, ImageReward) can meaningfully score partial sequences at any depth. This is analogous to how kd-trees organize data along maximally informative axes to enable efficient nearest-neighbor search — intermediate states are informative precisely because the tokenizer was trained to minimize reconstruction error at every prefix length. Methodology: The paper introduces the Search-over-Tokens (SoTo) framework, a controlled evaluation system with three independent axes: (A) search algorithm — Best-of-N sampling, Beam Search (width k=5, M=10 candidates/step), and Lookahead Search (full rollouts before scoring); (B) verifier — 8 scoring functions spanning image-text alignment (CLIP, ImageReward, CycleReward, HPSv2, PickScore), image-image alignment (DreamSim), image quality (Aesthetic Score), and rule-based detection (Grounded SAM), plus an ensemble via rank aggregation; (C) AR prior — text-conditional, unconditional, or uniform (no AR model). The primary model is FlexTok d18-d28 with a 3.4B-parameter AR transformer. The controlled 2D baseline exactly matches data, architecture, and training compute. Inference compute is measured in Number of Function Evaluations (NFE), where each token generation or verification counts as one evaluation. Three critical design decisions: (1) using nested dropout as the tokenizer training objective — well-justified as it directly minimizes intermediate reconstruction error; (2) evaluating on COCO Karpathy (300-image subset for beam/lookahead experiments, full for best-of-N), GenEval, and DreamBench++; (3) varying model sizes from 212M to 3.4B parameters to study training-compute vs. inference-compute tradeoffs. Key Results: On COCO Karpathy validation set, beam search produces substantially larger CLIPScore gains for FlexTok (1D ordered) vs. the matched 2D baseline; best-of-N and lookahead search perform comparably across both tokenizers. Comparing to Janus-1.3B (a competitive 2D grid AR model): FlexTok beam search consistently outperforms Janus across inference budgets despite Janus having marginally higher no-search performance. On DreamBench++ zero-shot concept preservation: FlexTok + beam search improves DINO-I from 32.5 to 50.9 (+18.4) while maintaining CLIP-T at 33.1; Janus + lookahead improves DINO-I from 34.8 to 40.7 (+5.9). Training-free generation (uniform prior + beam search) achieves 79% single-object and 32% two-object accuracy on GenEval; adding a conditional AR prior raises this to 100% and 81%. Model-size scaling follows a power law: optimal parameter count ∝ inference FLOPs^0.44 (a = 4.5×10³, b = 0.44); a 530M model with sufficient test-time compute outperforms a 3.4B model with limited compute. Among verifiers, ImageReward and HPSv2 achieve the best average cross-metric rankings; the ensemble ranks second on individual metrics but first overall. Verifier hacking is observed at large compute budgets. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FRAMEWORK. The claim that token structure (not just search algorithm or verifier) is the critical determinant of test-time scaling effectiveness is the genuine novel contribution. The SoTo framework systematizes this analysis across three design axes. Both the coarse-to-fine tokenizers and the individual search algorithms are prior work; the novelty is the controlled comparative study and the identification of tokenizer ordering as the enabling condition. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations over search algorithms, model sizes, verifiers, and AR prior strength are thorough. The controlled baseline (matched data/architecture/compute) is a methodological strength that isolates tokenizer structure as the variable. Experiments run on 300-image COCO subsets for expensive search conditions — the paper acknowledges this and provides stability analysis in an appendix. No error bars or significance tests are reported for the main CLIPScore comparisons; given the small evaluation subset, confidence intervals would be informative. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.15453v1, April 2026). Code, model weights, and interactive visualizations are released at https://soto.epfl.ch. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL paper. - Practical: FlexTok with the 3.4B AR model runs on commercially available multi-GPU hardware; the flow-based detokenizer requires repeated denoising steps per verification, creating latency overhead acknowledged by the authors. - Efficient: Beam search at width 5 with 10 candidates and 256 searched tokens requires substantially more compute than best-of-N at equivalent NFE; the detokenizer bottleneck scales with beam width and lookahead depth, making full lookahead search expensive for 2D baselines. - Effective: Evidence directly supports the core claim — beam search improves 1D tokenizer quality while providing negligible gains for 2D tokenizers — and extends to zero-shot concept preservation and training-free generation. - Scalable: Model-size scaling is tested from 212M to 3.4B parameters with inference compute varied over ~3 orders of magnitude; the paper does not test at 10B+ parameter scales, and power-law extrapolation beyond observed range is unverified. The paper delivers a software artifact (code + weights at soto.epfl.ch) alongside the research findings. Strengths: - The controlled experimental design (matched 2D baseline) credibly isolates tokenizer ordering as the causal factor in test-time scaling, rather than confounding architecture or data. - Training-free image generation via pure beam search over an ordered token space (no AR model, only a codebook prior) is a striking empirical finding that demonstrates the structural advantage of coarse-to-fine ordering in its most extreme form. - The multi-axis SoTo framework (search algorithm × verifier × AR prior) provides a reusable diagnostic tool for any team designing test-time scaling systems. - The power-law fit for optimal model size vs. inference compute (b = 0.44) is a concrete, actionable scaling characterization. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront five limitations: (1) generic search algorithms not exploiting the coarse-to-fine structure; (2) verifier hacking at high compute budgets; (3) computational bottleneck from the flow-based detokenizer during search; (4) evaluation limited to a small set of tokenizer models; (5) restriction to image generation with no multimodal or video extension. This is a credibly self-critical list. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The evaluation subset (300 images) for beam and lookahead experiments is small enough that per-metric variance could be non-trivial — the absence of confidence intervals is a gap for the main comparative claims. The paper compares FlexTok (3.4B) against Janus (1.3B) in Figure 7, a mismatch in model scale that could partially confound the tokenizer-structure comparison; the controlled 2D baseline addresses this for the primary result, but the Janus comparison is less clean. All evaluations measure final-output quality (CLIPScore, GenEval accuracy) and do not report wall-clock latency or GPU-hours for the search procedures, making it difficult to assess practical deployability under real latency budgets. The theoretical bound in Appendix B relies on a Lipschitz assumption on the verifier that is not empirically verified. Bottom Line: A practitioner building test-time scaling for autoregressive image generation gains a concrete, experimentally grounded design principle — choose a coarse-to-fine 1D tokenizer — and a ready-to-use evaluation framework; the controlled ablations and released artifacts make this a directly actionable paper.
Recommended 2604.15453 PDF
#16 — Highly Innovative (76)

SWE-chat: Coding Agent Interactions From Real Users in the Wild (7 ▲ )

Joachim Baumann, Vishakh Padmakumar, Xiang Li, John Yang, Diyi Yang, Sanmi Koyejo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (88) is high — first dataset to couple authentic agent transcripts to git-level line attribution, plus genuinely new empirical facts (9× vulnerability rate, bimodal coding mode distribution, code-survival metric). Non-Obviousness (80) is strong; the security finding and pushback rates are not what synthetic benchmarks would predict. Rigor (65) is held back by preprint status, opt-in self-selection, ~85% Claude Code dominance confounding cross-agent claims, the single-Semgrep-rule plurality of the vulnerability finding without language-mix controls, and only moderate IAA (ICC=0.50) on session success. Completeness (80) is high — Appendix A confronts limitations directly and at length. Resilience (70) is decent but the 9× headline rests on uncontrolled confounds. Practical Impact (80) reflects a public living dataset and analysis pipeline directly usable by benchmark and policy researchers. Weighted: 0.20*88 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*80 = 76.35 → 76.
empirical Novelty 88 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 80 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SWE-chat, the first large-scale dataset of real coding agent sessions (6,000 sessions, 63K user prompts, 355K tool calls from 200+ public GitHub repositories) collected from open-source developers in the wild, with line-level human vs. agent code authorship attribution, plus an initial empirical characterization of usage patterns and failure modes. - Why it matters: AI coding agents are deployed at scale, yet existing benchmarks evaluate agents on curated, one-shot tasks with complete instructions — a setting that diverges sharply from the iterative, multi-turn reality of real development; without real-world usage data, it is impossible to know whether benchmark progress translates to practical value. - How it does it: By instrumenting the Entire.io CLI to install git hooks that record session transcripts on a shadow branch and link them to commits, then replaying each file-modifying tool call in chronological order via Python difflib to reconstruct line-level agent provenance and measure code survival into commits. - The good: Vibe-coded commits introduce Semgrep-detected security vulnerabilities at 0.76 per 1,000 committed lines — 9× the human-only rate (0.08) and 5× the collaborative rate (0.14) — a striking, directly actionable empirical finding that synthetic benchmarks cannot surface. - The missing: Roughly 85% of sessions come from Claude Code users who opted in via Entire.io's own repository ecosystem, introducing agent-specific and self-selection confounds that the paper acknowledges but cannot correct for; sessions where users abandoned agent output entirely (no commit) are systematically absent, inflating reported success rates. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building coding agent benchmarks or studying human-AI collaboration in software engineering; the dataset and security findings alone justify attention, despite the opt-in sampling limitation. Problem & Motivation Developers increasingly delegate code authorship to AI coding agents, but the research community lacks empirical evidence on how real users actually interact with these systems. Existing benchmarks (SWE-bench and variants) evaluate agents on isolated GitHub issues with complete, expert-written instructions — a setting that rewards one-shot patch generation and ignores iterative correction, partial acceptance, and code quality degradation. No prior public dataset simultaneously captured human prompts, full agent tool-call trajectories, code diffs, and line-level human vs. agent code attribution from authentic sessions. The gap means benchmark leaderboard progress cannot be reliably interpreted as real-world improvement, and claims about agent autonomy or safety cannot be grounded in evidence. Key Idea The core insight is that real-world agent utility can only be measured by coupling session transcripts to git outcomes. The paper operationalizes this by instrumenting Entire.io's CLI to replay sequential file-modifying tool calls in chronological order, tagging each line as "base" or "agent," and computing what fraction of agent-written lines survive (unchanged), are self-overwritten (agent rewrites), are overwritten by the human, or are simply deleted. This "code survival rate" is the operational counterpart to pass-rate benchmarks and is the technical move that makes the paper's efficiency and safety analyses possible. Methodology The data collection pipeline uses the Entire.io CLI, which installs git hooks on developer machines, records session transcripts (user prompts, agent responses, tool calls, token usage) to a dedicated branch (`entire/checkpoints/v1`), and links each checkpoint to a commit. As of April 2026, five coding agents are captured, with Claude Code comprising ~85% of sessions. From raw logs, the pipeline extracts structured turns and per-turn token usage. Code attribution is computed post-hoc: for every committed file, the system replays each file-modifying tool call (write, edit) in chronological order, computing line-level diffs via Python's `difflib.SequenceMatcher` after each call, propagating provenance tags (base vs. agent) forward. Three derived rates are computed: coding efficiency (agent lines survived / agent cumulative lines produced), code survival rate (agent lines survived / agent lines in final state), and five cost-per-committed-line metrics (tokens, dollars, prompt characters, session time, agent runtime). Security is assessed by running Semgrep with `--config=auto` on pre- and post-commit repository snapshots per commit, counting new findings in modified files. Four annotation tasks (session success 0–100, user persona, prompt intent, user pushback) are applied at scale via validated LLM judges, each selected by evaluating 9–11 models against 100 human gold labels with inter-annotator agreement first established (Cohen's κ 0.66–0.83 for categorical tasks; ICC(2,1) = 0.50 for continuous session success). Critical design decisions: (1) opt-in via public repos avoids privacy issues but introduces self-selection bias; (2) LLM judges enable scalable continuous annotation but are acknowledged as imperfect; (3) efficiency analyses restrict to the 48.6% of sessions with unambiguous commit-session attribution. Key Results The dataset contains 6,000 sessions across 200+ repositories with 2.7M logged events. Coding mode distribution: human-only 22.7%, collaborative 36.5%, vibe coding (agent authors >99% of committed code) 40.8% — with vibe coding's share doubling from 20% to >40% over the three-month observation window. Code survival: overall 44.3% of agent-produced code survives into user commits; 59% in vibe coding, 44% in collaborative. Cost: vibe coding consumes a median of 204K tokens and $0.13 per 100 committed lines, vs. $0.05 for collaborative (3× more tokens, 2.6× more cost). Security: vibe-coded commits introduce 0.76 vulnerabilities/1K lines vs. 0.08 human-only (9×) and 0.14 collaborative (5×), with vulnerability types spanning path traversal, command injection (CWE-78), SQL injection (CWE-89), and unsafe format strings (CWE-134); a single JS path traversal rule accounts for the plurality of findings. User behavior: users push back in 39% of turns (corrections 32%, failure reports 7%, rejections 1%); agents proactively ask for clarification in only 1.1–2.6% of turns. Session success: mean and median both 82 (0–100 LLM-scored); 90% of sessions score ≥50. The most common user intent is understanding existing code (19%), not code creation (13.4%). These results are from a preprint (arXiv), not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The dataset is genuinely the first to combine authentic human prompts, full tool-call trajectories, code diffs, and line-level authorship attribution at scale. The empirical findings — code survival rates, 9× vulnerability rate for vibe coding, bimodal coding mode distribution, 39% pushback rate despite agent autonomy growth — are new quantitative facts about real-world agent behavior not previously measurable. The code-survival-via-trajectory-replay methodology is a practical contribution, though it uses standard diff algorithms. Rigor & Reproducibility Annotation validation is methodologically sound: codebooks developed iteratively, IAA measured on 90 independent labels before LLM annotation, LLM judges evaluated against 100 human gold labels. Session success ICC(2,1) between two human annotators = 0.503 — only moderate agreement — and the selected LLM achieves ICC(3,1) = 0.614 vs. human gold, meaning session-level success labels carry meaningful but not strong signal. The authors explicitly caution against treating LLM labels as definitive. The 48.6% loss of sessions in efficiency analyses (ambiguous commit attribution) reduces representativeness. Semgrep is a static analyzer measuring syntactic pattern matches, not confirmed exploitability; the paper does not claim otherwise but the distinction matters for interpreting the 9× figure. Dataset, website, and code links are provided; the dataset is described as living and continuously updated. No training/test split is defined, as this is an observational study, not a predictive model. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Data collection requires only installing the Entire.io CLI; analysis uses Python difflib, Semgrep, and standard LLM APIs — all commercially available. - Efficient: Incremental ingestion and annotation scale with dataset growth; the Semgrep and diff replay steps add modest overhead per commit. - Effective: The dataset demonstrably surfaces behavioral patterns invisible in controlled evaluations — code survival, pushback rates, vulnerability rates by coding mode — and findings are grounded in actual git commits, not proxy tasks. - Scalable: The pipeline is designed as a living system with automated GitHub Code Search discovery; the 42.6% noise rate in topic clustering and 48.6% clean attribution coverage suggest that analytic quality may not scale linearly with raw session count without additional engineering. The work delivers a public dataset and analysis code; it does not deliver a trained model or benchmark leaderboard. Strengths - The coupling of session transcripts to git-level code attribution is the methodological crux: measuring code survival rather than task pass rate captures the true operational value of agent output, and no prior dataset had this. - The 9× higher vulnerability introduction rate for vibe coding vs. human-only, measured on real commits via Semgrep, is a non-obvious, concrete finding with direct policy implications for secure software development practices. - The bimodal coding mode distribution — most sessions are either fully human or fully agent, with relatively few collaborative — is a structurally important observation that challenges assumptions about smooth human-AI collaboration, and the doubling of vibe coding share over three months provides longitudinal grounding. - The annotation validation framework (IAA first, then LLM judge selection against human gold, then transparent reporting of accuracy limits) is more rigorous than most LLM-as-judge setups in the literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront limitations directly and at length (Appendix A). Key ones: self-selection to early adopters of a public tool on public repos; survivorship bias (sessions without commits are never captured, inflating success and efficiency estimates); line-level attribution misses semantic survival where users rewrite agent suggestions in a different style; efficiency metrics exclude the user's cognitive effort outside sessions; LLM annotations are unreliable and should not be used at face value; a declining but still significant fraction of data originates from Entire.io's own repository. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 85% Claude Code dominance is a structural confound: behaviors measured (e.g., AskUserQuestion frequency, ExitPlanMode interruptions) are partly Claude Code-specific capabilities, not properties of coding agents generally; the paper normalizes some analyses to Claude Code data but not all, and does not systematically compare across agents. - The Semgrep vulnerability analysis is dominated by a single rule (JavaScript path join without sanitization), which likely reflects the JS-heavy composition of opted-in repos rather than a universal security signal; the paper does not control for language distribution across coding modes, leaving open whether the 9× figure is an artifact of language mix rather than coding mode per se. - No causal identification: collaborative coding appearing most cost-efficient could reflect user self-selection (expert developers choose collaborative mode for well-defined tasks) rather than a causal benefit of the mode itself; the paper correctly avoids causal language but the implication is present in the discussion. - The IAA for session success (ICC = 0.503) is only moderate, meaning the LLM-annotated success scores — used to characterize failure modes — rest on a shaky measurement foundation; analyses derived from these scores should be treated as exploratory. - The 42.6% of prompts classified as noise in topic clustering and the 48.6% coverage of efficiency analyses mean that many key quantitative findings are based on non-random subsets whose representativeness is unverified. Bottom Line A practitioner designing coding agent benchmarks, studying human-AI collaboration, or setting security policy for AI-assisted development gains substantially from reading this paper — the dataset fills a genuine empirical void, and the finding that vibe coding introduces security vulnerabilities at 9× the human rate is directly actionable despite the opt-in sampling constraints.
Recommended 2604.20779 PDF
#17 — Highly Innovative (75)

VLAA-GUI: Knowing When to Stop, Recover, and Search, A Modular Framework for GUI Automation (10 ▲ )

Qijun Han, Haoqin Tu, Zijun Wang, Haoyue Dai, Yiyang Zhou, Nancy Lau, Alvaro A. Cardenas, Yuhui Xu, Ran Xu, Caiming Xiong, Zeyu Zheng, Huaxiu Yao, Yuyin Zhou, Cihang Xie · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (80) drives the score — released GitHub artifact and concrete framework components (Completeness Verifier, Loop Breaker) that practitioners can adopt. Novelty (70) and Rigor (70) are good but bounded: the >86% false-completion finding is genuinely new, but the 0.4-point margin over HIPPO with no significance testing and no cost/latency analysis weaken claims. Non-Obviousness (72) reflects the principled three-tier escalation design. Completeness (78) and Resilience (72) benefit from honest limitation acknowledgment, though the verifier provably does not solve its target problem (FDF still >86%). Weighted: 0.15*70+0.15*70+0.10*72+0.15*78+0.15*72+0.30*80 ≈ 75.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 78 Resilience 72 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VLAA-GUI, a modular GUI agent framework with a mandatory Completeness Verifier, a three-tier Loop Breaker, and an on-demand Search Agent that achieves 77.5% on OSWorld-Verified and 61.0% on WindowsAgentArena — the first framework to surpass human performance on OSWorld in a single pass. - Why it's important: Over 86% of GUI agent failures across all tested backbones involve false completion (the agent believes it succeeded when it has not), and repetitive action loops waste substantial steps; these are the dominant reliability barriers blocking practical deployment of autonomous desktop agents. - How it does it: The framework wraps any MLLM backbone with mandatory post-action meta-checks: a two-level evidence-gated termination protocol (self-check gate + independent MLLM judge requiring direct visual proof) and a three-tier loop escalation mechanism (modality switch → strategy switch → reflection-judge override), plus on-demand LLM-native search via Gemini 3 Pro. - The good: Three of five tested backbones surpass human-level performance at 100 steps, and VLAA-GUI with Sonnet 4.6 at only 15 steps already outperforms the best published 50-step system (OS-Symphony at 63.6%) — demonstrating that the framework recovers steps rather than just spending more of them. - The missing: No API cost or latency analysis is provided despite the system invoking at minimum 2–3 LLM API calls per step, and the margin over the closest published competitor (HIPPO w/ Opus 4.5 at 74.49% vs. VLAA-GUI w/ Opus 4.5 at 74.89%) is 0.4% — too narrow to assess without significance testing. - Read it or not: Practitioners building desktop GUI automation pipelines should read it for the Completeness Verifier and Loop Breaker designs; researchers already well-versed in Agent S3 or OS-Symphony will find the contribution incremental but concretely executed. Problem & Motivation: Autonomous GUI agents operating on real desktop tasks fail in two systematic, backbone-independent ways. First, agents routinely declare success prematurely — opening a Save dialog without writing the file, toggling a setting without verifying the state changed — because termination is left to the model's implicit judgment rather than checked against observable UI evidence. The paper quantifies this as false completion, showing that >86% of all agent failures across both Sonnet 4.6 and Gemini 3 Flash involve the agent incorrectly believing it has succeeded. Second, agents cycle through the same failing actions without recovery; prior anti-looping heuristics operate at a single granularity (e.g., reflection or simple counters) and cannot escalate across interaction modalities or planning strategies. Both failure modes persist even in frontier models, establishing that the bottleneck is framework design, not raw model capability. Key Idea: The core technical insight is that early stopping and loop failures can be addressed orthogonally and structurally by imposing mandatory post-action meta-checks that are independent of the Manager Agent's own reasoning. The Completeness Verifier forces the agent to first operationalize success criteria as UI-observable predicates (button labels, file presence, toggle state) and then passes every `done()` signal through an independent MLLM judge that requires direct, unambiguous visual evidence and prefers false negatives over false positives. The Loop Breaker escalates recovery across three tiers — repeated no-change actions trigger a modality switch, repeated screen states trigger a strategy change, and an external Reflection Agent signals a hard override — so that local failures escalate to global strategy changes rather than stalling indefinitely. Methodology: VLAA-GUI centers on a Manager Agent in a perceive–reason–act loop augmented by two mandatory and three on-demand tool agents. At each step t, the Manager receives belief state b_t (instruction, trajectory history, current screenshot) and outputs a UI action (click, type, scroll, hotkey via pyautogui) or delegates to a specialist. Mandatory post-action checks: (1) Completeness Verifier — at task start, a Completion Gate derives K UI-observable success criteria; at every step, the Manager self-checks all K criteria before any action; when the gate outputs done, an independent MLLM verifier judge (same backbone model, temperature T=0.2) cross-examines the screenshot and trajectory and accepts only if every criterion has direct visual evidence. Rejection reasons are injected into trajectory context. (2) Loop Breaker — tracks n_t^a (action-level repetition: same action + no visible change) and n_t^o (screen-state repetition: same screen 3× in a window). Tier 1 (n_t^a ≥ τ_a): force modality switch (e.g., keyboard → menu → CLI). Tier 2 (n_t^o ≥ τ_o): force strategy change. Tier 3: Reflection Agent inspects the trajectory and signals KEEP/SWITCH; a SWITCH injects a hard directive blacklisting repeated actions and forcing the Manager to choose from remaining modalities. On-demand tools: Search Agent issues a single structured query to Gemini 3 Pro (with native search grounding) and injects plain-text tutorial results into the Manager's context; Coding Agent runs Python/Bash in an independent execution loop with a 20-step budget; Grounding Agent (Seed 1.8) translates natural-language element descriptions to screen coordinates. Three critical design decisions: (a) mandatory (not optional) invocation of verifier and loop breaker, justified by the >86% false completion finding; (b) two-level verification (gate + independent judge) to reduce agreement bias in MLLM verifiers; (c) LLM-native search over browser-based search, avoiding visual grounding overhead. Evaluation: OSWorld-Verified (361 tasks, Ubuntu, 5 domains), WindowsAgentArena (154 tasks, Windows), with step budgets of 15/50/100. Five backbones tested: Claude Opus 4.6, Opus 4.5, Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Gemini 3 Flash. Experiments run on AWS with official Docker/VM environments. τ_a and τ_o threshold values are not stated explicitly in the paper. Key Results: On OSWorld-Verified at 100 steps: VLAA-GUI w/ Opus 4.6 achieves 77.45%, Opus 4.5 74.89%, Gemini 3.1 Pro 72.47%, Sonnet 4.6 71.67%, Gemini 3 Flash 68.77%; human baseline is 72.4%. Prior best published result is HIPPO w/ Opus 4.5 at 74.49%; Agent S3 w/ Opus 4.5 was 67.46%; OS-Symphony w/ GPT-5 was 65.84%. On WindowsAgentArena at 100 steps: VLAA-GUI w/ Gemini 3 Flash reaches 61.0% vs. Agent S3 w/ GPT-5 at 56.6%. At 15-step budget, Opus 4.6 (64.75%) and Sonnet 4.6 (64.13%) both surpass the best prior 50-step result. Ablation (Sonnet 4.6 at 100 steps): removing Completeness Verifier costs −3.1%, removing Search Agent costs −1.9%, removing Loop Breaker costs −0.04% (Loop Breaker impact shifts to tighter budgets: −1.4% at 50 steps). Ablation (Gemini 3 Flash at 50 steps): removing Loop Breaker costs −4.2%, Search Agent −3.0%. The Loop Breaker reduces wasted steps from 4.9% to 2.8% for Gemini 3 Flash and 3.2% to 2.1% for Sonnet 4.6. The Completeness Verifier reduces false-done/all by 3.9% for Sonnet 4.6 and only 0.5% for Gemini 3 Flash at 100 steps — yet FDF (false-done/failed) remains above 86% for both backbones even with the verifier. Baselines are current and include multiple frontier model systems. Results appear to be single-run evaluations with no error bars or statistical tests reported. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The specific integration of mandatory evidence-gated termination checking + three-tier escalating loop recovery + LLM-native search as a post-action overlay on any MLLM backbone is the novel system contribution. The quantification that >86% of GUI agent failures are false completions across multiple frontier backbones is a genuinely new empirical finding that reframes the community's understanding of the dominant failure mode. Individual components — self-verification, loop detection, search augmentation — have precedents in Reflexion, ReAct, and OS-Symphony; the novelty lies in their mandatory, escalating integration rather than optional composition. Rigor & Reproducibility: Component-wise ablations are present across two backbones, three step budgets, and two benchmarks, clearly isolating each module's contribution. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed runs are reported, making narrow margins (e.g., +0.4% over HIPPO w/ Opus 4.5) uninterpretable statistically. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/UCSC-VLAA/VLAA-GUI. Full reproducibility is conditional on API access to multiple closed commercial systems (Claude Opus/Sonnet, Gemini 3 Pro with native search grounding, Seed 1.8 for pixel-space grounding) — no open-source-only configuration is evaluated. The primary benchmark (OSWorld) is third-party and externally developed, which mitigates Goodhart's Law concerns. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.21375v1, April 23, 2026) and has not been peer-reviewed. The threshold parameters τ_a and τ_o for the Loop Breaker tiers are defined symbolically but never given concrete values in the paper body or appendix. Deployability (SYSTEMS): - Practical: Runs entirely via commercial API calls from AWS instances with standard Docker environments; no specialized hardware required, but requires simultaneous access to Claude, Gemini (with search grounding), and Seed 1.8 APIs. - Efficient: Every step invokes at minimum 2 mandatory LLM API calls (Manager + Loop Breaker Reflection Agent) plus the Completeness Verifier on any termination claim — roughly 2–4× the per-step API cost of a vanilla single-agent setup; no latency or dollar-cost numbers are reported anywhere in the paper. - Effective: The system demonstrably surpasses human-level performance on OSWorld (77.45% vs. 72.4%) and outperforms all published baselines on both benchmarks; the case study illustrates concrete trajectory-level recovery that a baseline agent would fail. - Scalable: Tested on up to 361 tasks and 100 steps per task; no evaluation of longer-horizon tasks, continuous multi-session agents, or task counts beyond the benchmark sizes; scalability claims are not tested, only the existing benchmark footprint. The system is released as a software artifact (GitHub repository with code and prompt templates). Strengths: - The quantification that >86% of GUI agent failures across frontier backbones are false completions is a concrete, surprising empirical finding that validates the Completeness Verifier as a necessary (not merely helpful) component. - The three-tier Loop Breaker with escalating granularity (local modality switch → global strategy change → external judge override) is mechanistically more principled than prior single-level loop heuristics, and the complementarity of tiers (Tier 1 handles local failures, Tier 2 handles navigation dead-ends, Tier 3 catches patterns the Manager's local checks miss) is clearly justified. - Evaluating five diverse backbones across two model families demonstrates that the framework's benefits generalize across model capability tiers rather than being tuned to a specific backbone. - The honest reporting that tool overhead hurts weaker models under tight budgets (Loop Breaker costs Gemini 3 Flash −6.15% at 15 steps) is a practically important finding that the paper does not obscure. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state that VLAA-GUI lacks long-horizon task decomposition and cross-task knowledge transfer. They explicitly note that hierarchical planners performed poorly in their framework and were omitted, and that memory modules were removed for simplicity. They acknowledge that false completion remains the dominant failure mode even with the verifier, and propose using the framework's verified trajectories to train end-to-end models as future work. Limitations are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The margin over HIPPO w/ Opus 4.5 (74.49% → 74.89%, a gap of 0.4%) is well within plausible run-to-run variance given that no significance tests are reported; the claim of superiority over this specific competitor is unsubstantiated. The Completeness Verifier's absolute impact on the core false-completion problem is modest — FDF remains above 86% and FDA improves by only 3.9% (Sonnet) and 0.5% (Gemini 3 Flash) at 100 steps — meaning the verifier prevents early termination at the margins but does not solve the fundamental problem it was designed to address. The specific threshold values τ_a and τ_o governing when the Loop Breaker tiers activate are never disclosed, preventing independent reimplementation. The Search Agent relies on Gemini 3 Pro's proprietary native search grounding, a capability unavailable in open or self-hosted deployments, creating a non-portable dependency. No API cost or latency analysis is provided despite the system multiplying per-step inference costs by 2–4×, which is a critical deployment consideration the paper silently omits. Bottom Line: Practitioners building desktop GUI automation systems gain immediately applicable framework components — particularly the mandatory evidence-gated Completeness Verifier and tiered Loop Breaker — but the persistent >86% false-completion rate even with verification, the narrow margin over HIPPO, and the multi-API commercial dependency mean this is careful incremental engineering rather than a definitive solution to GUI agent reliability.
Recommended 2604.21375 PDF
#18 — Highly Innovative (75)

Where does output diversity collapse in post-training? (10 ▲ )

Constantinos Karouzos, Xingwei Tan, Nikolaos Aletras · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 20 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (82) and Rigor (80) drive the score — the parallel-lineage design (Think vs Instruct sharing identical SFT→DPO→RL recipe but differing only in data) gives a cleaner causal structure than any prior diversity-collapse analysis, and the CoT-suppression falsification experiment definitively rules out the format-imposed hypothesis (18–32% accuracy drop with zero diversity recovery). Four-metric measurement, contamination flagging, temperature-sensitivity appendix, and public code further strengthen rigor. Novelty (70) is a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING with sharper apparatus rather than a new method. Completeness (75) and Resilience (75) are solid; the main caveat is purely observational within existing checkpoints — no direct data-composition intervention (e.g., 2 vs 8 teachers at fixed volume) — and 7B-only scope. Impact (65) reflects code+analysis, not a deployable artifact. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*65 ≈ 75.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 75 Resilience 75 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates, through a controlled comparison of three parallel post-training lineages of the Olmo 3 7B model, that output diversity collapse is determined by training data composition—not post-training algorithm—and cannot be recovered at inference time. - Why it's important: Inference-time scaling methods (pass@k, majority voting, self-consistency) depend on output diversity; if post-training eliminates that diversity, the scaling benefits are lost, and value-laden tasks risk homogenized outputs. - How it does it: The paper exploits Olmo 3's public checkpoints across three lineages—Think (narrow two-teacher CoT distillation), Instruct (broad multi-source), and RL-Zero (RL directly from base)—that share the same SFT→DPO→RL recipe but differ in data, then measures diversity at each checkpoint across 15 tasks with four metrics, and surgically removes CoT at inference to separate format from weights. - The good: The CoT suppression experiment (Think-not-thinking) is definitive: removing chain-of-thought format costs 18–32% accuracy on hard tasks but leaves SBERT diversity unchanged at every stage, proving the collapse is weight-embedded, not format-imposed. - The missing: The study is purely observational within existing checkpoints; no direct data composition intervention is run, so the causal claim that "more teachers → less collapse" is supported by cross-lineage comparison rather than a controlled experiment. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on LLM post-training, inference-time compute scaling, or alignment diversity; it provides concrete numbers and a clean experimental argument that inference-side fixes for diversity collapse are insufficient. Problem & Motivation: Post-trained LLMs produce less varied outputs than their base counterparts, and this homogenization undermines inference-time scaling methods—pass@k, majority voting, and self-consistency—that require diverse samples to aggregate over. The problem extends to creative and value-laden tasks where output homogenization risks imposing a single perspective. Prior work attributed collapse to specific algorithms (DPO in narrative generation, SFT in reasoning, reward in creative tasks) without disentangling whether the algorithm or the training data caused the drop, and without testing whether the chain-of-thought generation format itself constrains diversity at inference. Key Idea: When two models share identical post-training algorithms (SFT→DPO→RL) but differ only in data—narrow two-teacher CoT distillation versus broad multi-source instruction tuning—diversity collapses at different stages and by different magnitudes, proving that data composition, not algorithm, determines when and how sharply collapse occurs. The secondary insight is that suppressing CoT at inference on a collapsed model does not restore diversity, demonstrating the collapse is baked into learned weights rather than imposed by generation format. Methodology: The study evaluates 13 Olmo 3 7B checkpoints across three post-training lineages. Think uses SFT on ∼2.3M synthetic CoT traces from two teachers (QwQ-32B and DeepSeek-R1), followed by DPO on ∼200K Delta Learning pairs and GRPO-based RL on ∼105K prompts. Instruct initializes from Think-SFT, then trains on ∼2.2M multi-source examples (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4.1) that strip reasoning traces, uses the same prompt pool for DPO but with GPT-judged pairs, and the same RL stage. RL-Zero applies RL directly to Base, bypassing SFT and DPO, in four domain-specific variants. Four complementary metrics are measured per checkpoint on 15 tasks across six categories: EAD (lexical, unique n-grams normalized for length bias), SBERT (semantic, mean pairwise cosine distance with all-mpnet-base-v2), NLI (logical, pairwise entailment/contradiction scoring with roberta-large-mnli), and Vendi Score (effective number of distinct semantic modes). K=16 outputs per prompt at T=0.6 are generated; for Think-lineage models, reasoning traces are stripped before computing metrics.
