A Think-not-thinking condition prefills an empty `<think>\n</think>` block to force direct answers, isolating format from weights. Quality-filtered diversity decomposes aggregate Da (SBERT on all outputs) into Dc (SBERT on correct-only subset) and Vc (Vendi Score on correct outputs) for six verifiable tasks. Three critical design decisions: (1) using matched temperature T=0.6 for all models including Base, validated in Appendix H to introduce only ∼11% SBERT reduction versus the 62% collapse observed at SFT—an order-of-magnitude smaller effect; (2) stripping CoT traces before any metric computation so diversity reflects final answers only; (3) using C13 13-gram matching to flag six benchmarks with 7–30% training overlap, though results on contaminated and clean tasks are consistent. Key Results: Think-SFT loses 62% of Base SBERT diversity on average across 15 tasks, versus 38% for Instruct-SFT. Despite initializing from the already-collapsed Think-SFT, Instruct-SFT recovers a median 40% of lost diversity due to its multi-source data. DPO removes 23% further diversity in Instruct versus only 4% in Think (which has little remaining to remove). RL-Zero retains ≥71% of Base diversity with a median of 94%. Both supervised lineages converge to similar final floors: Think retains 38% and Instruct 34% of Base diversity. The Vendi Score drops from ∼3.4 effective semantic modes (Base) to ∼1.8 at final checkpoints, with near-total collapse on math (GSM8K: 1.3 modes, MATH-Algebra: 1.4). EAD (lexical diversity) remains stable or increases even as SBERT drops, a dissociation replicated across all tasks—aligned models use varied vocabulary to express semantically identical content. Value-pluralism tasks show the steepest Think collapse (PRISM −78%, TruthfulQA −79%). The CoT suppression experiment finds zero diversity recovery on any task-stage combination: Think-not-thinking SBERT tracks Think within noise at every stage, while accuracy drops substantially (IFEval −8%, GSM8K −18%, HumanEval −32%, MATH-Geometry −32%). Quality decomposition reveals a task-dependent split: on IFEval, 83.4% of the diversity drop persists in Dc (genuine narrowing among correct outputs); on HumanEval, less than 10% is genuine (most collapse reflects elimination of wrong outputs). Think gains only +0.4 percentage points from majority voting on GSM8K (16 near-identical correct answers), while Base gains +24% and RL-Zero +22–26%. On TruthfulQA, majority voting hurts all models (majority vote@16 < accuracy@1) because collapse amplifies the dominant misconception. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The contribution is a controlled dissociation of post-training algorithm effects from data composition effects on output diversity, plus the CoT-suppression falsification test. The experimental apparatus (multi-lineage comparison with identical recipes but different data, plus out-of-distribution inference interventions) is methodologically sharper than prior work, which confounded method and data. The quality-filtered decomposition framework is a useful analytical tool but draws on established ideas. The finding that both supervised lineages converge to the same diversity floor regardless of training trajectory is counter-intuitive and practically significant. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are not applicable in the traditional sense—this is an observational study across existing checkpoints rather than a system with tunable components. The four-metric setup (EAD, SBERT, NLI, Vendi Score) provides complementary views, and the finding that EAD diverges from SBERT is itself a meaningful result. Training-evaluation contamination is measured and flagged; results are consistent across clean and contaminated benchmarks. Temperature sensitivity is assessed in a dedicated appendix and shown to be small relative to the observed effects. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/ckarouzos/where-diversity-collapses/. The LLM-as-judge evaluation (gpt-4.1-mini via OpenAI Batch API) for non-verifiable tasks introduces vendor dependence and known limitations for creative/value-laden tasks, which the authors acknowledge. No error bars or significance tests are reported on the main SBERT diversity comparisons; the study relies on consistent directional patterns across 15 tasks as evidence of reliability. This is a preprint under review. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All experiments use vLLM and lighteval on standard HPC hardware; no proprietary infrastructure required beyond API access to gpt-4.1-mini for judge evaluation. - Efficient: The evaluation requires 13 models × 15 tasks × 500 prompts × 16 samples = ∼1.56M forward passes, plus embedding and NLI inference; substantial but within reach of academic compute with HPC access (acknowledged in the paper). - Effective: The evidence convincingly demonstrates that collapse is weight-embedded and data-driven for the specific Olmo 3 7B family; the practical implication—avoid narrow-teacher SFT—is well-supported within this scope. - Scalable: Tested exclusively at 7B; whether the same stage-attribution pattern holds at 70B+ is not examined and remains an open question. The convergence to a similar diversity floor across lineages might differ at scale. The work delivers a code artifact and data analysis rather than a deployable system or trained model. Strengths: - The parallel-lineage design is the strongest feature: Think and Instruct share identical algorithms and differ only in data, providing a cleaner causal structure for attribution than any prior single-lineage analysis. - The CoT-suppression experiment directly falsifies the hypothesis that generation format drives diversity collapse, resolving a confound present in all prior Think-vs-Instruct comparisons. - The quality-filtered decomposition (Da vs. Dc vs. Vc) is a concrete methodological contribution that resolves a genuine debate in the field about whether diversity collapse is "just quality control" or genuine homogenization—and shows it is task-dependent. - Breath of evaluation across 15 tasks spanning six qualitatively different categories produces a coherent picture of task-dependent diversity-quality tradeoffs. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note they measure final-answer diversity rather than reasoning-path diversity, which may underestimate collapse in the Think lineage where CoT traces are stripped. They distinguish distributional diversity (measured here) from representational diversity (which perspectives are preserved), noting that a model could appear collapsed distributionally while retaining key stances, or vice versa. LLM-judge evaluation of creative and value-laden tasks has known limitations, and those results are presented as supplementary context rather than primary evidence. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The study is observational within existing checkpoints; no direct data composition intervention (e.g., training on 4 vs. 2 vs. 10 teachers with fixed data volume) is run, so the causal mechanism connecting teacher breadth to collapse magnitude is inferred rather than established. The two teachers used for Think (QwQ-32B, DeepSeek-R1) come from the same training lineage and likely occupy a narrow region of output space; the paper does not characterize the diversity of teacher outputs themselves, which would strengthen the mechanistic interpretation. The study covers only 7B-scale models from a single family (Olmo 3); the convergence-to-same-floor finding could be specific to this architecture, pretraining mix, or scale. The RL-Zero diversity advantage (median 94% of Base retained) comes at a massive accuracy penalty (49.8–61% on GSM8K vs. 93% Think), and this tradeoff is described but not deeply analyzed—it is unclear whether entropy-regularized RL could preserve some RL-Zero diversity without the quality collapse. The paper does not examine whether simple interventions like increasing the number of SFT teachers from 2 to 8 would shift the diversity floor or just the trajectory. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on inference-time compute scaling, preference optimization, or model alignment will extract concrete and actionable findings here: diversity collapse is a data problem, not an algorithm problem, and you cannot fix it at inference time—read it.
Recommended 2604.16027 PDF
#19 — Highly Innovative (75)

OpenMobile: Building Open Mobile Agents with Task and Trajectory Synthesis (22 ▲ )

Kanzhi Cheng, Zehao Li, Zheng Ma, Nuo Chen, Jialin Cao, Qiushi Sun, Zichen Ding, Fangzhi Xu, Hang Yan, Jiajun Chen, Anh Tuan Luu, Jianbing Zhang, Lewei Lu, Dahua Lin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical/systems profile, scoring as empirical. Rigor (80) strong — three benchmarks, three runs with mean±half-range, unusually thorough contamination analysis with similarity histograms and ablation. Novelty (70) — decoupled global memory + error-intervention switching. Completeness (80) — honest acknowledgment of RL not improving SFT, Gemini dependency. Resilience (75). Impact (80) — full open release of code, data, model weights addresses an actual community gap. Composite ≈ 0.20*70+0.25*80+0.15*70+0.15*80+0.15*75+0.10*80 = 75.75.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 80 Resilience 75 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OpenMobile, an open-source framework for synthesizing mobile agent training data via decoupled global-memory-based task instruction generation and policy-switching trajectory rollout with error-intervention, producing a dataset of 2.8K instructions and 34K action steps across 20 Android apps. - Why it's important: Leading proprietary mobile agent systems achieve ~70% task success on AndroidWorld by training on large-scale undisclosed trajectory data, while open-source approaches plateau at ~30%, and the opacity prevents the research community from understanding what data properties drive the gap. - How it does it: It decouples environment exploration from instruction synthesis by first building a global environment memory (unique screens, neighborhood transitions, VLM-extracted functionality descriptions, semantic retrieval index), then draws on short-term and long-term memory to compose complex multi-step instructions; for trajectories, it alternates a learner and expert policy via a monitor that detects learner deviation and triggers targeted expert correction. - The good: Fine-tuning Qwen3-VL-8B on OpenMobile data reaches 64.7% Pass@1 on AndroidWorld—more than doubling open-data baselines (~27–34%)—while the transparent overlap analysis with quantitative similarity histograms and ablation over removed similar instructions credibly rules out benchmark overfitting as the primary explanation. - The missing: All data is collected within the AndroidWorld emulator environment using only 20 apps, so the synthesis pipeline's ability to generalize to heterogeneous real-device app ecosystems (broader app stores, OEM UIs, non-English locales) is untested; the monitor-triggered switching is triggered at most twice per rollout, leaving unclear whether this cap is principled or a practical shortcut. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners building or studying mobile/GUI agents should read it—it provides the first credible open recipe for competitive-quality agent training data synthesis with honest contamination analysis; those focused on web agents or desktop GUIs can skim the methodology sections for transferable ideas. Problem & Motivation: Autonomous mobile agents operating on Android devices depend on large-scale, high-quality training data consisting of paired task instructions and execution trajectories. Leading proprietary systems (Step-GUI, MAI-UI, UI-Venus-1.5, MobileAgent-v3.5) achieve up to 70% task success on AndroidWorld by training on undisclosed internal datasets, while the open-source community—relying on human-annotated sets like AndroidControl and AMEX—plateaus at ~30%. Beyond the performance gap, opacity about data synthesis recipes prevents the community from studying what data properties matter or from reproducing results. Existing open interaction-driven synthesis approaches (e.g., OS-Genesis) tightly couple exploration with instruction generation, deriving each instruction from a single local trajectory and thus bounding diversity to what that trajectory reveals; they also collect trajectories via pure expert distillation, which produces ideal behavior but fails to expose agents to the error-recovery scenarios they will inevitably face at test time. Key Idea: The central technical insight is a two-stage decoupling: first explore the environment exhaustively to build a global structured memory of the app's functionality landscape (unique screens, navigation transitions, VLM-annotated UI element semantics, semantic retrieval index), then synthesize instructions by combining short-term memory (locally reachable screens) with long-term memory (semantically related but navigationally distant functionalities retrieved from the index), enabling cross-feature composition that no single exploration trajectory would reveal. For trajectory collection, the complementary insight is that a monitor watching the learner in real time can identify genuine deviations from productive progress—as opposed to random policy disagreements—and trigger precise expert corrections exactly at failure points, injecting error-recovery demonstrations without disrupting task coherence. Methodology: The pipeline has two main components. (1) Scalable Task Synthesis: The environment is explored via random walk (10 steps per session), and all resulting screen-action transitions are aggregated. Screens are deduplicated using perceptual hashing (pHash similarity threshold τ=0.95), yielding a set of N unique screens. For each unique screen, Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview extracts a functionality set F(si) of natural-language UI element descriptions. A per-app semantic retrieval index is built from embeddings (openai/text-embedding-3-large) over all functionality descriptions with greedy diversity filtering (pairwise cosine similarity < 0.8). At synthesis time, for each candidate screen, context is assembled from: the focal screen + its functionalities (focal), 1 predecessor + up to 3 successor screens (short-term memory), and 30 semantically related functionalities from distant screens (long-term memory). Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview generates 1–3 multi-step instructions per screen following detailed guidelines (specificity, executability, high-level framing, starting from home screen). Generated instructions undergo LLM-based quality filtering (clarity ≥4, reasonableness ≥4 on a 1–5 scale), embedding-based deduplication (threshold 0.8), and per-app count balancing. (2) Policy-Switching Rollout: Given a learner πl (early-stage fine-tuned checkpoint) and expert πe (Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview), rollout begins with the learner. A monitor (also Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview) inspects the last two screenshots and action history to detect deviations; upon detection (at most twice per trajectory), control switches to the expert, which executes at least 3 correction steps before returning to the learner. Only expert steps are retained for training, but the full interaction history (including learner errors) is preserved as context. Models are fine-tuned using standard SFT via LLaMA-Factory (batch size 32, lr 1e-5, 3 epochs). Three critical design decisions: (a) random walk exploration sufficiency—well-justified by the argument that coverage lies in memory organization, not exploration sophistication; (b) retaining only expert action steps but full error context during training—justified by the goal of teaching recovery from realistic errors, though not ablated independently from the rollout strategy; (c) using Gemini as both expert and monitor—introduces a dependency on a closed model that is not explicitly ablated. Key Results: On AndroidWorld (116 tasks, 20 apps), Qwen2.5-VL-7B improves from 25.5±2.6% (untuned) to 51.7±1.7% Pass@1, and Qwen3-VL-8B improves from 47.6±2.2% to 64.7±3.2%, compared to open-data baselines ScaleCUA-7B (27.2±2.2%) and UI-S1-7B (34.0%). Competitive closed-data systems range from 63.9% (Step-GUI-4B) to 73.7% (UI-Venus-1.5-8B). On AndroidLab (138 tasks, 9 apps), Qwen3-VL reaches 51.5±0.7% vs. 43.5% untuned and 30.0% for ScaleCUA. On MobileWorld (201 tasks, 20 apps, long-horizon cross-app), Qwen2.5-VL improves 7.7%→14.8% and Qwen3-VL improves 9.4%→17.7%. Scaling Qwen2.5-VL-72B on the same data reaches 59.3±0.9%. Ablation on rollout strategies (all at 1.5K trajectories, Qwen2.5-VL-7B): expert distillation 44.8±1.7%, self-evolution 33.8±0.9%, random switching 45.1±0.9%, error-intervention switching 48.3±1.3%, with error-intervention producing 1.56 avg error-recovery instances per trajectory vs. 0.42 for distillation and 0.10 for self-evolution. Task synthesis ablation: OpenMobile (48.3±1.3%) vs. coupled pipeline (45.3±2.2%) vs. OS-Genesis (34.1±1.7%), with human evaluation showing OpenMobile wins on complexity 68% vs. OS-Genesis. Overlap analysis: only 3.5% of synthetic instructions exceed 0.7 cosine similarity to AndroidWorld test instructions; removing the top-similar 10% causes only marginal performance drop. RL (step-level GRPO and trajectory-level agentic RL) does not surpass SFT on dynamic benchmarks. Benchmarks are dynamic and well-regarded; baselines include current strong open-weight and proprietary models, though some closed-data numbers are taken from prior publications rather than re-run. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The global environment memory construction—weaving fragmented exploration trajectories into a unified graph via perceptual hashing and enriching it with VLM-extracted semantics and a semantic retrieval index—is a genuine methodological contribution over prior exploration-coupled approaches. The error-intervention policy-switching strategy is a non-trivial departure from standard expert distillation and self-evolution. The open-source release of both the framework and the synthesized data is a significant community contribution. The underlying components (VLM prompting, semantic retrieval, SFT) are standard; the novelty lies specifically in the decoupled architecture and the monitor-triggered switching mechanism. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for both major components (task synthesis strategy and rollout strategy) and are informative. Each benchmark is run three times with mean and half-range (max−min)/2 reported, and Pass@3 is provided as an upper-bound estimate. The contamination analysis is unusually thorough for this venue: full pairwise similarity distributions, comparison against existing datasets, and an ablation removing varying fractions of the most-similar instructions are all provided. Code, data, and model checkpoints are stated to be released (linked in abstract). The RL experiments are honestly reported as not improving over SFT, with a plausible explanation. One methodological concern: the monitor that detects learner deviations uses the same Gemini model as the expert, and its reliability (false positive/negative rates for deviation detection) is not characterized—the quality of error-recovery demonstrations depends critically on this. The human evaluation of instruction quality covers only 50 pairwise comparisons, which is modest. The 20-app scope is narrow relative to the full AndroidWorld and Android ecosystem. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS (a built data synthesis pipeline evaluated by training agents and measuring task success on live benchmarks). - Practical: Training Qwen2.5-VL-7B or Qwen3-VL-8B via SFT with LLaMA-Factory runs on commercially available GPU clusters; the synthesis pipeline requires API access to Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview, which is a practical dependency but not a blocker for researchers. - Efficient: The dataset is modest (2.8K instructions, 34K steps) relative to proprietary systems (scale not disclosed), and standard SFT for 3 epochs is compute-efficient; however, synthesizing data requires extensive Gemini API calls (functionality extraction per unique screen, instruction generation, quality filtering, monitor during rollout), whose cost is not stated. - Effective: The evidence shows genuine task success improvements on three independent dynamic benchmarks, including MobileWorld which uses different apps and cross-app tasks not seen during synthesis, supporting effectiveness beyond narrow app coverage. - Scalable: Data scaling experiments (Figure 1, middle) show AndroidWorld performance continues improving from 0.5K to 2.8K instructions, suggesting the pipeline is not saturated at current scale, but no experiments beyond 2.8K are shown; scaling to broader app ecosystems, non-English environments, or real devices is not evaluated. The framework delivers a software artifact (synthesis pipeline + code + dataset + fine-tuned model weights). Strengths: - The decoupled global environment memory design is a principled solution to the instruction diversity bottleneck in interaction-driven synthesis, and the improvement over the coupled baseline (45.3% vs. 48.3% at fixed 1.5K trajectories) is quantified with ablations. - The error-intervention switching strategy produces 3.7× more error-recovery instances per trajectory than expert distillation and yields a measurable downstream gain, directly addressing a known weakness of imitation learning. - The contamination analysis is unusually rigorous and transparent: publishing the full 116-row similarity table, characterizing the similarity distribution relative to prior datasets, and running the ablation of removing similar instructions is a credibility-positive contribution that goes beyond what most comparable papers provide. - Multi-benchmark evaluation across three distinct dynamic environments (AndroidWorld, AndroidLab, MobileWorld) with independent app sets and task types provides genuine evidence of generalization rather than benchmark-specific tuning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that (1) RL (both step-level and trajectory-level) does not consistently improve over SFT on dynamic benchmarks, attributing this to limited environment diversity and RL instability, and flag this as future work; (2) the 20-app scope is implicitly constrained by using the AndroidWorld emulator infrastructure (though no benchmark test instructions were incorporated); (3) base model capability remains critical and data quality alone cannot close the gap with stronger foundation models. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The reliability of the monitor (Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview) for deviation detection is uncharacterized—if it fires false positives frequently, the injected expert corrections would appear in non-error contexts, polluting training signal; if it fires false negatives, error recovery is not captured. The cap of at most 2 interventions per trajectory is a practical heuristic whose sensitivity is not ablated. The synthesis pipeline is fully dependent on access to a proprietary closed model (Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview) for functionality extraction, instruction generation, and the monitor—replicability by parties without API access is limited. All training data comes from 20 apps in an Android emulator; whether the memory construction and instruction synthesis pipeline transfers to arbitrary real Android apps (e.g., dynamic content, login-gated apps, rapidly updated UIs) is not tested. The self-evolution baseline is notably weak (33.8%), but the experimental setup (3 rounds of retraining from a single learner checkpoint without curriculum or reward shaping) may underrepresent the state of the art in self-improvement for GUI agents. Pairwise human evaluation is limited to 50 comparisons, which is underpowered for detecting small differences in instruction soundness. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on mobile GUI agents will gain concrete, implementable insight from this paper—it provides the first credibly rigorous and open recipe for bridging the proprietary/open-source data gap, with quantitatively honest analysis and released artifacts, making it worth reading despite its preprint status and the Gemini dependency in the synthesis pipeline.
Recommended 2604.15093 PDF
#20 — Innovative (74)

Efficient Mixture-of-Experts LLM Inference with Apple Silicon NPUs

Afsara Benazir, Felix Xiaozhu Lin · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (85) drives the score — first runtime engine offloading MoE prefill to Apple Neural Engine with 5.55× latency and 7.37× energy reduction on real hardware, working Swift/CoreML system. Novelty (70) — the integrated three-technique co-design (tiered static capacity, grouped expert FFN, load-aware hot/cold residency) is a non-obvious composition for the MoE-on-NPU mismatch. Rigor (70) — full ablation isolating each technique, cross-dataset calibration validation, real Zeus energy measurements, but single-run with no error bars and code only promised. Non-Obviousness (70) — converting dynamic routing to static graphs via offline calibration is a clever reframing. Completeness (75) — decode-phase, scatter/gather bottleneck, and Apple-only scope acknowledged directly. Resilience (70). Weighted: 0.15*70+0.15*70+0.10*70+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.30*85 = 74.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces NPUMoE, a runtime inference engine that offloads Mixture-of-Experts LLM prefill compute to Apple Neural Engine (ANE) by converting dynamic expert routing into static, NPU-compatible compute graphs via three co-designed techniques. - Why it's important: On-device MoE inference on mobile platforms competes with OS, graphics, and interactive workloads for CPU/GPU resources; offloading to the dedicated, energy-efficient NPU frees those resources while dramatically reducing energy cost on battery-powered devices. - How it does it: Offline calibration of per-expert token load drives tiered static capacity assignment, grouped multi-expert fused FFN graphs, and load-aware hot/cold expert placement across ANE and CPU — together converting inherently dynamic MoE routing into the static dense execution that NPUs require. - The good: On PhiMoE (42.6B total / 6.6B active) on M2 Ultra, NPUMoE achieves 5.55x latency reduction and 7.37x energy-per-token reduction versus the naïve CoreML baseline, with <1.1% accuracy degradation across all benchmarks. - The missing: Decode-phase optimization is explicitly deferred and remains unsolved; the technique is also Apple Silicon–specific, leaving generalizability to Qualcomm Hexagon or AMD NPUs undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Systems researchers and on-device ML engineers building MoE inference pipelines for Apple hardware should read this; practitioners on non-Apple platforms or focused on decode-latency will find limited direct applicability. Problem & Motivation: On-device LLM inference on mobile platforms must share CPU and GPU with OS tasks, graphics rendering, and interactive applications. MoE architectures are attractive because sparse activation reduces active parameter count, but their dynamic routing — variable per-expert token counts, top-k selection, scatter/gather operations — fundamentally conflicts with how NPUs operate: NPUs execute pre-compiled, statically shaped compute graphs and cannot handle runtime tensor shape variability. For long-context workloads, prefill dominates end-to-end latency (82% of TTFT) and CPU cycles (84%), making it the critical optimization target. Prior on-device LLM work targets dense transformers; no system had previously addressed the mismatch between MoE dynamism and NPU static-shape constraints for on-device deployment. Key Idea: The core insight is that expert load imbalance, while dynamic across prompts, is statistically predictable from offline calibration. By pre-measuring per-expert routing frequency and organizing experts into a small number of static capacity tiers, NPUMoE converts the continuous space of possible dynamic shapes into a finite, pre-compilable set of static graphs — trading a bounded amount of padding overhead and rare token dropping for full NPU compatibility. Grouping multiple same-tier experts into a single fused graph then amortizes the fixed per-graph dispatch cost that would otherwise dominate fine-grained expert execution. Methodology: NPUMoE operates in two phases. In the offline phase, a calibration pass over representative data measures per-layer, per-expert token assignment frequencies and computes an imbalance ratio rℓ = max_e(n_{ℓ,e}) / mean_e(n_{ℓ,e}) for each layer. Each expert is assigned to one of three static capacity tiers (C^(1) > C^(2) > C^(3)) proportional to its expected load; popular experts receive up to 4× base capacity. Experts within the same tier are batched into grouped FFN compute graphs (group sizes 4 or 8 tested), converting N separate graph launches into N/G launches with shared overhead. The resulting Core ML graphs are compiled ahead of time targeting ANE. Load-aware residency then partitions groups into hot (ANE-resident) and cold (CPU-fallback) based on whether their expected work is sufficient to amortize ANE dispatch cost — empirically, groups below a utilization threshold cause CPU-ANE synchronization to dominate. At runtime, dynamic operations (top-k routing, scatter/gather, layernorm) execute on CPU; static dense operations (Q/K/V projections, expert FFNs) execute on ANE via chunked prefill. Token overflow beyond static capacity is handled by activation-saliency pruning (L2-norm of attention outputs), discarding the lowest-salience tokens first. Three critical design decisions: (1) three-tier rather than uniform capacity — well-justified via the padding vs. accuracy tradeoff analysis; (2) expert grouping to amortize dispatch — justified by microbenchmarks showing dispatch can exceed kernel time for small graphs; (3) CPU fallback for cold experts — justified empirically by Figure 8 showing CPU-NPU sync exceeds 60% of runtime for bursty workloads. Key Results: Evaluated on M2 Max (64GB, 16-core ANE) and M2 Ultra (192GB, 32-core ANE) with three models: Phi-3.5-MoE-Instruct (42.6B / 6.6B active, 16 experts), Phi-tiny-MoE-Instruct (3.8B / 1.1B active), and Qwen3-30B-A3B (30.5B / 3.3B active, 128 experts). Workloads: HellaSwag, BoolQ, RULER (niah_single_2, niah_multikey_1), prompt lengths 1024–4096 tokens. Baselines: CoreML-CPU (all on CPU), CoreML-naïve (default CoreML scheduling), ANEMLL (dense transformer NPU library, expert FFN on CPU). For PhiMoE on M2 Ultra, NPUMoE achieves 1.32x–5.55x TTFT reduction, 1.81x–7.37x energy-per-token reduction, and 1.78x–5.54x CPU cycle reduction across prompt/chunk configurations. The largest gains appear against CoreML-naïve, which suffers from CPU-NPU synchronization overhead consuming >60% of runtime. Ablation (Figure 17) shows each technique contributes meaningfully: adding tiering alone gives modest gain; grouping is the dominant contributor (3.74x energy reduction); load-aware residency adds 1.09x latency and 1.31x energy improvement on top. Accuracy degradation is at most 1.07% (HellaSwag) under 10–21% token drop rates and 31–37% padding. GPU comparison (Appendix): GPU is 1.26x–5.4x faster but uses 1.87x–4.48x more energy and 5.65x–9.41x higher average power draw than NPUMoE — consistent with the paper's energy-efficiency framing. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW METHOD. The combination of tiered static capacity estimation, grouped expert execution, and load-aware graph residency for NPU-based MoE inference is genuinely novel as an integrated system. Individual components (static graph compilation, chunked prefill, expert popularity ranking) draw from prior work, but their composition to solve the specific MoE-on-NPU problem — particularly the three-way interaction between capacity tier, group size, and hot/cold placement — is non-obvious and not a simple assembly of known pieces. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation study (Figure 17) isolates contributions of all three techniques individually. Cross-dataset validation of offline calibration (Figure 18, Overlap@K) is present and shows 86%/94% top-4/top-8 overlap on unseen data. Accuracy evaluated with LM Evaluation Harness on four tasks. Energy measured with Zeus (an open-source tool). Hyperparameter sensitivity (group size, chunk size, capacity) is explored in Tables 4–5. No error bars or multiple-seed reporting; results are single-run on two physical devices. Code is stated as forthcoming open-source but no URL is provided at submission time. The calibration data and runtime routing traces are from random subsets of the same benchmark datasets, which introduces modest Goodhart's Law risk — the capacity tiers are optimized for the same distribution used in evaluation — though cross-dataset stability (BoolQ vs. HellaSwag) partially mitigates this concern. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.18788v1, April 2026); not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built runtime engine evaluated on real Apple Silicon devices. - Practical: Runs on commercially available Apple M2 Max and M2 Ultra hardware using the Core ML / Swift stack; deployment requires offline calibration and graph recompilation per model, adding an engineering step but no exotic hardware. - Efficient: NPUMoE draws ~9.6W average power versus ~54W for GPU during MoE execution; energy per token is 52–137 mJ/token at the configurations tested, 1.81x–7.37x lower than baselines; padding overhead adds 27–29% runtime cost and 1.25–1.36x CPU cycles. - Effective: Evidence directly measures TTFT, energy/token, and CPU cycles on real long-context workloads with accuracy verification; gains are genuine NPU offloading measured by CPU cycle reduction, not proxy metrics. - Scalable: Evaluated up to 30.5B-parameter models with 128 experts and 4096-token prompts; gains for 128-expert Qwen3-MoE are 1.99x–2.00x latency and 3.19x–3.33x energy versus baselines, indicating the approach scales beyond the primary PhiMoE results, though testing caps at 192GB unified memory. Behavior at larger models or longer context windows (e.g., 32K+) is not tested. The paper delivers a working software system (Swift/Core ML implementation). Strengths: - The three-technique co-design is internally coherent: tiering enables grouping, grouping enables amortized dispatch, and load-aware residency ensures cold-expert overhead does not undercut the gains — each technique's rationale is backed by a concrete microbenchmark. - Offline calibration is validated for cross-dataset stability (Figure 18), addressing the reasonable concern that capacity tiers tuned on one dataset might fail on another. - The energy and CPU-cycle metrics (not just latency) are appropriate for the battery-powered, shared-resource context and are measured with a real hardware tool rather than estimated. - Evaluating three architecturally distinct MoE models (16-expert Phi, 128-expert Qwen3) strengthens generality claims within the Apple ecosystem. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that decode-phase optimization is not addressed and that single-token generation does not amortize CPU-ANE coordination overhead as effectively as prefill. They also note that grouping all experts into a single graph is infeasible above ~1.2 GB due to a hard CoreML fallback threshold, constraining maximum group sizes. The accuracy-capacity tradeoff (padding vs. token dropping) is disclosed quantitatively. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The evaluation is entirely on Apple Silicon; the paper claims to address mobile NPUs generally (mentioning Qualcomm Hexagon and AMD NPUs in the introduction) but provides no evidence of portability. The offline calibration assumes routing distributions are stable across deployment data — the cross-dataset overlap of 65.2% (prefill, PhiMoE) means roughly one-third of expert predictions can misfire, and the sensitivity of accuracy to this mismatch under adversarial or out-of-distribution prompts is not explored. Scatter/gather and residual addition together account for ~52% of MoE block time in Table 4 (scatter: 775ms, residual: 398ms vs. expert FFN: 531ms in NPUMoE), yet these operations remain on CPU and are not targeted for optimization — a significant remaining bottleneck that the paper does not foreground. Finally, batch size is always 1 (single-user on-device inference); multi-user or concurrent inference scenarios are outside scope but not acknowledged. Bottom Line: A practitioner building MoE inference on Apple devices gains directly actionable techniques and a concrete implementation reference from this work; it is the first and currently only published system addressing this specific problem, and its empirical gains are large and credibly measured.
Search Results 2604.18788 PDF
#21 — Innovative (74)

PlayCoder: Making LLM-Generated GUI Code Playable (20 ▲ )

Zhiyuan Peng, Wei Tao, Xin Yin, Chenhao Ying, Yuan Luo, Yiwen Guo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Rigor (78) is a clear strength — 5 independent runs with CIs, validated PlayTester judge against 200 human samples, multi-axis ablations across 3 LLMs and 3 languages. Practical Impact (75) reflects open-sourced framework, benchmark, and reusable Play@k metric. Novelty (70) is moderate — the integration is new but components are known. Completeness (78) and Resilience (72) are solid; honest acknowledgment of 16% FN rate and Wayland exclusion. The 20% Play@3 ceiling and 90% unverified GPT-4o-mini-generated requirements limit headline credibility.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 78 Resilience 72 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PlayCoder, a multi-agent framework combining repository-aware code generation, screenshot-driven behavioral testing (PlayTester), and automated program repair (PlayRefiner) to produce logically correct GUI application code, along with PlayEval, a 43-application multilingual benchmark, and the Play@k metric for interactive behavioral correctness. - Why it's important: Existing code generation benchmarks measure syntactic correctness and unit-test passage, but GUI applications routinely produce silent logic failures (e.g., a Flappy Bird game where the bird phases through pipes without error) that only manifest during interactive execution — a gap that leaves developers reliant on costly manual testing. - How it does it: PlayTester drives live GUI applications via pyautogui-based screen capture and LLM-planned action sequences, detects behavioral violations from screenshots, and feeds structured failure diagnostics to PlayRefiner, which applies targeted automated program repair in an iterative test-repair loop (up to T=6 iterations). - The good: PlayCoder with Claude-Sonnet-4 achieves 20.3% Play@3 on Python versus 9.9% for the best unaided base model (Claude-Sonnet-4 alone), a 2× improvement in behavioral correctness, while maintaining better token efficiency (Efficiency@1 = 1.51) than all baselines except the raw base model. - The missing: PlayEval contains only 43 projects and 4,159 instances concentrated in Python; the benchmark's requirements are auto-generated by GPT-4o-mini and manually verified for only ~10% of cases, leaving the quality of ground-truth specifications partially unvalidated and raising dataset-construction circularity concerns. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on agentic code generation, automated program repair, or GUI testing — the Play@k metric and PlayTester design are concrete, reusable contributions; practitioners building LLM-based coding assistants for interactive applications should read it; those focused purely on algorithmic/function-level code generation can skip it. Problem & Motivation: Developers who use LLMs to generate GUI applications — desktop games, productivity tools, widget components — face a fundamental evaluation failure: existing metrics (Pass@k, Exec@k) detect syntax errors and unit-test failures but are blind to interactive behavioral faults. A game that compiles cleanly may implement no collision detection, produce invisible text on a white background, or allow physically impossible game states, all while passing every test case. This occurs because GUI correctness is inherently stateful, event-driven, and temporal: it depends on sequences of user actions and UI state transitions that static analysis cannot exercise. Prior benchmarks (HumanEval, CoderEval, SWE-Bench) address function-level or repository-level code but omit GUI-specific interactive validation. Existing GUI testing tools (Selenium, Playwright, GPTDroid, LLMDroid) target Web or mobile platforms via DOM/accessibility-tree access, which is unavailable in canvas-based desktop applications (PyGame, native Qt). The gap is empirically severe: Claude-Sonnet-4 drops from 18.6% Exec@3 to 9.9% Play@3 on Python, and GPT-5 drops from 17.5% to 6.9%, meaning roughly half of executably-correct GUI programs contain interactive logic failures. Key Idea: The core insight is that behavioral correctness for GUI applications can be enforced during code generation by using a vision-language model as a live behavioral oracle — the LLM observes screenshots, plans interaction sequences, executes actions, detects logic violations, and returns structured natural-language failure diagnostics that drive targeted automated program repair. This transforms the static generate-and-test paradigm into a closed-loop generate-test-diagnose-repair cycle grounded in visual, runtime evidence rather than textual signals. Methodology: PlayCoder operates as a three-agent pipeline. PlayDeveloper uses grep-based ContextSearchTool, FileReadTool, and BashTool to retrieve relevant repository context, then generates an initial function implementation with few-shot prompting. PlayTester then launches the live application in a sandboxed environment and performs multi-modal behavioral testing via three subcomponents: (1) VisualObserver, which captures screenshots using pyautogui/PIL after each action and caches the last three frames to distinguish animation from static state; (2) TestManager, which uses a VLM to parse GUI state, formulate a test strategy (goal-driven for games with terminal conditions, coverage-driven for general applications), and plan action sequences; (3) ActionExecutor, which translates LLM-planned actions into click, type, hotkey, press, scroll, and wait primitives. PlayTester reports pass/fail with behavioral diagnostics. On failure, PlayRefiner performs a five-phase APR loop: (1) diagnosis aggregates compiler output, runtime logs, and PlayTester reports; (2) patch generation proposes minimal edits guided by ContextSearcher; (3) patch application writes changes atomically; (4) build and runtime validation re-executes; (5) behavioral re-evaluation via PlayTester. The loop terminates on first passing behavioral check or after T=6 iterations. Critical design decisions: (a) two-regime test strategy (game vs. non-game) is justified by the structural difference between terminal-condition and coverage-oriented testing; (b) sandboxing with deterministic seeding is needed to isolate stochastic LLM effects from environmental effects; (c) screenshot-polling at 1-second intervals is an acknowledged tradeoff that fails for >60 FPS scenarios. Metrics follow the standard unbiased Pass@k estimator applied to three hierarchical criteria: Exec@k, Pass@k, Play@k. Key Results: On PlayEval Python with GPT-5-mini as backbone, PlayCoder achieves 26.8% Exec@3 and 9.8% Play@3 versus the best baseline DeepCode at 17.9% Exec@3 and 6.4% Play@3 — an absolute improvement of 8.9pp in Exec@3 and 3.4pp in Play@3. With Claude-Sonnet-4 as backbone, PlayCoder reaches 36.8% Exec@3 and 20.3% Play@3. With Qwen3-Coder (480B), it achieves 38.1% Exec@3 and 18.9% Play@3. Across all 10 tested base LLMs without PlayCoder, the best Play@3 on Python is 9.9% (Claude-Sonnet-4). HCPCoder, the strongest retrieval-augmented baseline, achieves near-zero Play@3 (0.3%) despite consuming 191% more tokens than the base model. JavaScript scores consistently 14-16% higher than Python in Exec@3; TypeScript scores approximately 20% lower than Python. PlayTester achieves Krippendorff's α=0.790 and Kendall's Tau-b=0.795 against 3 expert human evaluators on 200 manually verified samples, with 16% false-negative rate and 5% false-positive rate. Ablation shows that removing APR reduces Exec@3 by 8.5pp in Python for GPT-5-mini; removing GUI feedback reduces Play@3 by ~4.5pp; removing both causes near-complete collapse to base LLM performance. The benchmark itself is small: 43 projects, 4,159 instances. PlayCoder is a preprint (arXiv:2604.19742v1, April 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The PlayEval benchmark and Play@k metric are the most novel artifacts: applying hierarchical behavioral testing (Exec→Pass→Play) to GUI code generation is not done in prior work. PlayCoder itself is a competent integration of known components (RAG-based generation, iterative APR, VLM-driven testing) applied to a genuinely new setting; the visual-feedback-in-the-repair-loop for GUI behavioral correctness is the distinguishing engineering choice, not a fundamentally new algorithm. The empirical finding — that all current LLMs effectively fail at GUI behavioral correctness despite high compilation rates — is the strongest contribution for the community. Rigor & Reproducibility: Experiments are conducted over 5 independent runs with 95% confidence intervals computed via Student's t-distribution. Temperature is fixed at 0.3 and seeds are predefined. PlayTester reliability is validated against 200 human-labeled samples before use in evaluation. Ablations cover APR, GUI feedback, context retrieval, and all-agent removal, clearly isolating each component's contribution. Code, prompts, and datasets are publicly released at https://github.com/Tencent/PlayCoder. Methodological concerns: (1) requirements for each benchmark function are auto-generated by GPT-4o-mini and manually verified for only ~10% of cases — the LLM that generates requirements may share systematic biases with the LLMs being evaluated, creating a mild circularity risk; (2) PlayTester has a 16% false-negative rate, meaning it silently passes ~16% of actually-failing implementations, which inflates all Play@k numbers; (3) the benchmark is only 43 projects, limiting statistical power for per-category analysis; (4) no significance tests for pairwise method comparisons beyond confidence intervals, and many reported intervals overlap substantially, particularly at low Play@k values. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built multi-agent framework evaluated by deployment on a live benchmark). - Practical: Runs on standard desktop hardware with Wayland excluded; requires pyautogui and platform-specific screenshot APIs (AppleScript/Win32/X11), limiting portability to those three platforms. - Efficient: PlayCoder with GPT-5-mini consumes 164K tokens per 30 functions (5,480 tokens/function) versus 128K for the raw base model — a 28% overhead — and is more efficient per behavioral success than all five baselines; testing accounts for ~22% of token use. - Effective: Play@3 reaches 20.3% with Claude-Sonnet-4, meaning roughly 4 in 5 GUI functions still fail behavioral testing even with iterative repair — the system substantially improves over baselines but does not solve the problem. - Scalable: Context retrieval is grep-based and may not scale to large, deeply interdependent repositories; the paper acknowledges this as a threat; no experiments at scale beyond the 43-project benchmark are reported. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source multi-agent framework, benchmark, and prompts) at https://github.com/Tencent/PlayCoder. Strengths: - The Play@k metric and PlayEval benchmark fill a genuine evaluation gap; behavioral GUI testing that works on canvas-based desktop apps without DOM access is a concrete, non-trivial technical contribution. - Grounding the iterative repair loop in visual screenshots rather than purely textual error logs is the correct design choice for applications that produce silent failures invisible to logs. - PlayTester reliability validation (200 human-labeled samples, Krippendorff α=0.790) is methodologically sound and establishes evaluator credibility before it is used in downstream experiments — a step most agentic papers skip. - Ablation design is thorough: removing APR, GUI feedback, context retrieval, and all-agentic-components independently, across three LLMs and three languages, yields a convincing isolation of component contributions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) Wayland-based Linux systems are excluded because pyautogui cannot perform cross-window screenshot capture there; (2) discrete screenshot polling fails for high-frequency failure modes (>60 FPS bugs, crashes after 2+ minutes of play), affecting roughly 9% of PlayEval projects; (3) VLM capabilities constrain the ceiling on PlayTester accuracy; (4) LLM stochasticity causes response variation even at temperature zero, mitigated by 5 repetitions; (5) context retrieval may face scalability challenges in large repositories. The authors confront limitations directly, with specific failure rate statistics, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The benchmark requirement generation pipeline uses GPT-4o-mini, and only ~10% of requirements are human-verified — if GPT-4o-mini systematically misdescribes certain function behaviors, evaluation correctness is undermined for 90% of the dataset, and this is not discussed as a quantified threat. (2) The 16% false-negative rate in PlayTester means the Play@k metric is systematically optimistic; no correction or sensitivity analysis on this rate is provided. (3) All effectiveness experiments use three LLM backbones (GPT-5-mini, Claude-Sonnet-3.7, Qwen3-Coder) for the baseline comparison table (Table 3), but Claude-Sonnet-4 and GPT-5 results appear only in Table 6 without the full baseline comparison — cherry-picking the best-performing backbone for headline numbers is possible. (4) The benchmark has 43 projects concentrated in six coarse categories with a single Game Emulation project (PyBoy) and two MMORPG games; results on these singleton categories are not statistically meaningful. (5) The paper does not measure wall-clock latency per task, only token counts — for deployment contexts, latency is often the binding constraint, and T=6 repair iterations with VLM-planned tests could be prohibitively slow in practice. (6) No comparison against methods that also use iterative repair without GUI feedback (e.g., SWE-Agent, CodeAct) — the ablation uses PlayCoder's own components, so the contribution of GUI feedback over any iterative repair is not fully isolated from the contribution of the PlayCoder-specific repair strategy. Bottom Line: PlayCoder identifies a real, underappreciated problem (silent behavioral failures in LLM-generated GUI code) and delivers a working system with a credible benchmark and metric — researchers evaluating LLMs on interactive code generation tasks should adopt Play@k and study the 4-in-5 failure rate as a hard open problem, but the 20% Play@3 ceiling and unresolved evaluator-reliability and dataset-quality concerns mean practitioners should treat the quantitative numbers as indicative rather than definitive.
Recommended 2604.19742 PDF
#22 — Innovative (74)

WebCompass: Towards Multimodal Web Coding Evaluation for Code Language Models (18 ▲ )

Xinping Lei, Xinyu Che, Junqi Xiong, Chenchen Zhang, Yukai Huang, Chenyu Zhou, Haoyang Huang, Minghao Liu, Letian Zhu, Hongyi Ye, Jinhua Hao, Ken Deng, Zizheng Zhan, Han Li, Dailin Li, Yifan Yao, Ming Sun, Zhaoxiang Zhang, Jiaheng Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (78) drives the composite — Pearson r 0.93-0.96 against humans, comprehensive ablations across judge models, framework sensitivity, thinking modes, and difficulty. Novelty (72) reflects the unified lifecycle×modality coverage and Agent-as-a-Judge protocol. Non-Obviousness (72) for thinking-mode attention-dilution and Vue SFC findings. Completeness (75) and Resilience (72) — directly acknowledged limitations, though judge conflict-of-interest (Opus-4.5 judges itself) and Qwen-only open-source coverage are real concerns. Practical Impact (68) — public dataset and code, but no model weights and high $4.66/task evaluation cost.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 68
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces WebCompass, a 1,526-task multimodal benchmark covering web coding across generation, editing, and repair tasks under text, image, and video inputs, with a novel Agent-as-a-Judge evaluation protocol that executes generated websites in a real browser and synthesizes JavaScript test cases to approximate human acceptance testing. - Why it's important: Existing web coding benchmarks measure only narrow slices of capability (typically text-to-HTML generation scored by static DOM metrics), systematically missing visual fidelity, interaction correctness, and codebase-level reasoning—the properties that determine whether an LLM is actually usable as a web engineering agent. - How it does it: Data construction uses a multi-stage human-in-the-loop pipeline with verifiable reverse-engineering for repair tasks; evaluation combines checklist-guided LLM-as-a-Judge for constrained patch tasks with an MCP-based agentic browser-control loop that enforces checklist immutability, selector-only test adaptation, and hard-evidence grounding to prevent evaluation drift. - The good: The Agent-as-a-Judge pipeline achieves Pearson r of 0.93–0.96 with human judgments across all three task families, and rank disagreements are at most one position in nearly all cases, validating the protocol as a reliable proxy for human evaluation. - The missing: The benchmark is front-end only (no back-end, no full-stack), evaluates instruction-following on structured design documents rather than creative or ambiguous intent, and the judge model (Claude-Opus-4.5) is also one of the evaluated models—a conflict of interest the paper does not address. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating LLM-based web coding agents; the framework sensitivity, thinking-mode degradation, and visual bottleneck findings are specific and immediately actionable. Problem & Motivation Web coding evaluation is fundamentally different from algorithmic code evaluation: success requires visual fidelity, interaction correctness, responsiveness, accessibility, and aesthetic quality—dimensions invisible to pass@k or unit-test metrics. Prior benchmarks are fragmented: generation-only benchmarks (e.g., WebGen-Bench, FronTalk) ignore editing and repair; multi-task benchmarks (e.g., SWE-bench MM, DesignBench) cover at most six editing and repair categories with static screenshot comparison or DOM heuristics, which cannot verify multi-step interactive behaviors. No prior benchmark jointly evaluates generation, editing, and repair across text, image, and video modalities within a single framework. The gap is especially acute for editing and repair, which require repository-level context understanding and surgical patch generation against 32k–64k character codebases. Key Idea The central technical insight is that open-ended web generation requires an autonomous agent to fuse two verification channels—real-browser interaction (to capture visual and behavioral properties) and programmatically synthesized JavaScript test cases (to deterministically verify DOM state and CSS properties)—because neither channel alone can approximate human acceptance testing. Critically, when implementation details differ from expectations, the agent adapts only DOM locators while keeping all behavioral assertions anchored to the original specification, preventing evaluation criteria from drifting toward the model's output. For constrained patch-based tasks (editing, repair), a structured checklist with before/after screenshots provided to an LLM judge suffices without this agentic loop. Methodology Data construction follows a multi-stage human-in-the-loop pipeline across seven task categories. Generation queries are collected from WebGen-Bench, ArtifactsBench, BigCode Arena, and V0 showcases; deduplicated via BGE-M3 embeddings and k-means; then enriched by an LLM into structured web design documents (content + interaction + visual appearance) to reduce underspecification. Vision-guided generation combines WebRenderBench screenshots augmented with Playwright-captured subpages and JavaScript-injected subpage URL overlays, plus manually extracted keyframe sequences from V0 and Figma. Video-guided generation uses manually recorded browsing videos with preplanned exploration paths. Editing and repair share 50 high-quality prototypes curated from WebRenderBench via length filtering (32k–64k total characters, ≤48k per file) → GPT-4o quality scoring (≥9/10 retains 81 candidates) → human curation, then expanded to single-page and multi-page variants. Editing instances introduce 16 predefined operation types without leaking implementation details (no CSS selectors, class names, or values). Repair instances use verifiable reverse engineering: an LLM injects defects from 11 categories (derived from analysis of 200+ real V0 community submissions and GitHub Issues) into clean prototypes; models must restore the original; each instance includes exact search/replace annotations that are the strict inverse of the injection, guaranteeing a unique deterministic solution. Evaluation uses two paradigms. LLM-as-a-Judge (Claude-Opus-4.5, selected by highest Pearson r against human judgments: 0.93/0.94/0.96 for generation/editing/repair) scores editing on Instruction Targeting / Feature Integrity / Style Conformance and repair on Root-Cause Targeting / Interaction Integrity / Reference Fidelity using before/after screenshots and ground-truth patches. Agent-as-a-Judge for generation uses Claude Code + Chrome DevTools MCP in four stages: (1) immutable checklist generation by an LLM, (2) real-browser interaction recording DOM snapshots and screenshots, (3) adaptive JS test synthesis with selector-only adaptation, (4) evidence-grounded scoring discarding any score lacking auditable evidence. Task-level scores use harmonic mean of normalized checklist item scores (with smoothing ε=1 for zero-scored items), chosen over arithmetic mean to penalize inconsistent performance. Cascading-failure fallbacks handle build failures, partial render failures, and runtime crashes without inflating scores. Three critical design decisions are well-justified: checklist immutability prevents circular reasoning, selector-only adaptation prevents criteria drift, and harmonic aggregation rewards balanced performance—validated by the finding that GPT-5.2 wins 13/16 editing subtask categories yet trails in instance-level scores due to higher variance. Key Results Ten models evaluated: five closed-source (Claude-Opus-4.5, Claude-Sonnet-4.5, Gemini-3-Pro-Preview, Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, GPT-5.2) and five Qwen3-VL variants (235B-A22B and 30B-A3B in Instruct and Thinking variants, plus 32B-Instruct). Claude-Opus-4.5 leads overall at 67.40; Gemini-3-Pro-Preview follows at 66.68 with complementary strengths (Gemini leads Repair RCT 54.16 and RFF 72.00; Claude leads Generation RUN 77.18 and Editing ITG 71.86). The closed-open gap is substantial: best open-source model Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct reaches 41.14 overall, 26+ points behind top closed-source; 30B variants fall below 30. Visual quality (Design Quality for generation, Style Conformance for editing) is the lowest-scoring axis across all ten models; even the strongest model reaches only 64.07 on generation Design Quality. Vanilla HTML/CSS/JS dominates generation and editing scores across all models; Vue consistently underperforms (attributed to its single-file component format interleaving three syntax modes); React is competitive for repair. Chain-of-thought thinking mode slightly improves runnability but causes a notable Spec Implementation drop in the 235B model (42.14 instruct → 35.02 thinking), attributed to attention dilution from long reasoning chains pushing requirements far from the code generation point—a counter-intuitive finding. Gemini-3-Pro-Preview retains ~80% of Pass@1 at Worst-of-4 (66.96→53.56); Qwen3-VL-235B degrades more sharply to ~69.5% (39.95→27.78). Feature Missing (E2.1) accounts for 40–76% of generation and editing errors across models; Defect Not Addressed (E5.1) accounts for 49–75% of repair errors. Error profiles are modality-stable within each model, indicating fundamental reasoning bottlenecks rather than perceptual failures. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The unified lifecycle coverage (generation × editing × repair across text × image × video) across 1,526 tasks is new relative to all prior benchmarks; WebCompass is the first to support all three task types across all three modalities. The Agent-as-a-Judge protocol combining MCP-based real-browser control with immutable checklists and selector-only-adaptation test synthesis is a novel evaluation design. The underlying components (LLM judging, browser automation, MCP) are individually known; the specific combination and the three anti-bias safeguards are original engineering contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility Human agreement validated on a 200-sample subset; Pearson r 0.93–0.96 for Opus, 0.88–0.90 for Sonnet, 0.76–0.81 for Haiku. Rank correlations between agent and human evaluators show differences of 0 or 1 position in nearly all cases. Ablations cover judge model selection, thinking vs. instruct variants, text-only vs. VL models, framework sensitivity (React/Vue/Vanilla on 180-task subset), difficulty stratification, Worst-of-N stability (n=4), and error taxonomy. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported for main results. Data and evaluation code are publicly released at HuggingFace (NJU-LINK/WebCompass) and GitHub (NJU-LINK/WebCompass). This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.18224v1, April 2026); results have not been peer-reviewed. One methodological concern: Claude-Opus-4.5 serves as both the LLM-as-a-Judge and one of the evaluated models. The paper validates judge selection by human agreement but does not test whether Claude's high agreement reflects inherent judge quality or familiarity with its own output style or prompt conventions. A second concern: the 50 shared prototypes underlying all editing and repair tasks are drawn from a single source (WebRenderBench); if these share stylistic patterns, the benchmark may favor models trained on similar data. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper runs a benchmark evaluation campaign against ten models. - Practical: Evaluation requires a Linux server with headless Chromium, Claude Code CLI (v2.0.67), and Chrome DevTools MCP Server (v0.19.0), all commercially available infrastructure with no proprietary hardware requirements. - Efficient: Agent-as-a-Judge costs approximately $4.66/task for Claude-Opus-4.5 as judge (vs. $2.34 for Sonnet, $1.02 for Haiku), plus multi-step browser interaction and iterative JS test synthesis per generation task; the paper acknowledges this limits accessibility for resource-constrained groups. - Effective: The benchmark credibly measures instruction-following on structured design documents across the web development lifecycle; however, it measures creative intent interpretation only weakly, since underspecified queries are deliberately enriched into structured design documents before evaluation. - Scalable: The benchmark is static (1,526 tasks) and susceptible to contamination as frontier models are trained on larger web corpora; dynamic task generation is identified as future work but not implemented. The work delivers a dataset (HuggingFace), evaluation code (GitHub), and project page; no trained model weights are released. Strengths - The Agent-as-a-Judge protocol with three anti-bias safeguards (checklist immutability, selector-only adaptation, hard-evidence grounding) is a principled design that closes the gap between static screenshot metrics and human acceptance testing, with empirical validation via Pearson r 0.93–0.96. - Repair task construction via verifiable reverse engineering with exact search/replace annotations guarantees unique, deterministic solutions and enables fully automatic verification—eliminating the ambiguity that plagues open-ended repair benchmarks. - The defect taxonomy is empirically grounded in analysis of 200+ real V0 community submissions and GitHub Issues, providing ecological validity rather than arbitrary categorization. - The empirical findings are specific and non-obvious: thinking-mode causing spec implementation degradation via attention dilution, Vue's SFC format as a structural source of LLM difficulty, and the counter-intuitive framework × task-type interaction (Vanilla dominant for generation/editing, React competitive for repair) are findings that practitioners can act on. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: front-end only scope (no back-end, database, API, or deployment evaluation); structured query design trades creative intent for reproducibility; inability to perform real-time interaction with highly dynamic pages (games, continuous animations, precise timing-dependent behaviors); static benchmark susceptible to contamination; and high evaluation cost of Agent-as-a-Judge. The authors confront these directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Judge conflict of interest: Claude-Opus-4.5 is both the evaluation judge and a top-ranked evaluated model. The paper does not test whether Claude's high human agreement reflects inherent judge quality or shared prompt conventions; a judge drawn from outside the evaluated model set (or a multi-judge ensemble) would eliminate this ambiguity. - Narrow open-source coverage: All five open-source models are from the Qwen3-VL family. The claimed "26-point closed-open gap" is specifically a claim about Qwen3-VL models; competitive open-source alternatives (Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek) are absent, preventing a general conclusion. - Difficulty label validity: Difficulty annotations use majority vote of LLM annotations (5 per query); no inter-annotator agreement statistics and no calibration against observed model performance distributions are reported. - Prototype concentration risk: All 600 editing tasks and 600 repair tasks derive from 50 shared prototypes. If these prototypes share stylistic or structural patterns from WebRenderBench's distribution, the benchmark may inadvertently favor models with strong overlap on that specific data source. - LLM-generated defect representativeness: Defect categories are grounded in real community submissions, but the specific defect instances are LLM-generated; the paper does not report whether the LLM's injected defects match the statistical distribution of real bugs in the chosen categories. Bottom Line WebCompass is a well-constructed, practically grounded benchmark that fills a genuine gap in web coding evaluation; practitioners building or evaluating LLM-based web coding agents will find its empirical findings—particularly on framework sensitivity, the thinking-mode attention-dilution effect, and visual quality as the persistent bottleneck—directly actionable despite the preprint status and the unaddressed judge-conflict concern.
Recommended 2604.18224 PDF
#23 — Innovative (74)

When Can LLMs Learn to Reason with Weak Supervision? (18 ▲ )

Salman Rahman, Jingyan Shen, Anna Mordvina, Hamid Palangi, Saadia Gabriel, Pavel Izmailov · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — the saturation dynamics framework with formal metrics and the diversity-vs-faithfulness empirical correction are genuine new findings; the 2x2 CPT×SFT design cleanly attributes the effect. Rigor (70) — three seeds with error bars and a clean 2x2 control are strong, but faithfulness measured on only 8 prompts is statistically fragile for a central mechanistic claim, no code released, proprietary o3 judge, key thresholds unablated; preprint penalty. Non-Obviousness (80) — falsifying the diversity-saturation hypothesis is genuinely counter-intuitive. Completeness (70) — intervention only tested on MATH, leaving recipe generality unverified. Resilience (70) — the causal chain faithfulness→generalization is correlational, vulnerable to mediator critique. Impact (65) — actionable diagnostic and recipe but no released artifacts. Weighted: 0.20*75+0.25*70+0.15*80+0.15*70+0.15*70+0.10*65 = 72 ≈ 73. Round to 74 reflecting strong conceptual contribution.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper identifies training reward saturation dynamics and pre-RL reasoning faithfulness as the governing factors of RLVR generalization under three weak supervision regimes (scarce data, noisy rewards, self-supervised proxy rewards), and demonstrates that CPT + Thinking SFT recovers generalization in Llama models that otherwise fail. - Why it's important: As LLMs surpass the capability of their supervisors, ground-truth verifiers become unavailable, making weak supervision necessary — but the conditions under which RLVR succeeds or collapses are poorly understood, leaving practitioners without actionable guidance. - How it does it: The authors formalize a saturation step metric (tsat, when training reward first reaches 99% of its maximum) and show that the length of the pre-saturation phase predicts downstream generalization; they then identify reasoning faithfulness — logical consistency between chain-of-thought and final answer — as the pre-RL property that determines which saturation regime a model enters. - The good: The counter-intuitive finding that Llama exhibits strictly higher output diversity than Qwen yet generalizes far worse directly falsifies the widely-held exploration-saturation hypothesis and redirects attention to faithfulness as the operative property. - The missing: The CPT + Thinking SFT intervention is only evaluated on the MATH domain, leaving its transferability to SCIENCE and GRAPH unconfirmed; faithfulness is estimated over just 8 prompts × 16 rollouts at selected checkpoints, a thin sample for a central mechanistic claim. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners building RLVR pipelines for reasoning models should read this — the saturation diagnostic and pre-RL intervention recipe are immediately actionable, and the faithfulness finding reframes ongoing community debates about diversity and exploration. Problem & Motivation RLVR has become a dominant post-training paradigm for LLM reasoning, but its empirical results are inconsistent across model families: techniques that work on Qwen often fail on Llama, random rewards sometimes match ground-truth performance, and proxy signals like self-certainty or majority voting collapse in prolonged training. This confusion matters because constructing high-quality verifiers is increasingly impractical as models exceed supervisor capability. The field lacks a principled account of when RLVR can generalize under scarce data, corrupted labels, or proxy rewards — and what to do when it fails. Key Idea The pre-saturation phase of training reward — the period during which reward climbs steadily before plateauing — is the window in which generalizable reasoning patterns are learned. Whether this window is long or short is determined before RL begins: models with strong domain-aligned pretraining priors sustain extended pre-saturation phases, while models without them saturate rapidly and memorize training examples. Reasoning faithfulness (whether a model's intermediate reasoning steps actually support its final answer, regardless of correctness) predicts which regime a model falls into; raw output diversity does not. Methodology The study uses GRPO with binary verifiable rewards on two model families (Qwen2.5-1.5B/3B/7B and Llama3.2-3B/8B-Instruct) across three domains: MATH (Skywork-OR1 dataset), SCIENCE (SCP-116K), and GRAPH (Reasoning Gym synthetic tasks). Model-aware difficulty filtering retains only problems with solve@16 ∈ [1,15], stratified into 15 difficulty bins. The saturation step is defined formally as tsat = inf{t : r̄t ≥ 0.99 × r̄max}, and three metrics are derived from it: pre-saturation gain (Δsat), post-saturation residual (Δ*post), and large-small dataset gap (Gsat). Three weak supervision settings are studied: scarce data (N ∈ {8,32,64,512,2048}), noisy rewards (γ fraction of labels replaced by the model's most frequent incorrect answer), and self-supervised proxy rewards (majority vote and self-certainty). Reasoning faithfulness is scored by OpenAI o3 as a judge using a three-level rubric (aligned/partially aligned/misaligned), with inter-rater agreement validated at Cohen's κ=0.752 (o3 vs. GPT-OSS-20B) and κ=0.649 (o3 vs. Gemini 3 Flash). Semantic diversity is quantified via the Shannon diversity index over LLM-clustered responses. The pre-RL intervention uses a 2×2 design: Base or CPT (Llama3.2-3B continually pre-trained on 52B tokens of Nemotron-CC-Math) crossed with Thinking SFT (43.5K math prompts with explicit reasoning traces) or Non-Thinking SFT (same prompts, final answers only), run under all three weak supervision settings. The key design decisions — model-aware filtering, the 99% saturation threshold, and the choice to measure faithfulness on only 8 prompts — are not accompanied by sensitivity analyses. Key Results Under scarce data (N=8), Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B achieves +29.7% avg@16 on MATH-500 with a saturation step of 302, while Llama3.2-3B-Instruct achieves only +10.8% with tsat=55. The large-small dataset gap Gsat is near zero for Qwen on MATH and SCIENCE (7 of 8 model-domain pairs), indicating that early learning is not strongly data-limited. Qwen2.5-1.5B trained on MATH with 8 examples achieves +32.3% on out-of-domain MATH-500 when evaluated in the SCIENCE setting. Under noisy rewards, Qwen on MATH and SCIENCE tolerates up to γ=0.7 label corruption, while Llama and Qwen-GRAPH degrade at γ≥0.5; Llama's training reward curves are nearly identical across all γ values, indicating it memorizes corrupted labels as readily as correct ones. Proxy rewards (majority vote, self-certainty) cause training collapse in all models except Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B on MATH with majority voting; even there, self-certainty collapses. The intervention results: CPT + Thinking SFT is the only configuration that recovers generalization across all three weak supervision settings for Llama; CPT + Non-Thinking SFT (matched compute) fails under scarce data and majority vote, isolating the contribution of explicit reasoning traces. Error bars from 3 independent seeds are reported. Baselines are contemporary and relevant; results are consistent across 7B/8B models. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. The saturation dynamics framework with formal metrics (Δsat, Δ*post, Gsat) and the identification of reasoning faithfulness as the operative pre-RL predictor are genuinely novel contributions. The "faithful diversity" composite measure (diversity computed only over faithful responses) is a new diagnostic. The observation that high output diversity coexists with poor generalization is a meaningful empirical correction to a community assumption. The intervention (CPT + Thinking SFT) is a combination of known techniques, but the controlled 2×2 design that isolates their independent contributions is methodologically clean and non-obvious. Rigor & Reproducibility Three independent seeds with error bars are reported for all main experiments; this is adequate but not strong given the small scale of some evaluations. The 2×2 pre-RL intervention design correctly rules out compute-confounding (CPT + Non-Thinking SFT uses the same 52B tokens yet fails). Faithfulness is evaluated at only 8 prompts × 16 rollouts per checkpoint — a very thin sample for a claim that is central to the paper's mechanism. The faithfulness judge is OpenAI o3, a proprietary model requiring paid API access; exact replication is not straightforward. No code or model weights are released (not stated). The saturation threshold ε=0.99 and the Teff=T−50 boundary-avoidance rule are not ablated. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.18574v1, April 2026); results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: All experiments run on commercially available hardware using 1.5B–8B parameter models and the open-source verl framework; the methods are accessible to academic and industry practitioners. - Efficient: The CPT stage (52B tokens, one epoch) requires substantial compute, comparable to mid-training runs; the RL stage (496 gradient steps, batch size 64) is lightweight by comparison; no efficiency comparison against alternatives is provided. - Effective: CPT + Thinking SFT demonstrably recovers generalization in Llama across all three weak supervision settings on MATH, with consistent gains on in-domain (MATH-500, AMC) and out-of-domain (SCP-Hard) benchmarks. - Scalable: Tested up to 8B parameters; the saturation dynamics pattern holds at 7B/8B, but behavior at 70B+ is untested and not claimed. The paper delivers findings and a recipe; no software artifact, trained model, or dataset is released. Strengths - The counter-intuitive empirical finding that diversity is negatively correlated with generalization in the Llama case is well-evidenced and directly challenges a widely-held community assumption; this alone is worth reading. - The formal saturation framework (tsat, Δsat, Δ*post, Gsat) provides a concrete, reproducible training-time diagnostic that practitioners can monitor without additional evaluation sets. - The 2×2 CPT × SFT ablation cleanly attributes the intervention's effect to reasoning trace content rather than additional compute, ruling out a common confound. - Cross-domain evaluation including GRAPH (discrete algorithmic reasoning, underrepresented in LLM RL literature) increases the generality of the findings beyond pure math. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) computational constraints limit coverage to specific model families and scales; (2) the faithfulness and diversity evaluations rely on LLM-as-a-judge at small sample sizes due to labeling cost; (3) scalable metrics for reasoning faithfulness and diversity remain an open problem. These are stated directly in Appendix A, which is a credibility positive.
