TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PubSwap, a federated RLVR framework that interleaves local GRPO steps with public-data off-policy coordination — periodically replacing locally incorrect client responses with globally correct ones from a shared pool — to reduce client drift during federated reasoning post-training. - Why it's important: Training LLMs with verifiable rewards on private data distributed across organizations (hospitals, financial institutions) is blocked by data-sharing constraints, yet isolated local training on heterogeneous data causes severe drift and degrades global model quality. - How it does it: Each PubSwap period, the server broadcasts shared public prompts, all clients generate responses and upload them, and the Balanced aggregation strategy replaces a client's locally incorrect responses with globally correct ones drawn from other clients — providing a common policy anchor without exposing private data. - The good: Consistent gains over FedAvg+GRPO across three model families (Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B, Qwen3-4B-Instruct, Llama3.2-3B-Instruct) and two task domains, with advantages growing at higher local-step counts (τ=120) where communication is most constrained. - The missing: All experiments use exactly 4 clients — the method's behavior at realistic federation scales (50–100 clients) is entirely untested — and no variance-reduction baselines such as SCAFFOLD are included despite their relevance to client drift. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building federated LLM post-training pipelines in heterogeneous, privacy-sensitive data settings; less compelling if your deployment requires formal privacy guarantees or involves large client counts. Problem & Motivation Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become the dominant approach for reasoning post-training of LLMs, but the relevant data — patient histories, financial records, client-specific notes — is distributed across organizations that cannot share it. Centralized training is infeasible; federated learning avoids raw data sharing, but scaling RLVR in this regime is doubly challenging. Full-model synchronization requires transmitting and aggregating large updates, and performing many local GRPO steps under heterogeneous client data distributions induces severe client drift — local models diverge from a globally useful objective. Standard mitigation strategies such as FedProx (parameter-space regularization) and communication compression degrade quality in LLM RL settings in ways not seen in supervised fine-tuning. A separate, underexplored resource — small shared public datasets available in most decentralized text-task settings — has not been systematically leveraged for cross-client coordination in RLVR. Key Idea The central insight is to use a shared public dataset not as additional training data but as a coordination signal: all clients generate responses to the same public prompts, the server pools these responses, and each client replaces its locally incorrect responses with globally correct ones from the pool before running a GRPO update. This creates a shared gradient direction across clients without any private data exchange, acting as a lightweight anchor toward a more globally aligned objective. The key design choice — the Balanced strategy — replaces incorrect responses only when a client has fewer than K/2 correct ones, keeping the update closer to the client's own policy while injecting just enough cross-client signal to limit drift. Methodology The framework has three interleaved phases per communication round. (1) Local GRPO steps: each of N clients performs τ_swap − 1 standard GRPO steps on minibatches from its private dataset D_n, updating only LoRA factors (rank r=32, alpha=64 applied to all linear layers); the backbone W_0 remains frozen. LoRA reduces per-layer communication from 2md to 2r(m+d). (2) Public step (every τ_swap local steps): the server samples b prompts from D_pub and broadcasts them; each client generates K responses and transmits them to the server. Under Balanced aggregation, for each prompt, if a client produced fewer than K/2 correct responses (c_n < K/2), up to K/2 − c_n of its incorrect responses are replaced with correct ones from other clients; the client then runs one GRPO update on this mixed response set. Under Random, the server samples K responses uniformly at random from the full NK-response pool and broadcasts the same set to all clients. (3) After τ total local steps (one communication round), each client transmits its LoRA matrices to the server, which averages A and B factors separately (FedIT inexact aggregation). The GRPO objective is the standard clipped surrogate with group-relative advantages (Eq. 2), and DAPO asymmetric clipping (ε_low=0.2, ε_high=0.25) is used to partially correct for the inherent off-policy nature of public-step responses. Critical design decisions: LoRA for both memory and communication (justified by evidence that RL reward signals are lower-information than token-level SFT labels); Balanced over Random (empirically better at high τ, theoretically justified by smaller off-policy distortion); fixed τ_swap rather than adaptive (limitation acknowledged). Key Results Experiments use 4 clients with Dirichlet-heterogeneous topic splits (α=0.3 standard; α=0.1 high heterogeneity), 360 GRPO steps total, 2–4 NVIDIA H100 GPUs. Math benchmarks: MATH (12.5K competition problems) and DeepMath (103K problems, 20K subsampled). Medical: MedQA + MedMCQA mix, evaluated on Llama3.2-3B-Instruct. Key numbers (Pass@1): Qwen3-1.7B on DeepMath at τ=120: FedAvg-PubSwap 55.8 vs. FedAvg-GRPO 50.7 (+5.1 pp) and FedProx-GRPO 47.7. Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B on DeepMath at τ=120: PubSwap 53.1 vs. FedAvg-GRPO 49.3 (+3.8 pp). Qwen3-4B-Instruct on DeepMath at τ=120: PubSwap 70.5 vs. FedAvg-GRPO 67.4 (+3.1 pp). Gains on MATH are smaller (≤1.0 pp), consistent with MATH being easier and less sensitive to drift. Medical (Llama3.2-3B, τ=90): PubSwap 58.5 vs. FedAvg-GRPO 57.9 (+0.6 pp). Counter-intuitive finding: FedProx consistently underperforms FedAvg+GRPO in RL settings, likely because constraining parameter-space proximity does not constrain policy-space or trajectory-space proximity in autoregressive models. Another counter-intuitive result: increasing τ does not monotonically harm performance; very frequent synchronization (τ=10) can interrupt local optimizer momentum and hurt learning. Swap period ablation (τ_swap ∈ {2,4,8}) shows τ_swap=4 often best for Qwen3-1.7B (55.3 at τ=90) but τ_swap=2 is most robust across models. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD — specifically the Balanced response-swapping mechanism for cross-client coordination in federated RLVR. All individual components (LoRA, GRPO, FedAvg, off-policy RL) are known; the genuine novelty is the insight that response-level coordination on shared public prompts provides a more effective and RL-compatible drift anchor than parameter-space regularization, backed by both empirical results and a supporting drift analysis. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present: swap period τ_swap, local step count τ, Balanced vs. Random aggregation, and two heterogeneity levels are each varied. No error bars or multiple-seed runs are reported; all results are single runs. Code is included in supplementary material (no public GitHub URL stated). The theoretical drift analysis (Appendix A) is explicitly described as a simplified two-client setting using SGD rather than AdamW, omitting importance-ratio clipping and KL regularization — useful for intuition but not a rigorous theorem under the actual algorithm. Methodological concern: the public dataset sizes (1,250–3,000 samples) are non-trivial but their sensitivity is never ablated. Methodological concern: evaluation under a strict differential privacy model (e.g., whether response sharing leaks private information via membership inference) is absent despite privacy being the primary motivation. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on 2–4 NVIDIA H100 GPUs per experiment; the response-level communication during public steps adds negligible bandwidth compared to LoRA matrix exchange, so it is deployable on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: LoRA rank 32 reduces per-layer communication from O(md) to O(r(m+d)); public-step overhead scales with number of clients × responses × prompt length, not parameter count — manageable at small client counts. - Effective: Consistently and reproducibly outperforms FedAvg+GRPO on four model families and two task domains; gains are largest on harder benchmarks (DeepMath) and larger τ, shrinking on easier tasks (MATH) and smaller τ. - Scalable: Tested only at N=4 clients; behavior at N=50–100 is not evaluated, claimed, or analyzed; the public-step communication cost scales linearly with N, which may become a bottleneck. The paper delivers code via supplementary material (not a reusable library), a paper-form method description, and informal theoretical derivations — no trained model weights released. Strengths - The Balanced response aggregation strategy is a principled, minimally invasive mechanism that preserves the GRPO on-policy flavor while injecting just enough cross-client signal, with the off-policy distortion formally bounded and shown to vanish as clients' local policies improve. - The theoretical drift analysis formally shows that Balanced steps induce drift controlled by L_pub (public gradient smoothness) rather than L_priv (private gradient smoothness), providing mechanistic justification for why the method is most useful under high data heterogeneity. - The negative result that FedProx consistently underperforms FedAvg in RLVR is a non-obvious and practically important finding that saves future practitioners from pursuing a natural but incorrect direction. - Multi-model, multi-domain evaluation (four model sizes spanning 1.5B–4B parameters, math and medical tasks) gives broader evidentiary support than most federated LLM papers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The Balanced strategy can be outperformed by Random when τ is small (clients haven't drifted far, making response replacement redundant or harmful). Stronger off-policy correction techniques (importance weighting, reward shaping, asymmetric baselines) are not incorporated and are acknowledged as complementary. Future work includes curriculum-based sampling of public prompts, adaptive prompt assignment by coordination value, and smarter response aggregation. Authors confront these gaps directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps The 4-client simulation is the most serious limitation: the FL literature consistently shows qualitative behavior changes (convergence speed, drift magnitude, aggregation quality) as client count grows from 4 to 50–100. No sensitivity analysis on public dataset size (what if D_pub has 100 rather than 1,250–3,000 samples?). No comparison to SCAFFOLD or MOON — variance-reduction methods that directly target client drift gradients and are the natural stronger baselines. The privacy claim is motivational but not analyzed: response sharing (responses to public prompts generated by privately-trained local models) could in principle leak private training data; no formal analysis is provided. Medical reasoning gains are very small (≤2 pp) and the artificial MedQA+MedMCQA split may not capture realistic hospital-level data distributions. Bottom Line A practitioner building federated RLVR pipelines on heterogeneous private data will find a concrete, reproducible method with consistent if modest gains, but the simulation's 4-client ceiling and absent comparison to gradient-level drift-correction baselines leave the scaling story unresolved.
Citations 2604.12160 PDF
#54 — Innovative (68)

The Long-Horizon Task Mirage? Diagnosing Where and Why Agentic Systems Break

Xinyu Jessica Wang, Haoyue Bai, Yiyou Sun, Haorui Wang, Shuibai Zhang, Wenjie Hu, Mya Schroder, Bilge Mutlu, Dawn Song, Robert D Nowak · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is solid — controlled horizon as an independent variable plus FMEA-grounded failure attribution is a genuine framework contribution. Rigor (55) is the main drag: serious unresolved model-identity inconsistency (GPT-5-mini vs GPT-4o-mini), LLM-judge validated on only 40/1995 traces, no released code URL, partial error bars. Non-Obviousness (75) is high — the structural shift in failure composition (not just rate) is a non-obvious finding. Completeness (65) and Resilience (55) are pulled down by the model-ID issue and underpowered Database domain. Impact (70) reflects a useful diagnostic framework with a public leaderboard. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*70 = 64.75 ≈ 65; nudged to 68 for the strong cross-domain framing and OpenClaw external grounding.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HORIZON, a cross-domain diagnostic benchmark for constructing long-horizon LLM agent task families with controlled horizon extension and attributing failures to a validated seven-category taxonomy across four domains (Web, OS, Database, Embodied). - Why it's important: LLM agents succeed at short-horizon tasks but fail systematically at long-horizon ones, and without structured failure attribution the field cannot design targeted architectural fixes — scaling base models alone demonstrably does not close the gap once agents enter the long-horizon failure regime. - How it does it: HORIZON defines agent-independent horizon metrics (intrinsic horizon H* and compositional depth s), systematically extends tasks via depth and breadth operations to produce monotonically harder variants, and attributes 3,132 trajectories using an FMEA-grounded seven-category failure taxonomy validated by a trajectory-grounded LLM-as-a-Judge pipeline. - The good: The empirical finding that performance collapses non-linearly beyond a domain-specific transition point — and that the failure composition shifts structurally from environment/instruction errors to planning and memory errors as horizon grows — provides concrete, actionable diagnostic evidence that single-point success-rate benchmarks cannot surface. - The missing: A serious inconsistency between the paper's main-text claim to evaluate "GPT-5 variants and Claude-4-Sonnet" and the appendix figures that explicitly label the evaluated models as "GPT-4o-mini" and "Claude 3.5 Sonnet" undermines the claim of frontier-model evaluation and must be resolved before results are treated as reliable benchmarks of SOTA systems. - Read it or not: Researchers building or diagnosing long-horizon agentic systems should read this for the HORIZON framework and taxonomy, but should treat the reported model comparisons with caution until the model identity discrepancy is clarified. Problem & Motivation LLM-based agents deployed on real-world long-horizon tasks — multi-file software debugging, operating-system workflows, embodied manipulation, multi-step data analysis — routinely fail in ways that are non-obvious and poorly characterized. The central problem is that existing benchmarks report aggregate terminal success rates, treat horizon (task length or complexity) as an uncontrolled variable, and provide no structured attribution of why failures occur. As a result, findings cannot be compared across domains, and the research community cannot determine whether improvements to base models actually address the dominant failure modes or merely shift them. The paper targets two questions: where in the horizon curve do agents break, and which failure categories dominate at different horizons? Key Idea The core insight is that horizon should be an explicit, independently controllable independent variable, not an artifact of benchmark size — and that failure attribution should be grounded in the agent's execution loop (observe → plan → act → update state) using an FMEA-style process/design-level risk hierarchy. By separating the horizontal dimension (horizon extension via compositional depth s, which controls task difficulty orthogonally across domains) from the vertical dimension (failure attribution to seven mechanistically distinct categories), the framework enables cross-domain comparisons that aggregate success-rate benchmarks preclude. Methodology HORIZON defines two complementary horizon metrics: intrinsic horizon H (minimum effective actions required by an optimal policy, agent-independent) and compositional depth s (nested sub-goals or conditional branches). Two task extension methods operationalize controlled horizon growth: Depth Extension inserts non-skippable sub-tasks between existing actions (used for OS and Database domains, where H(s) = H_base + Δ(s)), and Breadth Extension combines k independent baseline tasks into a single composite workflow (used for Web and Embodied), with H_breadth(s) = Σ H*_i + ε(s). Tasks at extension level s+1 include all tasks from level s, ensuring horizon is the primary varying factor. The seven failure categories are: Environment (disturbance or undetected state change), Instruction (ill-defined or partially understood), Catastrophic Forgetting (constraint/knowledge loss over trajectory), False Assumption (unchecked prior beliefs), Planning Error (sub-plan or action-level decomposition failures), History Error Accumulation (compounding early mistakes), and Memory Limitation (context or retrieval capacity exceeded). Failure attribution uses an LLM-as-a-Judge pipeline grounded in trajectory context, validated against human labels on 40 trajectories (inter-annotator κ=0.61; human-judge κ=0.84). The taxonomy construction is grounded in FMEA methodology, with categories iteratively refined by open-coding and cross-domain coverage checks against a structured literature spreadsheet. Three critical design decisions: (1) nested task sets at each horizon level to control confounders — well-justified; (2) LLM-based judge rather than full human annotation — justified by scale requirements, with strong validation agreement; (3) all baseline tasks verified solvable before horizon extension — well-justified to ensure degradation is horizon-induced, not task-ambiguity-induced. Key Results Across 3,132 trajectories (1,995 failures, 1,137 successes), the overall success rate is 36.3%: Web 24.1% (185/767), OS 40.3% (864/2,146), Database 36.9% (31/84), Embodied 42.2% (57/135). Three consistent horizontal patterns emerge across all domains: (1) performance degrades non-linearly with sharp collapse beyond a domain-specific transition in compositional depth s; (2) model-family performance gaps narrow substantially once agents enter the long-horizon failure regime; (3) identical extension level s induces markedly different difficulty across domains (embodied tasks degrade steeply at s=2; database and OS tasks sustain moderate performance until higher s). In the Embodied domain specifically, GPT-5-mini success drops from 0.926±0.064 at s=1 to 0.074±0.064 at the highest horizon; Claude-4-Sonnet drops from 0.963±0.064 to 0.037±0.064. Vertically, failure composition shifts structurally with horizon: Planning Errors dominate overall (64.9% of GPT failures, 46.5% of Claude failures), while Memory Limitation (18.3% for GPT vs. 2.2% for Claude) and Environment/Instruction failures (32.5%+16.5% for Claude) reveal distinct per-model failure profiles. CRITICAL CAVEAT: the main text consistently claims models evaluated are "GPT-5 variants and Claude-4-Sonnet," but appendix figures F/7 and F/8 explicitly label the models as "GPT-4o-mini" and "Claude 3.5 Sonnet." This inconsistency makes the claimed SOTA-model framing unreliable; the actual model generation evaluated is unclear. Novelty Classification NEW BENCHMARK/FRAMEWORK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuinely novel contribution is the two-dimensional diagnostic framework combining controlled horizon extension with trajectory-grounded failure attribution — no prior benchmark treats horizon as an explicit independent variable with domain-independent metrics while simultaneously providing structured failure attribution. The finding that failure composition shifts structurally (rather than just success rates dropping) is an empirical novelty. The seven-category taxonomy itself is largely a synthesis of known failure modes from the agent and planning literature, organized through a new FMEA-inspired hierarchical framing rather than invented from scratch. Rigor & Reproducibility Strengths: Three independent runs reported with mean ± standard deviation for each horizon setting (Embodied domain fully quantified with error bars). LLM-as-a-Judge validated against human annotations with κ=0.84 on 40 trajectories; inter-annotator human κ=0.61 confirms non-trivial labeling difficulty. Baseline tasks verified solvable before extension. Supplementary spreadsheet of literature-to-taxonomy mappings provided. Weaknesses: The LLM-as-a-Judge is validated on only 40 trajectories out of 1,995 failures — a ~2% validation sample. Error bars are reported only partially; most domain-level breakdowns (Web, OS, Database) show single-run aggregates in Appendix F without per-horizon variance. The model identity inconsistency (GPT-5-mini vs. GPT-4o-mini; Claude-4-Sonnet vs. Claude 3.5 Sonnet) is unresolved in the paper and constitutes a reproducibility concern. Code and benchmark data availability is mentioned via a "HORIZON Leaderboard" website but no GitHub URL is provided in the paper text. Database domain evaluated on only 31 successes/84 total tasks — small sample. Embodied domain involves manual success evaluation, introducing potential evaluator variability. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper constructs and runs agent evaluation experiments on four live/simulated environments. - Practical: HORIZON builds on existing benchmark environments (WebArena, AgentBench, MAC-SQL, IsaacSim 5.0) requiring those environments to be deployed; the embodied domain requires NVIDIA IsaacSim with two Franka Emika Panda robot arms, which limits accessibility beyond well-resourced labs. - Efficient: Running 3,132 trajectories with frontier LLM APIs is expensive at scale; the LLM-as-a-Judge reduces annotation cost but adds an additional LLM call per failed trajectory; exact compute costs are not stated. - Effective: The benchmark successfully surfaces non-linear horizon-dependent degradation and failure-type shifts that aggregate benchmarks miss, demonstrating diagnostic value beyond single success-rate metrics. - Scalable: Horizon extension procedures are defined algorithmically and applied to four domains with different extension strategies; the paper does not test beyond s≈8 for any domain, and whether the extension methods generalize to new domains without manual design effort is not evaluated. The work delivers the HORIZON diagnostic framework and annotated failure dataset (pending public release), not a trained model or software library. Strengths - The structural insight that long-horizon failure is a compositional shift in failure type — not just a quantitative drop in success rate — is a genuine empirical contribution that motivates architecture-level research priorities (planning, memory, constraint retention) over raw scaling. - The two-layer horizon definition (theoretical H* + operational extension depth s) cleanly separates agent behavior from task structure, enabling cross-domain comparison that prior work could not support. - The FMEA-grounded taxonomy with empirical validation (κ=0.84 human-judge agreement) provides interpretable, actionable failure categories that directly suggest specific interventions (e.g., catastrophic forgetting → explicit constraint tracking; planning errors → hierarchical subplanning). - The real-world mapping section (Section 7 / OpenClaw) grounding all seven taxonomy categories in documented production incidents from a deployed email-agent system (Shapira et al. 2026) substantially strengthens external validity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that a universal breaking point is unattainable and treat breaking points as transition regions rather than exact thresholds. They note that the taxonomy categories are not mutually exclusive — a single trajectory may instantiate multiple types simultaneously. They explicitly frame HORIZON as an "initial" step rather than a complete solution, and call for community extensions. The validation of LLM-as-a-Judge on only 40 trajectories is mentioned but not flagged as a quantitative concern. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Model identity inconsistency: The paper claims to evaluate GPT-5-mini and Claude-4-Sonnet (frontier 2025-2026 models) throughout the abstract, introduction, and main results, but Appendix F figures unambiguously label the models as GPT-4o-mini and Claude 3.5 Sonnet (2024-era models). This is the most serious gap — it renders uncertain which systems were actually studied and whether the "diminishing model gaps at high horizon" finding reflects frontier or mid-tier model behavior. - Single judge LLM, unspecified: The LLM-as-a-Judge is never identified (model, prompt, version). Given that the same paper uses multiple Claude and GPT variants, the choice of judge model could introduce systematic bias toward or against particular failure categories, especially catastrophic forgetting and memory limitation — categories that the judge model itself may be susceptible to on long traces. - Database domain severely underpowered: Only 84 total trajectories (31 successes) in the Database domain. Failure-type distributions derived from ~53 failed traces are unreliable at the sub-category level. - No ablation of extension method validity: The paper asserts that Depth and Breadth extension produce valid horizon increases, but provides no check that the extended tasks are qualitatively equivalent in difficulty per additional step — a single poorly-designed extension operation could confound the non-linear collapse finding. - Failure attribution is trajectory-level, not action-level: The taxonomy is applied to the overall trajectory, but which specific step caused the failure is only illustrated in qualitative mini-trajectory examples; no quantitative analysis of failure step location (early vs. late in trajectory) is provided, which would help distinguish cascading from isolated failures. Bottom Line Practitioners evaluating or building long-horizon agents will find the HORIZON framework and its structural finding (failure composition shifts, model gaps collapse, planning errors dominate) more actionable than any current aggregate benchmark — but must independently resolve the GPT-5-mini vs. GPT-4o-mini model identity question before placing quantitative confidence in the specific numbers.
Citations 2604.11978 PDF
#55 — Innovative (68)

Scepsy: Serving Agentic Workflows Using Aggregate LLM Pipelines

Marcel Wagenländer, Otto White, Britannio Jarrett, Pedro Silvestre, Yanda Tao, Guo Li, Huanzhou Zhu, Llúis Vilanova, Peter Pietzuch · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (70) — the Aggregate LLM Pipeline abstraction grounded in the relative-stability observation is genuinely new and the joint 3D allocation search closes a real gap. Rigor (55) drags: single-run results with no error bars, the Ayo comparison uses vLLM v0.2.2 vs v0.17 (acknowledged but uncorrected), profiling overhead never measured, code only promised. Non-Obviousness (75) — Fig. 3's relative-stability insight is the kind of empirical observation that converts an intractable problem into a tractable one. Completeness (65) — clean ablation, three limitations honestly acknowledged. Resilience (60) — Ayo version mismatch is an obvious reviewer target. Impact (75) — strong measured wins (up to 14.1× over Aegaeon SOSP'25) on real workloads with a real K8s deployment. Weighted: 0.15*70+0.15*55+0.10*75+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.30*75 ≈ 67.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Scepsy, a GPU cluster scheduling system for multi-LLM agentic workflows that jointly optimizes fractional GPU allocation, tensor parallelism degrees, and replica counts via a lightweight Aggregate LLM Pipeline abstraction built from execution traces. - Why it's important: Self-managed GPU clusters serving agentic workflows waste resources and incur high latency because existing systems either optimize each LLM in isolation (missing cross-LLM dependencies) or require custom programming models and leave GPU allocation to users. - How it does it: Exploits the empirical observation that per-LLM execution-time fractions are up to 4× more stable across requests than absolute latencies, enabling a pipeline-style throughput/latency predictor that drives a three-dimensional GPU allocation search without per-candidate profiling. - The good: Up to 27× latency reduction and 2.4× throughput improvement over Kubernetes autoscaler, and up to 14.1× latency and 7.3× throughput improvement over Aegaeon (SOSP'25), on a 16-GPU on-premise cluster with real workloads. - The missing: All evaluations use 1B–8B parameter models on a single 16-GPU cluster; profiling overhead is never quantified; the Ayo comparison uses an older vLLM version (v0.2.2 vs. v0.17 for Scepsy), potentially inflating advantage. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers and infrastructure engineers building multi-LLM serving platforms; skippable if your deployment is single-LLM or if you operate hyperscaler-scale infrastructure. Problem & Motivation Operators of small-to-medium on-premise GPU clusters need to serve agentic workflows that chain multiple heterogeneous LLMs — generators, verifiers, embedders, rerankers — with data-dependent branching, fan-out, and looping. End-to-end latency for a single beam-search request can range from 9 to 264 seconds depending on input, with invocation counts ranging from 24 to 844. Existing multi-LLM systems (AlpaServe, MuxServe, Aegaeon) treat each LLM as an independent service and miss cross-LLM resource coupling; workflow-aware systems (Parrot, Ayo, Autellix) exploit structure but require custom programming models and leave GPU allocation to users. No prior system jointly allocates GPUs across heterogeneous LLMs for arbitrary-framework workflows while co-optimizing latency and throughput. Key Idea While absolute per-LLM execution times vary enormously across requests, the fraction of total workflow execution time spent in each LLM is substantially more stable (empirically up to 4× lower variance, shown in Fig. 3). Because workflow LLMs are causally coupled, they are affected similarly by per-request cost. This relative stability means a simplified pipeline abstraction — where each LLM contributes a latency term scaled by its average invocation count and parallelism, and throughput is governed by the bottleneck LLM — can faithfully predict workflow performance under different GPU allocations without modeling explicit control flow. Methodology Scepsy builds and uses Aggregate LLM Pipelines in four steps. (1) Workflow tracing: an HTTP proxy intercepts completions API calls to each LLM serving engine, recording request/response contents and timestamps engine-agnostically, without framework-specific instrumentation. (2) Statistical aggregation: for each LLM m, compute average invocations per workflow request n_m and average request-level parallelism p_m from overlapping timestamps in traces. (3) LLM profiling: replay all traced LLM-level requests at varying arrival rates and multiple tensor parallelism degrees (TP=1,2,4), sweeping to saturation, to build empirical throughput–latency profiles per LLM. (4) ALP synthesis: transform per-LLM profiles to workflow-level units using L_wm(λ_w) = L_m(λ_w · n_m) · n_m/p_m for latency and T_w = min_m(T_m/n_m) for throughput. Predictions then require only profile lookups and arithmetic, making exploration of large allocation spaces cheap. The GPU scheduler performs a three-step search: enumerate fractional GPU assignments ordered by LLM latency ratios (proxy for compute demand) with contiguity and TP-limit pruning; pack fractions onto physical GPUs contiguously; resolve TP degree × replica count combinations such that TP × replicas evenly divides the assigned GPU count. Search time grows exponentially but stays below 70s for 128 GPUs with 3 LLMs in micro-benchmarks. Topology-aware placement uses a most-constrained-first heuristic: place large TP-requiring models into NVLink domains first (inter-node), then pack smaller fractions within nodes (intra-node). Realized via custom Kubernetes device plugin; Nvidia MPS enforces SM/memory limits per fraction. Three critical design decisions: (i) latency ratio as compute-demand proxy — reasonable approximation but not theoretically grounded; (ii) fractional GPU granularity (10 fractions/GPU) — well-justified to avoid over-provisioning small models; (iii) contiguity-only allocation — justified by symmetry exploitation but may miss valid non-contiguous placements. Key Results Cluster: 4 nodes × 4 NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPUs (PCIe 4.0 intra-node, 3rd-gen NVLink between GPU pairs, 100 Gbps InfiniBand inter-node). Workloads: RAG+reranker (e5-base-v2 + Llama-3-8B) and beam search (LLaMA-3.2-1B + LLaMA-3.1-8B-PRM). vs. Kubernetes HPA (autoscaler): beam search, 4 GPUs — 2.4× throughput, 1.4×–7.6× latency improvement; 16 GPUs — 1.8× throughput, 1.7×–10.4× latency. RAG+reranker, 16 GPUs — 1.2× throughput, 1.4×–27× latency improvement. vs. Aegaeon (multi-LLM multiplexing, SOSP'25): beam search, 4 GPUs — 7.3× throughput, 14.1× latency; 8 GPUs — 6.8× throughput, 12.9× latency. RAG, 4 GPUs — 2.5× throughput, 3.4× latency. Every Aegaeon prefill/decode split configuration loses to Scepsy. vs. Ayo (workflow-aware, EuroSys'25, using vLLM v0.2.2 for compatibility): beam search, 8 GPUs — 8.2× throughput; RAG, 4/8 GPUs — 2.1×/2.4× throughput. Ablation (§7.4): fractional co-location is the primary driver for RAG improvements; tensor parallelism is the primary driver for beam search latency. Both are required for full performance. A counter-intuitive finding: request-level scheduling optimizations (Ayo's primary mechanism) matter less than resource allocation at higher loads. Baselines are current and strong for the systems domain (Aegaeon from SOSP'25, Ayo from EuroSys'25). Scale is modest (16 GPUs, 1B–8B models). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The Aggregate LLM Pipeline abstraction — treating relative per-LLM time fractions as stable, workflow-level schedulable signals — is genuinely novel. The three-dimensional joint search over fractional allocation, tensor parallelism, and replica count for multi-LLM workflows has not been done before. Individual components (HTTP proxy tracing, LLM profiling, pipeline modeling, Kubernetes deployment, MPS enforcement) are standard; the novelty lies in their integration under the relative-statistics insight and the unified scheduling formulation. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study present (§7.4) with four configurations clearly decomposing contributions. No error bars or statistical significance tests on throughput/latency curves; single-run results are reported. Code promised post-acceptance at github.com/anon/Scepsy (anonymous placeholder, not yet public at time of submission). Two methodological concerns: (1) The Ayo comparison uses vLLM v0.2.2 while Scepsy uses v0.17 — vLLM made substantial throughput improvements across these versions, and the version mismatch is acknowledged but not corrected with a fair-comparison experiment. (2) Profiling overhead (time to trace + profile all LLMs before deployment) is never measured or reported, which is operationally significant for dynamic workloads. No evaluation under workload distribution shift. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built tool (25K Python LOC) evaluated by deployment. - Practical: Runs on commodity on-premise hardware using standard Kubernetes, vLLM, and SGLang; requires Nvidia MPS support and a custom device plugin, both straightforward to deploy. - Efficient: Fractional GPU allocation demonstrably reduces over-provisioning of small LLMs; profiling overhead is unquantified, creating an operational blind spot for workloads that change frequently. - Effective: End-to-end experiments show real latency and throughput improvements on the stated problem with current baselines; the Ayo engine-version mismatch partially undermines the workflow-aware comparison. - Scalable: Scheduler search scales to 128-GPU simulations in under 70s; actual deployment evaluation is limited to 16 GPUs with 1B–8B models, and behavior with 30B+ models requiring multi-GPU loading is not demonstrated. Delivers a software system (not yet public). Strengths 1. The relative-stability observation (Fig. 3) is non-obvious and empirically grounded: it turns an apparently intractable workflow scheduling problem into a tractable pipeline allocation problem without requiring control-flow analysis. 2. HTTP-proxy tracing achieves genuine framework agnosticism — any agentic framework that speaks the completions API is supported without instrumentation hooks, a significant practical advantage over Parrot/Ayo/TokenCake. 3. Joint three-dimensional search (fractional GPU × TP × replicas) closes a real gap: prior systems optimize at most one dimension, leading to systematic resource misallocation for heterogeneous LLM mixes. 4. The ablation (§7.4) cleanly demonstrates that fractional co-location and tensor parallelism are complementary — co-location helps throughput for heterogeneous-size workflows (RAG), TP helps latency for compute-heavy workflows (beam search) — providing genuine insight rather than just confirming that more features help. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify three limitations in §9: (1) cross-LLM fan-out (different LLMs running in parallel) is modeled as serial, causing latency over-estimation and potential suboptimal TP allocation for the non-critical-path LLM; (2) an LLM reused in multiple roles induces bimodal load distributions that a single aggregate profile misrepresents; (3) long-running tool executions (database queries, experiment monitors) are discarded from the ALP, potentially causing large prediction errors when tools dominate end-to-end latency. The authors propose concrete extensions for each. This direct acknowledgment is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Ayo comparison uses vLLM v0.2.2 for Ayo vs. v0.17 for Scepsy; the paper acknowledges this but offers no corrective experiment. Given vLLM's performance trajectory, the 8.2× beam-search throughput advantage over Ayo may be substantially inflated by the engine version gap. - Profiling overhead is never measured: the paper does not report how long tracing + per-LLM profiling takes before an allocation can be deployed, nor how often re-profiling is needed as workloads shift. For organizations with rapidly changing agentic pipelines, this is a deployment-blocking question. - All models are 1B–8B; no experiment involves a model that requires tensor parallelism just to fit in GPU memory (e.g., 70B). The TP search logic has not been stress-tested in a regime where TP is forced rather than optional. - The cluster is a single homogeneous 4×4 topology with symmetric NVLink pairs; the placement heuristic's effectiveness on asymmetric or multi-hop topologies common in larger clusters is untested. - No measurement of allocation re-computation latency when arrival rates change dynamically; §7.3 shows co-serving two workflows but not how quickly Scepsy re-adapts when one workflow's demand spikes. Bottom Line ML systems researchers and infrastructure engineers building multi-LLM serving platforms will find the Aggregate LLM Pipeline abstraction and the joint allocation framework both practically grounded and non-obvious, making this a worthwhile read; deployment teams should independently verify Ayo comparisons on a common engine version and benchmark profiling overhead before committing to Scepsy for dynamic production workloads.
Recommended 2604.15186 PDF
#56 — Innovative (68)

ClawEnvKit: Automatic Environment Generation for Claw-Like Agents (18 ▲ )

Xirui Li, Ming Li, Derry Xu, Wei-Lin Chiang, Ion Stoica, Cho-Jui Hsieh, Tianyi Zhou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (72) and Completeness (72) are solid — released tool, benchmark, audited 0% FN, declarative (P,M,C) formalism is genuinely useful. Rigor (62) is pulled down by absent component-level ablations, no error bars, the acknowledged coherence-metric measurement confound, and the unexplained MiniMax M2.5/M2.7 rank reversal. Novelty (65) — the formalism is novel for this domain but auto-benchmark generation via LLMs is an active area. The pseudonymous 'claw-like' naming is unusual and impedes independent verification.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 72 Resilience 62 Impact 72
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ClawEnvKit, an automated pipeline that generates verified evaluation and training environments for CLI-style LLM agent harnesses from natural language descriptions, and releases Auto-ClawEval, a 1,040-environment benchmark across 24 task categories. - Why it's important: Manual construction of agent benchmarks requires ~2 person-hours per task (208 hours for 104 tasks in Claw-Eval), produces static artifacts that saturate and leak into training data, and cannot scale alongside rapidly improving agent capabilities. - How it does it: A three-module LLM pipeline (Parser → Generator → Validator) decouples declarative task specification from deterministic verification by representing each environment as a (P, M, C) triple — natural language task spec, mock API interface, evaluation functional — so an LLM can generate valid environments without reasoning about full execution semantics. - The good: Auto-ClawEval-Mini (104 auto-generated tasks) scores higher than human-curated Claw-Eval on both Coherence (0.59 vs 0.51) and Clarity (3.54 vs 3.38) at 13,800× lower cost, with a manually audited 0% false-negative rate across 52 high-effort low-score cases. - The missing: The coherence advantage is partially self-defeating: the authors acknowledge it arises because the LLM judge can directly inspect the structured P↔M↔C alignment in generated tasks but cannot read Claw-Eval's rubrics embedded in code — a measurement artifact, not a genuine quality signal. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building agent evaluation infrastructure or studying harness engineering effects; less valuable if your interest is in novel LLM agent architectures or training algorithms, as the contribution is tooling and benchmarking rather than new learning methods. Problem & Motivation: Evaluating and training CLI-style LLM agents (called "claw-like agents" throughout — a pseudonymous term for a class of open-source agent harnesses) requires constructing environments that specify what the agent must do, what tools it can call, and how its actions are graded. Prior benchmarks (Claw-Eval, ClawArena, SkillsBench) are human-authored, requiring hundreds of person-hours, and freeze into static artifacts vulnerable to benchmark contamination as agents absorb them through training. Training pipelines (OpenClaw-RL, MetaClaw) are similarly bounded by whatever tasks users happen to perform. The gap is that neither evaluation nor training can scale alongside agent capabilities when environment construction is a manual bottleneck. Key Idea: The core insight is that modern agent environments have an asymmetry: they are semantically infinite from the agent's perspective (unbounded NL context, tool outputs, conversation history) but implementationally finite (a small set of in-memory mock service databases). Exploiting this asymmetry, the paper proposes a declarative formalism E = (P, M, C) — task spec, interaction interface, evaluation functional — that separates what to verify from how to verify it. An LLM can generate a valid (P, M, C) triple without reasoning about state transitions; the deterministic Validator enforces correctness structurally. This makes automated generation tractable where prior approaches required per-task grader logic. Methodology: The pipeline has three modules. The Parser converts a natural language request into structured "intent atoms" (actions, objects, constraints) via a single LLM call. The Generator has three sub-workflows: task generation (LLM writes task.yaml with tools, fixtures, scoring components), service generation (designs new FastAPI mock servers when needed), and fixture generation (mounts files into containers). The Validator runs 12 structural checks (required fields, weight sums, check type validity, LLM judge weight cap at 55%, safety/scoring contradiction detection) and semantic coverage checks (every intent atom must appear in P, M, or C). Execution uses Docker containers with `--network none`, mock services injecting 25% random HTTP 429/500 errors to test robustness, and three harness integration tiers (native plugin, MCP server, SKILL.md+curl). The GradingEngine applies 15 check types (audit-based, output-based, file-based, plus LLM judge) weighted by a safety gate that zeroes the entire score on any forbidden action. Critical design decisions: (1) capping LLM judge weight at 55% to keep the majority of every score deterministic — well-justified to reduce grading variance; (2) deduplication via action rotation and passing last 10 task names to the LLM — reasonable but heuristic; (3) using server-side audit logs rather than agent-reported actions to prevent credit for described-but-not-performed actions — well-justified. Key Results: On quality: Auto-ClawEval-Mini achieves Coherence 0.59 vs. 0.51 for Claw-Eval and Clarity 3.54 vs. 3.38, with 100% validity, generated in 1.8 hours vs. 208 person-hours. On agent performance across 1,040 environments: completion scores range from 34% (MiniMax M2.5) to 76% (MiniMax M2.7 — oddly, the smaller model variant scores worse), with no model saturating the benchmark. GPT-5.4 achieves the highest mean score (58.8). Safety (≥83%) and robustness (100% across all models) are near-perfect, making completion the primary discriminating axis. Across 8 harnesses, all structured harnesses outperform the ReAct Agent Loop baseline (53.3%), with NemoClaw reaching 69.0 (+15.7 pp). Counter-intuitively, Tier 3 SKILL.md harnesses (NemoClaw 69.0, Hermes 66.9) outperform several Tier 2 MCP harnesses (ZeroClaw 57.1, PicoClaw 53.2), suggesting harness integration tier does not determine performance. Auto-ClawEval and Mini scores differ by <2% across all models and harnesses. The system produces 1,040 environments for $80 in API costs (claude-sonnet-4.6). A false-negative audit of 52 high-effort low-score trajectories finds 0 genuine grading errors. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The declarative (P, M, C) environment formalism and the three-module generation pipeline are genuinely novel for the claw-like agent domain; the broader idea of automated benchmark generation via LLM (c.f. SWE-smith, RandomWorld, Agent World Model, Endless Terminal) is an active area, and this paper's specific contribution is the application to multi-harness API-calling agents with structured verification. The cross-harness evaluation design revealing that harness engineering matters as much as model choice is an empirical finding with practical significance. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on individual pipeline components (Parser, Validator, Generator sub-workflows) are absent — it is not possible to determine how much each module contributes to final quality. No error bars or significance tests are reported on coherence/clarity comparisons (small N for quality study: 104 tasks). The Pass³ aggregation (requiring success in 3 independent runs) is used for methodology description but not clearly applied as the primary reported metric in Tables 3–4. A notable methodological concern: the coherence metric is assessed by an LLM judge that can directly read ClawEnvKit's explicit P↔M↔C YAML structure but cannot inspect Claw-Eval's rubrics embedded in task-specific Python graders — the authors acknowledge this explicitly, which partially deflates the coherence advantage claim. Code and benchmark are publicly released at https://github.com/xirui-li/ClawEnvKit. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. All experiments ran on a single Apple M-series Mac with Docker Desktop. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built tool and infrastructure evaluated by deployment. - Practical: Runs on a single Apple M-series Mac with Docker Desktop; no GPU required; estimated $20–50 per 1,040-task run using Haiku. - Efficient: Generates 1,040 environments in 18 hours at $80 API cost vs. 208 person-hours for 104 human-curated tasks; evaluation cost scales linearly with task count and model API pricing. - Effective: The evidence that generated environments are evaluation-grade is moderately strong: 0% false negatives in audited cases and comparable agent score distributions to Claw-Eval; but the coherence comparison is partially confounded by judge visibility into task format. - Scalable: Tested at 1,040 environments across 8 harnesses and 5 model families; the authors claim unlimited scalability but do not demonstrate what happens at 10,000+ environments or whether task diversity degrades at scale due to the 24-category constraint anchored to Claw-Eval's service library. The work delivers a software tool (ClawEnvKit) and a benchmark dataset (Auto-ClawEval), both released publicly. Strengths: - The declarative (P, M, C) formalism is well-motivated and cleanly separates environment specification from verification, enabling LLM generation without explicit state-space reasoning — a genuine architectural insight. - The server-side audit log grading design (rather than agent-reported actions) closes a subtle but important loophole in agent evaluation where models receive credit for hallucinated tool calls. - The LLM judge weight cap at 55% and the Pass³ aggregation are thoughtful engineering choices that reduce non-determinism in grading without eliminating qualitative assessment. - The cross-harness evaluation (8 frameworks, 4 model families) is more comprehensive than any prior benchmark in this space and yields the non-obvious finding that harness tier does not strictly determine performance. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront three limitations: (1) mock services cannot replicate production API messiness (auth flows, schema drift, subscription-tier rate limits); (2) coverage is limited to 24 categories lacking GUI automation, voice, multi-agent delegation, and domain-specific workflows; (3) tasks are capped at 20 tool-calling rounds, not designed for long-horizon multi-session workflows. The authors also acknowledge the coherence metric advantage is partly a measurement artifact of judge visibility into structured format. This directness is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The entire "claw-like agent" ecosystem (OpenClaw, NanoClaw, IronClaw, etc.) uses pseudonymous names for what appear to be real systems (OpenClaw maps to open-source Claude Code-style CLI agents; the paper explicitly includes Claude Code and Codex as Tier 2 MCP harnesses alongside these fictional names). This opacity makes it impossible to independently verify the harness results or replicate experiments without access to the named systems. - All 1,040 Auto-ClawEval environments are seeded from the same 104 Claw-Eval scenarios (10 variants per scenario), meaning task diversity is anchored to human-curated scenarios — the system is a diversity-amplifier, not a truly unconstrained generator. The benchmark's coverage is bounded by Claw-Eval's original 24 categories and 15 services. - No ablation shows what fraction of generated tasks fail validation and require regeneration, which would reveal how often the Generator produces invalid environments and the true cost of validation. - The MiniMax M2.7 (more capable) vs. M2.5 result is anomalous: M2.7 scores 49.4 on Auto-ClawEval but M2.5 scores 43.6, yet on Auto-ClawEval-Mini, M2.7 scores only 44.9 vs. M2.5's 51.4 — a rank reversal with no discussion. - Diversity within generated task categories is not quantified; 10 variants per scenario might produce semantically redundant tasks that over-represent narrow sub-patterns. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating CLI-style LLM agent harnesses gains concrete infrastructure and a ready-to-use benchmark from this work, but should weight the coherence/clarity claims conservatively given the measurement confound, and should note that the pseudonymous ecosystem naming complicates independent replication.
Recommended 2604.18543 PDF
#57 — Innovative (68)

Seeing Fast and Slow: Learning the Flow of Time in Videos (13 ▲ )

Yen-Siang Wu, Rundong Luo, Jingsen Zhu, Tao Tu, Ali Farhadi, Matthew Wallingford, Yu-Chiang Frank Wang, Steve Marschner, Wei-Chiu Ma · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (75) is high — the audio-pitch-shift cross-modal signal and the temporal resampling equivariance loss are physically principled and reusable beyond this paper. Practical Impact (75) reflects SloMo-44K (44K clips, 70× larger than prior datasets) and a coherent perception+generation pipeline, partially weighed down by 4×GB200 training requirement and unconfirmed dataset release. Non-Obviousness (70) is solid (pitch shift as supervision is underexplored). Rigor (50) is moderate — no error bars, 111-video speed-estimation test set and 48-triplet generation eval are statistically thin; preprint. Completeness (60) and Resilience (55) — small evaluation sets and unreported public release plans limit confidence. Weighted: 0.15*75 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*75 ≈ 64; lifted to 68 for the dataset infrastructure value.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a self-supervised framework for perceiving and controlling temporal playback speed in videos, comprising a speed-change detector, a speed estimator, a 44K-clip slow-motion dataset (SloMo-44K), speed-conditioned video generation, and extreme temporal super-resolution. - Why it's important: Current video generation and understanding models cannot reason about temporal speed — VLMs hallucinate when asked to estimate playback rate, and video generators ignore speed prompts — blocking applications in temporal forensics, slow-motion content synthesis, and physically grounded world modeling. - How it does it: Two complementary self-supervised signals drive the perception models: audio pitch shift (time-frequency scaling) as a cross-modal label for speed-change detection, and temporal resampling equivariance as a label-free objective for speed estimation; the resulting annotated dataset then enables LoRA fine-tuning of Wan2.1 for generation tasks. - The good: Speed-change detection reaches 92.4% accuracy versus 59.5% for Gemini 2.5, and temporal super-resolution achieves >90% human preference in the real-input setting over all baselines including Wan2.1-VACE. - The missing: Speed estimation is evaluated on only 111 manually verified videos and generation quality on only 48 image-text-speed triplets — both test sets are too small to establish statistical confidence. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in video understanding, controllable generation, or temporal reasoning; the audio-pitch and equivariance training signals are principled and reusable, and SloMo-44K is a genuine infrastructure contribution. Problem & Motivation: Video understanding and generation models trained exclusively on 24–60fps footage never encounter the spectrum of temporal speeds present in the physical world, so they cannot detect speed manipulations, estimate playback rates, or synthesize content at specified temporal speeds. This affects video forensics practitioners who need to detect speed-altered footage, content creators who need controllable slow-motion generation, and researchers building world models that must understand how events physically unfold. Prior work on speed perception (SpeedNet, Pulse-of-Motion) covers only a narrow speed range (≤4×) and relies on optical flow proxies or labeled datasets, leaving extreme slow-motion regimes (100×+) unaddressed. Key Idea: Two physically motivated self-supervised signals make label-free temporal training tractable: (1) when a video is sped up or slowed down, its audio pitch shifts proportionally via the time-frequency scaling principle, providing free cross-modal labels for speed-change events; (2) a correct speed estimator must be equivariant to temporal subsampling — accelerating a clip by factor k must multiply the predicted speed by k — so training the estimator to enforce this proportional constraint converts temporal resampling into a free self-supervised signal with no ground-truth speed labels required. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. (1) Speed-change detection: automatically collect ~8K binary labels by locating audio pitch-shift events in videos with original audio tracks, then fine-tune VideoMAEv2 with binary cross-entropy; the detector is vision-only at inference. (2) Speed estimation: train a speed regressor with a self-supervised equivariance loss L = (log fθ(Vk) − log(k · fθ(V)))², calibrated with supervised regression on Adobe240fps; iterative prediction (3 iterations) is applied at inference to handle ultra-slow videos where the initial estimate is underconfident. (3) Dataset curation (SloMo-44K): collect videos from YouTube/Vimeo/Flickr; apply the above models for shot segmentation and speed annotation; filter for genuine slow-motion clips via a two-stage pipeline combining Gemini 2.5 (74.8% accuracy) and a fine-tuned VideoMAEv2 classifier (84.4% accuracy), achieving 98% precision at 44% recall; caption with InternVL3. (4) Downstream generation models: (a) speed-conditioned image-to-video generation via LoRA fine-tuning of Wan2.1-I2V-14B, with speed discretized into 10 log-spaced buckets injected through sinusoidal embeddings added to the timestep embedding and via frame-wise latent modulation; (b) temporal super-resolution via LoRA fine-tuning of Wan2.1-VACE-14B, trained on synthetically blurred low-FPS inputs generated from SloMo-44K. Critical design decisions: log-space speed representation for scale invariance across 100× range (well-justified), iterative prediction for extreme slow-motion (ablated — 3 iterations sufficient), and LoRA-only fine-tuning to limit compute (pragmatic but limits model adaptation). Key Results: Speed-change detection: 92.4% accuracy vs. 59.5% (Gemini 2.5) and 80.4% (optical flow baseline). Speed estimation on 111 verified videos: Pearson ρ = 0.735 vs. human expert 0.880, Pulse-of-Motion 0.508, SpeedNet 0.476, Gemini 0.426; eRMSE = 1.913 vs. human 1.636. Temporal super-resolution (clean input, DAVIS): FVD 394.0 vs. Wan2.1 634.5, FILM 711.3; FloLPIPS 0.242 vs. FILM 0.252. Temporal super-resolution (blurred input, SloMo-44K-Test): FVD 182.2 vs. Wan2.1 594.0, FILM 257.6; FloLPIPS 0.067 vs. Wan2.1 0.126. Human preference study (real-input setting): >90% preference for the proposed method over all baselines. Speed-conditioned generation (48 triplets): FVD 1114.1 vs. Wan2.1 1266.8, ATI 1473.5. VBench results (Table 9) show near-identical scores to the base Wan2.1, acknowledged by the authors as reflecting VBench's insensitivity to slow-motion quality. Ablations confirm that both SloMo-44K (vs. standard-FPS training) and iterative prediction contribute meaningfully to speed estimation accuracy. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. Genuinely novel: (1) using audio pitch shift as a cross-modal self-supervised signal for visual speed-change detection — a physically principled and underexplored technique; (2) the temporal resampling equivariance loss for speed estimation without ground-truth labels; (3) SloMo-44K, the first large-scale general-purpose slow-motion dataset (44K clips, 18M frames, up to 10,000+ fps — 70× more videos and 150× more frames than prior datasets). Standard combination of existing ideas: fine-tuning Wan2.1 with LoRA using a new dataset and conditioning signal — the injection mechanism via timestep embeddings follows prior work (e.g., AC3D). Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for iterative prediction (Table 7) and dataset importance (Tables 6, 8), covering the most critical design choices. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported anywhere. The speed estimation test set of 111 videos is very small; results could easily fluctuate with additional examples. The generation evaluation uses only 48 triplets — this is a narrow base for FID/FVD claims. No GitHub repository or dataset download URL is stated in the paper (a project page URL is provided). A partial circularity concern exists: the speed estimation and generation models are trained and evaluated partly on data curated by the same pipeline models, though the test splits are held out. Training requires 4 GB200 (NVIDIA Grace-Blackwell) GPUs — top-tier infrastructure not widely accessible. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.21931v1, April 2026); results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS profile — the paper builds and evaluates working tools including a dataset, a speed perception pipeline, a video generation model, and a super-resolution model. - Practical: Fine-tuning requires 4 GB200 GPUs for two days; inference requires a 14B-parameter Wan2.1 backbone — accessible only to well-resourced groups. - Efficient: LoRA fine-tuning limits added parameters, but the base 14B-parameter model dominates cost; no inference latency numbers are reported. - Effective: Speed-change detection (92.4%) and temporal super-resolution (>90% human preference) show strong task-level evidence; speed estimation still lags human accuracy (ρ = 0.735 vs. 0.880) and generation is evaluated on an unusually small set (48 triplets). - Scalable: SloMo-44K contains 44K training clips; generation generalization to out-of-distribution content beyond the curated dataset is not tested; the 44% recall of the slow-motion filter means the dataset may underrepresent some slow-motion subdomains. The paper delivers trained models and a dataset as artifacts (via project page), though explicit download links are not stated in the text. Strengths: - Audio pitch shift as a cross-modal training signal is physically motivated and delivers essentially free supervision at scale — a technique that should generalize beyond this specific task. - Temporal resampling equivariance is a principled constraint that converts an unlabeled operation into a training signal; the formulation is clean and the ablation confirms its contribution. - SloMo-44K is a substantial infrastructure contribution: 44K clips spanning generic scenes at up to 10,000+ fps, with speed annotations and dense captions — no prior dataset approaches this scale or diversity. - Connecting perception (speed detection, estimation) to generation (conditioned synthesis, super-resolution) within a single coherent framework, where perception produces the data that enables generation, is well-structured and scientifically satisfying. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: Speed understanding models fail on videos with limited motion or when subjects deliberately move slowly (e.g., slow human gestures that look the same at multiple speeds). Generation models are constrained by the pretrained Wan2.1 backbone architecture and could benefit from full fine-tuning or architectural innovations. The authors briefly note both limitations in the Discussion section and frame them as directions for future work rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The speed estimation test set of 111 videos with metadata-verified speeds is too small to validate ρ = 0.735 with confidence; the selection criteria (videos where speeds are explicitly stated in titles/descriptions) may favor videos with obvious, unambiguous motion, biasing accuracy upward relative to real-world usage. The generation evaluation on 48 triplets (a subset of 224 generated videos, limited because most pairs lack matched ground-truth at the exact target speed) is statistically inadequate for FID/FVD claims. The slow-motion filter's 44% recall means over half of genuine slow-motion clips are discarded during curation — no analysis of what motion types are missed is provided. Downstream forensics utility is demonstrated only on a single X-Men movie clip; no systematic forensics benchmark is introduced. The paper does not state whether SloMo-44K will be publicly released, which is a significant reproducibility concern for downstream researchers. Bottom Line: Practitioners in video understanding or controllable generation will find the audio-pitch training signal, the equivariance objective, and SloMo-44K directly actionable, though the small evaluation sets and high compute requirements temper confidence in the quantitative claims.
Recommended 2604.21931 PDF
#58 — Innovative (68)

LLaTiSA: Towards Difficulty-Stratified Time Series Reasoning from Visual Perception to Semantics (5 ▲ )

Yueyang Ding, HaoPeng Zhang, Rui Dai, Yi Wang, Tianyu Zong, Kaikui Liu, Xiangxiang Chu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 24 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) is solid: a new 83k-sample hierarchical CoT dataset (HITSR) with principled Bloom/Bertin-grounded taxonomy plus a clever zero-architecture-change dual-image numerical-table trick. Non-Obviousness (75) — rendering raw values as a visual table to exploit existing OCR is a non-obvious engineering insight, validated by 52+ pp gain. Rigor (60) — thorough ablations across CoT, curriculum, encoding strategy, recent baselines (Qwen3-VL, ChatTS, Time-R1, GPT-4o), Cohen's kappa documented, code+data released; pulled down by no seeds/significance tests, 100-sample L3 eval, and circular GPT-5 generate/validate/baseline pipeline. Completeness (65) — author-acknowledged limitations are honest, but ECG diagnosis 24.5 pp below GEM is glossed. Resilience (60) — circular annotation and tiny L3 test set are real vulnerabilities. Impact (75) — open model weights, code, and dataset released; directly usable. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*60+0.15*75+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.10*75 = 66.5 → 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LLATISA, a VLM-based time series reasoning model trained via three-stage curriculum fine-tuning on HITSR, a new 83k-sample hierarchical dataset with verified Chain-of-Thought trajectories covering four progressive cognitive levels of temporal understanding. - Why it's important: Even GPT-4o achieves only 54.2% accuracy on basic numerical min/max localization in time series, and existing benchmarks contain semantic ambiguities and lack CoT annotations that would enable diagnosing where models fail — blocking systematic progress toward unified Time Series Reasoning Models. - How it does it: LLATISA pairs a standard time series line plot with a second image rendering the raw index-value pairs as a structured numerical table, giving the VLM simultaneous macroscopic visual intuition and point-accurate numerical grounding through its existing OCR-style image reading capabilities, then fine-tunes sequentially on L1→L2→L3 difficulty levels. - The good: On held-out OOD benchmarks, LLATISA reaches 86.8% L1 min/max accuracy and 67.0% L3 semantic accuracy, outperforming GPT-4o by 32.6 pp and 19.0 pp respectively, while also outperforming all tested open-source models across all levels. - The missing: The L3 evaluation set contains only 100 randomly sampled MCQ2 questions — a sample size where a 5 pp difference is within noise — and GPT-5 both generates and validates the L3 training QA pairs, creating circular annotation bias. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building multimodal time series understanding systems or constructing reasoning benchmarks — the HITSR dataset construction methodology and dual-image encoding ablations are the most transferable contributions. Problem & Motivation Large language and vision-language models fail at systematic temporal reasoning despite having broad world knowledge. The failure has two structural causes. First, no unified taxonomy organizes TSR by cognitive difficulty: existing benchmarks like TSQA and BEDTime conflate high-level forecasting with simple pattern description or focus narrowly on scenario-agnostic patterns, making cross-benchmark comparison meaningless and obscuring whether models can perform basic numerical read-out before attempting semantic judgment. Second, current benchmarks contain semantic ambiguities — questions with multiple valid answers, undefined location terms (e.g., "end" of a series), and insufficient domain context — plus no Chain-of-Thought annotations, making it impossible to train models to reason rather than pattern-match. Downstream practitioners in ECG interpretation, industrial monitoring, and financial analysis need models grounded in precise temporal evidence before semantic inference, but no training resource supports this structured learning. Key Idea The core technical insight is that a VLM can access precise numerical values from a time series without text serialization by rendering the raw index-value data as a structured newspaper-column-layout image, enabling the model to read exact values from a visual table while simultaneously perceiving global trends from the line plot — both through the same vision encoder with no architectural change. Paired with a curriculum that builds from point-level numerical grounding (L1) through pattern perception (L2) to domain-specific semantic reasoning (L3), this dual-image design exploits the VLM's existing OCR-style image comprehension to close the gap between qualitative visual intuition and quantitative numerical precision. Methodology The system has two components: dataset construction and model training. For HITSR, L1 (30,000 samples) tasks — min/max localization, start/end comparison, multi-series comparison, subseries localization — are generated from synthetic time series using a rule-based pipeline with exact ground truth. L2 (50,703 samples) combines rule-based numerical perception tasks with LLM-assisted local and global pattern differentiation; GPT-5 generates descriptions and CoT annotations from visual plots plus metadata, cross-validated by Qwen3-235B (Cohen's kappa 0.847–0.865), with 10% held out for human expert audit. L3 (3,121 samples, all real-world) is fully human-verified: GPT-5 generates scenario-aware MCQs with explicit Pattern, Value, and Semantic error distractors; every item is dual-checked by GPT-5 and human reviewers. LLATISA uses Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct as backbone. The second image renders index-value pairs in a multi-column grid read left-to-right, top-to-bottom. Training uses sequential SFT: Stage 1 (1 epoch, L1, ~27k samples), Stage 2 (2 epochs, L2, ~45k samples), Stage 3 (2 epochs, L3, ~2,700 samples), all at lr=1e-5 with cosine schedule on 8×H20 GPUs. Three critical design decisions: (a) table-as-image rather than text serialization — justified by 52+ pp L1 accuracy gain over single-plot baseline; (b) sequential curriculum over joint training — justified by 14.93 pp OOD L3 gap in ablation; (c) synthetic data for L1/L2 to scale supervision without annotation cost. Key Results On OOD benchmarks (BEDTime, MMTS-Bench, MCQ2 — Table 1): LLATISA achieves 86.8% / 96.0% (Acc / half-Acc) on L1 min/max localization versus GPT-4o 54.2% / 88.6% and best prior VLM (Qwen3-VL-8B w/ index text) 34.2% / 68.0%. On L2 local pattern differentiation, LLATISA achieves 75.6% versus GPT-4o 65.8% and ChatTS 57.0%. On L2 global, LLATISA matches at 97.5% versus GPT-4o 96.7%. On L3 semantic comparison (100 MCQ2 samples), LLATISA achieves 67.0% versus GPT-4o 48.0% and ChatTS 59.0%. ECG transfer (Table 17): after fine-tuning on 30k ECG-Grounding samples (2.5% of GEM's 1.186M instruction samples), LLATISA achieves 84.03% lead assessment coverage (ID) versus GEM(LLaVA) 71.07%, though diagnosis accuracy (62.77%) remains well below GEM(LLaVA) (87.24%). L4 OOD (Table 6, Appendix): full curriculum LLATISA reaches 83.3% on TRQA, outperforming Claude-3.5-Sonnet (82.2%) and GPT-4.1 (79.1%). Ablations are current and strong; baselines include GPT-4o, Qwen3-8B/VL, Time-R1, ChatTS, OpenTSLM, InternVL3-8B, Gemma3-12B. Baselines are recent (2025–2026). Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (HITSR: 83k samples with verified CoT, principled four-level taxonomy, training-test splits); NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (LLATISA model, training pipeline, code); NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (state-of-the-art VLMs systematically fail L1-L2 numerical grounding, achieving under 55% on local pattern tasks). The dual-image numerical table input is a pragmatic engineering adaptation — not a new architecture — applying the VLM's existing OCR reading to rendered tabular data. The taxonomy draws on Bloom's Taxonomy and Bertin's Levels of Reading, which are established frameworks; the genuine novelty is their operationalization into a verifiable training benchmark. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: CoT removal (Table 4), curriculum versus joint training (Tables 4, 7), intermediate stage evaluations (Table 10), encoding strategy comparison (Table 2), and multi-model baseline sweep (Tables 8, 9). No error bars or significance tests are reported anywhere; all results are single runs. The L3 MCQ2 test set is 100 samples — statistically underpowered. Cohen's kappa 0.831–0.865 for human/LLM agreement is credible. Code and dataset are publicly released (https://github.com/RainingNovember/LLaTiSA). Key concern: GPT-5 generates L3 QA pairs, GPT-5 cross-validates them, and GPT-5 is also a baseline — any systematic biases in GPT-5's conception of correct time series descriptions propagate undetected into both training and evaluation. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL — methods and experiments the paper actually runs. - Practical: Qwen3-VL-8B backbone runs on standard A100/H100 hardware; the dual-image input adds a preprocessing step (numerical grid rendering) but no hardware constraint beyond the base model. - Efficient: Inference requires two forward passes over two images versus one for standard VLMs; training on 8×H20 GPUs over three stages is reproducible at academic scale. - Effective: OOD evaluation on distinct benchmarks (BEDTime, MMTS-Bench, MCQ2) provides solid cross-domain evidence; ECG transfer demonstrates domain generalization with high data efficiency (30k samples). Diagnosis accuracy on ECG remains 25 pp below GEM, limiting completeness of the effectiveness claim. - Scalable: Tested only at the 8B scale; series lengths up to 2048 demonstrated for L3, but no systematic analysis of model behavior with longer series or larger parameter counts. Delivers a trained model, open-source code, and the HITSR dataset. Strengths - The four-level taxonomy grounded in Bloom's Taxonomy and Bertin's Levels of Reading is principled rather than ad-hoc, providing a reusable diagnostic framework that maps model failures to specific cognitive bottlenecks (perceptual vs. reasoning). - The numerical-table-as-image trick is simple, architecturally zero-cost, and demonstrably effective: it produces a 52.6 pp gain on L1 min/max accuracy over single-plot VLM baseline without modifying the model. - L3 achieves 100% human verification and inter-annotator kappa ≥ 0.831, with deliberate distractor engineering (Pattern/Value/Semantic error categories) — substantially more rigorous than typical LLM-generated evaluation benchmarks. - Curriculum ablations across three independent comparisons (CoT vs. no CoT, sequential vs. joint, stage-by-stage) consistently validate the training design with concrete numbers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper explicitly excludes RL fine-tuning (RFT) on HITSR, citing reward design complexity for multi-level supervision. L4 (Predictive Inference) is treated as a distinct paradigm and covered only in an appendix. Authors confront these boundaries directly and propose concrete future directions, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The L3 MCQ2 evaluation uses 100 randomly sampled questions; at this n, a 5–10 pp performance difference is within realistic sampling variance, making claims like "67.0% vs 48.0%" more reliable than "67.0% vs 59.0%" (ChatTS). - GPT-5 generates, annotates, and validates L3 QA pairs — a circular pipeline where GPT-5's implicit model of time series descriptions determines both what counts as correct and what the distractor errors are; Qwen3-235B cross-validation only partially breaks this circularity. - The main results table (Table 1) does not include GPT-4.1 or o3, which appear only in the L4 appendix; GPT-4o is not the strongest available closed-source model as of the paper's submission date (April 2026). - LLATISA's ECG diagnosis accuracy (62.77% ID) is 24.5 pp below GEM(LLaVA) (87.24%), but the paper foregrounds lead coverage gains and does not discuss the diagnosis accuracy gap explicitly — this matters for clinical deployment. - The synthetic L1/L2 training data uses a fixed generation pipeline from ChatTS; if the OOD test series share structural properties with this synthesis process (similar seasonality types, noise models), the L2 OOD improvements may overstate generalization. Bottom Line A researcher building multimodal time series understanding systems gains real value from HITSR's construction methodology, the dual-image encoding ablations, and the curriculum training results — read the methods and dataset sections, but treat the L3 performance numbers with skepticism given the 100-sample evaluation and circular GPT-5 annotation.
Recommended 2604.17295 PDF
#59 — Innovative (68)

Evaluating Language Model Pluralism through In-the-wild Crowd Discussions

cseweb.ucsd.edu · Julian McAuley - new articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — set-coverage retrieval framing for open-ended pluralism is a clean reformulation; new dataset (WildSCOPE) and a precise asymmetric-sycophancy finding. Non-Obviousness (70) — the asymmetric collapse under user-belief injection (vs. neutral subjective queries) is a non-obvious, well-isolated effect, reinforced by the random-pairing robustness check. Rigor (65) — entailment judge validated at 80% human agreement, clustering preferred 65.5% over GoalEx, robustness experiment included; but no pre/post-RLHF causal comparison, code unreleased at submission, single-platform Reddit data. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) — limitations confronted directly (demographic skew, k-response vs. single-response, judge leniency) but face validity of PLURALEVAL against human pluralism perception is untested, weakening adversarial defense. Practical Impact (60) — dataset and methodology useful for alignment evaluation, but code pending and pipeline cost not quantified. Composite ≈ 0.20·75 + 0.25·65 + 0.15·70 + 0.15·65 + 0.15·60 + 0.10·60 ≈ 67.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 60
Show full summary & analysis
