TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PLURALEVAL, an evaluation framework that measures LLM pluralism in open-ended generation by decomposing crowd responses into atomic opinion units, clustering them into semantically distinct viewpoints, and computing precision/recall/F1 coverage of those viewpoints in LLM outputs, alongside WildSCOPE, a 1.2K-thread Reddit dataset spanning three subjective domains. - Why it's important: LLMs optimized via preference alignment systematically suppress minority viewpoints on subjective topics, and no prior evaluation method covers open-ended generation—the dominant real-world use case—against naturalistic crowd opinion diversity. - How it does it: Reddit comments are decomposed into atomic, non-overlapping opinion units, clustered into semantically distinct viewpoints using a goal-oriented LLM clustering method, and LLM responses are then scored via entailment-based matching against the resulting reference set. - The good: Experiments quantitatively demonstrate asymmetric sycophancy: on AITA with GPT-4.1, neutral pairwise popularity accuracy drops from 77.4% to 71.2% under user-belief injection, and the effect replicates under a fully random pairing strategy with minimum vote gap of 10, confirming it is not an artifact of the experimental design. - The missing: No human study validates that higher PLURALEVAL scores correspond to human perceptions of greater pluralism—the metric's face validity rests on the assumption that recall over clustered atomic opinions proxies perceived viewpoint breadth, which the authors acknowledge but do not directly test. - Read it or not: Researchers working on LLM alignment, bias evaluation, or pluralistic AI should read this; it provides the first automatic, open-ended pluralism benchmark with a natural-language reference corpus and produces actionable empirical findings about sycophancy that are directly relevant to RLHF post-training design. Problem & Motivation: LLMs fine-tuned via RLHF and related preference alignment methods systematically favor certain positions on subjective queries, potentially homogenizing public discourse when deployed at scale. Prior pluralism benchmarks rely almost exclusively on multiple-choice and structured question-answering formats, which do not capture how models actually behave when asked open-ended subjective questions in the wild. Researchers evaluating alignment-induced bias therefore lack tools calibrated to natural, free-form generation, and practitioners deploying conversational systems have no rigorous way to measure whether their models suppress legitimate minority viewpoints. Key Idea: The core insight is to reframe open-ended pluralism evaluation as a set-coverage retrieval problem: decompose human crowd responses into atomic, non-overlapping opinion units, cluster those units into semantically distinct viewpoints to form a reference set, and then score any LLM response by how much of that reference set it covers via entailment. This sidesteps manual rubric design and enables scalable, reproducible evaluation against naturalistic opinion distributions. Methodology: The pipeline proceeds in four stages. First, Reddit threads from three subcommunities (AskEconomics, AITA, AskPolitics) are collected to form WildSCOPE (1.2K threads); threads are filtered to retain those with sufficient contradictory viewpoints (this filter applies to Experiment 6.1 only). Second, each comment is decomposed into atomic, non-overlapping opinion claims using an LLM. Third, atomic opinions are clustered into semantically distinct viewpoints using a goal-oriented LLM clustering method the authors developed (shown to outperform GoalEx in a human preference study, preferred 65.5% of the time); each cluster is assigned a popularity score aggregated from the Reddit vote scores of its constituent opinions. Fourth, LLM responses are evaluated against the reference cluster set via entailment-based matching (LLM judge, validated at 80% agreement with human raters on 100 class-balanced pairs). Three experiments are run: (6.1) coverage recall across k model-generated responses with and without user-belief injection to measure sycophantic viewpoint suppression; (6.2) whether models can rank opinion clusters by vote-grounded popularity; (6.3) pairwise comparison of whether models identify the more popular of two opinion clusters. Critical design decisions: (a) atomic decomposition before clustering reduces granularity mismatch—justified by reducing false entailment matches; (b) entailment-based coverage uses an LLM judge rather than lexical overlap—justified as standard practice in factuality evaluation; (c) popularity is operationalized via Reddit vote aggregation rather than raw upvote counts, enabling controlled pairwise experiments—justified, with average vote gaps of 64.7 (AskEconomics), 101.2 (AITA), and 144.3 (AskPolitics). Key Results: The headline empirical finding is asymmetric sycophancy: injecting a user's stated belief into prompts causes LLMs to suppress opposing viewpoints, measurably degrading recall over the reference opinion set. On the pairwise popularity task (Experiment 6.3), GPT-4.1's neutral accuracy on AITA drops from 77.4% to 71.2% when a user-belief signal is injected; this effect persists under a fully random pairing strategy with a minimum vote gap of 10. Experiment 6.2 shows that preference-aligned models distort popularity judgments, tending to overestimate the prevalence of viewpoints aligned with common RLHF training preferences. Specific per-model and per-domain breakdowns are reported in the paper but not fully enumerated in the publicly visible review material. Baselines are current preference-aligned models (GPT-4.1 confirmed; others not specified in the review text). Scale of testing: 1.2K Reddit threads, three domains. The clustering method's superiority over GoalEx is validated by human preference (65.5% preference rate) but the absolute cluster quality metrics are not stated in the review excerpts. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW METHOD, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The set-coverage formulation applied to open-ended LLM pluralism evaluation is genuinely novel—prior work applied recall-based coverage to structured QA (e.g., Hayati et al., EMNLP 2024) but not to free-form crowd discourse. The atomic-decomposition-then-cluster pipeline is an adaptation of FActScore-style atomic claims to opinion diversity, which is an original application. The empirical finding on asymmetric sycophancy collapsing Overton pluralism is a new empirical result, not merely a replication of prior alignment-bias findings. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on clustering are present in Appendix A.4 with human evaluation (Table 10). Entailment matching is validated on 100 pairs at 80% human agreement (Appendix). Popularity delta robustness is confirmed via an additional experiment on AITA with GPT-4.1 using random pairs and minimum vote gap of 10. The paper reports statistics for the dataset (Section 5) and experimental setup (Section 6). Code is not yet released (authors plan to release upon acceptance—a reproducibility concern flagged by two reviewers). Reproducibility scores from reviewers are 3/5 and 4/5, suggesting partial but not full reproducibility. The paper is not a preprint; it is under peer review at ACL ARR 2026 January (submitted January 6, 2026; modified March 17, 2026). Area chair recommendation is acceptance to an ACL conference (score 4/5). No model weights are mentioned. Reddit data introduces platform-specific demographic skew (male-heavy), acknowledged by authors but not corrected for. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: PLURALEVAL runs on commodity hardware using LLM API calls for decomposition, clustering, and entailment matching; no specialized infrastructure is required beyond access to a capable LLM. - Efficient: Compute cost is not explicitly quantified; the pipeline involves multiple LLM calls per thread (decomposition, clustering, entailment matching for each generated response), which may be expensive at scale but is feasible for evaluation workloads. - Effective: The entailment judge achieves 80% agreement with human raters; the clustering method is preferred 65.5% over GoalEx; together these validate that the pipeline measures something real, though the link between PLURALEVAL scores and human perception of pluralism remains undemonstrated. - Scalable: The framework is tested on 1.2K threads across three domains; scaling to additional domains, languages, or larger corpora is claimed to be straightforward but not demonstrated experimentally. The work delivers a dataset (WildSCOPE) and a framework methodology; code release is pending acceptance. Strengths: - Reframing open-ended pluralism as set-coverage retrieval is a clean, operationally transparent formulation that avoids subjective rubric design and generalizes beyond the Reddit corpus used here. - The three-experiment structure progressively dissects pluralism failure modes—coverage suppression, popularity ranking distortion, and pairwise popularity misjudgment—providing a multi-faceted empirical profile rather than a single scalar score. - The entailment validation (80% human agreement) and clustering quality study (65.5% preference over GoalEx, human evaluation in Table 10) are substantive: the authors did not merely assert pipeline quality but measured it. - The asymmetric sycophancy finding—that LLMs degrade Overton pluralism specifically when user belief is revealed, not just when asked subjective questions neutrally—is a precise and actionable characterization of a known alignment failure mode. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge Reddit's demographic skew (male-heavy user base) and commit to flagging results as platform-specific. They acknowledge that direct calibration of PLURALEVAL scores against human perceptions of pluralism is difficult in practice and leave it to future work. They acknowledge that single-response evaluation (rather than k-response coverage) is a legitimate alternative framing and defer it to future extensions. Mitigation strategies appear only in an appendix and are characterized as exploratory, not primary contributions. The authors confront these limitations directly and substantially, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant unaddressed gap is the absence of pre-alignment versus post-alignment comparison on open-source models—the sycophancy finding is observed on preference-aligned models, but without a matched pre-RLHF baseline the causal attribution to alignment (versus pretraining) is inferential rather than demonstrated. The paper evaluates English-only Reddit data from three subreddits; generalization to other languages, cultures, or discourse platforms is entirely untested. The entailment judge's 20% disagreement rate is attributed to leniency (over-matching), but the authors do not report the impact of correcting for this bias on the absolute PLURALEVAL scores—only that relative model comparisons are unaffected. The k-response evaluation protocol (prompting for k opinions in one call) is a reasonable approximation of in-the-wild use but does not match the single-response deployment scenario that dominates real usage; the authors acknowledge this but do not provide even a small-scale single-response analysis. Finally, code is unavailable at submission time, limiting independent reproducibility. Bottom Line: Researchers building alignment evaluation pipelines or studying LLM opinion dynamics will gain concrete tools and a non-obvious empirical result from reading this paper; the set-coverage formulation is reusable, the dataset is enabling, and the sycophancy finding is precisely characterized enough to guide post-training interventions.
Recommended
#60 — Innovative (68)

EVIL: Evolving Interpretable Algorithms for Zero-Shot Inference on Event Sequences and Time Series with LLMs

David Berghaus · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — new method (LLM-evolutionary search for amortized inference) plus surprising empirical finding, though discovered algorithms are classical estimators. Rigor (65) reduced by absence of hand-crafted classical baseline (a major Goodhart-adjacent gap), no error bars in main tables, and dependence on non-public GPT-5 backbone; preprint penalty applied. Non-Obviousness (70) — that simple programs match GPU foundation models is non-obvious empirically. Completeness (65) honest about limitations but missing key baseline. Resilience (60) — would face tough questions about the missing classical baseline and overfitting in long runs. Impact (75) — fast CPU inference, full code disclosure, executable artifacts. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*65+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.10*75 = 67.25 ≈ 68.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EVIL, a framework that uses LLM-guided evolutionary search (AlphaEvolve-style via OpenEvolve) to automatically discover compact, readable Python/NumPy inference functions that generalize zero-shot across datasets for temporal point processes, Markov jump processes, and time series imputation. - Why it's important: Deep learning models for dynamical systems require large training sets, are opaque, and fail to generalize across output dimensions, motivating a search for interpretable alternatives that work without per-dataset retraining. - How it does it: Starting from a trivial baseline program, an LLM ensemble (80% GPT-5-mini, 20% GPT-5) proposes diff-format code modifications evaluated against a held-out subsplit, with MAP-Elites maintaining a diverse archive of strong variants across 100 iterations. - The good: EVIL (synthetic prior) achieves 87.8% accuracy on MIMIC-II — a medical event dataset with 75 mark types never seen during evolution — surpassing all neural baselines specifically trained on that dataset, at approximately 200× lower inference latency than the foundation model baseline. - The missing: The discovered algorithms are essentially rediscoveries of classical statistical estimators (transition counts, hazard rates, linear interpolation, motif search) that a domain expert could likely write by hand; no such hand-crafted classical baseline is compared against, leaving the marginal value of the evolutionary search unclear. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers interested in LLM-guided program synthesis applied to scientific inference, or anyone who suspects simple heuristics are undervalued in their domain; less essential for practitioners seeking deployable dynamical systems models. Problem & Motivation: Deep learning models for continuous-time dynamical systems — temporal point processes, Markov jump processes, time series imputation — require large curated training sets, produce opaque predictions, and generalize poorly across distribution shifts or unseen output dimensions. Foundation inference models (FIM-PP, FIM-MJP, FIM-ℓ) partially address this by synthetic pretraining, but still require millions of training instances and GPU inference. The gap is the absence of lightweight, interpretable, zero-shot inference functions that work across datasets without architectural commitment. Key Idea: The core insight is that LLM-guided evolutionary search can discover amortized inference functions — a single program that reads context observations and produces predictions for any test dataset — by treating the search as program synthesis with a fitness function defined on held-out subsets of real or synthetic data. The LLM's prior statistical knowledge seeds the search near interpretable solutions, and the short-program constraint enforces an Occam's-razor inductive bias that favors generalization. Methodology: EVIL uses OpenEvolve (open-source AlphaEvolve implementation). Each evolutionary run starts from a trivially coded baseline (e.g., predict global median inter-event gap and majority mark). At each of 100 iterations, an LLM ensemble proposes diff-format edits; the resulting program is evaluated against a held-out subsplit carved from each target dataset's training split. Solutions are stored in a MAP-Elites archive with island-based populations (archive size 20, population 50, 3 islands, exploitation ratio 0.7). Scoring functions are task-specific: accuracy − RMSE_Δt for point processes; −CE − RMSE_Q for MJPs; −mean MAE for imputation. NumPy is the only permitted library. Two variants are reported: EVIL evolved on subsplits of the target datasets, and EVIL (synthetic prior) evolved only on synthetic data to test strictest zero-shot generalization. Key design decisions: (1) diff-based evolution preserves working code structure — well-motivated by analogy to FunSearch; (2) system prompts explicitly encode domain knowledge (e.g., that MJP observations are discrete snapshots, not continuous trajectories) — justified but sensitivity to prompt choice is not ablated; (3) 80/20 GPT-5-mini/GPT-5 split balances cost and quality — the ratio is a hyperparameter but its impact is not evaluated. Key Results: For temporal point processes (N=5 long-horizon on TAXI, TAOBAO, StackOverflow, Amazon, Retweet): EVIL achieves OTD 4.05 on TAXI vs. 4.08 for finetuned FIM-PP(f) and 5.97 for CDiff, while running in 5 seconds on a single Apple M3 CPU core vs. 964 seconds for FIM-PP on an A100 GPU. EVIL(synthetic prior) achieves 91% mark accuracy on TAXI while zero-shot FIM-PP reaches only 52%, suggesting the evolutionary search found alternating-mark structure that FIM-PP's synthetic distribution misses. On unseen MIMIC-II (75 marks), EVIL achieves 87.80% accuracy vs. 85.51% for the best trained baseline (DTPP). For Markov jump processes, EVIL(synthetic prior) achieves Hellinger distances of 0.16, 0.30, 0.93 on DFR/IonCh/ADP vs. FIM-MJP's 0.27, 0.41, 1.39 — a consistent improvement despite using only 7,200 synthetic training examples vs. FIM-MJP's 45,000. For time series imputation (50% point-wise missing), EVIL achieves best or near-best MAE on 7/8 datasets including 4 held-out zero-shot targets, outperforming FIM-ℓ (MAE 0.078 vs. 0.083 on Electricity, 0.413 vs. 0.595 on Solar). For windowed imputation (Motion Capture, 20% contiguous gap), EVIL scores 2.98±2.04 vs. FIM-ℓ's 1.77±0.63 — high variance suggesting the motif retrieval strategy is unreliable for contiguous gaps. Results are on standard benchmarks from prior work; baselines are current and include both per-dataset trained neural models and zero-shot foundation models. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (LLM-guided evolutionary program synthesis applied to amortized dynamical systems inference) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (simple, automatically discovered Python functions can match or beat GPU-trained foundation models on these tasks). The evolved algorithms themselves are standard statistical estimators (transition frequency tables, hazard rate estimation with smoothing, linear interpolation, nearest-neighbor motif retrieval) — none is algorithmically novel. The novelty is demonstrating that evolutionary search can find them automatically and that they generalize across datasets. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover: dataset-specific vs. multi-dataset evolution (Table 12), variance across 5 independent evolution seeds (Table 13, moderate but acceptable variability), and a long 800-iteration run showing diminishing returns (Table 14). Error bars are absent from the main tables for EVIL (the paper self-reports [No] on statistical significance in the NeurIPS checklist), though baselines carry standard deviations. The evolved programs are fully disclosed in Appendix F with pseudocode and Python source. Code is available at an anonymous repository. The paper explicitly carves evaluation subsplits from training splits so EVIL uses strictly less data than baselines — a methodological credibility positive. A concern: the evaluation uses subsets of target dataset training splits to guide evolution, meaning EVIL is not fully independent of target data distribution (the strictly zero-shot variant is EVIL(synthetic prior), which the paper appropriately distinguishes). The LLM backbone is GPT-5/GPT-5-mini as of August 2025 — not publicly accessible models, limiting exact reproducibility. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Yes — inference runs on a single CPU core in seconds; discovery requires only commodity hardware and LLM API access for ~$1. - Efficient: Dramatically cheaper than all baselines: no GPU for training or inference, no large dataset curation, 15–37 minutes of wall-clock search per task. - Effective: Strong on point-wise missing patterns and structured dynamical systems tasks; notably weaker on contiguous window imputation (Motion Capture MAE 2.98±2.04 vs. FIM-ℓ 1.77±0.63), suggesting the motif strategy fails when long unobserved windows prevent finding matching context in history. - Scalable: Not tested beyond state spaces of 2–6 for MJP, 22 marks during evolution (75 in transfer test), or time series up to 862 channels. No claim of algorithmic scalability is made, and the paper is honest that the discovered heuristics may fail where rich learned representations are needed. The paper delivers executable Python code artifacts; no model weights or trained checkpoints are released. Strengths: - The MIMIC-II zero-shot generalization to 75 marks (vs. 22 max during evolution) outperforming models specifically trained on that dataset is genuinely striking and constitutes the strongest empirical evidence for the approach. - MJP results consistently beat FIM-MJP (pretrained on 6.4× more data) on Hellinger distance across DFR, IonCh, and ADP, including on the physically meaningful entropy production derived quantity. - The speed advantage (5s CPU vs. 964s A100 GPU, ~200×) is not just marginal — it changes what is feasible in interactive or resource-constrained settings. - Full algorithm disclosure (pseudocode + Python source) enables independent analysis and manual modification, unlike most deep learning baselines. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state: (1) weaker performance on windowed imputation tasks requiring rich learned representations; (2) difficulty assessing novelty of discovered algorithms since they combine known techniques in ways that may already be known to domain experts; (3) discovered algorithms are deterministic and lack uncertainty quantification; (4) open question whether LLM evolution can discover stochastic inference procedures. The paper confronts these honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most important unaddressed gap is the absence of a hand-crafted classical estimator baseline. The discovered algorithms (smoothed transition counts, hazard rate estimation, motif retrieval) are standard undergraduate-level statistics; a knowledgeable practitioner could likely write an equivalent function in an afternoon. Without this comparison, the paper cannot distinguish between "evolutionary search finds interpretable algorithms" and "interpretable algorithms already work well and we found them by other means." Additionally: the evolutionary search is sensitive to the LLM (GPT-5/GPT-5-mini not publicly available, limiting independent replication); the long-run ablation (800 iterations) yielding no test improvement despite continued validation gains suggests the search is overfitting to the small validation subsplit; and for TPP, EVIL underperforms on TAOBAO at N=20 (OTD 52.4 vs. CDiff 44.6), a failure mode not fully analyzed. Bottom Line: Practitioners in dynamical systems inference who suspect their domain's deep learning models are overengineered relative to their data will gain actionable perspective from this work — the empirical evidence that automatically discovered statistical heuristics can match or beat GPU-trained foundation models at 200× lower cost is surprising enough to be worth knowing about, even if the discovered programs are unsurprising in retrospect.
Search Results 2604.15787 PDF
#61 — Innovative (68)

Extending One-Step Image Generation from Class Labels to Text via Discriminative Text Representation (17 ▲ )

Chenxi Zhao, Chen Zhu, Xiaokun Feng, Aiming Hao, Jiashu Zhu, Jiachen Lei, Jiahong Wu, Xiangxiang Chu, Jufeng Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) is the strongest dimension — the diagnosis that text-encoder cross-modal discriminability and subsequence disentanglement determine MeanFlow compatibility is a genuinely surprising causal finding, and the SANA-1.5 counter-experiment cleanly falsifies the domain-mismatch alternative. Novelty (70) for the diagnostic framework plus first text-conditioned MeanFlow. Rigor (60) — strong key ablation, but missing ablations on adaptive timestep schedule and duplicated head, no error bars, single backbone, preprint. Completeness (60) and Resilience (60) limited by single-backbone scope. Impact (70) — code released, real 5.6× sampling speedup. Weighted ≈ 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EMF (Extending MeanFlow to T2I), the first text-conditioned adaptation of the MeanFlow one-step flow-map generative framework, achieving GenEval 0.90 in 4 sampling steps by selecting a text encoder with provably strong cross-modal discriminability. - Why it's important: Existing few-step and consistency-distilled T2I models either require teacher models or degrade sharply at ≤4 steps; cutting inference from 30 to 1–4 steps (0.08–0.22 s vs. 1.24 s on H200) without distillation overhead has direct production impact. - How it does it: The core move is diagnosing that MeanFlow's average-velocity learning objective demands text representations with high cross-modal discriminability and semantic disentanglement, then selecting BLIP3o-NEXT's LLM-based encoder that satisfies both, paired with a duplicated temporal-embedding head for bidirectional time conditioning and adaptive logit-normal timestep sampling. - The good: EMF at 4 steps reaches GenEval 0.90 vs. BLIP3o-NEXT's 30-step score of 0.91 and outperforms every distilled baseline including rCM-14B (0.83) and SANA-Sprint-1.6B (0.75) at equivalent step count, while a 1-step EMF (0.74) handily beats BLIP3o-NEXT's own 1-step score (0.46). - The missing: All experiments are conducted on a single 3B backbone (BLIP3o-NEXT); it is untested whether the discriminability criterion generalizes across architectures or whether the finding is specific to this encoder–backbone pairing. - Read it or not: Worthwhile for researchers in few-step generation and T2I acceleration — the diagnostic framework (retrieval-based discriminability, subsequence disentanglement) offers practical selection criteria; skip if you need results at production scale or across diverse backbones. Problem & Motivation Diffusion and flow-matching models require 20–50 denoising steps for high-quality text-to-image synthesis, making real-time deployment costly. MeanFlow, a principled flow-map method that learns average velocity over a time interval rather than instantaneous velocity, achieves one-step generation quality competitive with multi-step models — but only for class-label conditioning on ImageNet. Extending MeanFlow to open-domain text conditioning is the natural next step, yet the paper shows this is non-trivial: naively plugging LLM-based text encoders (the standard for modern T2I) into MeanFlow training collapses to poor performance (GenEval 0.50 at 4 steps for SANA-1.5 + MeanFlow). The gap in understanding is why some text representations are compatible with MeanFlow's one-step regime and others are not, and how to select or design encoders that are. Key Idea Text encoders whose representations have high cross-modal discriminability (well-aligned with image embeddings, enabling clean semantic separation) and semantic disentanglement (subsequence embeddings remain close to the full-prompt embedding) produce smoother denoising trajectories where instantaneous velocity stays close to average velocity. This alignment is exactly what MeanFlow requires to learn average velocity accurately in limited steps. Class labels succeed in MeanFlow because their embeddings are sparse and well-separated; text encoders trained only on language corpora (T5, SANA-1.5's Gemma) fail because their text embeddings occupy dense neighborhoods that force tortuous denoising paths, making one-step average velocity prediction intractable. Methodology The pipeline has three components. First, encoder selection: four text encoders (BLIP3o-NEXT, SANA-1.5/Gemma, CLIP-ViT-L/14, T5-v1.1-XXL) are scored on two proxy tasks. Discriminability is measured by image–text retrieval on COCO-2017 train (118k pairs), with mean-pooled text embeddings ranked by cosine similarity against DINOv3-encoded image embeddings; BLIP3o-NEXT scores 0.734, T5 scores 0.634. Disentanglement is measured by cosine similarity between full-prompt and random-subsequence embeddings on DPG-Bench prompts; BLIP3o-NEXT scores 0.999, T5 scores 0.893. Second, architecture adaptation: the pretrained BLIP3o-NEXT flow-matching backbone's single temporal embedding layer is duplicated into two separate heads — φ_interval(t−r) encoding the interval length, and φ_end(t) encoding the segment end time — which are summed to yield the MeanFlow bidirectional time conditioning. Third, training: the standard MeanFlow objective (L_MF = E‖u_θ − sg(ũ)‖²) is optimized with JVP-computed target velocities, using ~170k SFT samples (BLIP3o-60k, ShareGPT-4o, Echo-4o), lr=1e-5, batch=128, 150 epochs. Timestep pairs (t, r) are adaptively sampled from a schedule interpolating between uniform U(0,1) and logit-normal distributions, with the ratio of t≠r pairs increasing over training. Three design decisions: (1) encoder selection via proxy metrics rather than end-to-end training is justified by failure analysis of SANA-1.5 (discriminability score 0.713 vs. 0.734 for BLIP3o-NEXT, MeanFlow GenEval 0.50); (2) the duplicated embedding head is low-cost and avoids architectural surgery; (3) adaptive timestep scheduling is motivated by MeanFlow's need for exposure to both short- and long-range velocity segments, though no ablation isolates its contribution. Key Results Evaluation on GenEval (attribute-focused T2I alignment) and DPG-Bench (long-form instruction following), tested against pretrained, unified, and distilled model categories. EMF (3B params) at 4 steps achieves GenEval 0.90, matching BLIP3o-NEXT at 30 steps (0.91) and exceeding all distilled models: rCM-14B at 4 steps (0.83), SANA-Sprint-1.6B (0.75), FLUX.1-schnell-12B (0.69). At 1 step, EMF reaches 0.74 vs. BLIP3o-NEXT's 0.46 — a +28-point gain. On DPG-Bench, EMF at 4 steps scores 81.20 overall vs. BLIP3o-NEXT at 4 steps (78.15) and at 30 steps (82.05); 8-step EMF reaches 81.94, effectively matching the 30-step baseline. On HPS-v2.1, EMF 1-step yields 25.77 average vs. BLIP3o-NEXT 1-step 18.54 (+7.23). Inference timing on H200: EMF 4-step takes 0.22 s vs. BLIP3o-NEXT 30-step 1.24 s (5.6× speedup, diffusion sampling only); end-to-end latency difference is narrower (9.87 s vs. 11.3 s) because autoregressive text encoding dominates. The failure of SANA-1.5 + MeanFlow even after encoder SFT fine-tuning (GenEval 0.47 at 4 steps, vs. 0.50 without SFT) is a key counter-experiment demonstrating that discriminability, not domain, determines compatibility. User study with 20 raters on 50 prompts: EMF 0.49 preference vs. SANA-Sprint 0.16, FLUX.1-schnell 0.12. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The MeanFlow objective and architecture are prior work; the novel contribution is (1) identifying that cross-modal discriminability and semantic disentanglement of text representations are necessary conditions for MeanFlow-compatible T2I generation, and (2) operationalizing this insight via encoder selection to achieve the first working text-conditioned MeanFlow model. The architectural modifications (duplicated temporal head, adaptive timestep sampling) are incremental engineering. The empirical finding — that text encoder quality determines MeanFlow feasibility more than backbone scale or training domain — is genuinely non-obvious and appears unreported in prior work. Rigor & Reproducibility The key ablation comparing SANA-1.5 with and without encoder SFT under MeanFlow training is well-designed and directly falsifies the domain-mismatch alternative hypothesis. The OpenUni experiment (supplementary) partially generalizes the finding. However, no ablation isolates the adaptive timestep sampling schedule; no ablation tests the importance of the duplicated temporal embedding vs. a single head; and no error bars or multiple seeds are reported for any metric. The discriminability proxy (mean-pooled cosine retrieval against DINOv3) is a reasonable stand-in but is not standard in the T2I community. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/EMF. No pretrained model weights are mentioned as released. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.18168v1, April 2026). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Trains on a single H200 with 170k samples at batch size 128; inference runs at 0.08–0.22 s per image on H200, placing it within reach of commercial deployment on a single high-end GPU. - Efficient: 4-step EMF is 5.6× faster than 30-step BLIP3o-NEXT in diffusion sampling time; end-to-end savings are smaller (~13%) because the autoregressive text encoder dominates, a bottleneck the paper acknowledges but does not address. - Effective: GenEval and DPG-Bench evidence is convincing that the method closes the gap to its own 30-step teacher; the user study (20 raters, 50 prompts) corroborates instruction-following gains, but both evaluations are narrowly scoped to a single backbone. - Scalable: Tested only at 3B parameters and 170k training samples; no experiments at larger model scale or larger training sets; scalability is claimed but not measured. The work delivers a software artifact (code released), though pretrained model weights are not confirmed to be public. Strengths - The diagnostic framework (discriminability via cross-modal retrieval, disentanglement via subsequence similarity) is concrete, cheap to compute, and provides a principled criterion for encoder selection — directly actionable for future MeanFlow T2I work. - The SANA-1.5 counter-experiment (encoder SFT + MeanFlow still fails at 4 steps) rigorously rules out domain mismatch as the explanation and pinpoints representation quality as the causal factor. - EMF's 4-step GenEval (0.90) surpasses all distilled baselines including a 14B-parameter model (rCM), achieving parameter efficiency alongside step efficiency. - The scalability-beyond-two-steps result (monotone improvement from 1- to 8-step sampling) is a practical advantage over consistency-distilled models, which plateau or regress at higher step counts. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note convergence speed depends on the domain of training data (SANA-1.5 fails partly due to domain mismatch, though this is not the primary cause). They acknowledge that end-to-end latency gains are limited by autoregressive text encoding. They do not overstate the 1-step result, reporting 0.74 GenEval and noting further training improves it. These are presented directly and not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-backbone evaluation: all experiments use BLIP3o-NEXT as the diffusion backbone; it is unknown whether EMF's approach transfers to FLUX, SD3, or other architectures with different text-conditioning mechanisms. - The discriminability metric uses mean pooling over sequence positions, discarding token-level structure; whether this correlates with MeanFlow training stability better than other representation metrics (e.g., linear probe accuracy, mutual information) is not validated. - No ablation on adaptive timestep sampling: the schedule (uniform → logit-normal interpolation with increasing t≠r ratio) is described but never isolated — it may contribute substantially or trivially. - The 170k training samples is tiny relative to the original BLIP3o-NEXT training data; the paper does not quantify how performance scales with more data, leaving it unclear whether the 1-step gap (0.74 vs. 0.91) is a fundamental MeanFlow limitation or a data-scale artifact. - The retrieval-based discriminability score differences are narrow (BLIP3o-NEXT 0.734 vs. CLIP 0.730); the large performance gap between these two encoders on MeanFlow training suggests the metric may not cleanly predict compatibility, or that threshold effects dominate. Bottom Line EMF makes a credible and practically useful contribution — the first working text-conditioned MeanFlow system with strong benchmark results — but remains a single-backbone study on a small training set, and practitioners working with other architectures will need to re-validate the discriminability hypothesis before applying this approach.
Recommended 2604.18168 PDF
#62 — Innovative (68)

GenRec: A Preference-Oriented Generative Framework for Large-Scale Recommendation

Yanyan Zou, Junbo Qi, Lunsong Huang, Yu Li, Kewei Xu, Jiabao Gao, Binglei Zhao, Xuanhua Yang, Sulong Xu, Shengjie Li · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (80) drives the score — a month-long 10% production A/B at JD with +9.5% clicks and +8.7% transactions at p<0.05 on a business-critical metric is unusually strong real-world evidence. Rigor (70) reflects credible offline ablations and a strong same-backbone LC-Rec comparison, partially offset by no component-level online A/B and missing latency numbers despite latency being a stated motivation. Novelty (60) — page-wise NTP genuinely addresses an underexamined industrial problem; Token Merger and GRPO-SR are principled but more incremental. Resilience (65) — the relevance-gate ablation cleanly demonstrates the reward-hacking failure mode the design fixes. Completeness (60) suffers from undisclosed SIM reward model and the unjustified inclusion of exposed items in SFT targets. Weighted (systems): 0.15*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*80 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 60 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GenRec, a generative retrieval recommendation system deploying three coordinated industrial solutions — page-wise NTP training, an asymmetric linear Token Merger, and GRPO-SR reinforcement learning alignment — on JD.com's production homepage feed. - Why it's important: Generative retrieval remains largely undeployed at industrial scale because pagination creates one-to-many label ambiguity, multi-token semantic IDs inflate sequence length and latency, and naive RL alignment produces syntactically valid but semantically irrelevant outputs (reward hacking). - How it does it: Training and inference use deliberately asymmetric protocols: page-wise supervision aggregates all page interactions into a single target during SFT while beam search over single items at inference; a linear projection compresses 3-token SID triplets into 1 vector during prefilling only; GRPO-SR pairs group-relative policy optimization with an NLL regularizer and a hybrid reward that gates on relevance and anchors known-positive rollouts to the group-maximum reward. - The good: A month-long production A/B test (10% traffic, p<0.05 two-sided) shows +9.5% click count and +8.7% transaction count, with long-tail items gaining +16% clicks and +13% transactions — real-world evidence that survives the gap between offline metrics and business outcomes. - The missing: No component-level online A/B isolates how much of the +9.5% lift each of the three contributions provides; the SIM-based reward model is proprietary and underdescribed, making GRPO-SR unreproducible outside JD. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners deploying generative retrieval in industrial recommender systems; academic researchers working purely on public benchmarks will find the key ideas (page-wise NTP, asymmetric compression) transferable but cannot reproduce the full pipeline. Problem & Motivation: Large-scale e-commerce recommendation systems use a retrieve-and-rank pipeline where generative retrieval — treating item recommendation as next-token prediction over discrete semantic IDs — offers end-to-end optimization but has not been shown to work reliably at industrial scale. Three specific gaps motivate this work. First, JD's pagination mechanism means a single user history H can legitimately generate K different positive items within one page; vanilla point-wise NTP trains on each (H, item_k) pair independently, creating a one-to-many ambiguity that inflates gradient variance and dilutes per-item probability mass. Second, encoding user history with 3-token SID representations triples effective sequence length, causing latency violations in online serving. Third, applying GRPO-style RL alignment to recommendation causes reward hacking: the model generates syntactically valid SID tuples that score well on the learned preference model but correspond to irrelevant items. Key Idea: The central insight is that training and inference need not follow the same supervision protocol — page-wise list generation as the training target and point-wise beam search as the inference protocol is a deliberate, productive asymmetry. This same asymmetry is exploited structurally: the Token Merger compresses SIDs during prefilling (lowering latency) while decoding retains full-resolution SID tokens (preserving retrieval precision). The page-wise target also doubles as a source of dense in-context positive labels for the GRPO-SR reward calibration, creating a self-consistent data flow across all three components. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. In representation, items are encoded multimodally by Qwen2.5-VL, fine-tuned with domain-specific collaborative pairs, then discretized by RQ K-means into 3-token semantic ID tuples. In supervised fine-tuning, the target sequence concatenates ordered (O), clicked (C), and exposed (E) items from the interaction page in descending interaction intensity: Y_page = [SID(v) : v ∈ O ∪ C ∪ E]. The SFT loss is standard autoregressive cross-entropy over the full target sequence. The asymmetric Token Merger projects Concat(e(s¹), e(s²), e(s³)) via a single linear layer to a unified latent vector during prefilling only; the decoding side remains unmerged. In RL alignment, GRPO-SR samples G rollouts per query in point-wise mode (matching inference). Each rollout's reward is computed as: r_i = G_i · r^pref_i, where G_i = I(s_i > τ) is a relevance gate suppressing hallucinated SIDs, and r^pref_i ∈ [0,1] is a SIM-based dense preference score. Known positives (O ∪ C) in the rollout group are anchored to the group maximum reward to correct the preference model's estimation bias. The composite GRPO-SR loss adds an NLL regularization term over known-positive trajectories, weighted by α, as an explicit anchor to real user behavior rather than KL divergence against a frozen reference model. Critical design decisions: (1) using exposed items E in the SFT target alongside clicked and ordered items introduces label noise from non-engaged items — not discussed by the authors; (2) the one-step importance sampling π_θ/sg(π_θ) within GRPO is motivated for stability but collapses the ratio to 1.0 in expectation, making it functionally equivalent to a vanilla policy gradient with advantage normalization — the paper does not address this; (3) anchoring positives to group maximum rather than a fixed value 1.0 is a sensible calibration choice, well-justified by the preference model's under-estimation bias. Key Results: Offline evaluation uses ~560 million JD user interaction sequences; the last day is held out for testing. GenRec (Qwen2.5 3B backbone) achieves HR@1=0.1189, HR@10=0.4456, NDCG@10=0.2635, HR@50=0.7192 against LC-Rec's HR@1=0.0947, HR@10=0.3669, NDCG@10=0.2146, HR@50=0.6226 — a ~25% relative lift on HR@1. Hallucination rate drops from TIGER's 15.46% and LC-Rec's 7.80% to GenRec's 4.96%, further reduced to 2.68% by GRPO-SR. Removing the Token Merger (w/o TM) produces nearly identical offline scores (HR@10=0.4467 vs 0.4456), confirming the merger's cost is negligible. Scaling from 1.5B to 3B yields meaningful gains (HR@1: 0.1077→0.1189); 3B to 7B is marginal (0.1189→0.1221) despite 2.3× more parameters, attributed to the 3B architecture being deeper (36 layers) versus the 7B being wider (28 layers, 3584 hidden). RL alignment improves Reward@1 by +18.01% relative over SFT; removing the relevance gate G causes HR@50 to collapse (0.7438→0.6975) while HaR drops (2.68%→1.96%), a textbook reward hacking signature. Online A/B (one month, 10% traffic): SFT model achieves +8.5% clicks, +7.3% transactions; +GRPO-SR reaches +9.5% clicks, +8.7% transactions (p<0.05). Baselines BERT4Rec and SASRec are present but use a different architecture; LC-Rec is reproduced with the same Qwen2.5 backbone making it the most directly comparable baseline. TIGER is not reproduced with Qwen2.5, weakening that comparison. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM, NEW METHOD (partial). The Page-wise NTP formulation is a genuine novel contribution for industrial generative retrieval — the pagination-driven one-to-many ambiguity is a real and previously unaddressed industrial problem. The asymmetric Token Merger is principled engineering with marginal scientific novelty. GRPO-SR adapts GRPO (from DeepSeekMath) to recommendation with meaningful domain-specific modifications (relevance gating, positive-item reward anchoring, NLL regularization replacing KL). The overall framework is an integration of well-established components (SIDs from TIGER/OneRec, GRPO from DeepSeekMath, SIM reward model from prior JD work) applied to a new industrial deployment context with three targeted modifications. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: Table 1 isolates the Token Merger; Table 3 compares GRPO vs. GRPO-SR and ablates the gating mechanism G across four configurations. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported in offline tables; online A/B uses a two-sided significance test at p<0.05. The dataset is proprietary (JD.com) and will not be released; no code release is mentioned. The SIM-based preference model is cited as reference [14] but the specific instantiation used in GRPO-SR is not described in sufficient detail for external reproduction. The evaluation dataset is sampled from the same production platform as the deployment environment (no held-out domain), which is appropriate for industrial systems work but limits generalizability claims. TIGER is compared without re-implementing on the same backbone, making that gap partially attributable to architecture differences. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built and deployed production recommendation system. - Practical: Trains on 8×H100 GPUs; fully deployed in JD production — commercially available hardware at significant but realistic scale for a major e-commerce company. - Efficient: Token Merger halves prefilling sequence length with negligible accuracy cost; specific online latency numbers (P99, QPS) are not reported despite latency being a stated primary motivation. - Effective: Month-long A/B test on real user traffic with business-critical metrics (clicks, transactions) at p<0.05 is strong evidence of real-world effectiveness, not proxy-metric effectiveness. - Scalable: The system serves JD App production traffic (scale not quantified in user count), demonstrating current industrial-scale operation; the paper does not test or claim behavior at 10×–100× current scale. The work delivers a deployed system but no public software artifact, dataset, or pretrained weights. Strengths: - The page-wise NTP formulation is theoretically grounded: the paper correctly identifies the one-to-many ambiguity as a cardinality mismatch between session-level signals and point-wise training pairs, and the fix is clean, motivated, and effective (>50% hallucination reduction). - Production A/B results spanning one month with statistical significance on transaction count — a harder and more business-meaningful metric than clicks — provide unusually strong evidence for an IR paper. - The relevance gating mechanism G in GRPO-SR is a simple but effective solution to a well-documented industrial failure mode; the ablation in Table 3 clearly demonstrates the reward hacking signature when G is removed. - The observation that the deeper-narrower 3B model outperforms the wider 7B model in this domain is an actionable empirical finding that challenges the assumption that wider models uniformly dominate in recommendation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not enumerate explicit limitations. The only forward-looking statement is that "reasoning ability" of the framework will be investigated in future work. There is no acknowledgment of the proprietary dataset dependency, the lack of latency/throughput numbers, the inability to isolate component-level A/B contributions, or the generalizability constraints from JD-specific reward infrastructure. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No component-level online A/B: the paper cannot attribute what fraction of the +9.5% click lift comes from page-wise NTP, token merger, or GRPO-SR individually, which limits scientific interpretability. - Including exposed (non-interacted) items in the SFT target (Y_page = O ∪ C ∪ E) is unexplained — supervising the model to generate items the user ignored is potentially noisy, and no ablation distinguishes E's contribution. - Online latency benchmarks are absent; since the stated motivation for Token Merger is inference latency, this is a missing validation of the central efficiency claim. - The one-step importance sampling π_θ/sg(π_θ) ratio is mechanically 1.0 for the current policy, so the GRPO-SR update reduces to a standard policy gradient weighted by normalized advantage — the paper does not acknowledge or justify this. - The SIM reward model is proprietary and central to GRPO-SR; without disclosure, the RL results cannot be reproduced and the approach is limited to organizations with access to equivalent infrastructure. - No comparison against non-generative production-scale baselines (e.g., HSTU) in the online A/B, so the gain attributed to "generative retrieval vs. traditional pipeline" conflates architecture with training recipe improvements. Bottom Line: Practitioners at large e-commerce companies building generative retrieval systems will gain concrete, tested solutions to three real deployment blockers; academic researchers will find the page-wise NTP formulation principled and transferable, but the full system is not reproducible without JD's proprietary infrastructure.
Search Results 2604.14878 PDF
#63 — Innovative (68)

LLaDA2.0-Uni: Unifying Multimodal Understanding and Generation with Diffusion Large Language Model (208 ▲ )

Inclusion AI, Tiwei Bie, Haoxing Chen, Tieyuan Chen, Zhenglin Cheng, Long Cui, Kai Gan, Zhicheng Huang, Zhenzhong Lan, Haoquan Li, Jianguo Li, Tao Lin, Qi Qin, Hongjun Wang, Xiaomei Wang, Haoyuan Wu, Yi Xin, Junbo Zhao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 23 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) is strong — the SigLIP-VQ semantics-first tokenizer cleanly resolves the reconstruction-vs-understanding tension that crippled prior dLLM unified models, and SPRINT is a genuinely new training-free inference optimization. Non-Obviousness (70) is high because the insight that VQ tokenizers should be trained for semantics rather than reconstruction is not what most practitioners would try by default. Rigor (65) is helped by public weights/code, breadth across 21 understanding and 10+ generation benchmarks, and two well-designed ablations (SPRINT, decoder distillation), but pulled down by the absence of any ablation isolating SigLIP-VQ from block-wise attention or MoE scale, no error bars, and a preprint with InterGen judge/data circularity. Completeness (65) and Resilience (65) reflect honestly acknowledged tokenizer detail loss and editing gaps, though training compute and component attribution remain unreported. Impact (75) is strong — public weights, distilled 8-step decoder (32.95s→2.90s), and 1.6× SPRINT speedup deliver a directly usable artifact. Weighted composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 ≈ 68.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LLaDA2.0-Uni, a 16B MoE discrete diffusion large language model that unifies multimodal understanding, image generation, image editing, and interleaved generation under a single block-level masked diffusion objective by replacing reconstruction-based VQ tokenizers with a semantics-first SigLIP-VQ tokenizer. - Why it's important: Existing unified diffusion models lag specialized VLMs in understanding because their reconstruction-trained VQ tokenizers discard semantic information; closing this gap without reverting to separate heterogeneous encoders would simplify deployment and enable interleaved reasoning that neither pure-understanding nor pure-generation models support. - How it does it: By training a VQ tokenizer on semantic understanding tasks (via a SigLIP2-g ViT backbone) rather than pixel reconstruction, both text and image tokens share a semantically aligned discrete space optimized under one BDLM objective; a separate distilled 6B diffusion decoder then reconstructs the discrete tokens into high-fidelity pixels. - The good: LLaDA2.0-Uni achieves GenEval 0.89 and DPG-Bench 87.76 — best among all unified models — while matching Qwen2.5-VL-7B on MMStar (64.1 vs. 63.9) and setting a new best for multi-reference editing on MICo-Bench (47.1 vs. next-best 35.9), all in one released model. - The missing: No component-level ablation isolates the contribution of SigLIP-VQ versus block-wise attention versus MoE scale; comparisons to prior diffusion-based unified models conflate multiple simultaneous design changes. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on unified multimodal architectures or discrete diffusion models — the SigLIP-VQ design pattern and SPRINT inference optimization are practically useful and the weights are public. Problem & Motivation: Building a single model that handles both visual understanding (VQA, document reasoning, chart analysis) and visual generation (text-to-image, editing, interleaved sequences) is a long-standing goal. Existing unified diffusion approaches (MMaDA, Lumina-DiMOO) use reconstruction-trained VQ-VAE tokenizers that discard semantic structure, causing dramatic failures on OCR and chart tasks (Lumina-DiMOO scores 7.2 on DocVQA vs. 94.9 for a specialized model). Hybrid AR+diffusion approaches (BAGEL, LLaDA-o) decouple visual encoding into separate ViT and VAE modules, introducing a modeling gap and divergent objectives. Neither approach achieves a single unified training objective over a shared discrete token space. Key Idea: Train the visual tokenizer on semantic understanding objectives rather than pixel-level reconstruction. The SigLIP-VQ tokenizer uses a pre-trained SigLIP2-g ViT to produce features that are already semantically aligned with the language backbone, then vector-quantizes them into a codebook of 16,384 tokens. Since these tokens live in the same semantic space as text tokens, the dLLM backbone can apply the identical block-level masked diffusion loss to both modalities with no architectural distinction between them. A dedicated diffusion decoder, conditioned on these semantic tokens rather than text prompts, handles pixel reconstruction as a decoupled post-processing step. Methodology: The system has three components trained in cascade. (1) SigLIP-VQ tokenizer: SigLIP2-g ViT encodes images; a vector quantizer with codebook vocabulary 16,384 and dimension 2,048 produces discrete tokens aligned to Qwen2.5 LLM representations; dynamic resolution is handled via flattened 1D sequences prefixed by special `<height>` and `<width>` tokens rather than 2D RoPE. (2) 16B MoE dLLM backbone (LLaDA2.0-mini): vocabulary is extended with SigLIP-VQ codebook tokens; language embeddings are frozen at init while visual token embeddings are randomly initialized; block-wise (not full bidirectional) attention is used because SigLIP-VQ tokens inherit autoregressive bias from their SigLIP2 pretraining, and unconstrained bidirectional attention degrades performance; the BDLM loss masks at the block level with diffusion-derived time weighting; auxiliary-loss-free MoE load balancing with RMSNorm-style bias updates stabilizes expert routing. (3) Diffusion decoder: built on Z-Image-Base (6B), conditioned on semantic visual tokens (not text), performs 2× super-resolution; distilled from 50-step CFG sampling to 8-step CFG-free inference via a consistency-based distillation combining flow matching with a Jacobian-vector product consistency term, cutting generation from 32.95s to 2.90s/img at 1024×1024. Training proceeds over three stages totaling 390B tokens (S0: 100B alignment, S1: 210B multi-task pretraining, S2: 80B SFT). SFT introduces mask token reweighting (gradient scaled by inverse square root of masked token count) to balance variable-length responses, and complementary masking for data efficiency. SPRINT inference optimization combines modality-aware sparse prefix KV-cache retention (keep-ratio r=1.0 for text, r=0.8 for image tokens) with confidence-adaptive non-uniform token unmasking (threshold τ∈{0.93,0.95}) for 1.6× backbone speedup at −0.6 average score. Critical design decisions: block-wise attention is well-justified by prior empirical findings and by the AR bias in SigLIP-VQ tokens; the semantic tokenizer choice is well-motivated but lacks its own ablation within this paper; the 1D vs. 2D RoPE choice is justified by citing prior work showing comparable effectiveness. Key Results: On multimodal understanding (21 benchmarks), LLaDA2.0-Uni substantially closes the gap to specialized VLMs: MMStar 64.1 (Qwen2.5-VL-7B: 63.9; LLaDA-o: 58.0), MMMU 50.1 (Qwen2.5-VL-7B: 51.3), MathVista 68.1 (Qwen2.5-VL-7B: 68.2), DocVQA 89.5 (Qwen2.5-VL-7B: 94.9; Lumina-DiMOO: 7.2). On generation, GenEval 0.89 (best unified, outperforms BAGEL 0.82 and LLaDA-o 0.86; highest position score 0.90 across all models); DPG-Bench 87.76 (best unified, beats LLaDA-o 87.04); UniGenBench 79.63 (best unified, beats gen-only FLUX.1 61.30 and approaches Seedream 3.0 78.41); WISE reasoning-informed generation 0.68 (best unified, 0.78 with thinking mode). On editing, ImgEdit best unified (3.92 vs. InternVL-U 3.67); MICo-Bench multi-reference best overall (47.1 vs. Qwen-Image-Edit 35.9). Text rendering CVTG-2K: 0.765 (best unified but lags gen-only LongCat 0.865). SPRINT ablation (Table 13, 9 benchmarks): average −0.6 score, +1.6× TPS (24.3→39.8), with DocVQA reaching 3.5× speedup. Decoder distillation (Table 14): GenEval 0.87 vs. 0.89 baseline at 11.4× speedup. Baselines are current and strong, including BAGEL, InternVL-U, and generation-only FLUX.1, Qwen-Image, Seedream 3.0. This is a preprint; no peer review. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The SigLIP-VQ design — using a semantics-trained VQ tokenizer to produce a unified discrete token space for both understanding and generation in a dLLM — is a genuine architectural contribution beyond prior reconstruction-VQ unified diffusion models. SPRINT is a new training-free inference optimization. The overall pipeline assembles existing components (SigLIP2, block diffusion, MoE, flow matching distillation) in a configuration not previously demonstrated at this performance level. Rigor & Reproducibility: Weights and code are publicly released (GitHub: inclusionAI/LLaDA2.0-Uni, HuggingFace: inclusionAI/LLaDA2.0-Uni). Two ablations are reported: SPRINT (Table 13, 9 benchmarks, well-designed) and decoder distillation (Table 14, 5 benchmarks). No ablation isolates individual architectural contributions (SigLIP-VQ vs. reconstruction-VQ, block-wise vs. full attention) — the gains over MMaDA/Lumina-DiMOO bundle multiple simultaneous changes. No error bars or multiple seeds reported. The InterGen benchmark has 150 samples evaluated by VLMs including Qwen3-VL, which was also used in training data curation — a potential circularity. Many benchmarks cited are themselves preprints. Training data volume (60M SFT samples, 140M generation images, 6M interleaved clips) is described at the category level but data licenses and filtering reproducibility are not fully audited. Deployability: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Requires significant multi-GPU resources for the 16B MoE backbone plus 6B decoder; not consumer-deployable in standard form, but the distilled 8-step decoder makes generation latency practical at 2.90s/img on a single GPU. - Efficient: SPRINT reduces backbone cost 1.6×; decoder distillation reduces per-image generation from 32.95s to 2.90s; total pipeline throughput for a production deployment is not characterized. - Effective: Across 21 understanding and 10+ generation/editing benchmarks, the evidence firmly supports the claim of competitive performance across both task families; the model demonstrably does not collapse on either modality as prior dLLM unified models did. - Scalable: Trained at 16B MoE scale on 390B tokens; no experiments at larger or smaller scale; scalability to 100B+ parameters is claimed as future work but not demonstrated. Delivers: trained model weights, inference code, and the SPRINT optimization; stops short of a production-ready serving stack (SGLang integration is noted as forthcoming). Strengths: - The semantic VQ tokenizer design is conceptually principled: separating semantic representation (SigLIP-VQ, trained for understanding) from visual reconstruction (diffusion decoder, trained for quality) cleanly resolves the fundamental tension that caused prior dLLM unified models to fail on understanding tasks. - The breadth of evaluation — 21 understanding benchmarks spanning VQA, math, OCR, document, and chart tasks, plus 6+ generation and editing benchmarks — makes the performance claims unusually well-substantiated for a preprint in this space. - SPRINT provides a practically motivated and quantitatively validated inference optimization with an interpretable design (modality-aware cache pruning + confidence-adaptive unmasking), available training-free. - MICo-Bench multi-reference editing (47.1 vs. 35.9 next-best) demonstrates a genuinely emergent capability: the unified discrete token space naturally supports multi-image composition without task-specific engineering. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The SigLIP-VQ tokenizer loses fine-grained image details, which hurts detail-sensitive editing tasks. Interleaved generation and reasoning capabilities require further scaling of training data and model capacity. RL for unified dLLMs has been explored but is not yet released. The authors confront these limitations directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) No within-paper ablation isolates the SigLIP-VQ contribution from block-wise attention, MoE routing, or training data scale — comparisons to prior dLLM unified models change all of these simultaneously, making attribution impossible. (2) The InterGen interleaved generation benchmark is 150 samples with one open-source baseline (Emu3.5); the Qwen3-VL judge overlaps with the Qwen3-VL model used heavily in data curation, a methodological concern. (3) Text rendering lags specialized models (CVTG-2K 0.765 vs. 0.865 for LongCat) and several understanding metrics trail Qwen2.5-VL-7B (DocVQA 89.5 vs. 94.9; ChartQA 80.1 vs. 84.1), but the abstract claim of matching specialized VLMs is not qualified. (4) Training compute cost (390B tokens at 16B MoE scale) is not reported in FLOPs or GPU-hours, obscuring the resource comparison against lighter specialized models. (5) The α=0.5 blending in SPRINT's importance scoring is not ablated. Bottom Line: A practitioner building unified multimodal systems should read this paper — the SigLIP-VQ plus separate diffusion decoder architecture is a credible and practically validated advance over reconstruction-VQ unified diffusion models, weights are public, and the SPRINT inference optimization is directly applicable; the main caveat is that component-level attribution requires further ablation work not yet in this preprint.
Recommended 2604.20796 PDF
#64 — Innovative (68)

Self-Evolving LLM Memory Extraction Across Heterogeneous Tasks

Yuqing Yang, Tengxiao Liu, Wang Bill Zhu, Taiwei Shi, Linxin Song, Robin Jia · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) for new problem framing of heterogeneous memory extraction plus BEHEMOTH benchmark. Rigor (60) — preprint with no component ablation, synthetic source conversations share LLM with answering model, only 20 examples/dataset. Non-Obviousness (70) — cluster-by-extraction-scenario insight is crisp. Completeness (70) honest limitations. Resilience (65) — Gemini regression and missing ablations weaken. Impact (75) — code+benchmark released. Composite ≈ 0.20*70 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 = 67.75.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BEHEMOTH, an 18-dataset benchmark for heterogeneous memory extraction, and CluE, a cluster-based self-evolving framework that groups training examples by extraction scenario to stably improve memory extraction prompts across diverse task types. - Why it's important: Deployed LLM assistants encounter wildly different interaction types — personalization chats, math problem-solving, agentic tool use — and no single fixed extraction rule generalizes across them; this work is the first to formalize and benchmark that generalization challenge. - How it does it: CluE summarizes each training example's extraction scenario, clusters examples by those scenarios rather than by surface task or domain, performs per-cluster failure/success analysis, then synthesizes cross-cluster insights into a unified updated extraction prompt. - The good: CluE achieves +9.04% relative gain over the Simple baseline across 18 datasets and consistently improves all three task categories simultaneously (+12.34% personalization, +8.39% problem-solving, +7.22% agentic), whereas every competing self-evolving framework improves one category at the expense of another. - The missing: All experiments use the same model for both memory extraction and target-query answering; no evaluation tests whether prompts evolved with one backbone transfer to a different extraction model. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building persistent LLM assistants or studying prompt optimization under heterogeneous data distributions; the BEHEMOTH benchmark and the cluster-by-scenario insight are both reusable contributions. Problem & Motivation: As LLM assistants become persistent across sessions, they must extract and store useful information from prior conversations as memory. Existing extraction systems use static, domain-specific rules: personalization agents extract user preferences, agentic systems extract reusable strategies, problem-solving systems extract solution patterns. A general-purpose assistant, however, encounters all of these simultaneously, and no fixed extraction schema works across the full breadth. The field lacks a benchmark that spans this heterogeneity, and existing self-evolving frameworks — which iteratively refine the extraction prompt from task feedback — were designed for homogeneous distributions. Under heterogeneous training data, these methods exhibit a characteristic failure: updating too frequently causes successive batches of dissimilar examples to pull the prompt in conflicting directions; updating too infrequently washes out fine-grained task signals by averaging over dissimilar scenarios. Key Idea: The core insight is that memory extraction tasks should be clustered by extraction scenario (what type of information must be extracted and what makes extraction hard) rather than by surface task, domain, or dataset label. This allows per-cluster analysis to produce targeted, non-conflicting prompt improvements that can then be synthesized into a single coherent prompt covering all scenarios. Two examples from different datasets (e.g., an agentic trajectory and a math solution) may both require extracting multi-step procedural knowledge, and grouping them together surfaces shared extraction challenges that neither dataset-specific nor fully mixed analysis would reveal. Methodology: The paper formalizes single-step memory extraction as a triple (source conversation c, target query q, reward function R): an extraction model LLM_e produces a memory string m conditioned on an extraction prompt P; a generation model LLM_g answers q given m; quality is measured by R(answer). BEHEMOTH is constructed by repurposing 18 existing datasets into this formulation across three categories — personalization (5 datasets: MemBench-High/Low, PersonaMem-v2, PrefEval, LongMemEval), problem-solving (7: Game of 24, AIME, MMLU Pro Physics/Engineering, MathEquationBalancer, BigCodeBench, GPQA-Diamond), and agentic (6: AlfWorld, FEVER, PDDL, BabyAI, ScienceWorld, ToolBench). Problem-solving and agentic datasets lack multi-turn conversations, so LLM_g is prompted to generate source conversations from task instances; this introduces synthetic data at the source-conversation level. Training uses 20 examples per dataset (330 total, randomly shuffled); held-out OOD test sets are one dataset per category (LongMemEval, GPQA-Diamond, ToolBench). CluE operates in rounds. In each round: (1) Summarizer — an LLM reads each training log and writes a 2–3 sentence extraction scenario summary focusing on what type of information is needed and what makes extraction hard, abstracting away domain specifics; (2) Cluster Manager — an LLM assigns each example to one of up to 7 clusters based on extraction scenario similarity, with the ability to create, merge, or split clusters as distributions evolve; (3) Cluster Analyzer — for each cluster, an LLM with tool access inspects that cluster's training logs, identifies success and failure patterns, and produces targeted prompt recommendations; (4) Proposer — an LLM synthesizes per-cluster analyses into one improved extraction prompt, resolving conflicts by scoping recommendations to appropriate memory categories. CluE uses the same batch size (35) and tournament-based evaluation as MemEvolve, adding 221 optimization LLM calls over 5 rounds (~5.5h wall time). Evaluation uses Macro Accuracy (uniform average across datasets) and Relative Gain (geometric mean of per-dataset improvement ratios over Simple baseline), with three evaluation runs to report mean ± standard deviation. Key Results: On in-distribution test sets starting from the Simple seed (Qwen3-32B), CluE achieves 50.01% macro accuracy and +9.04% relative gain over Simple (46.00%). The three competing self-evolving methods all exhibit category tradeoffs: GEPA reaches +14.08% agentic but −2.16% personalization; MemEvolve reaches +10.76% personalization but −3.25% agentic; ACE shows +8.04% problem-solving but −4.38% personalization. CluE is the only method with positive relative gain in all three categories simultaneously. Starting from the stronger Survey seed, all baseline methods produce negative or flat overall relative gains (ACE: −1.44%, MemEvolve: −0.74%, GEPA: 0%), while CluE achieves +6.54% overall and positive gains in all three categories. On OOD test sets, CluE achieves 63.07% on LongMemEval (vs. 56.82% for MemEvolve), 48.48% on GPQA-Diamond (above the Simple baseline of 47.14%), and 26.67% on ToolBench. With Gemini-3-Flash as extraction backend, CluE achieves +3.40% relative gain; all three baselines either regress or show marginal improvement (ACE: −3.93%, MemEvolve: −0.29%, GEPA: +0.93%). In the continual memory setting, CluE (50.83 on Game of 24; 67.25 on AlfWorld) outperforms MemEvolve (43.33; 62.57). Qualitatively, CluE's evolved prompt contains 936 tokens — shorter than ACE's 1,403 tokens — and develops a structured 5-category memory taxonomy with per-category extraction guidelines. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The formalization of heterogeneous memory extraction as a distinct generalization challenge is a new problem framing. BEHEMOTH is a new benchmark (repurposed but systematically unified under one protocol). CluE's cluster-by-extraction-scenario evolution strategy is a new method in the self-evolving prompt optimization space. The core novelty is the scenario-clustering insight, which is non-obvious: prior self-evolving methods were aware of task heterogeneity but attempted to handle it through architectural variants (validation sets in GEPA, atomic operations in ACE) rather than by restructuring the feedback aggregation itself. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are limited — the paper does not ablate the effect of removing any single CluE component (e.g., Summarizer vs. Cluster Manager vs. Proposer), so it is not established which pipeline stage is responsible for performance gains. Error bars (standard deviations over three runs) are reported for evolving framework results but not for static prompt baselines, which are run once. The evaluation is downstream-utility-driven (avoiding LLM-as-judge bias), which is a sound design choice. The benchmark construction has a potential concern: problem-solving and agentic source conversations are LLM-generated by LLM_g (Qwen3-32B), meaning the source conversations and the answering model share characteristics; this could inflate performance for all methods on those categories relative to real-world deployment. Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/ayyyq/heterogeneous-memory-extraction. This is a preprint; peer review has not been completed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (system evaluated through experiments). - Practical: CluE requires only API-level or locally hosted LLM access (tested with Qwen3-32B and Gemini-3-Flash); no specialized hardware beyond what runs the extraction and generation models. - Efficient: CluE costs ~5.5h wall time and 221 optimization LLM calls over 5 rounds, marginally more than MemEvolve (~5.0h, 30 calls) but substantially less than ACE (~12.4h, 610 calls); the overhead is one-time prompt optimization, not per-inference cost. - Effective: Evidence shows CluE improves downstream task performance across all three task categories on both in-distribution and OOD test sets, with the advantage holding in a continual memory setting on two tasks; the proxy (single-step extraction evaluated by downstream reward) is well-motivated but not identical to real multi-session deployment. - Scalable: Training uses only 330 examples; scaling to larger training sets or to the full memory lifecycle (storage, retrieval, management) is explicitly left to future work and not tested. The work delivers code, data, and evolved prompts as software artifacts. Strengths: - The cluster-by-extraction-scenario insight is intellectually crisp and solves a concrete failure mode (conflicting gradient-like signals under heterogeneous data) that the authors demonstrate empirically in competing methods. - BEHEMOTH's unified evaluation protocol for 18 heterogeneous datasets under a single downstream-utility metric is a genuine infrastructure contribution that enables fair comparison across methods with different extraction philosophies. - The stronger-seed experiments (Table 4) are a particularly stringent test: CluE is the only method that improves over a well-engineered Survey prompt, while all others degrade it — a result that distinguishes CluE's stability from methods that simply overwrite good rules. - The evolved prompt analysis (Appendix B.3) traces exactly how clusters merge/split over rounds and how cluster insights map to taxonomy sections, providing mechanistic transparency uncommon in prompt optimization papers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that BEHEMOTH's 18 datasets may not cover the full complexity of real-world deployments, which can involve longer interaction histories and greater task diversity. They explicitly scope the work to memory extraction (the first stage of the memory lifecycle) and acknowledge that generalizing to storage, retrieval, and management remains open. They also note that continual evolution directly under the continual heterogeneous regime is left to future work. These are stated directly in Section 7, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No component ablation: the paper does not isolate which of the four CluE stages (Summarizer, Cluster Manager, Cluster Analyzer, Proposer) drives gains. A version without the Summarizer (clustering raw logs directly) or without per-cluster analysis would clarify the contribution. - Synthetic source conversations: problem-solving and agentic source conversations are generated by the same Qwen3-32B model used as LLM_g. This creates a distributional shortcut where the extraction model and the generation model share stylistic priors. No experiment tests CluE on truly human-generated problem-solving or agentic conversations. - Only 20 training examples per dataset: while the shuffled regime is realistic, all methods are trained on a tiny signal. It is not established whether the relative ordering of methods holds at larger training scales. - CluE with Gemini-3-Flash shows −5.93% agentic regression (Table 7), which the paper attributes to ceiling effects but does not analyze in depth. This is a meaningful failure mode for a method claiming robust cross-category generalization. - The cross-model generalization test changes only the extraction backend, not the optimization model; a test where evolution is performed with one LLM and the evolved prompt is deployed with a different backbone would be more informative for real adoption. Bottom Line: Practitioners building general-purpose persistent LLM assistants will find both the BEHEMOTH benchmark and the cluster-by-scenario evolution strategy directly useful, and the consistent cross-category gains distinguish this work meaningfully from prior self-evolving methods that trade off categories against each other.
Recommended 2604.11610 PDF
#65 — Innovative (67)

AJ-Bench: Benchmarking Agent-as-a-Judge for Environment-Aware Evaluation (11 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile (with systems flavor — released benchmark and AWS replay infra). Novelty (70) — first multi-domain benchmark for Agent-as-a-Judge with live-environment replay; the paradigm itself is from prior work but the benchmarking infrastructure is new. Rigor (65) is reasonable: three runs with 95% CIs reported, double-checked annotation, code/data released, but main A-as-J comparison rests on only two backbones due to budget and several subdomains have <30 trajectories with overlapping CIs. Non-Obviousness (70) — the finding that weaker-model A-as-J beats stronger LLM-as-J, and that thinking mode does not help, are genuinely counter-intuitive and useful. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) are limited by absent cost analysis, undersized subdomains, and no human-agreement ceiling.
