TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OMatG-FC, a generative model for inorganic crystalline materials that fixes atomic fractional coordinates and unit-cell shape (the "structural framework") and learns only the chemical species assignment and unit-cell volume scaling. - Why it's important: Most stable, novel materials discovered by generative models map new chemistry onto known geometric arrangements rather than inventing genuinely new atomic packings, so explicitly exploiting this structure-reuse heuristic could yield higher stability rates with a simpler learning problem. - How it does it: The model co-evolves atomic species via discrete flow matching (masked CTMC over 100 element types) and log-volume via stochastic interpolants, while holding fractional coordinates and unit-volume lattice fixed via identity interpolants, reducing the generative task from (A, L, F) to (A, V | L̄, F). - The good: OMatG-FC-LinearODE achieves 0.4% SUN rate and OMatG-FC-LinearSDE achieves 0.17 eV average energy above hull and RMSD of 0.30 Å — the best figures among non-pre-relaxed models on the LeMat-GenBench leaderboard — and reaches 100% structural validity by construction. - The missing: Novelty rate collapses to 30–35% (vs. 66%+ for unconstrained baselines), and the paper provides no DFT validation, no ablation isolating the contribution of the framework constraint versus other modeling choices, and no test on framework sets drawn from sources beyond MP-20. - Read it or not: Worth a quick read for materials ML practitioners interested in constraint-based generation strategies, but the workshop format and limited novelty analysis mean it should be treated as a proof-of-concept rather than a definitive result. Problem & Motivation Machine-learning generative models for inorganic crystals must jointly predict stable chemical compositions and atomic packings from a vast, poorly-sampled space. Fully de novo methods (MatterGen, OMatG, FlowMM) learn this joint distribution but produce few genuinely novel structural prototypes in practice. Meanwhile, empirical observation across the materials science literature shows that experimentally confirmed new materials typically adopt known geometric frameworks with new chemical occupancies. The challenge addressed here is whether deliberately factoring out the geometric generation task — fixing the structural framework and learning only which atoms go where — can yield better stability-novelty tradeoffs than unconstrained generation. Key Idea The key insight is that a crystal's structural framework (fractional coordinates + unit-cell shape up to uniform scaling) can be treated as a fixed conditioning input, leaving the model to learn only the joint distribution over atomic species and cell volume. Because fixed geometry resolves most sources of positional noise and space-group ambiguity, the resulting generative problem is dramatically lower-dimensional and the learned distribution is implicitly biased toward the convex hull. Methodology OMatG-FC represents a unit cell as (A, V, L̄, F), where A ∈ Z^N is the atomic-number vector, V = log|det(L)| is the log-volume, L̄ is the unit-volume lattice, and F are fractional coordinates. Generation conditions on a framework (L̄, F) sampled from the dataset and co-evolves A and V. Atomic species use discrete flow matching (Campbell et al. 2024; Gat et al. 2024): a masked CTMC starting from all-mask tokens and unmasking to one of 100 element types, trained with cross-entropy loss L_DFM. Log-volume uses a stochastic interpolant with a linear schedule (α(t)=1−t, β(t)=t), trained with MSE velocity and denoiser losses; the base distribution is a log-normal fit to MP-20 volumes. Both L̄ and F use identity interpolants (held fixed throughout the trajectory). The neural backbone is CSPNet from DiffCSP — six-layer fully-connected message-passing GNN with sinusoidal distance embeddings, translation-invariant and rotation/permutation-equivariant — with linear output heads for volume drift/denoiser and species logits. Two variants are trained: LinearODE (deterministic sampling, 610 steps) and LinearSDE (stochastic, 620 steps). Hyperparameters are tuned via Ray Tune + Optuna against a composite metric averaging combined validity and five Wasserstein distances. The MP-20 dataset (45,229 structures, ≤20 atoms/unit cell, 60-20-20 split) provides both training data and the test-time framework pool. Key Results On distributional metrics (Table 1, 10,000 generated structures), OMatG-FC-LinearSDE achieves the best mass-density Wasserstein distance (0.0860 vs. 0.1155 for OMatG-EncDecODE) and both variants achieve the best space-group Wasserstein distance (4.4–5.9 vs. 13–25 for baselines), attributable to symmetry being preserved by construction. Structural validity is 100% for both variants. On LeMat-GenBench (Table 2, 2,500 structures, three ML interatomic potentials — MACE, UMA, Orb-v3), OMatG-FC-LinearODE achieves 0.4% SUN rate (best among non-pre-relaxed models; DiffCSP achieves 0.1%, OMatG-EncDecODE 0.1%) and 0.27 eV mean hull energy. OMatG-FC-LinearSDE achieves 6.8% MSUN rate, 0.17 eV mean hull energy, and 0.30 Å RMSD — best or near-best on these metrics. However, novelty rates are 30.4% and 34.9% respectively, well below DiffCSP (66.2%) and MatterGen (70.5%), because generated structures reuse known frameworks that may already exist in the reference set. Pre-relaxed models (MatterGen†, PLaID++†) are shown for reference but explicitly excluded from the best-score competition. The framework duplicate analysis finds ~18% of MP-20 structures are exact framework duplicates (numerical noise only), and at the default StructureMatcher tolerance only ~10% of frameworks are unique. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD applied to an existing problem setting. The structural framework constraint as an explicit conditioning variable for flow-based crystal generation is genuinely new; the combination of discrete flow matching for species and stochastic interpolants for volume within a fixed-geometry regime has not been demonstrated before. However, the neural architecture (CSPNet), the generative frameworks (DFM, stochastic interpolants), and the dataset (MP-20) are all inherited from prior work (OMatG, DiffCSP, FlowMM). The contribution is a new conditional generation formulation, not a new architecture or algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations are presented isolating the effect of framework conditioning versus other modeling choices (e.g., the log-normal volume prior, velocity annealing, or the CSPNet backbone). No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported on the benchmark numbers. Hyperparameter tuning is disclosed (Optuna + Ray Tune) but the tuned values are not tabulated. Stability is assessed via three ML interatomic potentials rather than DFT, which is standard in the field but introduces potential errors for structures far from the training distribution of those potentials. Benchmarking uses the public LeMat-GenBench leaderboard, which standardizes evaluation and is a reproducibility positive. Code and model weights are not mentioned as released. Published as a workshop paper (AI4Mat-ICLR 2026), not peer-reviewed at full-paper standard. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on standard GPU hardware; the CSPNet backbone and flow-matching sampling are not computationally exotic, and the framework pool can come from any known crystal database. - Efficient: Requires 610–620 integration steps per generation batch, comparable to OMatG (840 steps) and substantially fewer than DiffCSP (1000 steps); training cost is not reported. - Effective: The method achieves the claimed SUN rate improvement on LeMat-GenBench, but absolute SUN rates remain low (≤0.4% stable, unique, and novel) across all non-pre-relaxed methods, and the novelty rate penalty (30–35%) is substantial and directly tied to the constraint. - Scalable: Only tested on MP-20 (≤20 atoms/unit cell, 45k structures); behavior on larger cells or richer databases (e.g., AFLOW, Alexandria) is untested and unclaimed. The work delivers no software artifact beyond what is implied by the paper; no public code or model release is stated. Strengths: - The framework-constraint formulation is conceptually clean and directly motivated by an empirical regularity in the materials database (framework reuse), quantified here via the duplicate analysis. - Achieving 100% structural validity by construction — not as a post-hoc filter — is a genuine practical advantage. - The space-group Wasserstein distance improvement (4–6 vs. 13–25 for baselines) is a direct mechanistic consequence of the approach and is well-explained. - The framework duplicate analysis (showing ~18% exact duplicates and only ~10% unique frameworks at default stol in MP-20) is a useful empirical finding for the community, independent of the model. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that low novelty rate is a direct consequence of the framework constraint and that generated structures may overlap with known materials. They also note that the current study uses only frameworks from MP-20 rather than theorized or hypothetical point patterns, limiting extrapolation. Chemical disorder modeling is flagged as a direction but not evaluated in this work. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The SUN rate claim of "state-of-the-art" is technically accurate (0.4% vs. 0.1–0.2%) but potentially misleading given that the absolute numbers differ by fractions of a percent and no statistical significance testing is performed. The absence of any ablation — particularly comparing species-only generation with a fixed volume against the joint (A, V) model — makes it impossible to attribute the stability gains to the framework constraint specifically versus the log-normal volume prior or other design choices. The framework pool at test time is drawn from the MP-20 test set, which raises a subtle data-leakage question: the frameworks condition the generation on geometric arrangements already observed in the training-adjacent test split. The 34.9% novelty rate is partially a consequence of this pool choice, but the paper does not discuss how novelty rate would change if frameworks were drawn from a held-out database. Finally, the paper benchmarks only against models trained on MP-20, excluding newer large-scale generative models trained on richer datasets, which limits the relevance of the comparison to the broader field. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on inorganic crystal generation who wants a mechanistically motivated, high-stability baseline will find OMatG-FC a worthwhile reference point, but should be cautious about interpreting the SUN rate improvements as decisive given the absolute magnitudes, the novelty penalty, and the absence of ablations.
Citations
#136 — Moderately Innovative (52)

MCA-KT: An expression-behaviour multimodal knowledge tracking model based on cross-attention

link.springer.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (55) is a NEW METHOD applying cross-attention to KT with theory-grounded Q/K/V asymmetry. Rigor (55) has good ablations including w/o IMG control and 5-seed t-tests, but no code release, frozen FER2013 features, and baseline comparability concerns pull it down. Non-Obviousness (55) the ACIT-grounded asymmetric design is moderately insightful. Completeness (50) and Resilience (45) are limited by the tiny single-institution Hsd2024 dataset (132 students, 0.4-0.8% of training) and uncontrolled-setting untested. Impact (45) reflects no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*55 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*45 ≈ 52.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 50 Resilience 45 Impact 45
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MCA-KT, a knowledge tracing model that fuses students' question-answering behavior sequences with facial expression features via an asymmetric cross-attention mechanism in which behavioral representations serve as queries to selectively weight affect features. - Why it's important: Existing knowledge tracing models rely solely on behavioral logs and cannot account for affective states (anxiety, confusion) that directly modulate cognitive performance, leaving a systematic blind spot in predicting why students struggle with questions they nominally have knowledge to answer. - How it does it: Behavioral features form the Query and facial expression features (ResNet-18 on FER2013, 512-dim) form the Key/Value in a Scaled Dot-Product cross-attention layer, followed by RNN-based sequence modeling; a causal triangular mask plus a validity mask suppress future information leakage and missing-image noise. - The good: A purpose-built ablation (MCA-KT w/o IMG, replacing all real face features with zero vectors while keeping the architecture intact) confirms the architecture itself does not confer structural inductive bias over baselines — AUC drops to or below DKT/DKVMN without real images, isolating the contribution of affect to the gains. - The missing: The only multimodal data is Hsd2024 — 132 students, 2,508 interactions, a single university, two courses taken via a single proctoring platform — making it impossible to assess whether the affect–cognition link generalizes beyond a narrow Chinese undergraduate exam context. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on affective computing in intelligent tutoring systems and want a worked example of the cross-attention-as-cognitive-filter design pattern; skip it if you need evidence at realistic deployment scale, as the multimodal dataset is too small and narrow to support strong empirical conclusions. Problem & Motivation: Knowledge tracing (KT) models are tasked with estimating a student's latent mastery of concepts from historical interaction sequences, then predicting future performance. The dominant paradigm (DKT, SAKT, AKT, HiTSKT, etc.) treats the learning process as a pure cognitive sequence over question–answer pairs, ignoring the well-established finding from educational psychology that affective states — anxiety, confusion, engagement — directly modulate attention, working-memory load, and decision strategy. When a student in a high-anxiety state answers a question they would otherwise know, behavioral-only models misattribute the error to knowledge deficit. Integrating facial expressions as a real-time affective signal into KT has remained underdeveloped: the few existing multimodal KT studies either expand behavioral logs with response-time features (still unimodal in the true perceptual sense) or use simple feature concatenation that fails to capture the asymmetric, cognitively-dominant nature of the affect–cognition relationship posited by Affective-Cognitive Interaction Theory (ACIT). Key Idea: The single core technical insight is that the cross-attention mechanism's Q/K/V asymmetry is an ideal computational analog of the psychological principle that cognitive state selects which affective signals are relevant, rather than all affective information being equally weighted. By casting behavioral state as the Query and facial expression as Key/Value, the model operationalizes the ACIT principle of "cognitive dominance, affective regulation" directly in the attention computation, enabling context-sensitive, time-indexed retrieval of relevant emotional context rather than static fusion. Methodology: The pipeline has four sequential modules. (1) MSEL (Multimodal State Encoding Layer): Behavioral features (question ID + correctness, embedded and projected) form Q ∈ R^{B×L×dh}; facial expression features (ResNet-18 512-dim vectors, mean-pooled over frames per time step) form K, V ∈ R^{B×L×dh} via separate linear projections. (2) CAFL (Cross-Attention Fusion Layer): Scaled dot-product attention A = QK^T/√dh, modified by a dual mask: a lower-triangular causal mask enforcing temporal ordering, element-wise combined with a validity mask that assigns −∞ to time steps where MTCNN failed to detect a face (7.70% of steps in Hsd2024) or where zero-padded vectors stand in for absent images. Softmax after masking forces invalid weights to exactly zero. The fused affective representation F′ = A′·V is appended (concatenated) to the original behavioral features. (3) SML (Sequential Modeling Layer): A single-layer vanilla RNN processes the concatenated [S; F′] to produce hidden states ht capturing temporal knowledge evolution. Design choice of plain RNN over LSTM/GRU is justified by empirical finding that deeper structures increased overfitting without AUC gain. (4) POL: A linear layer + Sigmoid maps ht to P(correct at t+1); binary cross-entropy loss. Training: Adagrad optimizer, lr=0.002, batch=64, early stopping (patience=5). All models run 5 seeds per dataset; paired t-tests with α=0.05. Key Results: On three augmented datasets (public KT data + Hsd2024 appended, "++" suffix), MCA-KT achieves AUC of 0.841 (ASSISTments2009++), 0.8448 (Statics2011++), 0.8171 (KDDCup2010++), outperforming the next-best baselines HiTSKT (0.8142, 0.8012, 0.7663) and GKT (0.7962, -, 0.7691). The improvement over DKT is +16.16%, +12.38%, +10.85% on the three datasets respectively. All pairwise t-tests are significant (p < 0.05, typically p < 0.001). On the self-constructed Hsd2024 dataset alone via 8-fold cross-validation, MCA-KT achieves mean AUC=0.8303 ± 0.0392, ACC=0.7601 ± 0.0374. Ablation: removing the image modality drops AUC by 10.7–12.7% (largest contributor at 11.90% average); removing cross-attention drops 1.19–9.18% (5.91% average, task-dependent); removing the mask drops 0.83–6.64% (3.88% average). Attention fusion comparison shows cross-attention outperforms gated fusion by 4.0–5.6% AUC on longer-sequence and higher-cognitive-load datasets. Inference latency is 0.770ms per sequence on a 2080Ti with 0.167M parameters — faster than SAKT (1.078ms), DKVMN (24.443ms), and AKT (5.175ms). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The paper applies the existing cross-attention mechanism (Vaswani et al., 2017) and ResNet-based expression features to the knowledge tracing domain with a theoretically motivated asymmetric encoding design. The "affective anchor" dataset augmentation strategy (mixing a small multimodal dataset into large unimodal public datasets and relying on masking for robustness) is a practical contribution not clearly established elsewhere in KT. Genuine novelty is the theory-grounded Q/K/V role assignment; the surrounding components (RNN sequence modeling, MTCNN face detection, FER2013 pre-training) are standard. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and well-designed, including the critical w/o IMG control that isolates architectural inductive bias. Five-seed averaging with paired t-tests is appropriate. The asymmetric attention direction is empirically justified against affect-led and symmetric variants. However, no code or dataset release is mentioned; the Hsd2024 dataset involves 132 undergraduate students from a single Chinese university under exam conditions, raising questions about distribution shift. Hyperparameter selection is partly post-hoc ("prior research experience"), with limited sensitivity analysis. The w/o IMG ablation performance (AUC ~0.732–0.737) falls below some baselines, but it is not entirely clear whether the baselines were also trained on augmented "++" datasets (they appear to be, based on Table 4 which shows all models evaluated on "++" datasets), meaning baseline comparisons may not perfectly reflect published numbers. The grouped performance analysis (image-present samples vs. zero-padded samples) is explicitly noted as missing and deferred to future work, leaving the generalization mechanism of the affective anchor strategy partially opaque. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Runs on a single consumer GPU (NVIDIA 2080Ti 11GB); MTCNN + ResNet-18 are widely available; requires only a front-facing webcam in a controlled lighting environment, which is feasible in proctored online exam settings. - Efficient: 0.167M parameters and 0.770ms inference latency — extremely lightweight; the marginal overhead over DKT (0.154M, 0.246ms) is minor, representing a reasonable trade-off. - Effective: Consistent AUC improvements over all seven baselines on all three datasets, statistically significant; the w/o IMG control confirms real expression features drive the gains. However, effectiveness is demonstrated only in examination scenarios with fixed-interval camera capture and controlled lighting; uncontrolled naturalistic learning environments (e.g., personal study at home, variable webcam quality) are untested. - Scalable: Tested on datasets up to 609K interactions (KDDCup2010++); the actual multimodal component covers only 2,508 samples. No test at 10×–100× the multimodal data scale. The 7.70% face detection failure rate is reported for controlled exam conditions; real-world rates are likely higher. The work delivers model architecture and code in principle (PyTorch), but no public software artifact or trained weights are released. Strengths: - The asymmetric Q/K/V role assignment grounded in ACIT is a conceptually clean and empirically validated design decision; the three-way ablation (behaviour-dominant vs. affect-led vs. symmetric) directly tests the key claim. - The validity masking strategy gracefully unifies two distinct missing-data scenarios (detection failure and absent modality) under a single mathematical mechanism, and the w/o IMG ablation convincingly rules out structural inductive bias. - Computational efficiency is genuinely impressive: cross-attention multimodal fusion at 0.167M parameters and sub-millisecond inference outperforms heavier unimodal models like DKVMN and AKT on both accuracy and speed. - Attention visualization (Fig. 9) provides interpretable evidence of three theoretically predicted patterns (diagonal concentration, selective historical focus, temporal regression) consistent with ACIT, lending credibility beyond quantitative metrics alone. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly flag: (1) Hsd2024 is small (132 students, single institution) and may not generalize; (2) the model uses only facial images and does not incorporate speech, gesture, or physiological signals; (3) grouped performance analysis (image-present vs. zero-padded subsets) is missing; (4) no direct linkage to instructional intervention systems. These are disclosed directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Several concerns the authors do not fully confront. First, the Hsd2024 dataset is collected during exams on a single online proctoring platform under uniform lighting and camera placement — a highly controlled environment that differs fundamentally from naturalistic learning. The claim that MCA-KT is deployable in real-time online education is not supported by evidence in naturalistic conditions. Second, the augmentation strategy (appending 2,508 multimodal samples to datasets of 328K–609K interactions) means multimodal data constitute 0.4–0.8% of training samples; the "affective anchor" mechanism relies on the claim that a very small proportion of multimodal samples shapes global representation learning, but this is asserted rather than rigorously characterized. Third, the baseline models are also evaluated on augmented "++" datasets rather than original datasets, so comparisons cannot be directly cross-referenced with published literature. Fourth, ResNet-18 is frozen at FER2013-pretrained weights — no fine-tuning to the domain or student population is attempted, which may leave significant room for improvement and limits the claim that the expression features are well-suited to learning-context emotions (FER2013 is a general expression recognition dataset, not an educational one). Fifth, the vanilla RNN in SML is a questionable architectural choice given its known gradient issues with long sequences; LSTMs or GRUs are standard and no systematic comparison is provided. Bottom Line: MCA-KT offers a theoretically well-motivated and practically efficient design for incorporating facial affect into knowledge tracing, with ablations solid enough to isolate the multimodal contribution, but the conclusions rest on a single tiny multimodal dataset from one institution that is unlikely to represent the distribution of affect in real learning environments.
Search Results
#137 — Moderately Innovative (51)

Towards Robust Endogenous Reasoning: Unifying Drift Adaptation in Non-Stationary Tuning

Xiaoyu Yang, En Yu, Wei Duan, Jie Lu · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (50) and Resilience (45) pull the composite down: the robustness evaluation is partially circular (in-distribution counterfactual perturbation type), no error bars or significance tests, single 7B model + 1 epoch, no code release, preprint. Completeness (50) — sub-1% gains over CPO on most benchmarks, knowledge-graph quality and synthesized-counterfactual quality not measured. Novelty (55) is moderate — extension of existing CPO with visual counterfactuals via inverse-matching. Impact (45) — no released artifact. Weighted ≈ 51.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 50 Resilience 45 Impact 45
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes Counterfactual Preference Optimization++ (CPO++), a training framework that extends DPO-style preference optimization with domain-knowledge-guided counterfactual negative samples — spanning both chain-of-thought text and visual inputs — to stabilize multimodal LLM reasoning against token-level distributional perturbations. - Why it's important: Safety-critical deployments of MLLMs (medical diagnosis, autonomous driving) can fail catastrophically when minor synonymic word choices in a reasoning chain cause cascading visual attention collapse and diagnosis reversal — a failure mode orthogonal to the exogenous distribution shifts that existing RFT alignment work addresses. - How it does it: The model itself generates counterfactual CoT trajectories via controlled attribute substitutions in a domain-specific hierarchical knowledge graph, then uses those trajectories — alongside hard-negative visual inputs selected by an inverse matching task — as rejected samples in a DPO objective, forcing the policy to disentangle causal from spurious correlations in both modalities. - The good: Robustness experiments show CPO++ sustains near-flat accuracy under 80% counterfactual token injection in medical pathology classification, whereas DPO collapses to near-random for pneumothorax — a concrete, verifiable demonstration that the method addresses a real instability rather than a nominal one. - The missing: The robustness evaluation is partially circular: the model is trained with counterfactual negatives and then tested on prompt-injected counterfactual tokens drawn from the same perturbation distribution, providing no evidence of generalization to out-of-perturbation-family adversarial inputs. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on multimodal RFT alignment, causal robustness, or safety-critical MLLM deployment — the endogenous drift phenomenon is real and well-illustrated; skip if your interest is in reasoning at scale (experiments stop at 7B parameters, single epoch). Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) of MLLMs is increasingly applied in safety-critical domains where reliable multi-step reasoning is required. Existing work on distribution shift during RFT focuses on exogenous sources: OOD data, reward hacking, or entropy collapse. This paper identifies and names a distinct failure mode termed endogenous reasoning drift: during autoregressive decoding, the stochastic choice between semantically near-equivalent tokens (e.g., "lung opacity" vs. "opacity") can cause the model's downstream visual attention to collapse entirely and produce opposite clinical diagnoses. This is not an external perturbation — it arises from internal decoding stochasticity. The gap in prior work is that no RFT method addresses instability originating from within the generation process itself. Key Idea: The core insight is that autoregressive CoT generation can be modeled as a non-stationary data stream subject to concept drift, and that counterfactual causal reasoning provides a principled way to construct negative training examples that explicitly encode what detrimental drift looks like. By building a structural causal graph where latent concept drift (D) acts as a confounder on the chain-of-thought mediator (T) and output (Z), the paper shows that the spurious path D→T→Z can be blocked by training on interventional distributions. The practical realization — using the model itself to generate attribute-swapped CoT trajectories and to select hard-negative images via inverse matching — is the key engineering move that makes the approach self-supervised. Methodology: CPO++ proceeds in four stages. (1) Theoretical framing: endogenous drift is formalized as multi-modal concept drift per Definition 3.1 — a distributional shift in P(t,z|v) that decomposes into perception grounding P(t|v) and thinking consistency P(z|t,v). The adaptive objective minimizes cumulative loss over a sliding window of cognitive states (Eq. 4). (2) Causal disentanglement: a structural causal model with nodes X (inputs), T (CoT), Z (predictions), D (latent drift) identifies D as a confounder introducing backdoor bias T←D→Z. Counterfactual potential outcomes (Eq. 5) quantify the direct causal effect of T on Z under fixed D. (3) Self-counterfactual generation with domain knowledge: a hierarchical graph G_K organizes domain entities, attributes, and exclusion/association relationships. For medical chest X-ray: 12 disease entities, 53 clinically relevant attributes. The target MLLM πθ substitutes key attributes in an initial CoT to synthesize candidate counterfactual trajectories Tcand — no external LLM required. For visual counterfactuals, the model performs an inverse matching task (given ground-truth CoT, identify the correct image from a pool of semantically similar distractors); failures identify hard negative visual samples. (4) CPO++ preference optimization: standard DPO loss (Eq. 9, Bradley-Terry model) with positive samples = expert reasoning trajectories and negative samples = both textual counterfactuals and hard-negative image-CoT pairs. Three design decisions: (a) using the model itself as generator avoids external LLM costs but risks circular quality — not well-analyzed; (b) the knowledge graph imposes hard causal constraints that prevent physically implausible counterfactuals; (c) perception counterfactuals are selected by model-self discrimination, creating a feedback loop whose failure modes are not characterized. Base model: Qwen2.5-VL 7B. Training: 1 epoch, batch 4, 2×2 A100 GPUs, AdamW lr=2e-5. Key Results: On MIMIC-CXR (368K training, 5,159 test), CPO++ achieves METEOR 0.271 and CIDEr 0.378, versus CPO (NeurIPS 2025) at 0.236/0.375 and DPO at 0.260/0.352. On MS-CXR-T classification (Top-1 accuracy across 5 pathologies), CPO++ reaches 83.3% average vs. CPO 81.8% and DPO 76.4%. On autonomous driving BDD-X reasoning (all splits), CPO++ achieves CIDEr 1.045 vs. HoP 1.022 and CPO 0.990; on Hard split specifically, CIDEr 0.642 vs. CPO's 0.624 and ADAPT's 0.527. Robustness under 80% counterfactual injection: CPO++ holds near-baseline accuracy while DPO drops 20–30 percentage points per pathology. CODA-LM GPT-Score: CPO++ 68.8 vs. GPT-4V 59.0, with corner case perception 85.2 vs. 83.1 (Percept-DriveLM). Zero-shot medical generalization (four unseen datasets): CPO++ best on all four (e.g., PadChest 82.3 vs. CPO 82.0; ChestXDet10 81.4 vs. CPO 80.1). Zero-shot driving (DriveLM): 40.0 vs. CPO 36.4. The majority of improvements over the prior CPO are statistically small (1–2 absolute points) except in robustness, where the gap is large and the phenomenon appears genuine. Baselines include peer-reviewed 2024–2025 conference papers; no obviously weak or cherry-picked comparison set. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The contribution is an extension of DPO-style preference optimization with domain-knowledge-guided counterfactual negatives applied symmetrically to text and vision modalities. The endogenous drift framing is conceptually new, but the mathematical apparatus (DPO loss, structural causal models, concept drift) is assembled from existing work. The genuine novelty lies in: (a) the visual-reasoning consistency protocol for generating hard visual negatives without manual annotation; and (b) the self-evolutionary knowledge graph embedding that avoids external LLM supervision. The prior CPO (NeurIPS 2025) covered the text-only version; CPO++ adds the perception component and the self-evolution loop. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations (Fig. 7) confirm that visual and textual counterfactuals contribute independently and synergistically: reasoning-only counterfactuals improve CIDEr from 0.352 to 0.363; visual-only from 0.352 to 0.375; combined 0.378. Decision robustness shows the largest gap: reasoning-only 72.3%, visual-only 67.5%, combined 78.1% under 80% interference. No error bars or multiple-seed results reported. No statistical significance tests. Code and model weights availability not stated. The knowledge graph construction (iterative semantic parsing to extract 12 entities, 53 attributes from MIMIC-CXR) is described schematically but lacks algorithmic detail sufficient for independent replication. The self-counterfactual generation quality — how often the model produces coherent, plausible attribute substitutions — is not measured. The inverse matching task (used to identify hard visual negatives) is clever but the false positive/negative rate of this discriminator is not reported. The paper is a preprint submitted to IEEE, not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on 2×2 NVIDIA A100 GPUs (4 × 80GB), which is commercially available but not lightweight; single-epoch fine-tuning is feasible for domain practitioners with access to this hardware tier. - Efficient: Training cost is not reported in GPU-hours or wall-clock time; the self-counterfactual generation adds inference passes during training (the model generates Tcand and runs inverse matching), creating overhead whose magnitude is not quantified relative to standard DPO. - Effective: Evidence shows genuine robustness improvement under counterfactual injection and consistent best-or-near-best performance across seven benchmark tasks; however, several margins over the prior CPO are sub-1%, and the robustness test is partially in-distribution (same perturbation family as training negatives). - Scalable: Tested exclusively at 7B parameters, single epoch; no experiment at larger model scales or longer training. Claims of generalizability rest entirely on zero-shot cross-domain tests, not on scaling experiments. The paper delivers a training methodology, not a software artifact; no code release is mentioned. Strengths: - The motivating phenomenon (endogenous reasoning drift) is demonstrated with concrete, reproducible evidence in Fig. 1: swapping "lung opacity" for "opacity" flips pneumonia diagnosis probability from 0.61 to 0.48 and eliminates visual attention on pathological regions — a striking and non-obvious finding. - The self-evolutionary counterfactual generation scheme, where the model acts as its own counterfactual generator and hard-negative discriminator, is a practical and principled contribution that avoids costly external LLM dependencies. - Ablations demonstrate orthogonality of visual and textual decoupling components, ruling out redundancy between the two mechanisms. - The causal formalization (structural causal graph, interventional distributions) is appropriately rigorous and connects the phenomenon to an established theoretical framework rather than treating it as an engineering heuristic. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors identify that CPO (the prior version) addressed only the text (thinking) perspective and left the perception perspective unresolved — CPO++ corrects this. They note that Long CoT self-reflection and counterfactual training are complementary and suggest future integration. No explicit acknowledgment of the circular robustness evaluation, missing scale experiments, or the sensitivity of results to knowledge graph quality. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The robustness evaluation (Section IV-D) injects the same type of counterfactual tokens used as training negatives — this tests in-distribution generalization, not adversarial robustness against novel perturbation types, which limits the strength of the safety claim. (2) The knowledge graph construction is domain-specific and manual-like (iterative semantic parsing of MIMIC-CXR); cost and transferability to a truly novel domain without existing structured ontologies is not demonstrated. (3) All experiments use a single 7B backbone trained for one epoch; it is unclear whether the method scales to larger models or longer training, and whether gains persist or erode as the base model becomes stronger. (4) The "self-evolution" loop assumes the base model can generate coherent counterfactuals before it has been trained with CPO++ — but if the model already suffers from endogenous drift, its generated counterfactuals may themselves be unreliable; no quality metric for synthesized counterfactuals is reported. (5) No comparison against simpler inference-time robustness baselines (e.g., self-consistency sampling, majority voting, temperature annealing) that might address the same symptom without training cost. Bottom Line: A practitioner building safety-critical MLLM systems will find the core finding — that near-synonymic token choices can collapse visual grounding in fine-tuned MLLMs — genuinely important and worth acting on, and the CPO++ framework provides a concrete, self-supervised training recipe to address it, though the scale and scope of validation remain narrow for a method targeting deployment in high-stakes domains.
Citations 2604.15705 PDF
#138 — Moderately Innovative (50)

CoInteract: Physically-Consistent Human-Object Interaction Video Synthesis via Spatially-Structured Co-Generation (58 ▲ )

Xiangyang Luo, Xiaozhe Xin, Tao Feng, Xu Guo, Meiguang Jin, Junfeng Ma · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 22 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The asymmetric co-attention trick that embeds HOI structure priors into shared weights for zero inference overhead is a real engineering contribution (Novelty 65, Non-Obviousness 65). Rigor (40) is the limiting factor: 50 proprietary in-distribution clips, a non-reproducible 50-question Gemini-3-Pro VLM-QA serving as the headline metric, no code/weights release, no error bars, and a methodologically inconsistent InteractAvatar comparison. Completeness (40) and Resilience (40) are weak — no direct interpenetration measurement, no cross-domain testing, and the 'zero-overhead' framing ignores heavy preprocessing. Practical Impact (45) — promising for a narrow e-commerce niche but no artifact released. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*40 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*45 ≈ 49.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 40 Resilience 40 Impact 45
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CoInteract, an end-to-end speech-driven video generation framework that jointly trains an RGB appearance stream and an auxiliary texture-stripped HOI structure stream in a shared DiT backbone, then drops the structure branch at inference via an asymmetric attention mask to achieve zero-overhead physically-plausible human-object interaction video synthesis. - Why it's important: E-commerce and digital advertising increasingly require product-demonstration videos with realistic hand-object contact, which current diffusion models systematically fail to produce — generating hand-object interpenetration and structural hand/face collapse. - How it does it: An asymmetric co-attention mask (HOI queries attend both streams; RGB queries attend only RGB) causes the HOI stream's flow-matching loss to backpropagate through shared DiT parameters, embedding interaction geometry priors into the RGB generator even after the HOI branch is removed at inference. - The good: Removing the HOI co-generation stream drops VLM-QA from 0.72 to 0.48 (−33%), providing the strongest single piece of evidence that the auxiliary stream is load-bearing and not just a regularizer. - The missing: The entire evaluation rests on 50 proprietary clips in the e-commerce/live-streaming domain with a non-reproducible VLM-QA metric (unpublished 50-question Gemini-3-Pro questionnaire), making generalization claims unsubstantiated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building e-commerce video generation pipelines or researchers designing structure-aware training paradigms for video diffusion; skippable for those seeking rigorous, reproducible benchmarking. Problem & Motivation Speech-driven human-object interaction (HOI) video synthesis for e-commerce product demonstrations requires coordinated hand movements, precise object manipulation, and physically plausible contact. RGB-only diffusion models trained purely on pixel-level supervision have no built-in notion of 3D hand-object spatial relationships, leading to two systematic failure modes: structural collapse in hands and faces (finger merging, facial blur) and physical contact violations (hands passing through product surfaces). Prior work divides into two paradigms: multi-condition methods that require heavy per-frame preprocessing (pose extraction, multi-view object features), and multi-reference methods that lack explicit interaction-structure supervision and produce implausible grasping. Neither paradigm internalizes HOI geometry constraints within the generative backbone. Key Idea The core trick is training a dual-stream DiT where an auxiliary HOI structure stream — human body reduced to a silhouette projected from a recovered 3D mesh, fused with the object mask, with all texture removed — shares weights with the RGB stream and attends to RGB tokens under an asymmetric attention mask. The HOI stream's flow-matching loss backpropagates into the shared backbone, embedding interaction geometry priors. At inference, because the RGB stream never attended to HOI tokens (enforced by the mask), the HOI branch can be cleanly removed with zero additional cost, while the geometry priors remain encoded in the shared weights. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. (1) Data curation: raw HOI videos (40 hours of product demonstrations and live-streaming footage) are processed using Qwen-Edit to separate person and product references, SAM3 for object masks, and SAM3D-body for human mesh recovery; the projected mesh is fused with the object mask to form texture-stripped HOI structure frames; 12K high-quality clips are retained with paired RGB and HOI representations, plus face/hand bounding boxes from off-the-shelf detectors. (2) Dual-stream DiT: RGB and HOI streams are tokenized by modality-specific patch embeddings and fed into shared DiT blocks with stream-specific adaptive layer-norm modulation (scale and shift), concatenated along the width dimension with distinct 3D RoPE coordinates (RGB: w∈[0,W]; HOI: w∈[−W,0]; reference images anchored at t=30,31; historical motion at t<0). (3) Two-stage asymmetric co-attention training: Stage 1 (5K iterations) uses full bidirectional self-attention for rapid convergence; Stage 2 (2K iterations) enforces the asymmetric mask M_{i,j}=1 iff (i∈T_r, j∈T_r) or (i∈T_h, j∈T_r∪T_h). Joint flow-matching objective: L_flow = L_r + λ_h·L_h (λ_h=1). (4) Human-Aware MoE: a spatially supervised router (stop-gradient applied to hidden states before routing) uses cross-entropy loss with ground-truth region labels from bounding boxes to dispatch tokens to four experts — Shared (original DiT FFN), Head, Hand, and Base (each a small FFN with hidden dim 256). Total objective: L_total = L_flow + η·L_route (η=1). Critical design decisions: the asymmetric mask (well-justified — enables inference branch removal), stop-gradient before routing (well-justified — prevents routing loss from corrupting DiT representations), and texture stripping of HOI stream (justified — forces geometry learning, not appearance shortcuts). Key Results Evaluation is on a held-out set of 50 proprietary clips. Six baselines: AnchorCrafter, Phantom, Humo, InteractAvatar, SkyReels-V3, VACE. CoInteract achieves the highest VLM-QA (0.72; next best InteractAvatar 0.62), highest HQ hand quality (0.724; next best InteractAvatar 0.696), highest temporal Smooth (0.9951), and highest FaceSim (0.696). It is competitive on DINOid (0.671, second to InteractAvatar's 0.658 under the underline convention). AES (0.554) is below Phantom (0.579) and Humo (0.565), which the authors attribute to those methods hallucinating aesthetically pleasing but reference-inconsistent backgrounds. Ablation: removing co-generation is the most damaging (VLM-QA: 0.72→0.48, −33.3%); removing MoE drops HQ (0.724→0.658) and FaceSim (0.696→0.662); retaining HOI at inference improves VLM-QA to 0.76 and HQ to 0.738 but at 4.13× inference cost. MoE adds only 1.04× overhead. User study (24 crowdsourced evaluators, 10 cases each, blind ranking): CoInteract achieves best mean rank across Object Consistency (2.17), Human/Background Consistency (1.92), and Interaction Plausibility (1.79). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The asymmetric co-attention paradigm — where HOI-stream loss backpropagates into shared weights while the attention mask makes the RGB path independent — is a genuine technical contribution for inference-free structure supervision transfer. Individual components (dual-stream training, MoE, flow matching, 3D RoPE) exist in prior work; the specific integration mechanism enabling zero-overhead HOI geometry injection is novel. The application domain (speech-driven HOI for e-commerce) is a known target but the approach of structuring supervision via a dropped auxiliary stream is non-obvious. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study covers all four core components with consistent metrics — this is a strength. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported. No code, model weights, or dataset release is mentioned. The VLM-QA metric uses an unpublished 50-question binary questionnaire evaluated by Gemini-3-Pro, making it neither reproducible nor standardizable — it is functionally a vendor-evaluated self-report. HQ (DWPose keypoint confidence) is a proxy for hand quality but does not directly measure interpenetration. The InteractAvatar baseline comparison is non-standard: Qwen-Image compositing is applied before feeding to the model, creating a partially favorable pre-processing condition that is not applied to other baselines. The test set of 50 clips is from the same e-commerce/live-streaming distribution as the training data. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Initialized from WanS2V and generates 480p video at 40 steps with CFG=5; runs on commercially available GPU hardware, though required VRAM for the full DiT backbone is not stated. - Efficient: MoE adds 1.04× overhead at inference; the zero-overhead claim is accurate for the DiT forward pass, but the training data pipeline requires per-clip SAM3D-body mesh recovery and SAM3 segmentation whose computational cost in a deployment setting is not discussed. - Effective: VLM-QA improvement over the closest baseline (InteractAvatar, 0.62) is +16%, and ablation causally isolates co-generation as the driver; qualitative figures show reduced interpenetration, but direct geometric interpenetration measurement is absent. - Scalable: Tested only at 480p on 50 in-distribution clips; higher-resolution, longer video, and cross-domain scaling are entirely unevaluated and unclaimed. No software artifact is released; the work stops at paper form. Strengths - The asymmetric co-attention mechanism elegantly solves the training/inference efficiency tension: structure supervision is embedded in shared weights during training, then the HOI branch is cleanly removed at inference — this is backed by an ablation showing 4.13× cost if the branch is retained. - The ablation is causally informative: removing co-generation produces the largest single performance drop (VLM-QA −33%), establishing that the dual-stream design — not the MoE — is the primary contribution. - The stop-gradient before the MoE router is a principled engineering choice that prevents the auxiliary cross-entropy routing loss from corrupting DiT representation learning — a failure mode the authors correctly anticipate. - The 3D RoPE coordinate assignment — separate width ranges for RGB/HOI streams, negative temporal indices for historical motion, far-field indices for reference anchoring — is a coherent multi-modal positional encoding design that enables a single attention mechanism to handle heterogeneous input types. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that retaining the HOI branch at inference marginally outperforms the asymmetric version (VLM-QA 0.76 vs. 0.72; HQ 0.738 vs. 0.724), framing this as a deliberate quality-efficiency trade-off. They note that lower AES scores relative to Phantom and Humo reflect those methods' tendency to hallucinate aesthetically pleasing but reference-inconsistent backgrounds, positioning this as a consistency-vs-aesthetics trade-off rather than a deficit. No failure modes, domain limitations, or training costs are discussed. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 50-clip proprietary test set is in-distribution with training data (product demonstrations, live-streaming), making generalization to other HOI domains (cooking, sports, industrial tasks) entirely unevaluated. - VLM-QA is not reproducible without the unpublished question set; using a commercial model as an evaluator on a proprietary test set is a weak evidentiary standard for the headline metric. - The claim of zero inference overhead applies only to the DiT forward pass; the training data pipeline requires per-clip 3D mesh recovery (SAM3D-body) and segmentation (SAM3), which would add substantial preprocessing cost in a real deployment — this is not addressed. - No direct geometric interpenetration measurement is provided; hand keypoint confidence (HQ) and VLM-QA are indirect proxies that do not quantify the core claimed improvement. - The InteractAvatar comparison is methodologically inconsistent: applying Qwen-Image compositing to that baseline's input but not to others creates an incomparable condition. - Parameter count of the full model, training hardware, and training time are not reported, preventing cost-benefit assessment relative to simpler alternatives. Bottom Line Practitioners building e-commerce video generation systems will find the asymmetric co-attention training trick — which embeds interaction structure priors at zero inference cost — genuinely useful and worth implementing, but the 50-clip proprietary evaluation, non-reproducible headline metric, and absence of cross-domain testing leave the breadth of the claimed improvements unsupported.
Recommended 2604.19636 PDF
#139 — Moderately Innovative (50)

Enhancing Tabular Anomaly Detection via Pseudo-Label-Guided Generation

Wei Huang, Yuxuan Xiong, Hezhe Qiao, Yu-Ming Shang, Xiangling Fu, Guansong Pang · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (55) — ECDF-as-prompt and λ-weighted FRS uncertainty is a clean integration. Rigor (45) limited by no error bars on ablations, narrow 5-dataset healthcare-only scope, undisclosed MCM circularity, unexplained DRL exclusion, no code release, no LLM cost reporting. Non-Obviousness (60) — ECDF-to-prompt translation is a non-trivial bridge. Completeness (45) and Resilience (45) hurt by missing baselines and unreported compute. Impact (50) — useful plug-in but no released artifact. Weighted ≈ 50.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 45 Resilience 45 Impact 50
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PLAG, a pseudo-label-guided framework that uses an LLM to synthesize tabular anomalies guided by per-feature ECDF rarity scores derived from unlabeled data, then filters candidates via fuzzy rough set uncertainty estimation before training an MLP classifier. - Why it's important: Ground-truth anomaly labels are rarely available in practice, so methods that require them are inapplicable, and purely unsupervised methods lack anomaly-discriminative power because they model only the normal distribution. - How it does it: ECDF-based feature-level rarity decomposition (each feature scored independently by tail probability) converts tabular anomalousness into a prompt-compatible signal that guides the LLM, while fuzzy rough set upper/lower approximations filter noisy LLM output by class-imbalance-aware uncertainty rather than raw distance. - The good: Consistent F1 improvements of 0.08–0.21 across all 8 architecturally diverse base detectors on all 5 datasets, demonstrating genuine plug-and-play utility independent of the base detector choice. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to 5 small datasets (max 3,772 samples, max 30 features) all from healthcare/biology, with no reported LLM inference cost, no error bars on ablation runs, and no comparison against DRL (ICLR 2025), which is cited but excluded from baselines. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building semi-supervised tabular anomaly detection pipelines, but the narrow dataset scope and unreported compute costs require independent validation before adoption in production settings. Problem & Motivation Tabular anomaly detection in finance, healthcare, and network security suffers from extreme label scarcity — ground-truth anomaly labels are almost never available at training time. Purely unsupervised methods (OCSVM, DeepSVDD, ICL, MCM) fit only the normal distribution and produce vague decision boundaries. Methods that leverage labeled anomalies (NNG-Mix, SOEL) become inapplicable when labels are absent. A secondary problem is that existing methods compute anomaly scores globally in a latent embedding space, which dilutes localized, feature-specific anomalous signals — a particular liability given the inherent heterogeneity of tabular features, where a single extreme value in one column may constitute a genuine anomaly even if the overall embedding looks normal. Key Idea Tabular anomalies can be characterized as instances with rare feature values across multiple dimensions, measurable independently per feature via the Empirical Cumulative Distribution Function (ECDF). This decomposed, distribution-theoretic view of anomalousness can be communicated to an LLM as a structured prompt without any labeled data, enabling synthetic anomaly generation that respects fine-grained feature distributions rather than operating in an opaque latent space. The key move is converting a statistical rarity measure (tail probability per feature) into a natural-language-compatible analytical instruction that is LLM-agnostic and requires no model fine-tuning. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. (1) Pseudo-label generation: an existing unsupervised detector (default: MCM) scores the test set; the top-p1% scoring instances are designated pseudo-anomalies. (2) LLM-guided synthesis: for each pseudo-anomaly, per-feature rarity τ_ij = 2·min(F̂_j(x_ij), 1 − F̂_j(x_ij)) is computed via ECDF, quantifying each feature value's distance from the distribution center. Feature-level rarity counts are summed to form an aggregate anomaly score. A structured prompt with four sections (Task & Objective, Data Description, Analytical Method, Output Requirement) instructs the LLM to generate candidates whose aggregate scores fall within the pseudo-anomaly score range. Tested LLMs include GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4, DeepSeek-V2.5, and Qwen3-14B. (3) Two-stage filtering: Stage 1 is format verification (structural validity, feature range checks, deduplication). Stage 2 uses fuzzy rough sets: a Gaussian kernel constructs pairwise similarities; information granules aggregate each sample's relations to all others; upper/lower approximations of the anomaly class concept are computed; uncertainty is α'([x_i]) = 1 − λ · |RL|/|RU| where λ = |[x_i]|/|U| weights by granule size relative to the full system, correcting for class imbalance. Candidates above a 3σ threshold (progressively relaxed toward the pseudo-anomaly α' range) are retained. A final MLP trained with Focal Loss on normal samples plus filtered synthetic anomalies performs classification. Critical design decisions: ECDF decomposition (well-justified: scale-invariant and avoids global embedding space issues); FRS uncertainty over geometric proximity (well-justified: handles fuzzy class boundaries; the λ correction for granule size is a non-trivial contribution); progressive threshold relaxation for diversity (partially justified via ablation but the relaxation schedule is ad hoc). Key Results Five datasets from ADBench: Wbc (278 samples, 30 features, 5.6% anomaly ratio), Pima (768, 8, 35%), Cardio (1,831, 21, 9.6%), Yeast (1,484, 8, 34%), Thyroid (3,772, 6, 2.5%). Eight baselines covering heuristic augmentation (Cutout, CutMix, Gaussian noise), label-dependent generation (NNG-Mix, SOEL), and general tabular synthesizers (TabDDPM, GReaT, TabSyn) — all adapted to the pseudo-label setting. PLAG (GPT-3.5) achieves best or second-best AUC-PR and F1 on all 5 datasets; while NNG-Mix achieves higher AUC-ROC on Pima (0.7153 vs 0.6596) and Thyroid (0.9943 vs 0.9796), PLAG substantially outperforms it on AUC-PR (+0.1244 on Pima, +0.0851 on Thyroid) and F1 (+0.2630 on Pima, +0.2801 on Thyroid), confirming that NNG-Mix's AUC-ROC advantage is misleading under class imbalance. As a plug-in to 8 base detectors, PLAG yields F1 improvements of 0.08–0.21, AUC-ROC improvements of 0.03–0.16, AUC-PR improvements of 0.01–0.20. Counter-intuitive finding: larger or newer LLMs do not strictly improve generation quality — GPT-3.5-turbo matches or exceeds GPT-4 on most datasets, suggesting that numerical boundary adherence rather than reasoning capacity is the bottleneck. Ablation: removing the generation module degrades performance on larger datasets (Cardio, Thyroid) while sometimes outperforming on smaller ones (Wbc F1 0.8128 vs 0.7550); removing the selection filter causes F1 drops of 0.06–0.10 on larger datasets, with a catastrophic drop on Wbc (0.3999 vs 0.7550). All results at small scale (toy-level, max 3,772 samples). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The specific pipeline of ECDF-guided LLM prompting for anomaly synthesis combined with fuzzy rough set uncertainty filtering is novel as an integrated system. Individual components are known: ECDF-based anomaly scoring appears in ECOD [20]; FRS is an established theoretical framework; LLM tabular generation exists in GReaT. The genuine novelty is using ECDF feature-level rarity as a prompt-engineering strategy that makes LLM generation semantically grounded without any model training, and the λ-weighted FRS uncertainty measure that corrects for class imbalance in anomaly detection. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation experiments cover both major components and provide clear per-component attribution. Main Table II results are averaged over 5 independent runs, but ablation Table IV and most other experiments use single runs — no standard deviations or confidence intervals are reported anywhere, making statistical significance unassessable. No code repository is mentioned; no model weights are released; datasets are from the public ADBench benchmark. A notable circularity: MCM is both the default pseudo-label provider and one of the 8 base detectors evaluated in Table III; the MCM+ result benefits from MCM-generated pseudo-labels guiding generation that is then evaluated on MCM as the base — this self-referential dependency is not discussed. Additionally, DRL (Ye et al., ICLR 2025 — cited as [35]) is excluded from the generation method comparison in Table II without explanation, despite being a recent directly relevant baseline. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on an RTX 2080Ti (12GB); requires external LLM API access (GPT-3.5/GPT-4) or a local model, which introduces a cloud-API dependency not present in any baseline. - Efficient: LLM inference cost (token counts, wall-clock time, API cost) for generating 100–200 anomalies per dataset is entirely unreported; FRS pairwise similarity computation is O(N²) in the number of samples, which may become prohibitive beyond the tested scale. - Effective: Consistently improves F1 across all 8 base detectors on all 5 datasets; the plug-and-play evidence is the strongest signal in the paper. - Scalable: Tested only up to 3,772 samples; the paper explicitly acknowledges LLM context window limits as a reason for restricting to 5 small datasets, and does not claim or test scalability to larger tables or higher-dimensional feature spaces. The work delivers a method description and experimental results; no software artifact or code release is mentioned. Strengths - The ECDF-based feature-level decomposition for LLM prompting is a clean, non-obvious mechanism that translates statistical rarity into a prompt-compatible signal without any labeled data or model training — a genuinely useful insight. - The FRS uncertainty measure with the λ granule-size weighting factor addresses class imbalance in anomaly detection in a principled way that distinguishes a rare anomalous sample (small granule, high uncertainty) from a common normal sample with the same raw approximation accuracy ratio. - The plug-and-play result across 8 architecturally diverse base detectors is compelling evidence that the gains are not specific to a particular detector choice. - The empirical finding that LLM generation quality for tabular anomalies is insensitive to model scale (GPT-3.5 ≈ GPT-4) is practically important and provides a cost justification for deployment. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge: (1) LLM context window constraints prevent scaling to larger or more numerous datasets; (2) the diversity control mechanism is not adaptive (planned future work); (3) anomaly diversity requirements scale with dataset size, observed empirically. These are stated in the conclusion without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Dataset scope is critically narrow: 5 datasets, all small-scale (max 3,772 samples, max 30 features), all from healthcare/biology. Finance, network intrusion, and industrial datasets — the other primary deployment domains cited in the introduction — are absent, leaving generalization claims unsubstantiated. - LLM inference cost, latency, and token consumption are completely unreported, making it impossible to assess whether PLAG is practical in cost-sensitive or low-latency settings relative to baselines that require no API calls. - MCM's dual role as both pseudo-label provider and evaluated base detector in Table III creates an undisclosed circularity in the MCM+ result. - Ablation results in Table IV are from single runs only; without error bars, the differences between w/o generation and full PLAG on smaller datasets (e.g., Wbc F1 0.8128 vs 0.7550) may not be statistically meaningful. - DRL [35] (ICLR 2025), a recent decomposed representation learning method for tabular anomaly detection that is directly relevant, is cited but excluded from Table II with no stated reason. - The 3σ threshold for initial FRS candidate selection and the subsequent progressive relaxation are heuristics with no sensitivity analysis, making reproducibility on new datasets uncertain. Bottom Line Practitioners building label-scarce tabular anomaly detection pipelines will find the ECDF-guided LLM prompting and FRS filtering ideas genuinely useful, but should treat the performance claims with caution until the approach is validated on larger, more diverse datasets with proper confidence intervals and unreported compute costs are disclosed.
Search Results 2604.18266 PDF
#140 — Moderately Innovative (50)

Assessing the Performance-Efficiency Trade-off of Foundation Models in Probabilistic Electricity Price Forecasting

Jan Niklas Lettner, Hadeer El Ashhab, Veit Hagenmeyer, Benjamin Schäfer · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. The cross-market NHITS+QRA-tiny matching ChronosX-large finding (Non-Obviousness 70) is the standout — counter-intuitive and practically important. Rigor (45) is mixed: appropriate Diebold–Mariano with HAC, carbon footprinting, but no seeds/error bars on headline comparison (13.54 vs 13.63), NF implementation uncompetitive without diagnosis, Moirai feature degradation unexplained, default-vs-tuned asymmetric baseline in E1. Novelty (45) — competent application of known techniques to a new setting. Completeness (45) and Resilience (40) — single market (DE-LU 2024), key failure modes undiagnosed, headline difference may not be significant. Impact (50) — concrete trade-off numbers but no artifact released. Weighted: 0.20*45+0.25*45+0.15*70+0.15*45+0.15*40+0.10*50 = 47.5 → 48.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 50
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper benchmarks two Time Series Foundation Models (Moirai and ChronosX) against two task-specific deep learning models (NHITS+QRA and a Transformer-conditioned Normalizing Flow) for day-ahead probabilistic electricity price forecasting on the Germany-Luxembourg bidding zone, evaluating zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuned regimes alongside feature enrichment experiments. - Why it's important: Energy market participants and grid operators must quantify price uncertainty under renewable intermittency, and it is unclear whether expensive foundation models justify their compute cost over well-tuned conventional forecasters. - How it does it: Four experiment blocks isolate model comparison, hyperparameter tuning, feature group selection, and cross-market transfer learning, all evaluated using strictly proper scoring rules (CRPS, Energy Score, ECE) on a 2024 held-out test set. - The good: The cross-market experiment reveals that a tuned NHITS+QRA-tiny achieves a test CRPS of 13.54 in zero-shot transfer mode — essentially matching fine-tuned ChronosX-large (13.63) at a fraction of the model size and compute cost, a counter-intuitive and practically important finding. - The missing: The study evaluates a single test market (DE-LU); whether findings generalize to structurally different markets (e.g., Nordic hydro-dominated zones, isolated Iberian market) is untested and unaddressed. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on time-series foundation model evaluation or energy forecasting and need concrete compute/performance trade-off numbers; skip it if you need peer-reviewed claims with multi-market validation, as this is a workshop preprint with limited scope. Problem & Motivation: Day-ahead electricity price forecasting in European wholesale markets is complicated by renewable intermittency, cross-border market coupling, and structural regime changes (e.g., the 2022 energy crisis). Market participants — generators, retailers, traders — require calibrated predictive distributions, not point forecasts, to make risk-aware bidding and hedging decisions. Prior work evaluated TSFMs primarily on point forecast metrics; no systematic comparison of TSFMs vs. strong probabilistic baselines under realistic feature masking, multi-size scaling, and cross-market transfer had been conducted in electricity price settings. Key Idea: The core insight is that a lightweight, well-tuned task-specific model (NHITS+QRA) can match or surpass large foundation models in probabilistic accuracy when either augmented with domain-relevant exogenous features or pre-trained on cross-market data, undermining the assumption that scale and pre-training breadth translate into meaningful gains for this domain. Methodology: The pipeline operates in four structured experiment blocks on the DE-LU bidding zone (hourly, 2018–2024), with input horizon 168 h and forecast horizon 24 h. E1 establishes baselines with price-only inputs across three model sizes for each method. E2 applies multi-objective hyperparameter search (Optuna NSGA-II) minimizing CRPS/ECE or Energy Score/ECE on the validation split. E3 conducts forward-selection over five predefined exogenous feature groups (CO2+load, gas+synthetic price, generation mix, cross-border flows, load+generation mix) using Diebold–Mariano tests with Newey–West HAC correction at α=0.05 to gate group inclusion. E4 trains models on 14 neighboring European bidding zones and evaluates zero-shot, one-shot (one 8-day window), and few-shot (30 days) transfer to DE-LU. The NF uses a Transformer encoder-decoder conditioning a masked autoregressive flow (MAF) trained with energy score rather than NLL. NHITS+QRA generates a stochastic ensemble via Monte-Carlo dropout or SWAG, then fits per-horizon LASSO quantile regressions on the ensemble, enforcing monotonicity via isotonic regression. Critical design decisions: (1) causal 14-hour covariate masking to respect day-ahead market timing — well-justified; (2) energy score training for NF over NLL — empirically motivated but not ablated against NLL at scale; (3) one-week input window fixed from prior work — not re-validated in this study. Key Results: On the 2024 DE-LU test set, fine-tuned ChronosX-large achieves the best CRPS of 13.63 (ECE 0.057); fine-tuned Moirai-base reaches 14.22 (ECE 0.042); tuned NHITS+QRA-tiny reaches 16.87 in the single-market setting but drops to 13.54 in the cross-market zero-shot setting — matching ChronosX despite having only 115K parameters vs. ChronosX's 710M. Normalizing Flow performs poorly throughout (tuned CRPS 24.23), indicating the implementation is uncompetitive. NHITS+QRA with exogenous feature groups improves incrementally from 16.87 to 15.98 (R3+R4+R5). Moirai degrades when exogenous features are added (CRPS jumps from 14.22 to 18.39 with R1), a finding the authors acknowledge but do not diagnose. The NHITS+QRA-small and -base default configurations collapse catastrophically (CRPS 27.99 and 41.90 respectively), revealing severe sensitivity to hyperparameters. The simple historical baseline (same-hour last 28 days) achieves CRPS 19.90, beating the default Normalizing Flow entirely. The paper is a preprint submitted to a workshop (ACM e-Energy '26); results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD (minor). The empirical contribution — first probabilistic TSFM vs. task-specific comparison in electricity price forecasting across zero/few-shot regimes — is genuine and fills a documented gap. The NHITS+QRA combination in this context is claimed as novel. The energy-score-trained NF is a design variant rather than a fundamentally new method. The overall framing is a competent application of known techniques to a new setting, with the cross-market transfer finding as the most surprising result. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are partial: the paper does not ablate the energy score vs. NLL training for NF, nor does it explain why Moirai degrades with exogenous features. Multiple seeds are not reported; the authors explicitly note computational constraints prevented this. No error bars are provided on CRPS values. Hyperparameter search spaces are referenced as available on a GitHub page but no URL is given in the paper text. The Diebold–Mariano test is appropriately applied for feature selection gating. The catastrophic default NHITS+QRA performance (CRPS 41.90 for base) is a methodological concern: reporting default (untuned) task-specific models against pre-trained TSFMs in E1 disadvantages the conventional models and muddies the comparison until E2 corrects it. ChronosX is excluded from E3/E4 without quantitative justification of the computational overhead. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: All models were trained on bwUniCluster (academic HPC); the smallest competitive model (NHITS+QRA-tiny, 115K parameters) can run on commodity hardware, but TSFMs require GPU memory comparable to academic clusters. - Efficient: NHITS+QRA-tiny trains in under 1 hour and emits ~0.35 kg CO2e; Moirai-base trains in 0.2 h fine-tuning but required 1M pre-training steps on 8×A100s — pre-training cost is not the user's burden but is real; NQ cross-market training runs 14 h on the same hardware. - Effective: Evidence shows NHITS+QRA matches TSFMs under cross-market transfer and with exogenous features, and beats them in those settings — the stated problem of probabilistic calibration is addressed, though only for one market in one year. - Scalable: Tested only on DE-LU (one market, 2024 test year); cross-market training uses 14 neighboring zones, but generalization to structurally distinct markets (isolated grids, capacity-constrained zones) is not tested or claimed. The work delivers no software artifact or trained model weights — it stops at paper form with hyperparameter tables. Strengths: - The cross-market transfer experiment (E4) produces the paper's most actionable finding: NHITS+QRA-tiny achieves CRPS 13.54 in zero-shot mode without any DE-LU data, matching 710M-parameter ChronosX-large fine-tuned on DE-LU — a result that directly challenges the prevailing assumption that scale dominates. - Feature selection via Diebold–Mariano tests with HAC correction is methodologically sound and avoids naïve greedy selection, providing statistical discipline absent from most applied forecasting benchmarks. - Carbon footprinting (Table 14–16) quantifies compute cost per model and phase, making the efficiency side of the trade-off concrete rather than qualitative. - The finding that exogenous features help NHITS+QRA but hurt Moirai is unexpected and, if reproducible, is a meaningful insight about TSFM covariate integration. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that a more exhaustive evaluation of all feature combinations was not feasible within available time and computational budget. They acknowledge that NHITS+QRA requires additional seed runs to confirm stability. They note that ChronosX is excluded from E3/E4 due to computational overhead without quantifying it. They flag that feature masking for market timing is a design constraint but do not evaluate sensitivity to the mask window. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) Single-market evaluation: all conclusions are based on DE-LU 2024 test data; the German market underwent specific structural changes during Energiewende that may not represent other European markets. (2) The NF's poor performance (CRPS ~24 vs. baseline 19.90) is never diagnosed — a reader cannot tell whether the architecture, the ES loss, the Transformer conditioning, or the hyperparameter tuning is responsible. (3) Comparing default (untuned) task-specific models against pre-trained TSFMs in E1 creates an asymmetric baseline that the paper partially corrects in E2 but which still influences the headline narrative. (4) The NHITS+QRA cross-market result (13.54 CRPS zero-shot) is the paper's most striking finding, but it is based on a single run with no statistical test against ChronosX (13.63) — the difference may not be significant given the absence of error bars. (5) Moirai's degradation under exogenous features (CRPS 14.22 → 18.39) is unexplained — this is a significant failure mode that receives only one sentence of acknowledgment. Bottom Line: A practitioner evaluating whether to deploy TSFMs for electricity price forecasting will find the cross-market NHITS+QRA result genuinely useful, but should treat all conclusions with caution given the single-market scope, missing significance tests on the key comparison, and workshop-preprint status.
Citations 2604.14739 PDF
#141 — Moderately Innovative (50)

EasyVideoR1: Easier RL for Video Understanding (32 ▲ )

Chuanyu Qin, Chenxu Yang, Qingyi Si, Naibin Gu, Dingyu Yao, Zheng Lin, Peng Fu, Nan Duan, Jiaqi Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 21 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (70) is the main contributor — public open-source framework with verified evaluation harness, concrete 1.47× training and 6–7× evaluation speedups. Novelty (35) low — offline tensor caching is standard data-pipeline practice, applied here in a new context. Rigor (40) drags the score: no ablations isolate the headline interfaces (mixed offline-online, joint image-video), 200 steps is unusually short, three benchmark regressions unexplained, no comparison against Video-R1/VideoChat-R1 under matched compute. Non-Obviousness (35), Completeness (45), Resilience (40) all middling. Weighted: 0.15·35+0.15·40+0.10·35+0.15·45+0.15·40+0.30·70 ≈ 47.
systems Novelty 35 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EasyVideoR1, an open-source RLVR training framework extending EasyR1/veRL with video-specific optimizations: offline tensor caching, a 11-task reward library, hybrid offline-online training, joint image-video batching, and an asynchronous 22-benchmark evaluation pipeline. - Why it's important: Existing RL frameworks (EasyR1, OneThinker, R1-V) redundantly decode video up to 3× per training step and lack reproducible, large-scale evaluation protocols, creating a reproducibility and throughput barrier that prevents systematic video RL research. - How it does it: Offline preprocessing decouples video decoding from the training loop by caching frames as keyed .pt tensors; a VideoMetadata propagation protocol prevents double-processing across pipeline stages; vLLM's AsyncLLMEngine with chunked prefill and background I/O threads eliminates CPU-GPU serialization during evaluation. - The good: Cache-based training yields a measured 1.47× throughput improvement (797→1,175 tokens/s) with the reference model forward pass alone improving 2.85×, and after 200 GRPO steps on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct the model gains +6.6 on Video-Holmes and +6.7 on VideoMathQA. - The missing: No ablations isolate the contributions of mixed offline-online training or joint image-video training; three benchmarks regress (MMVU −1.7, Video-MMMU −0.6, TempCompass −0.3) without explanation; only one model at one scale is tested. - Read it or not: Read it if you are building or evaluating video RL pipelines — the engineering decisions and benchmark coverage are practically valuable — but do not expect algorithmic novelty. Problem & Motivation: Training large vision-language models with RLVR on video is significantly harder than on text or images. Video introduces three compounding problems: (1) CPU-bound decoding and preprocessing that must be repeated redundantly at each pipeline stage (dataset loading, rollout, reference forward, actor update), creating a major throughput bottleneck; (2) reward design complexity, since video tasks span multiple-choice QA, temporal grounding, spatial grounding, OCR, dense segmentation, and code generation — each requiring different scoring logic; and (3) evaluation fragility, where video benchmark accuracy is acutely sensitive to frame sampling rate, FPS, resolution, token budget, and prompt template — making apples-to-apples comparisons extremely difficult. Open-source frameworks like EasyR1 and OneThinker address image RL well but lack purpose-built video optimizations, unified reward routing, and evaluation pipelines whose scores match upstream model releases.
