Key Idea: The core insight is that the four main inference tuning knobs (speculative token budget γ, KV cache window k_win, quantization tier q_tier, early-exit threshold τ_exit) should be selected jointly per request rather than set globally, and that deferrable batch requests should be time-shifted to minimize carbon cost under SLA deadline constraints using a carbon intensity forecast. The practical realization of the joint optimizer is, however, simply a pre-computed lookup table indexed on discrete request classes. Methodology: Green Decoding consists of three layers. (1) An offline profiling phase exhaustively benchmarks all discrete policy vectors θ ∈ Θ against a set of request classes (varying prompt length and SLA profile) on an NVIDIA A100 80GB, measuring energy via NVML at 50 Hz, latency, and quality proxies; the optimal θ per class is stored in a lookup table. (2) At runtime, a lightweight policy engine retrieves θ from the table using prompt length and SLA profile as keys, then dynamically configures the vLLM-based inference core (PagedAttention, speculative decoding with TinyLlama-1.1B as draft, quantization layers, and early-exit heads). (3) The carbon-aware scheduler queries the ElectricityMaps API, computes a relative savings metric S_relative = (I_now − I_min) / I_now over the SLA deadline horizon, and defers the request if S_relative exceeds a configurable threshold. A safety gating mechanism collapses the effective policy space to a conservative fixed policy (γ=1, k_win=full, q_tier=FP16, τ_exit=1.0) when a toxicity classifier flags a request. Critical design decisions: (a) offline rather than online optimization — justified on reproducibility grounds but ignores runtime distribution shift; (b) single-GPU A100 evaluation — limits scope claims; (c) quality measured via unspecified "quality proxy" metrics rather than task-specific benchmark scores in the primary results table. Key Results: Tested on Llama-2-7B and Llama-2-13B with TinyLlama-1.1B as the draft model across five workloads (RAG Q&A on Natural Questions, summarization on CNN/DailyMail, code generation on HumanEval, translation on WMT16 En-De, chat on ShareGPT). Against vLLM-Default (PagedAttention + continuous batching): Table 2 reports 27.3–30.6% p95 latency reduction and 33.3–36.4% energy reduction per request across all five workloads, with quality scores of 0.89–0.94 (metric unspecified). Against static ablations (Quantization-Only, Speculation-Only, KV-Cache-Only), individual components contribute 8–15% energy savings vs. 35% for the full system. Carbon scheduling over a simulated 24-hour window using real grid intensity data achieves 50% reduction in gCO2e for batch workloads. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds are reported. The "65B parameter" model referenced in the discussion of Fig. 4b is not in the experimental setup table, making those results unverifiable. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW FORMULATION. The ELQ weighted multi-objective formulation (Eq. 2–3) is a standard scalarized multi-objective program applied to a known problem. Carbon-aware time-shifting for LLM serving is a genuine extension of prior work on HPC scheduling. The joint per-request policy selection across four parameters simultaneously is a reasonable integration contribution. However, implementing that policy as a static lookup table rather than a learned policy substantially reduces the technical novelty, and early-exit for standard Llama-2 (which lacks intermediate exit heads) is not explained as a model modification, raising questions about implementation correctness. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablation establishes that the four policy components are actually jointly optimized rather than independently tuned at lookup time. No error bars or significance tests are provided for any number in Table 2 or Figures 3–4. The paper claims an open-source release but provides no URL or repository link. The "quality proxy" used in Table 2 and Figures 3–4 is never defined — task-specific metrics (F1 for Q&A, ROUGE-L for summarization) are mentioned in the setup but the unified quality score reported is unexplained. The energy model E(R,θ) and quality model Q(R,θ) used by the lookup table construction are described as "empirically modeled functions" without any specification of how they were fitted. The figures show suspiciously smooth, synthetic-looking curves (notably Fig. 3 top panels), and the carbon scheduling simulation uses unnamed real-world grid data with no provenance. The 65B-parameter claim in Fig. 4b discussion is inconsistent with the experimental setup, which lists only 7B and 13B models. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (system built and evaluated). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A100 80GB, which is commercially available but not commodity hardware; the framework assumes vLLM 0.4.0 and CUDA 12.2. - Efficient: The lookup table policy engine adds near-zero runtime overhead; energy per request is reported at 1.2–4.0 J range (plausible for A100 at small scale), with 33–36% reduction over a non-adaptive baseline. - Effective: The evidence is directionally positive but methodologically weak — no error bars, undefined quality proxy, and an unverified early-exit implementation for Llama-2 undermine the claimed quantitative gains. - Scalable: The paper verbally claims benefits grow with model size (Fig. 4b discusses 65B parameters) but only 7B and 13B models appear in the hardware table; no multi-GPU or multi-node evaluation exists; the offline profiling cost grows exponentially with policy space and request class granularity, which is unaddressed. No software artifact is publicly available. Strengths: - The framing of LLM inference as a joint ELQ constrained multi-objective problem is well-motivated and the formalization (Eqs. 1–3) is cleaner than most prior work that treats objectives separately. - Carbon-aware scheduling applied specifically to the mixed-SLA (interactive vs. batch) LLM serving environment is a concrete and practically relevant extension of prior HPC carbon scheduling work. - The component-wise ablation (Fig. 3 bottom-right, Fig. 4f) at least attempts to disaggregate contributions, which is better than many system papers that only report end-to-end numbers. - The safety gating mechanism — constraining the policy space rather than modifying output distributions — is an architecturally clean separation of system-level safety from model-level safety. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that the offline lookup table does not adapt to dynamic runtime conditions (queue depth, changing load patterns) and propose reinforcement learning or Bayesian optimization as future work. They also note that the carbon scheduler currently performs only temporal optimization and could be extended to spatio-temporal (geographic) load shifting. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The early-exit mechanism for standard Llama-2 architectures requires inserting intermediate exit heads and fine-tuning — this is a non-trivial model modification that is never described, raising the possibility that the early-exit component is simulated rather than actually implemented. (2) The key claimed challenge — that quantization degrades speculative decoding acceptance rates — is never directly measured; no experiment shows how acceptance rate varies across (q_tier, γ) combinations, which is precisely the interaction the joint optimizer is supposed to handle. (3) Table 2 quality scores are undifferentiated across tasks (all 0.89–0.94) despite using different underlying metrics (F1 vs ROUGE-L vs pass@1), suggesting these numbers are post-hoc normalized in an undisclosed way. (4) The 50% carbon reduction figure applies only to the batch portion of the workload, not overall; the paper's abstract presents it unqualified. (5) Experiments on a single A100 with models up to 13B parameters cannot support claims about production-scale serving where the interesting regime involves multi-GPU tensor parallelism and continuous batching at 100s of concurrent requests. (6) No comparison against AlpaServe, Orca, or other production-grade adaptive serving systems — vLLM-Default with static settings is not a strong adaptive baseline. Bottom Line: This preprint describes a reasonable high-level architecture for sustainable LLM inference but delivers a prototype built on a static lookup table with unverified early-exit implementation, undefined quality metrics, no statistical rigor, and no public code — a practitioner working on green LLM serving will find more operationally complete baselines in the vLLM, Orca, or SarathiServe literatures.
Search Results
#170 — Incremental (28)

Integrating Graphs, Large Language Models, and Agents: Reasoning and Retrieval

Hamed Jelodar, Samita Bai, Mohammad Meymani, Parisa Hamedi, Roozbeh Razavi-Far, Ali Ghorbani · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical (survey) profile. Novelty (35) — taxonomy with mild differentiation via scene-graph and agent paradigms, plus decision table. Rigor (15) — no systematic search methodology, mislabeled reference [106], retained LaTeX template header, no comparison vs. competing Ren et al. survey. Non-obviousness (25) — three-axis decomposition is organizational, not surprising. Completeness (25) — uneven coverage, opaque selection. Resilience (25) — would not survive scrutiny on selection methodology. Impact (35) — unvalidated decision table. Multiple dimensions are weak and rigor is severely compromised. Weighted (empirical): 0.20*35+0.25*15+0.15*25+0.15*25+0.15*25+0.10*35 = ~25.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 15 Non-Obv 25 Completeness 25 Resilience 25 Impact 35
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents a survey that taxonomizes graph-LLM integration methods along three axes — functional purpose, graph modality, and integration strategy — across six paradigms: LLM-assisted KG construction, GraphRAG, hybrid GNN-LLM models, KGQA, scene graph integration, and graph-agent systems. - Why it's important: Practitioners choosing among graph-LLM approaches lack a systematic guide for when, why, and how to combine graphs with LLMs, leading to ad hoc or ill-fitted design choices in high-stakes domains. - How it does it: The survey organizes ~113 cited works into comparative tables and a practical decision framework (Table V) that maps use-case conditions to recommended integration paradigms. - The good: The inclusion of scene graphs and agent-based graph integration as first-class paradigms, combined with a practical "when/why/where/what" decision table, provides marginally more actionable guidance than purely taxonomic surveys. - The missing: The survey lacks any systematic literature search methodology (no inclusion/exclusion criteria), making coverage completeness unverifiable and selection bias unknown. - Read it or not: Worth a single-pass read for someone new to graph-LLM integration who needs a conceptual map; researchers already active in this space will find it too descriptive and too shallow to supersede existing surveys. Problem & Motivation LLMs encode knowledge implicitly in parameters and struggle with explicit multi-hop relational reasoning, verifiable factual grounding, and structured dependency modeling. Graph representations capture entities, relationships, and topological structure but lack semantic flexibility and natural language understanding. A growing body of work exploits their complementarity, but no prior survey systematically addresses the design-level questions of when, why, where, and what type of graph-LLM coupling is appropriate for a given task, domain, and data regime. The gap is practical: practitioners risk choosing a paradigm (e.g., GraphRAG when a hybrid GNN-LLM suffices, or agent-based integration when simple graph prompting would do) without principled guidance. Key Idea The organizing insight is a three-axis decomposition of the design space: (1) functional purpose (reasoning, retrieval, generation, recommendation), (2) graph modality (knowledge graph, scene graph, interaction graph, causal graph, dependency/program graph), and (3) integration mechanism (prompting, augmentation, training-time coupling, agent-based). The intersection of these axes defines a cell in design space for which the survey tries to identify representative methods, strengths, and failure modes. This is organizational rather than algorithmic — there is no novel technical contribution. Methodology The paper is a literature survey with no original experiments. The review process is not described: there is no stated search strategy, database, date range, or inclusion/exclusion criteria. The authors classify existing works under six paradigms and produce five comparative tables. Table I contrasts Direct LLM, RAG, and GraphRAG in terms of workflow and trade-offs. Table II covers 14 GNN-LLM hybrid methods (2022–2025) with paradigm, interaction direction, and key mechanism. Table III catalogs 21 LLM-powered scene graph frameworks by input modality, LLM role (reasoning/parsing/planning/evaluation), and graph type (2D/3D, static/dynamic). Table IV summarizes 14 graph-agent-LLM systems. Table V is the practical decision guide mapping each paradigm to use conditions and application domains. Section IX reviews applications in cybersecurity, healthcare, recommendation systems, and education/governance by summarizing individual cited papers. Key Results The survey reports no original quantitative results. Selected headline numbers from cited works include: DeCoda (obfuscated JavaScript detection) achieves F1 94–97% on two benchmark datasets; GRACE (vulnerability detection) outperforms token-based and graph-based baselines on Reveal, FFmpeg+Qemu, and Big-Vul; READS achieves reported SOTA on WebQSP and Complex Web Questions (CWQ); GraphSearch evaluated on six multi-hop RAG benchmarks shows improved answer accuracy over standard RAG. These numbers are reported second-hand with no meta-analysis, no comparison on shared benchmarks, and no attempt to assess whether improvements are large or marginal relative to baselines. Novelty Classification This is a SURVEY/POSITION paper. The contribution is organizational: a taxonomy and practical decision guide for the graph-LLM design space. It is not a new method, dataset, system, or empirical finding. The "when/why/where/what" framing is a modest differentiator from prior surveys (notably Ren et al., KDD 2024, which is cited but not compared against as a competing survey). The scene graph section and the graph-agent section receive more systematic treatment than is typical in comparable surveys, representing the strongest incremental contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations, error bars, or significance tests exist, as expected for a survey. However, the literature selection process is entirely opaque: no search query, database, date cutoff, or paper count by paradigm is stated, making the coverage unverifiable. Several citations are described in one to two sentences at a level too shallow to evaluate the claimed contributions. One apparent labeling error exists: reference [106] is described in the text as a study evaluating LLMs for software vulnerability detection on Juliet, OWASP Juliet, and CVEFixes, but the cited paper is "LLM-botguard," a framework for detecting LLM-driven bots using mixture-of-experts and GNNs — a different topic entirely. The paper also retains the "JOURNAL OF LATEX CLASS FILES, VOL. 14, NO. 8, AUGUST 2021" header from the LaTeX template, confirming this is an unpolished preprint. No code or artifact is released. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile not applicable — this is a SURVEY paper) The paper delivers no software artifact, trained model, dataset, or reusable proof technique. Its output is a taxonomy and a decision table in paper form. The practical guide (Table V) is usable for design decisions but is not validated empirically — it reflects the authors' synthesis of the literature rather than any controlled evaluation of which paradigm works best in which setting. Strengths - The inclusion of scene graphs as a distinct paradigm with a role-based taxonomy (reasoning / parsing / planning / evaluation) and a graph-type taxonomy (2D/3D, static/dynamic) is more structured than most comparable surveys. - Table V provides a directly actionable decision guide that is concrete enough to inform system design choices, unlike surveys that only describe what exists without advising on selection. - The graph-agent integration section organizes an emerging and scattered literature into a coherent category with a comparative table. - Covering six distinct paradigms with domain application examples (cybersecurity, healthcare, recommendations, governance) gives breadth that helps readers locate their domain within the landscape. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors identify four open challenges: scalability (LLM inference + graph traversal costs), graph-LLM alignment (LLMs may not respect graph constraints), benchmark fragmentation (no unified evaluation across paradigms), and trustworthiness/explainability in high-stakes deployments. They confront these as structural problems in the field, not as limitations of the survey itself. The survey does not acknowledge its own selection bias, shallow treatment of many cited works, or failure to compare its scope against prior surveys. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No systematic search methodology is reported; the set of covered papers cannot be assessed for completeness or representativeness. - The competing survey by Ren et al. (KDD 2024, reference [41]) is cited within the paper but never compared against as a prior survey, leaving unclear what this survey adds beyond that work. - Coverage is heavily uneven: the scene graph section has a detailed role/type taxonomy with 21 entries, while the healthcare and recommendation sections are brief narrative summaries of 3–4 papers each. - The mislabeled reference [106] (botguard vs. vulnerability detection) suggests insufficient editorial review of citation-content alignment. - Table V's practical recommendations are not empirically validated — there is no ablation or case study showing that following the guide leads to better system choices than alternatives. - The survey does not cover graph-LLM integration for code generation/understanding at the repository level (only one sentence in the intro), despite being a major active area. Bottom Line A practitioner new to graph-LLM integration will find this a useful 30-minute orientation, but anyone with more than passing familiarity should read a methodologically stronger survey and primary papers directly, as this work's shallow per-paper summaries and opaque selection process limit its analytical value.
Citations 2604.15951 PDF
#171 — Incremental (28)

A Cognitively Plausible Neural - Symbolic Model for Syntactic Disambiguation in Minimalist Grammar

scitepress.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (10), Completeness (15), and Resilience (15) collapse the score: the paper reports zero experiments — no model trained, no annotations collected, no comparison to any baseline. The architectural proposal is internally coherent (Novelty 55, Non-Obv 55) but every performance claim is aspirational. Impact (15) — no artifact and toy-scale lexicon (~1100 items). Weighted ≈ 28; this is a research agenda, not an evaluated contribution.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 10 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 15 Resilience 15 Impact 15
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes a neural-symbolic architecture for syntactic disambiguation that uses a Minimalist Grammar parser to exhaustively enumerate grammatically valid derivations and a neural performance module combining explicit psycholinguistic priors (Minimal Attachment, Dependency Locality Theory, Expectation/Surprisal) with a CVAE lexical encoder and Tree-LSTM to rank them. - Why it's important: Existing statistical and neural parsers conflate grammaticality with probability and cannot guarantee that all and only grammatical interpretations of an ambiguous utterance are considered, which matters for safety-critical NLP applications where misresolved ambiguity has real consequences. - How it does it: The architecture separates competence (symbolic MG parser enumerates a finite candidate set D(s)) from performance (a Boltzmann-normalized energy function combining a neural score with three weighted psycholinguistic cost functionals selects the preferred parse). - The good: The theoretical framing is coherent and self-consistent: the ablation design is well-specified, the falsifiability commitments are explicit, and the competence-performance decomposition cleanly localizes failure modes to either grammar coverage or preference modeling. - The missing: The paper reports zero empirical results — no model has been trained, no human annotation has been collected, and no numbers appear anywhere; the entire evaluation section describes future intentions, not findings. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in cognitively-motivated NLP or neural-symbolic integration who want a carefully grounded theoretical proposal; skip if you need empirical evidence that the approach works at any scale. Problem & Motivation: Natural language surfaces hierarchical syntactic structures as linear strings, creating many-to-one mappings where a single utterance admits multiple grammatical interpretations. Existing systems handle this poorly in complementary ways: LLMs resolve ambiguity implicitly via next-token probability and cannot guarantee they enumerate all valid parses; statistical parsers (Collins, Berkeley) assign structure indiscriminately to grammatical and ungrammatical inputs, producing candidate sets bounded by a free parameter rather than grammar; and purely symbolic parsers enumerate valid structures but provide no preference selection mechanism. The gap is a system that maintains formal grammaticality guarantees while modeling human disambiguation preferences in an interpretable, data-efficient way. Key Idea: Frame syntactic disambiguation as selection over a grammar-delimited finite candidate set rather than generation from an open token space. A symbolic Minimalist Grammar parser reduces the search space from approximately |Σ|^20 ≈ 10^100 possible token sequences for a 20-word sentence to typically 2–10 grammatically valid derivations. A neural-symbolic scorer then ranks this small set using explicit, ablatable psycholinguistic priors plus learned lexical-structural representations — transforming an intractable generation problem into a tractable reranking problem. Methodology: The pipeline has two modules. The competence module is a Minimalist Grammar parser (Stabler 1997 formalism, implemented by Alers-Valentín and Fong 2024) that encodes MERGE (External and Internal), AGREE, c-selectional features, φ-features, case assignment, and cyclic derivation phases; it exhaustively enumerates D(s) = {d₁,…,d_K} with no probabilistic pruning. The performance module scores each derivation d via a unified energy u(d) = s_θ(d) − w^T C(d), where C(d) = (C_MA, C_DLT, C_EXP) are three cost functionals: Minimal Attachment (node count + depth penalty), Dependency Locality Theory (integration cost summed over head-dependent edges plus storage cost), and Expectation/Surprisal (negative log-probability under a PCFG trained on MG-projected trees). Lexical representations come from a Category-Conditioned Variational Autoencoder (β-VAE objective) conditioned on MG feature bundles; these leaf embeddings feed a child-sum Tree-LSTM that produces a derivation embedding z(d), passed to a two-layer MLP scorer s_θ(d). The distribution p_Θ(d|s) is a softmax over u(d) values. Training proceeds in three stages: (A) unsupervised CVAE pretraining and PCFG estimation from parser output; (B) fitting prior weights w from 50–100 pairwise human preference judgments via logistic loss; (C) end-to-end neural training of CVAE + Tree-LSTM + MLP via pairwise ranking loss with frozen w. Critical design decisions: (i) child-sum Tree-LSTM treats children as an unordered set, matching MERGE's set-theoretic nature — well-motivated but conflates non-isomorphic trees with identical child multisets, partially addressed by structural role features; (ii) the PCFG for EXP is projected from MG derivations, avoiding commitment to CFG as the competence model — reasonable; (iii) prior weights are frozen during Stage C, preventing co-adaptation — justified for interpretability but potentially suboptimal. Key Results: None. The paper presents no experimental results. The corpus described for planned evaluation contains approximately 400 unambiguous and 140 ambiguous sentences; the lexicon covers approximately 1,100 lexical items. Target metrics stated for future evaluation include top-1 accuracy > 0.7 and Kendall's τ > 0.6 for the priors-only baseline. No baselines have been run, no human annotation has been collected, and no model has been trained or tested. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD, NEW FORMULATION. The genuinely novel contribution is the formal integration of Minimalist Grammar exhaustive enumeration as a hard constraint bounding the reranking candidate set, combined with a three-prior psycholinguistic cost framework and a CVAE+Tree-LSTM neural scorer within a single Boltzmann-normalized scoring function. The individual components — MG parsing (Stabler 1997), Tree-LSTM (Tai et al. 2015), CVAE (Sohn et al. 2015), Minimal Attachment / DLT / Surprisal as parsing priors — are all existing ideas applied in a new combined architecture; the novelty lies in the principled integration and the competence-performance separation as an architectural commitment rather than a post-hoc interpretation. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations have been run; they are described as a planned evaluation protocol. No error bars, seeds, or significance tests are reported because no experiments exist. No code, data, or model weights are released. The corpus of ~540 sentences is tiny and self-constructed by the authors, raising Goodhart's Law concerns: the grammar and the evaluation corpus are developed by the same group, and coverage of the lexicon (~1,100 items) is calibrated to the phenomena the corpus targets. No comparison to any existing neural or statistical parser appears in the results, because there are no results. The paper acknowledges using Claude (Anthropic 2024) as a writing assistance tool. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (the paper claims to describe a system, though none has actually been run). - Practical: The MG parser and neural components require only standard CPU/GPU hardware; the lexicon constraint (~1,100 items) would be the limiting practical factor for real deployment. - Efficient: Not evaluated. The candidate enumeration is polynomial-time (MGs are weakly equivalent to MCFGs), but wall-clock performance on the 2–10 candidate sets is unstated; at scale the CVAE+Tree-LSTM scoring should be fast, but the parser's scaling with sentence length and lexicon size is not analyzed. - Effective: Not demonstrated. The paper claims effectiveness will be measured by top-1 accuracy > 0.7 and τ > 0.6 against human preferences on 140 ambiguous sentences; these targets have not been met or tested. - Scalable: The 1,100-item lexicon and ~540-sentence corpus represent toy scale. No claim about scaling to open-domain text, large lexicons, or typologically diverse languages is supported by evidence. The paper explicitly flags cross-linguistic evaluation as future work. The system delivers no software artifact; it stops at paper form. Strengths: - The competence-performance decomposition is implemented architecturally, not just rhetorically: errors are localizable to grammar coverage (competence) versus preference ranking (performance), enabling scientific hypothesis testing. - The ablation design (priors-only, single-prior, neural-only, CVAE vs. category-mean, hierarchical vs. string DLT) is well-specified and would, if executed, yield interpretable contributions from each component. - The Tree vs. String DLT ablation (Eq. 11) directly operationalizes a falsifiable psycholinguistic prediction — whether preferences track hierarchical or surface-order dependency distance — giving the architecture genuine scientific content beyond engineering. - Grounding the performance module in psycholinguistically motivated priors with learnable weights makes the model a computational-level hypothesis about human sentence processing rather than a black-box regressor. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (i) absence of incremental processing — the model processes complete utterances rather than word-by-word; (ii) exclusion of semantic and pragmatic factors, which they frame as a methodological choice but note must eventually be relaxed; (iii) no timing predictions or reading-time modeling; (iv) the VAE and Tree-LSTM are described as implementation-level estimators, not cognitive claims. The authors confront these directly and with appropriate nuance, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Several critical gaps go unacknowledged. First, and most fundamental: the paper reports no results whatsoever — it is a theoretical proposal masquerading as an evaluated system, and no performance claim is backed by data. Second, the MG parser covers approximately 1,100 lexical items; at this scale the system handles toy sentences, and the path to open-domain coverage is not addressed. Third, the pairwise preference annotation protocol (50–100 sentences, 3–5 annotators) is too small to support statistically reliable weight estimation for three prior weights, let alone end-to-end neural training of a CVAE + Tree-LSTM; the paper offers no analysis of how much data Stage C actually requires. Fourth, the paper does not compare against any existing parser (Berkeley, Collins, a fine-tuned BERT-based parser, or even a simple left-corner parser with MA preferences) — the claimed advantages of data efficiency and interpretability are asserted, not measured. Fifth, the CVAE is trained on one-hot lexical embeddings from a 1,100-item lexicon, which is so small that the latent space regularization and the β-disentanglement argument are unlikely to be meaningful in practice. Sixth, the exhaustive enumeration guarantee relies on the lexicon being fixed and finite; garden-path sentences involving novel words or constructions outside lexicon coverage would simply produce an empty D(s), with no fallback mechanism described. Bottom Line: A theoretically motivated and internally consistent architectural proposal for grammar-constrained syntactic disambiguation, but one that has not been implemented or evaluated and therefore offers no empirical grounding for any of its performance claims — practitioners should treat it as a detailed research agenda rather than a deployable or validated system.
Search Results
#172 — Incremental (28)

Knowledge-Based Decision Support for E-Governance: A Semantic Analytics Platform for Public Service Delivery Optimization in Brazilian Municipal Governments

ijkmc.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Synthetic-only evaluation (690 author-generated records), undefined 'Conventional DSS' baseline, KE metric circularly defined, no ablation, possible figure-template integrity issues, predatory-venue concerns. Novelty (35), Rigor (15), Non-Obviousness (30), Completeness (30), Resilience (20), Impact (35). Composite around 28.
systems Novelty 35 Rigor 15 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 30 Resilience 20 Impact 35
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes SGDSS-BR, a four-module semantic decision support system integrating a purpose-built Brazilian Municipal Government Ontology (BMGO, 563 concepts) with multi-source data fusion, semantic enrichment, and an evidence-weighted recommendation engine for Brazilian municipal administrators. - Why it's important: Brazilian municipalities generate large volumes of administrative data from e-governance platforms but make consequential budget and service decisions without systematic access to evidence synthesized from that data, defaulting to anecdotal or politically driven reasoning. - How it does it: SGDSS-BR maps heterogeneous departmental data schemas into a unified BMGO ontology via RDF triple generation, then applies temporal pattern analysis, a Knowledge Efficiency metric, and Difference-in-Differences causal inference to produce ranked decision recommendations. - The good: The system achieves an 84.5% F1 score versus 65.5% for an unspecified "Conventional DSS" baseline, and the 53.2% improvement in administrator actionability ratings is the single most concrete outcome supporting the design rationale. - The missing: Every metric is produced on a fully synthetic dataset of 690 records generated by the authors themselves to simulate municipalities that never used the system — there is no real-world deployment, no real data, and no independent ground truth for recommendation quality. - Read it or not: Skip for researchers seeking validated systems contributions; the absence of real data and an undefined baseline make the reported numbers uninterpretable, and the domain-specific ontology engineering offers nothing transferable beyond the Brazilian municipal context. Problem & Motivation: Brazilian municipal governments operate digital e-governance platforms (ConectaGov, Plataforma de Cidadania Digital) that generate administrative records across citizen services, infrastructure, social programs, finance, and HR, but these records sit in departmental silos. Municipal secretaries and mayors making budget and infrastructure decisions routinely lack access to synthesized evidence from their own systems, relying instead on departmental reports of variable quality and institutional memory. The gap is framed not as a data shortage but as a knowledge management failure: platforms were optimized for transaction processing, not for knowledge generation and decision support. Prior Brazilian e-governance work (Cunha & Miranda 2013) confirmed that data-for-decision-support remained embryonic despite technical infrastructure investment. Key Idea: The core claim is that a domain-specific ontology (BMGO) can serve as a semantic unification layer: by mapping heterogeneous proprietary schemas from five municipal system types (CSMS, FBMS, UIMS, SPAS, HRMS) into a shared RDF representation, cross-silo querying and evidence aggregation become tractable, enabling recommendation generation that a schema-agnostic conventional DSS cannot achieve. The semantic bridging via ontology-guided schema mapping is the distinguishing architectural move — not any novel algorithm within the analytics engine itself. Methodology: The pipeline has four modules: (1) Data Integration and Fusion Module (DIFM) pulls from five municipal system types, applies a schema-mapping layer that translates proprietary schemas into BMGO-conformant RDF triples, and enforces a data quality gate (records below a completeness threshold are flagged for audit); (2) Semantic Enrichment Module (SEM) performs BMGO annotation on integrated records; (3) Knowledge Analytics Engine (KAE) applies three analytical techniques — temporal pattern analysis for seasonal demand trends, a Knowledge Efficiency metric (KE = Useful Knowledge Output / Total Knowledge Input, operationally undefined beyond the ratio of processed-to-input records), and Difference-in-Differences causal inference for policy intervention assessment; (4) Decision Recommendation Interface (DRI) assembles and presents evidence-weighted recommendations. Critical design decisions: (a) BMGO covers 563 concepts across seven domains aligned to PCASP, IBGE, and the Federal Transparency Portal taxonomy — this alignment is well-motivated for Brazilian interoperability but is asserted rather than validated against actual municipal system schemas; (b) the quality gate threshold (shown as ">0.800" in the workflow diagram) is stated but its sensitivity to threshold choice is never analyzed; (c) evidence weighting in the recommendation engine is referenced but the weighting function is never formally specified. Key Results: Evaluation used 690 synthetic records across five service categories (Infrastructure 182, Citizen Services 198, Social Programs 157, Financial Transactions 98, HR Events 55) simulating three medium-sized Brazilian municipalities over 24 months. Six municipal administrator evaluators each assessed 28 benchmark decision scenarios. Against an unspecified "Conventional DSS" baseline: Decision Recommendation Accuracy 87.8% vs. 71.2% (+23.3%); Semantic Query Precision 85.4% vs. 66.8% (+27.8%); Knowledge Recall 83.7% vs. 64.3% (+30.2%); F1 84.5% vs. 65.5% (+29.0%); average query response 118 ms vs. 347 ms (-66%); Administrator Actionability Rating 78.6% vs. 51.3% (+53.2%). KE values range from 0.836 (HR) to 0.890 (Infrastructure), computed as the ratio of records reaching the recommendation stage to total input records — a definition that measures pipeline yield, not knowledge quality. The violin plot comparison (Figure 4) shows consistently higher medians and tighter score distributions for SGDSS-BR across all five categories. Infrastructure recommendations showed the largest precision gain at 34.1%. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (domain-specific application) + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (synthetic). The BMGO ontology with 563 Brazilian-administrative-specific concepts is the most concrete deliverable. The integration architecture is a standard semantic web pipeline (ETL → ontology annotation → RDF → SPARQL-style querying → analytics → dashboard) applied to a new domain; the individual components (DSS, semantic enrichment, DID causal inference, ontology engineering) are all established techniques. The genuine novelty is the BMGO ontology scoped to Brazilian municipal classification systems — but even this extends prior work by Isotani and Bittencourt (2015) rather than departing from it. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablation study isolates which component drives improvement — removing BMGO, removing semantic enrichment, or removing the quality gate individually to assess contribution. No error bars or significance tests accompany any reported metric; with six evaluators and 28 scenarios per evaluator (168 total evaluations), statistical significance is not established. The "Conventional DSS" baseline is never named, cited, or described — its accuracy and precision numbers appear with no provenance, making the central comparison unverifiable. The KE metric is circular: "Useful Knowledge Output" is operationally equated to records that pass the pipeline, so KE measures pipeline completion rate, not epistemic value. No code, ontology files, or dataset are released. The synthetic dataset was constructed by the authors to simulate the target municipalities, meaning the system was evaluated on data it was designed to fit — a textbook Goodhart's Law concern. The journal (IJKMC) is in its inaugural volume (Vol. 1, No. 1, 2026), raising unresolved questions about peer review depth. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: The architecture relies on standard web technologies (RDF, SPARQL-style queries, REST interfaces) and runs on conventional server hardware; no specialized compute is required. - Efficient: 118 ms average query response on 690 records is not informative — at municipal scale (millions of transactions) the RDF triplestore and ontology reasoning costs are untested and likely non-trivial. - Effective: The evidence that it solves the stated problem is entirely from synthetic data scored by six evaluators against an undefined baseline; no real municipal decision outcome is measured. - Scalable: The paper does not test beyond 690 records; real Brazilian municipalities of 100,000–500,000 population generate orders of magnitude more administrative transactions annually, and scalability is listed as future work. The work delivers a system design and ontology specification; no software artifact, trained model, or dataset is publicly released. Strengths: - The BMGO ontology aligned to PCASP, IBGE, and Federal Transparency Portal taxonomies is a concrete, domain-useful artifact if released — it would address a real gap in Brazilian e-governance interoperability. - The framing of the problem as a knowledge management failure (not a data shortage) is intellectually sharp and correctly diagnoses why prior infrastructure investment has not translated to decision support. - The BPMN workflow design with an explicit data quality gate provides a deployable procedural scaffold that practitioners could adapt, independent of the semantic components. - The discussion section honestly identifies two failure modes in the 21.4% non-actionable recommendations (political feasibility blindness and insufficient evidence confidence), which is more self-critical than typical systems papers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: (a) the 21.4% non-actionable recommendation rate and its two root causes (political feasibility modeling absent, insufficient evidence confidence); (b) real-world deployment has not occurred and is listed as future work; (c) predictive analytics and broader ontology extension are absent. The authors do not acknowledge the synthetic-data evaluation problem, the undefined baseline, or the lack of statistical testing — which are the most serious methodological concerns. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The entire evaluation is conducted on self-generated synthetic data with no real municipal deployment — all quantitative results are internally constructed and cannot be interpreted as evidence of real-world effectiveness. (2) The "Conventional DSS" baseline is completely undefined: no citation, no description of its architecture, no indication of whether it uses the same data — this single omission makes the headline numbers scientifically uninformative. (3) The KE metric is not operationally defined in a way that distinguishes it from pipeline yield; "Useful Knowledge Output / Total Knowledge Input" is stated but never measured against an independent criterion of knowledge usefulness. (4) No ablation isolates the value of semantic enrichment versus simple data integration, making it impossible to attribute gains to the ontology rather than to better ETL. (5) With six evaluators, no significance test, and no inter-rater reliability reported, the actionability ratings cannot be distinguished from evaluator-specific bias. (6) Figure 4 is titled "Federated Round Knowledge Contribution — Stacked Bar with Accuracy Overlay" in the section heading, but the actual figure is a violin plot — "federated" terminology appears nowhere else in the paper, suggesting the figure may be a template placeholder never updated, raising concerns about manuscript integrity. Bottom Line: A practitioner in e-governance or knowledge management systems should not act on SGDSS-BR's reported numbers: the synthetic evaluation, undefined baseline, and missing ablations mean the paper demonstrates architectural plausibility but not effectiveness, and existing work on semantic government data integration (e.g., Ding et al. 2011's LOGD system, evaluated on real linked open government data) covers the same ground with stronger evidence.
Search Results
#173 — Incremental (25)

Beyond Structure: A Five-Aspect Model of Meaning, Sensibility, and Epistemic Determination

philpapers.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Theoretical profile. Non-Obviousness (35) and Novelty (30) are weak: the five-aspect taxonomy repackages well-established distinctions from Chalmers, Nagel, Husserl, and Floridi's levels-of-abstraction without engaging IIT, GWT, or the Kantian/phenomenological lineage on 'sensibility'. Rigor (15) is very low — irreducibility is asserted via the rhetorical move that each aspect 'answers a different question', no formal derivation, and the foundation rests on a self-citation chain to unreviewed self-published works. Resilience (20) and Completeness (25) collapse under scrutiny: the framework is unfalsifiable, defers operationalization, and its central concept (sensibility) is defined only negatively. Impact (20) — no algorithm, criterion, or follow-up-enabling formalism. Weighted: 0.25*30 + 0.20*15 + 0.25*35 + 0.10*25 + 0.15*20 + 0.05*20 = 24.
theoretical Novelty 30 Rigor 15 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 25 Resilience 20 Impact 20
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes the Five-Aspect Model (5-S Model), a philosophical framework in which entities are described under five irreducible epistemic modes — structure, system, semantics, sense, and sensibility — intended to show that meaning and qualitative experience cannot be reduced to structural description alone. - Why it's important: If correct, the framework would clarify a principled boundary between AI systems that process meaning and systems that genuinely experience, redirecting debates about machine consciousness away from functional benchmarks toward qualitative manifestation. - How it does it: The paper asserts that each of the five aspects introduces a form of epistemic access that cannot be derived from the preceding ones, situating Chalmers' hard problem of consciousness specifically at the L4-to-L5 (sense-to-sensibility) transition within the layered model. - The good: The explicit localization of the explanatory gap between functional processing and qualitative appearance within a systematic taxonomic structure is a clarifying move that makes the hard problem tractable as a structural claim rather than a free-floating puzzle. - The missing: The irreducibility of the five aspects is asserted throughout but never formally demonstrated; no criterion is given that would allow an observer to determine whether a given system exhibits sensibility, making the framework unfalsifiable and the AI implications practically empty. - Read it or not: Skip unless you are building a survey of contemporary process-ontology or consciousness-taxonomy frameworks; specialists in philosophy of mind will find the contribution thin relative to Chalmers (1996), Tononi's IIT, and the phenomenological tradition. Problem & Motivation: Contemporary structural realism and informational ontology — the dominant framework in philosophy of science and AI — describe entities in terms of relations, patterns, and configurations but cannot explain how those configurations become meaningful, contextually relevant, or experientially accessible. The question is not empirical but conceptual: no increase in structural complexity explains the appearance of qualitative experience (the hard problem of consciousness). Philosophers, AI researchers, and cognitive scientists lack a unified framework that integrates the ontological question of what exists with the epistemic question of how entities become accessible and meaningful within natural or artificial systems. Key Idea: The core claim is that epistemic access to entities is layered across five irreducible modes — structure (what is there), system (relational context), semantics (what it means), sense (why it matters, teleological relevance), and sensibility (qualitative appearance) — and that each layer presupposes but is not derivable from the one below. The hard problem of consciousness is re-located as a structural boundary between L4 and L5, not a mysterious residue but a principled limit of reductive explanation built into the taxonomy itself. Methodology: This is a conceptual-analytic position paper with no experiments, formal proofs, or datasets. The method is philosophical argument by conceptual differentiation and contrast with existing positions. The paper proceeds in three main moves: (1) critique existing structural and informational accounts for failing to explain meaning and experience; (2) introduce the 5-S Model as a replacement taxonomy with five named aspects and a schematic concentric-layer diagram; (3) apply the model to artificial systems to argue that functional performance does not entail sensibility. Design decisions include (a) treating the five aspects as aspects of the same entity rather than distinct ontological kinds — a motivated choice to avoid Cartesian dualism but one left unjustified formally; (b) grounding the framework in the author's own prior self-published works (Gamboa 2025, 2026) rather than in peer-reviewed literature, creating a self-referential foundation; (c) defining "sensibility" broadly to cover any system's qualitative accessibility rather than restricting it to human phenomenology — an inclusive move that is not cashed out with criteria. No objective function, loss, or formal inference rule is stated anywhere. Key Results: The paper reports no quantitative outcomes, experimental measurements, or formal derivations. Its "results" are the seven theses listed in the appendix, all of which are assertions. Representative claims: (1) meaning, sense, and sensibility are irreducible to structure; (2) the hard problem arises specifically at the L4→L5 transition; (3) AI systems that process structure through sense operate within L1–L4 without thereby exhibiting sensibility. No baselines are compared, no predictions are made that could discriminate this framework from competitors, and no case studies or worked examples apply the model to a concrete system (biological or artificial). The framework is developed only at the abstract level. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION. The paper re-frames existing distinctions — particularly Chalmers' easy/hard problem divide and Nagel's first-person phenomenality — within a new five-level taxonomy. The individual distinctions (structure vs. semantics, semantics vs. qualia, functional vs. experiential) are well-established in philosophy of mind; the contribution, if there is one, lies in systematizing them under a unified layered model. The claim that the 5-S Model is irreducible is standard non-reductive pluralism; its packaging as a novel framework overstates originality. The paper does not engage with closely relevant prior systematic frameworks: Husserl's phenomenological distinctions between noesis, noema, and hyle; Merleau-Ponty's sensibility-centered embodied ontology; Integrated Information Theory (Tononi 2004, 2008) which offers a formal, quantitative treatment of the structure-experience gap; Global Workspace Theory (Baars) or Higher-Order Theories — all of which address the same problem with greater formal or empirical content. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations, no experiments, no error bars, no significance tests. The irreducibility of the five aspects is the central empirical (or quasi-empirical) claim of the framework, yet it is supported only by the argument that each aspect answers a different question — a rhetorical rather than logical demonstration. The paper cites Kant and Kuhn to legitimize the claim that all cognition is mediated, but this is a widely accepted background assumption, not a novel contribution. The framework is built on a self-citation chain to two prior works by the same author (Gamboa 2025, 2026), one self-published with an ISBN and one a Zenodo preprint, neither of which has been peer-reviewed. No code, dataset, or reproducible artifact is released. The framework cannot be falsified in its current form: no operationalization of "sensibility" is given that would allow one to test whether a given system exhibits it. This is acknowledged implicitly (the author defers a "criterion" to a forthcoming paper) but not flagged as a limitation. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: THEORETICAL — the paper proves no theorems and runs no experiments; it advances a conceptual framework. - Sound: The core assumptions (aspects are irreducible, ontology and epistemology are internally coupled) are stated as theses without formal derivation; the argument for irreducibility rests on the observation that each aspect answers a different question, which does not rule out that one is a proper subset or emergent consequence of others. - General: The framework claims universal applicability to any entity (biological, artificial, imaginary), but the key concept — sensibility — is defined only negatively (not reducible to the other four), so the scope of the claim is indeterminate. - Tight: No bounds are established; the framework does not produce testable predictions or quantitative constraints, so tightness is not evaluable. - Implementable: The paper explicitly defers operationalization to a subsequent contribution; in its current form the framework produces no algorithm, criterion, or decision procedure. It remains at the level of conceptual taxonomy. The work delivers a paper-form conceptual framework only — no software, dataset, proof technique, or trained model. Strengths: - The explicit placement of the hard problem of consciousness within a structured taxonomy (L4→L5 boundary) is a genuine clarifying contribution: it makes the explanatory gap a structural feature of the framework rather than an anomaly to be explained away. - The distinction between "sense" (teleological relevance, why it matters) and "semantics" (what it means) is a non-trivial refinement that captures a real difference often collapsed in information-theoretic accounts. - The integrative ambition — coupling ontological constitution with epistemic access rather than treating them as separate — aligns with neo-Kantian and pragmatist traditions in a way that could ground further work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper explicitly states that the analysis of sensibility as a criterion for distinguishing artificial from biological systems is deferred to a subsequent contribution. The framework is described as developed "in its minimal form." Beyond this, the author does not acknowledge: the absence of falsifiability criteria, the dependence on unreviewed self-published prior work, the lack of engagement with IIT and other formal consciousness frameworks, or the circularity of grounding irreducibility claims on the claim that each aspect answers a different question. Analyst-Identified Gaps: A critical reviewer would flag the following. First, the irreducibility argument is circular: the five aspects are irreducible because they answer different questions, but "answering different questions" does not imply irreducibility in any logical or metaphysical sense — it may simply reflect a useful descriptive partition. Second, the paper ignores the most formally developed literature on its central problem: IIT (Tononi) provides a mathematical measure (Φ) for integrated experience; the paper's dismissal of structural accounts as insufficient for experience is made without engaging IIT's explicit counter-argument. Third, "sensibility" is a term with deep technical history in Kant's Critique of Pure Reason (Sinnlichkeit, the faculty of receptive intuition) and in phenomenology (Husserl, Merleau-Ponty directly theorize sensory givenness); the paper cites only Nagel and Chalmers, missing this entire lineage. Fourth, the claim that the 5-S Model "cannot be subsumed under existing forms of structuralism" is asserted but not demonstrated — Floridi's levels of abstraction (LoA) framework is a direct structural precursor that is not cited. Fifth, building a framework on a self-published self-citation chain and archiving the result on Zenodo without peer review does not substitute for scholarly vetting of foundational assumptions. Bottom Line: Practitioners in philosophy of mind, AI ethics, or consciousness studies will find the 5-S Model a modestly useful taxonomic re-labeling of well-known distinctions, but should read Chalmers (1996) and Tononi (2004) instead for the same ground covered with greater rigor and, in Tononi's case, with falsifiable formal content.
Search Results
#174 — Incremental (25)

Design and Development of a Framework for Extracting Interpretable Symbolic Knowledge from Video Streams Exploiting Core Knowledge

webthesis.biblio.polito.it · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile (M.Sc. thesis, not peer-reviewed). Rigor (25) is the dominant drag — single-seed runs, no error bars, no comparison against vanilla DQN, ICM/RND, or PPO baselines, custom toy simulators instead of ALE, no code release. Resilience (20) collapses under the central observation that the RL agent runs on clean simulator attributes, not on the U-Net/symbolic outputs — the claimed end-to-end pipeline is not actually evaluated. Completeness (25) is similarly weak with the playable-environment synthesis deferred. Novelty (35) and Non-Obviousness (35) get modest credit for the event-chain reward observation. Impact (25) is a non-released prototype. Weighted: 0.20*35 + 0.25*25 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*25 + 0.15*20 + 0.10*25 ≈ 27.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 25 Resilience 20 Impact 25
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis introduces a neuro-symbolic pipeline that combines U-Net semantic segmentation, heuristic symbolic rule inference grounded in Core Knowledge theory, and a DQN agent driven by composite intrinsic rewards to extract interpretable world models from 2D arcade game frames. - Why it's important: Deep RL agents that memorize pixel statistics fail under trivial environmental changes; a system that instead builds an explicit, human-readable causal model of the environment could generalize where black-box models collapse. - How it does it: Raw frames are segmented by a U-Net, segmented objects are post-processed into structured logs, a population-based symbolic module induces object classes and interaction rules, and a DQN trained with event-chain causal rewards learns over the reconstructed symbolic state space. - The good: The pure causal + curiosity reward (no task-specific shaping) achieves a 99% win rate on a 24-brick Arkanoid environment in 5,000 episodes, and the policy remains stable under ball-size, brick-count, and color modifications without retraining. - The missing: The full pipeline is not end-to-end: the RL agent operates on clean structured simulation attributes, not on actual U-Net outputs, and the automatic synthesis of a playable environment from symbolic rules is explicitly deferred to future work. - Read it or not: Skim the RL reward-design chapter (§4.2.5, §6.2.3) if you work on intrinsic motivation in structured environments; skip otherwise — the claimed contributions are either unimplemented or insufficiently validated against standard baselines. Problem & Motivation: Deep reinforcement learning agents trained on pixel inputs optimize fragile perceptual heuristics that collapse under minor visual changes and yield no inspectable knowledge about environment dynamics. The gap is that no existing lightweight system both extracts an interpretable causal world model from raw video and uses that model to guide a reward-free RL agent. This work targets 2D arcade-style game environments (Arkanoid, Pong) as a tractable domain for validating the hypothesis that structured symbolic representations can substitute task-specific reward engineering. Key Idea: The core claim is that an event-centered intrinsic reward — rewarding the agent proportionally to the length of causal event chains it triggers, without any knowledge of task goals — is sufficient to produce near-optimal game-playing behavior, because structured causal engagement with the environment implicitly captures goal-directed dynamics. The secondary claim is that symbolic rule inference grounded in Core Knowledge primitives (object persistence, spatial continuity, shape) produces transferable interaction rules. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. Stage 1 (Perception): a 3-encoder-block U-Net with 64/128/256/512 channels performs multi-class semantic segmentation, trained with cross-entropy loss using Adam optimizer; segmentation outputs are post-processed by a rule-based reconstruction script that corrects malformed contours against known geometric priors and assembles per-frame structured logs (position, shape, hitbox per object). Stage 2 (Knowledge Module): a heuristic population-based symbolic induction system (adopted from a pre-existing framework with minor modifications) performs object tracking via spatial/temporal proximity heuristics, correlational rule inference linking contact events to state changes, coherence-based pruning of competing interpretations, and generalization into object classes. This module was not designed from scratch; the authors explicitly state it was incorporated as a pre-existing component. Stage 3 (Agent): a standard off-policy DQN with experience replay, target network, and ε-greedy exploration operates over automatically extracted numerical state attributes (positions, velocities). The intrinsic reward is: R_total = R_event + w_causal·R_causal + w_curiosity·R_curiosity + w_density·R_density, where R_event scales with causal chain length (power-law), R_causal is the L2 difference between action-induced and no-op state transitions, and R_curiosity is the prediction error of an online forward model. Four reward configurations were ablated: pure causal+curiosity, +shaping (paddle-ball alignment bonus), +density, and all combined. Critical design decision 1: the DQN operates on structured simulation attributes, not on U-Net output — the perception and reasoning modules are not used end-to-end during RL. Critical design decision 2: the reconstructed playable environment (the intended intermediate between symbolic rules and RL training) is defined at the design level only and not implemented. Key Results: On Arkanoid (24 bricks, 5,000 training episodes, evaluation over 100 test episodes): pure causal+curiosity configuration achieves 99% win rate (last checkpoint), avg 23.98 bricks destroyed, avg 1,687 steps per winning episode, compared to a supervised reference at 100% wins and 1,791 steps. Adding only shaping (paddle-ball alignment) collapses the best-reward checkpoint to 0% wins due to reward hacking. Adding density reward achieves 100% wins on the best-time checkpoint (1,804 steps) but with high cross-checkpoint variance (57% on the last checkpoint). Combined shaping+density reaches 97% wins on the last checkpoint. Generalization experiments: ball size increased 5×, brick count increased beyond 24, and color changes — all evaluated without retraining — show stable performance, attributed to the observation space encoding normalized physical variables rather than pixels. Pong cross-domain transfer: survival time reaches ~1.3 s after 1,000 episodes, substantially slower than Arkanoid. Symbolic rule inference: correctly recovers velocity inversion on contact (vy[i+1] = -1·vy[i]) and brick disappearance on hit in deterministic settings; correctly abstains from extracting rules under stochastic bounce dynamics. Baselines: no comparison against standard DQN on pixel input, PPO, or any published intrinsic motivation method (e.g., ICM, RND) is provided. Evaluation environments are custom toy simulators, not the standard ALE Atari benchmark. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM (partial). The event-chain causal reward formulation is a minor variant of existing intrinsic motivation approaches (ICM, empowerment). The U-Net perception component is standard. The symbolic rule induction module is a pre-existing system adopted without redesign. What is genuinely novel is the specific combination of event-chain reward shaping with a domain-agnostic environment wrapper, and the empirical finding that handcrafted shaping can degrade performance when intrinsic causal reward is already informative. The cognitive framing (Core Knowledge theory as design principle) is conceptual scaffolding rather than a technical contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations isolate which symbolic components matter (tracking heuristics, pruning, class generalization) independently of each other. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for any RL experiment; all results are single-run. The symbolic rule recovery is qualitatively validated against ground-truth simulator rules but not quantitatively benchmarked. Code and data are not released. The most serious methodological concern: the RL agent operates on clean structured numerical state attributes extracted directly from the simulator, not on the output of the segmentation or symbolic modules — the claimed end-to-end pipeline is not what is actually evaluated. This substantially weakens the evidence for the system as a whole. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: The RL component trains on standard CPU/GPU hardware in 5,000 episodes on a toy simulator; the U-Net requires GPU for inference but is a standard architecture. - Efficient: No wall-clock or compute comparisons are provided; the symbolic module's computational cost under increasing rule populations is not characterized. - Effective: The reward design is effective at producing goal-directed behavior in the toy Arkanoid domain, but the state representation (clean numerical attributes from a known simulator) is dramatically simpler than raw pixels; generalization to real video is undemonstrated. - Scalable: Pong results are notably weaker than Arkanoid after the same training budget, suggesting the approach is sensitive to event density; no stress-scale testing is performed. The symbolic module faces acknowledged combinatorial growth in rule populations that is not addressed. The work delivers a software prototype (not released), not a reusable library or trained model. Strengths: - The empirical finding that a pure event-chain causal reward (no task-specific engineering) achieves 99% win rate on a multi-brick game is a concrete and non-trivial positive result, even if the state representation is favorable. - The ablation across four reward configurations cleanly demonstrates that handcrafted shaping can be harmful when informative intrinsic rewards are already present, which is a useful methodological observation. - Transfer without retraining across ball size, brick count, and color variations is genuinely demonstrated (not merely claimed) and is architecturally well-motivated. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state: (1) the reconstructed playable environment is design-level only and not implemented; (2) the knowledge base is immature; (3) the symbolic module lacks formal causal or counterfactual reasoning; (4) rule induction may face combinatorial growth; (5) the perceptual module is not fully integrated (segmentation outputs are refined post hoc, not fed directly to the symbolic module). These admissions are candid and credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The authors do not acknowledge that (1) the RL evaluation does not test the claimed end-to-end pipeline — the DQN never receives input from the U-Net or symbolic module during training, making most of the framework irrelevant to the performance numbers; (2) the "causal impact bonus" is purely operational (state-difference vs. no-op) and does not implement causal inference in any formal sense; (3) no comparison against vanilla DQN on clean state features is provided, so it is unknown whether the intrinsic reward adds value over a standard extrinsic score; (4) all evaluation environments are custom toy simulators far simpler than ALE games; (5) single-seed evaluation without confidence intervals makes all performance numbers unreliable. The thesis is an M.Sc. thesis from Politecnico di Torino, March 2026 — it is not peer-reviewed. Bottom Line: The RL reward-design experiments contain a useful positive result, but the system's headline contribution — an end-to-end pipeline from pixels to symbolic world model to RL — is not actually implemented or evaluated, making this a proof-of-concept thesis whose core claim rests on a gap rather than on evidence.
Search Results
#175 — Incremental (22)

Fact4ac at the Financial Misinformation Detection Challenge Task: Reference-Free Financial Misinformation Detection via Fine-Tuning and Few-Shot Prompting of …

Cuong Hoang, Le-Minh Nguyen · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (15) — a shared-task LoRA fine-tuning recipe with no algorithmic contribution. Rigor (30) — single-run leaderboard numbers, no error bars, 0.4% margin over runner-up not significant; likely memorization of dataset construction artifacts. Resilience (20) — author-acknowledged failure on out-of-distribution numeric manipulations is exactly the real-world threat model. Impact (30) — fine-tuned weights on HuggingFace but tied to one shared task. Weighted ≈ 22.
empirical Novelty 15 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 15 Completeness 35 Resilience 20 Impact 30
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper describes the first-place system at the MisD@ICWSM2026 shared task, applying LoRA fine-tuning of Qwen2.5 models (14B and 32B) to binary classification of financially manipulated text paragraphs without external reference sources. - Why it's important: Detecting financially manipulated narratives (e.g., subtly altered numeric metrics in news reports) without access to ground-truth external sources is a practically relevant problem, as real-world misinformation monitoring cannot always rely on verified reference databases. - How it does it: Qwen2.5 models are fine-tuned via LoRA on task training data using a 2-shot prompt template, allowing the model to internalize distributional patterns of manipulation rather than retrieving external facts. - The good: Fine-tuning jumps models from near-random (~50% accuracy) to 94–97% accuracy across all model sizes, a 40%+ absolute gain, and the 32B model achieves 97% F1 on the held-out dev set. - The missing: The paper provides no evidence the method generalizes beyond this specific dataset's manipulation style; the error analysis itself reveals the approach fails on out-of-distribution numeric manipulations, which are exactly the real-world threat. - Read it or not: Skip unless you are participating in the same shared task or need a minimal baseline recipe for LoRA fine-tuning of Qwen2.5 on financial NLP classification; there is no transferable scientific finding beyond "fine-tuning beats zero-shot on in-distribution data." Problem & Motivation: Financial news and analyst reports can be manipulated through subtle alterations — particularly numeric changes (e.g., changing a stock gain from 11.2% to 0.6%) — that mislead investors without triggering syntactic anomalies detectable by rule-based systems. The specific challenge is reference-free detection: the model must identify manipulation from the text alone, without access to external databases or paired authentic versions. This is framed as a binary classification task over the RFC-BENCH dataset (Jiang et al. 2026), a benchmark explicitly constructed for this setting. Pretrained LLMs fail at this task under zero-shot and few-shot conditions (≈50% accuracy), establishing the need for domain-specific adaptation. Key Idea: The paper's central claim is that LoRA fine-tuning on task-specific training data teaches models to detect distributional signatures of financial manipulation (flawed causal reasoning, sentiment misalignment) without memorizing facts — enabling inference-time classification without any reference. This is not a novel insight; it is a straightforward application of standard parameter-efficient fine-tuning to a new domain. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. First, zero-shot and 2-shot prompt templates are constructed, with one True and one False exemplar hand-selected to serve as demonstrations. Second, LoRA fine-tuning is applied to Qwen2.5 models ranging from 0.5B to 32B parameters, using the shared task's training data at an 80:20 train/validation split (revised from 70:15:15 after observing class imbalance in splits). Third, inference is run with the fine-tuned model using the same prompt template. The classification objective is a standard binary sequence classification loss; no custom objective is introduced. Critical design decisions: (1) restricting to 2-shot rather than 8-shot, justified by prior work showing negligible gain at 8-shot for sub-32B architectures; (2) switching to 80:20 split for final models after discovering the 70:15:15 split caused class absence in some partitions — a reasonable correction but indicative of limited data handling rigor; (3) selecting Qwen2.5 over larger Qwen3 models for computational efficiency, not performance maximization. Key Results: On the shared task leaderboard (MisD@ICWSM2026), the system achieves 95.4% accuracy / 95.4% F1 on the public test set and 96.3% accuracy / 96.29% F1 on the private test set, ranking first out of six teams. The nearest competitor (Alsper on private test) scores 95.9% F1, a gap of 0.4%. The GPT-4.1 2-shot baseline scores 55.2% F1 on the public test. On the internal dev set, all fine-tuned models regardless of size reach 94–97% accuracy, versus 49–56% for pretrained baselines. The 32B few-shot fine-tuned model achieves the best dev accuracy at 97%. Notably, fine-tuning with few-shot prompts hurts most sub-32B variants relative to zero-shot fine-tuning, suggesting the few-shot template introduces optimization bias at smaller scales. The dataset size and test set size are not stated in the paper. Novelty Classification: ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION applied to a NEW PROBLEM (the specific RFC-BENCH shared task). The method is a standard combination of LoRA fine-tuning and in-context prompting applied to a new domain-specific dataset. There is no new algorithm, no new formulation, and no new theoretical insight. The contribution is exclusively the winning submission recipe and the associated ablation across model sizes. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations across six model sizes (0.5B–32B) with zero-shot vs. few-shot variants provide meaningful coverage. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported — all numbers are single-run results on competition test sets. Fine-tuned models (14B and 32B) are released on HuggingFace (https://huggingface.co/KaiNKaiho), which supports reproducibility. The dataset is controlled by the shared task organizers (RFC-BENCH); accessibility for independent replication is not confirmed. A methodological concern: the 80:20 final training split trains on data that overlaps with the reported dev set comparisons, making it unclear whether the final leaderboard models were evaluated on held-out data or on training-proximate samples. The claim that fine-tuning "implicitly" learns linguistic cues rather than memorizing facts is asserted, not demonstrated — the error analysis reveals exactly the opposite: the model fails on manipulation patterns absent from training. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Qwen2.5-14B and 32B with LoRA require a single or dual A100-class GPU, which is commercially available but non-trivial for production deployment. - Efficient: LoRA fine-tuning is parameter-efficient relative to full fine-tuning, but runtime inference cost for a 32B model is high for streaming financial news; no latency or throughput numbers are reported. - Effective: The method achieves 96.3% accuracy on the private test set, but this is on a balanced in-distribution dataset; the error analysis shows it fails on out-of-distribution numeric manipulations, which are precisely the most dangerous real-world case. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 32B parameters; no evidence of performance under distribution shift, adversarial manipulation styles, or higher-volume financial text streams. The work delivers fine-tuned model weights on HuggingFace but no library, CLI, or dataset release beyond the competition artifacts. Strengths: - The systematic sweep across six Qwen2.5 model sizes (0.5B–32B) under four conditions (pretrained/fine-tuned × zero-shot/few-shot) provides a clean empirical characterization of where fine-tuning helps and where few-shot prompting backfires for smaller models. - The error analysis identifying that false negatives concentrate on novel out-of-distribution narratives is the most scientifically honest and interesting finding in the paper — it precisely characterizes the failure mode of the reference-free paradigm. - The observation that few-shot fine-tuning degrades performance for sub-32B models while benefiting the 32B model is a non-obvious, replicable finding with practical implications for prompt selection during fine-tuning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) evaluation is restricted to Qwen2.5 only, limiting generalizability claims; (2) sequence classification bypasses explicit reasoning chains, limiting interpretability; (3) the system fails on purely numeric manipulations absent from training distribution. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper does not acknowledge the most fundamental concern: the 40%+ accuracy jump from fine-tuning almost certainly reflects pattern memorization of the dataset's manipulation construction methodology rather than any generalizable misinformation detection capability. The RFC-BENCH dataset is likely generated by a systematic process (e.g., replacing numeric values), and a fine-tuned model may simply learn stylometric signatures of that generation process. No out-of-distribution generalization is tested. Additionally, the competition has only six teams, two of which score below 76%, making the competitive field weak; the absolute advantage over runners-up is 0.4% on the private test, which cannot be considered statistically significant without error bars. The claim of "robustness and generalization" from public→private test improvement (95.4%→96.3%) is unjustified — this is within normal stochastic variation. No comparison to non-LLM baselines (e.g., fine-tuned BERT, RoBERTa-base) is provided. Bottom Line: This is a shared-task system report with no transferable scientific finding beyond the expected result that LoRA fine-tuning outperforms zero-shot prompting on in-distribution data; practitioners needing generalizable financial misinformation detection should look to the RFC-BENCH dataset paper itself (Jiang et al. 2026) for deeper analysis.
Citations 2604.14640 PDF
#176 — Incremental (22)

S-AI-Solver: Toward Numerical Artificial Intelligence—A Hormonally Regulated Sparse AI Architecture for the Formally Certified Resolution of Analytically Intractable …

researchsquare.com · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (15) is the dominant drag: all evaluation is on the author's proprietary SAI-UT++ testbench with self-constructed instances, no code released, no error bars in tables, and the entire prior-work reference chain is the author's own preprints — a textbook Goodhart situation. Novelty (30) is low because the core iteration is a known signed subgradient method with a re-skinned adaptive stopping criterion. Resilience (20) and Completeness (35) suffer because the only formally certified residual bound is ~0.857 (vacuous) and there is no comparison to basic derivative-free baselines (Nelder-Mead, coordinate descent). Impact (15) — no artifact released, paper itself recommends use only as a warm-start preconditioner. Weighted: 0.20*30 + 0.25*15 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*20 + 0.10*15 = 23.
empirical Novelty 30 Rigor 15 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 35 Resilience 20 Impact 15
Show full summary & analysis
